Title: Artificial intelligence for BIM content management and delivery: Case study of association rule mining for construction detailing
Abstract: The proliferation of Building Information Modeling (BIM) applications, in tandem with the extensive variation of building products, pose new demands on design and engineering firms to efficiently manage and reuse BIM content (i.e., data-rich parametric model objects and assembly details). Tasks such as classifying BIM objects, indexing them with meta-data (e.g., category), and searching digital libraries to load objects into models still plague practice with inefficient manual workflows. This research aims to improve the productivity of BIM content management and retrieval by developing an AI-backed BIM content recommender system. Using data from a case-study firm, this research extracted content from over 30,000 technical BIM views (e.g., plans, sections, details) in historical projects to build an unsupervised machine-learning prototype with association rule mining. This prototype explicated the strength of relationships among co-occurring BIM objects. Using this prototype as the backbone AI-engine in live BIM sessions, this research developed a context-aware recommender system that dynamically provides BIM users with a set of objects associable with their modeling context (e.g., type of view, existing objects in the model) and human–computer interactions (e.g., objects selected by the user). By mining association data from hundreds of historical projects, this development marks a departure from the existing prototypes that rely on explicit coding, recurring user input, or subjective ratings to recommend BIM content to users. The simulation and experimental implementation of this recommender system yielded high efficacy in predicting content needs and achieved significant savings in the time spent on conventional BIM workflows.
Authors: Abdirad, H.; Mathur, P.
Construction Application Area: BIM content management and delivery, construction detailing, object recommendation systems
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (BIM, drones, laser scanning, robotics, digital twins, sensor networks)
Methodology: The paper uses Association Rule Mining (ARM) as an unsupervised machine learning approach to mine BIM object data from over 30,000 technical views in historical projects. The methodology includes data collection from 400+ projects, data cleaning and transformation, model development using FP-Growth algorithm, simulation-based evaluation on test data, and experimental implementation with BIM users to measure time savings.
Research Goal: The research aims to develop an AI-backed BIM Content Recommender System (BCRS) that automatically predicts and recommends BIM objects to users based on modeling context and historical associations, thereby minimizing manual search and retrieval inefficiencies in BIM workflows.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses gaps in existing BCMD solutions that rely on manual queries, explicit coding, extensive user input, subjective ratings, or heterogeneous non-BIM data. It also addresses the lack of context-aware systems that learn from exogenous relationships and logical associations between different BIM objects in construction assemblies.
Future Research: The paper suggests that future research should address limitations related to generalizability across different firms and disciplines, account for new BIM objects or assemblies not present in historical data, and explore recurring maintenance tasks to keep the system updated with evolving BIM standards and practices.
Results: The simulation achieved over 80% efficacy in predicting required BIM objects for creating views. The experimental implementation with five BIM users showed statistically significant time savings of 15% (mean difference = 2.1 min; t(49) = 7.66; p < 0.001) when using the BCRS compared to conventional workflows.
AI Model Used: Association Rule Mining (ARM) using FP-Growth algorithm with Kulczynski measure for evaluating association strength
Data Types: BIM content usage data from over 30,000 technical views (plans, sections, details) across 400+ projects, including BIM object instances, view types, and transaction sets of co-occurring objects. The data was extracted from Autodesk Revit models (versions 2014-2021) and stored in JSON format for analysis.

Title: Generative BIM workspace for AEC conceptual design automation: prototype development
Abstract: Purpose: The integration and automation of the whole design and implementation process have become a pivotal factor in construction projects. Problems of process integration, particularly at the conceptual design stage, often manifest through a number of significant areas, from design representation, cognition and translation to process fragmentation and loss of design integrity. Whilst building information modelling (BIM) applications can be used to support design automation, particularly through the modelling, amendment and management stages, they do not explicitly provide whole design integration. This is a significant challenge. However, advances in generative design now offer significant potential for enhancing the design experience to mitigate this challenge. Design/methodology/approach: The approach outlined in this paper specifically addresses BIM deficiencies at the conceptual design stage, where the core drivers and indicators of BIM and generative design are identified and mapped into a generative BIM (G-BIM) framework and subsequently embedded into a G-BIM prototype. This actively engages generative design methods into a single dynamic BIM environment to support the early conceptual design process. The developed prototype followed the CIFE "horseshoe" methodology of aligning theoretical research with scientific methods to procure architecture, construction and engineering (AEC)-based solutions. This G-BIM prototype was also tested and validated through a focus group workshop engaging five AEC domain experts. Findings: The G-BIM prototype presents a valuable set of rubrics to support the conceptual design stage using generative design. It benefits from the advanced features of BIM tools in relation to illustration and collaboration (coupled with BIM's parametric change management features). Research limitations/implications: This prototype has been evaluated through multiple projects and scenarios. However, additional test data is needed to further improve system veracity using conventional and non-standard real-life design settings (and contexts). This will be reported in later works. Originality/value: Originality and value rest with addressing the shortcomings of previous research on automation during the design process. It also addresses novel computational issues relating to the implementation of generative design systems, where, for example, instead of engaging static and formal description of the domain concepts, G-BIM actively enhances the applicability of BIM during the early design stages to generate optimised (and more purposeful) design solutions.
Authors: Abrishami, S.; Goulding, J.; Rahimian, F.
Construction Application Area: Conceptual design automation, Building Information Modelling (BIM), design optimization, space planning
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (BIM, generative design / design automation)
Methodology: The research followed the CIFE 'horseshoe' methodology aligning theoretical research with scientific methods. It involved software development process prototyping with seven sequential stages, developing a Generative BIM (G-BIM) prototype that integrates genetic algorithms with BIM. The prototype was validated through a focus group with five AEC professionals conducting comparative design sessions using conventional methods versus the G-BIM tool.
Research Goal: The research aims to leverage conceptual design automation within AEC projects using BIM-integrated generative design through developing a proof of concept Generative BIM Workspace that enhances designers' abilities to procure evolving novel solutions and assist them throughout the design process with change management and model modification capabilities.
Gaps Addressed: Existing design automation and decision-making support tools mainly focus on form finding/planning and are incapable of engaging in information generation during early design stages. Previous generative tools fail to embed information into generated alternative designs and do not meet basic principles of information modelling and data management requirements.
Future Research: Due to expansion beyond time limits, the final prototype has not yet been fully examined in a range of real-life design settings. Future work aims to improve G-BIM through user participation and reflection on the design process, with propositions for further enhancement in each context of use to adjust to variable contexts and different AEC disciplines.
Results: The G-BIM prototype successfully demonstrated the ability to capture project design information and create optimal design solutions. Participants in the focus group showed higher degrees of innovation, creativity, and productivity in developing alternative strategies and designs when using G-BIM compared to conventional methods, demonstrating improved abstract thinking and producing generalizable information content.
AI Model Used: Genetic Algorithm (GA) with components including elitism, tournament/roulette wheel selection, one-point and two-point crossover, and mutation operations
Data Types: Design brief specifications including number of spaces, site boundary, number of floors, space dimensions, building parameters, and constraints. The data includes both user inputs (design requirements) and generated alternatives with associated fitness values, which are stored and can be exported/imported for reuse.

Title: A novel feature engineering-based hybrid approach for precise construction cost estimation using fuzzy-AHP and artificial neural networks
Abstract: The integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) into construction cost estimation is transforming the way engineers tackle complex challenges. AI algorithms play a pivotal role in predicting project costs by scrutinizing historical data from previous construction projects. This study introduces a novel hybrid approach combining the Fuzzy-Analytic Hierarchy Process (F-AHP) and Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) to enhance the accuracy of construction cost estimations. The F-AHP identifies the most influential features affecting project costs and assigns appropriate weights, while the ANN captures the intricate and 'unknown' mathematical relationships between these features and the corresponding costs. The approach was validated using real-world data from 96 residential and specialized housing projects in Amman, Jordan spanning from 2014 to 2021. Eleven key features are selected to train the ANN prediction model. To enhance prediction accuracy, the ANN model is optimized in terms of training/learning algorithms, hidden-neuron activation functions and the number of hidden neurons. The Qusai Newton, an exponential linear unit (Elu) function and 8 neurons were identified, respectively, as the optimal configuration, based on standard accuracy metrics. This optimized setup yields a Root Mean Square Error of 31,000 JD, a Mean Absolute Percentage Error of 3% and a maximum Coefficient of Determination of 98%.
Authors: Abu-Mahfouz, E.; Al-Dahidi, S.; Gharaibeh, E.; Alahmer, A.
Construction Application Area: Construction cost estimation
Methodology: The study proposes a hybrid approach combining Fuzzy-Analytic Hierarchy Process (F-AHP) and Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs). F-AHP is used to identify and weight the most influential features affecting construction costs, while ANN captures complex nonlinear relationships between features and costs. The methodology involves feature engineering through clustering (F0, F1, FAHP), feature selection using F-AHP weights, and cost prediction using optimized ANN models with various training algorithms and activation functions.
Research Goal: To develop and validate a hybrid framework that integrates F-AHP with ANNs for more accurate construction cost estimations in residential and specialized housing projects. The study aims to improve prediction accuracy by identifying influential features and optimizing the ANN model configuration.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of hybrid approaches combining F-AHP and ANN techniques for construction cost estimation. It also addresses the need for engineers to have expertise in integrating AI-based cost estimation tools into their design processes, which is currently lacking in the construction industry.
Future Research: Future research directions include expanding the dataset to include diverse construction projects from different regions, investigating other machine learning techniques that can be integrated with F-AHP (such as decision trees or ensemble methods), and exploring the impact of additional features like socio-economic factors or environmental variables on construction cost estimation.
Results: The optimized ANN model using Quasi-Newton training algorithm, Exponential Linear Unit (ELU) activation function, and 8-9 hidden neurons achieved impressive results with a Root Mean Square Error of 31,000 JD, a Mean Absolute Percentage Error of 3%, and a Coefficient of Determination (R²) of 98%. The model demonstrated close alignment between estimated and actual costs across the 96 construction projects studied.
AI Model Used: Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) with Fuzzy-Analytic Hierarchy Process (F-AHP)
Data Types: The dataset consists of real-world construction data from 96 residential and specialized housing projects in Amman, Jordan, spanning from 2014 to 2021. After preprocessing, 115 projects with 42 features were used, including variables such as materials costs (bricks, steel, concrete), excavation costs, finishing types, building area, number of floors, location, and total project costs measured in Jordanian Dinars (JD).

Title: Decentralizing construction AI applications using blockchain technology
Abstract: Decentralizing the Artificial Intelligence (AI) applications is deemed the next wave of Blockchain Technology (BT) in the construction industry. Most of previous research studies employed the AI and the BT separately with limited efforts providing a conceptual view for possible convergence in real-life applications. Nevertheless, such efforts do not address construction applications. This research introduces a novel tailorable decentralized AI system that utilizes BT as a computing-oriented technology. The proposed system is developed as an inference engine while having a number of interesting features. First, the system validates and audits the decision-making process while sharing and recording the input data and the computed outcomes in a synchronized trusted manner. Second, the system allows the formation of distributed AI repository that can absorb and manage concurrent use-cases while targeting different scopes and covering diverse AI branches. Third, it provides a workable solution for the AI applications' distribution problem, which hinders their wide employment. Fourth, the introduced system guarantees sustainable versioning and evolution over time for AI applications based on their performance or the newly acquired data. A case study of estimating the construction cost for road projects is provided to illustrate the system's workability and demonstrate its performance.
Authors: Adel, K.; ElHakeem, A.; Marzouk, M.
Construction Application Area: Construction cost estimation for road projects
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (BIM, drones, laser scanning, robotics, digital twins, sensor networks)
Methodology: The paper develops a decentralized AI system using blockchain technology (Hyperledger Fabric) integrated with a machine learning model (artificial neural network). The system consists of three layers: intelligent chaincode layer (holds ML models), permissioned blockchain network layer (manages actors and validation), and frontend access layer (CLI-based interface). The ML model was trained using historical road project data and embedded in smart contracts/chaincodes for on-chain inference.
Research Goal: To introduce a novel tailorable decentralized AI system that utilizes blockchain technology as a computing-oriented platform to validate and audit AI decision-making processes, enable distributed AI repository formation, solve AI applications' distribution problems, and guarantee sustainable versioning and evolution of AI models over time.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses three major gaps: (1) lack of provenance and authenticity of data sources in centralized AI systems leading to biased outcomes, (2) centralized data ownership exposing data to security risks and tampering, and (3) absence of secure sharing platforms causing repetitive efforts in developing same-purpose AI models.
Future Research: Future research can extend the system to provide a completely decentralized AI platform using blockchain and Inter-Planetary File System to collect, refine, and learn from chained data to produce on-chain trained AI models. This would enable decentralized learning processes, support image processing models and visuals' storage, and avoid repetitive records using content hashing mechanisms.
Results: The system successfully demonstrated acceptable computational speed with writing latency ≤3000ms and reading latency ≤500ms. The embedded ML model achieved cost estimation accuracy within parametric cost estimate limits with MAEP of 6.64%, 5.79%, and 9.73% for training, cross-validation, and validation sets respectively. The system proved scalable for adding new participants and capable of detecting incomplete data submissions.
AI Model Used: Artificial Neural Network (Multi-buffer Perceptron) with Levenberg Marquardt algorithm for supervised learning and hyperbolic-tangent activation function
Data Types: Historical construction project data from the General Authority for Roads, Bridges, and Land Transport in Egypt, including seven parameters: type of work, grade of road, construction duration, construction year, sector, road length, and road width. The dataset was divided into training (60 projects), cross-validation (5 projects), and validation (10 projects) sets.

Title: The attribute impact concept: Applications in case-based reasoning and parametric cost estimation
Abstract: The success of every construction project depends on the satisfactory achievement of a client's needs relating to cost, duration, and quality. Among them, costs must be managed with special awareness. In an effort to improve the estimate accuracy of cost during the initial stages of a building project, this research introduces the concept of 'attribute impact' (AI), which can measure the weights of attributes quantitatively and prioritize them. This study will also explain AI development, which adopts the impulse-momentum theorem of physics. For a case study, the project analyzes 163 public apartment buildings from 15 housing complex projects in Korea. To examine the validity of the proposed AI, the case study carries out two types of validation in terms of estimate accuracy using the parametric method and the case-based reasoning (CBR) applicability test. The validation results support the acceptable use of the suggested AI in measuring the weights of attributes and its estimate accuracy when combined with parametric or CBR estimation.
Authors: Ahn, J.; Ji, S.-H.; Park, M.; Lee, H.-S.; Kim, S.; Suh, S.-W.
Construction Application Area: Cost estimation for building projects, specifically parametric cost estimation and case-based reasoning (CBR) for early-stage construction cost prediction
Research Method: Mixed method (quantitative analysis with case study validation)
Methodology: The paper develops the Attribute Impact (AI) concept based on the impulse-momentum theorem from physics, where AI equals attribute weight multiplied by attribute range. The methodology involves: (1) collecting cost data from 163 public apartment buildings in Korea, (2) selecting 11 influential attributes, (3) calculating attribute weights using correlation coefficients, (4) computing attribute ranges from the database, (5) deriving AI values, and (6) conducting two validations - one using parametric estimation with multiple regression analysis and another testing CBR applicability by comparing AI with other weighting methods (regression coefficient, feature counting, genetic algorithm).
Research Goal: To introduce and validate the concept of 'attribute impact' (AI) that can quantitatively measure the weights of attributes and prioritize them for improved cost estimation accuracy during the initial stages of building projects, considering both the relationships between attributes and costs as well as the range of various quality buildings in the database.
Gaps Addressed: Existing attribute weight calculation methods (AHP, GA, PCA, FC, CA) do not consider the possible range of each attribute in historical databases. Since parametric and CBR-based cost estimation models depend on historical databases, attribute weights should be derived considering not only relationships between attributes and costs but also the database's diversity level and range of various quality buildings.
Future Research: Future research should validate AI logic for generalization with other building types beyond public apartments. A method to automatically update attribute weights when additional data are added to cost databases is needed. Sensitivity analysis regarding how many attributes should be used and accuracy comparisons using various methods (CBR with neural networks) should be examined. The AI concept itself requires further examination and development.
Results: Validation-I showed that using AI resulted in lower average absolute error ratios (AER) for parametric estimation (2.75%-3.29%) compared to correlation coefficients (3.22%-4.04%) and traditional unit cost method (4.04%). Validation-II demonstrated that CBR cost estimation using AI weights achieved overall lower AER (4.01% for 1-NN, 4.34% for 10-NN) and better stability compared to other weighting methods like regression coefficient, feature counting, and genetic algorithm.
AI Model Used: Case-Based Reasoning (CBR), Multiple Regression Analysis, Genetic Algorithm (used for comparison), Correlation Analysis, Feature Counting (used for comparison)
Data Types: The study uses historical cost data from priced bills of quantities of 163 public apartment buildings from 15 housing complex projects in Korea (2005-2008), including 11 attributes such as number of households, gross floor area, number of floors, height between stories, and other building characteristics. The data were normalized for escalation using Korean construction cost indices.

Title: MOCAP and AI-Based Automated Physical Demand Analysis for Workplace Safety
Abstract: Worker safety and productivity and the factors that affect them, such as ergonomics, are essential aspects of construction projects. The application of ergonomics and the identification of the connections between workers and assigned tasks have led to a decrease in worker injuries and discomfort, beneficial effects on productivity, and a reduction in project costs. Nevertheless, workers in the construction area are often subjected to awkward body postures and repetitive motions that cause musculoskeletal disorders, in turn leading to delays in production. As a systematic and widely used procedure that generates a final document or form, physical demand analysis (PDA) assesses the health and safety of workers engaged in construction or manufacturing activities and proactively evaluates ergonomic risks. However, to gather the necessary information, traditional PDA methods require ergonomists to spend significant time observing and interviewing workers. To increase the speed and accuracy of PDA, this study focuses on developing a systematic PDA framework to automatically fill a posture-based PDA form and address the physiological aspects of task demands. In contrast to the traditional observation-based approach, the proposed framework uses a motion capture (MOCAP) system and a rule-based expert system to obtain joint angles and body segment positions in different work situations, convert the measurements to objective identification of activities and their frequencies, and then automatically populate the PDA forms. The framework is tested and validated in both laboratory and on-site environments by comparing the generated forms with PDA forms filled out by ergonomists. The results indicate that the MOCAP-/AI-based automated PDA framework successfully improves the performance of PDA in terms of accuracy, consistency, and time consumption. Ultimately, this framework can aid in the design of job tasks and work environments with the goal of promoting health, safety, and productivity in the workplace.
Authors: AliAsgari, R.; Fan, C.; Li, X.; Golabchi, A.; Hamzeh, F.
Journal: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Construction Application Area: Workplace safety and ergonomics, specifically Physical Demand Analysis (PDA) for assessing ergonomic risks and work-related musculoskeletal disorders (WMSDs) in construction activities
Methodology: The paper develops a MOCAP/AI-based automated PDA framework using motion capture (MOCAP) systems with inertial measurement units (IMUs) and a rule-based expert system. The framework collects joint angles and body segment positions during construction activities, converts measurements to objective identification of activities and their frequencies using IF-THEN rules, and automatically populates PDA forms. The methodology includes laboratory tests with 10 participants performing 41 construction activities, a simulated masonry task in lab, and on-site validation with a bricklayer. Results are compared with traditional time-study methods and feedback from five certified ergonomists.
Research Goal: To develop an automated and systematic MOCAP/AI-based framework for Physical Demand Analysis (PDA) that objectively identifies construction activities and their frequencies, and automatically populates posture-based PDA forms to improve accuracy, consistency, and efficiency compared to traditional manual observation methods.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses three main gaps: (1) lack of comprehensive PDA forms encompassing common construction activities, (2) absence of quantitative definitions for construction activities based on joint angles or positions, and (3) the subjective, time-consuming, and inconsistent nature of traditional PDA methods that rely on ergonomists' manual observations and assessments.
Future Research: Future research should incorporate force sensors to determine muscle loads and force directions for improved accuracy in analyzing activities like pushing and pulling. Additional research directions include examining sensory and environmental demands beyond postural movements, integrating finger joint angle detection using glove sensors, incorporating photoplethysmography for physiological data, and extending the framework to other applications by including additional activities such as balancing, traveling, and fine finger movements.
Results: The developed MOCAP/AI framework demonstrated strong alignment with ergonomist assessments, achieving an absolute difference of less than 20% for 37 out of 41 activities in laboratory tests (average 9.34% difference) and 34 out of 41 activities in on-site tests (average 13.45% difference). The framework showed superior accuracy and consistency compared to traditional time-study methods (average 23.08% difference) and addressed issues of inter-rater variability among ergonomists (IRA of 0.99, IRR of 0.73). The automated system successfully captured subtle positional tasks involving smaller body parts that were difficult to assess using manual observation methods.
AI Model Used: Rule-based expert system (production system) with IF-THEN rules based on joint angles and coordinates
Data Types: The paper uses motion capture data collected from IMU sensors including joint angles (shoulder, elbow, pelvis, wrist, neck, ankle flexion/extension), 3D joint coordinates (position data for hands, pelvis, lower legs, shoulders), and time-series motion data captured at 60 Hz sampling rate. Data was collected from both laboratory experiments with 10 graduate students and on-site experiments with construction workers performing masonry tasks. Video recordings were also used for validation by ergonomists and time-study observers.

Title: Enhancing robustness of deep learning models against adversarial attacks for construction worker safety
Abstract: The increasing use of deep neural networks (DNNs) in construction safety systems highlights their potential but also reveals vulnerabilities to adversarial perturbations. Such weaknesses can lead to false detections, increasing the risk of accidents on dynamic construction sites. This paper advances construction safety research by developing a framework to enhance AI robustness through adversarial training (AT) using the TRADES method with L<inf>∞</inf> and L<inf>2</inf> norms on a ResNet-18 architecture. The approach was evaluated using a combined dataset of publicly available construction images and custom-collected lab data representing unsafe behaviors. Results show the adversarially trained model achieved 92.50 % benign accuracy and 90.36 % robust accuracy under L₂ attacks. To assess model transparency, LIME (Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations) was used to visualize regions influencing predictions for both benign and adversarial inputs. These improvements support safer, AI-assisted monitoring in real-world settings by enabling more reliable decision-making and reducing the risk of AI system failures.
Authors: Anjum, S.; Nnaji, C.
Construction Application Area: Construction worker safety monitoring, unsafe behavior detection, hazard detection
Methodology: The paper develops a TRADES-based adversarial training framework applied to a ResNet-18 architecture using L∞ and L2 norms under PGD attacks. The approach trains models on both benign and adversarially perturbed images to enhance robustness. LIME (Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations) is integrated to provide visual explanations of model predictions for transparency.
Research Goal: The goal is to enhance the robustness of deep learning models against adversarial attacks for construction worker safety monitoring by developing an adversarial training framework that improves model reliability and interpretability in real-world construction environments.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the vulnerability of CV-based construction safety systems to adversarial attacks, which can cause misclassifications and compromise worker safety. Prior research has not adequately explored robustness of these models against adversarial perturbations in construction safety applications.
Future Research: Future research should extend the framework to sensor-based data such as IMU and physiological signals for worker posture analysis and fatigue monitoring. Validation in real-world construction environments is necessary to assess adaptability and effectiveness in dynamic, uncontrolled settings. Comparative evaluation of advanced adversarial attacks like Elastic-Net and Carlini & Wagner alongside existing defense strategies should be conducted.
Results: The adversarially trained model achieved 92.50% benign accuracy and 90.36% robust accuracy under L2 attacks (ε=4.0, β=1.0), and 90.71% benign accuracy and 89.64% robust accuracy under L∞ attacks (ε=0.03, β=1.0). LIME visualizations confirmed that the model maintains focus on semantically meaningful regions even under adversarial perturbations.
AI Model Used: ResNet-18 architecture with TRADES (TRadeoff-inspired Adversarial DEfense via Surrogate-loss minimization) using PGD (Projected Gradient Descent) attacks under L∞ and L2 norms. LIME (Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations) for model interpretability.
Data Types: Combined dataset of approximately 2381 images including publicly available construction images from Roboflow and custom-collected lab data representing unsafe behaviors (falling, working without helmets), safe working conditions (proper safety equipment), and signs of worker fatigue. Images were resized to 224×224 pixels and normalized.

Title: Computer vision for anatomical analysis of equipment in civil infrastructure projects: Theorizing the development of regression-based deep neural networks
Abstract: There is high demand for heavy equipment in civil infrastructure projects and their performance is a determinant of the successful delivery of site operations. Although manufacturers provide equipment performance handbooks, additional monitoring mechanisms are required to depart from measuring performance on the sole basis of unit cost for moved materials. Vision-based tracking and pose estimation can facilitate site performance monitoring. This research develops several regression-based deep neural networks (DNNs) to monitor equipment with the aim of ensuring safety, productivity, sustainability and quality of equipment operations. Annotated image libraries are used to train and test several backbone architectures. Experimental results reveal the precision of DNNs with depthwise separable convolutions and computational efficiency of DNNs with channel shuffle. This research provides scientific utility by developing a method for equipment pose estimation with the ability to detect anatomical angles and critical keypoints. The practical utility of this study is the provision of potentials to influence current practice of articulated machinery monitoring in projects.
Authors: Arashpour, M.; Kamat, V.; Heidarpour, A.; Hosseini, M.R.; Gill, P.
Construction Application Area: Equipment monitoring, safety, productivity, sustainability, quality management, pose estimation of construction equipment
Methodology: The paper develops three regression-based deep neural networks (DNNs) using different backbone architectures (channel shuffle, depthwise separable convolutions, and compound scaling) to perform anatomical analysis of construction equipment. Two annotated image libraries of earthmoving equipment are used for training, validation, and testing, with data augmentation applied to increase dataset size fivefold.
Research Goal: To develop regression-based deep neural networks for anatomical analysis of articulated equipment in civil infrastructure projects, focusing on detecting critical keypoints and anatomical angles to improve safety, productivity, sustainability, and quality of equipment operations.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses limitations of marker-based pose estimation (which fails in dusty construction environments) and the disadvantages of adopting human pose estimation networks for equipment monitoring, including computational expense and performance issues with low-resolution imagery.
Future Research: Future research should focus on deploying regression-based DNNs for pose estimation of other site equipment (mobile cranes, scissor lifts, front loaders), undertaking dense keypoint estimation for detailed analysis in inclement weather conditions, and using aerial images complementary to site cameras for full body pose estimation.
Results: The DNN with depthwise separable convolutions achieved the best performance with lowest normalized error (NE) of 5.97×10⁻³ for boom_arm detection and average RMSE of 0.1975. The DNN with channel shuffle was most time-efficient at 58.44 milliseconds per image, showing approximately 4% improvement in NE compared to recent equipment pose estimation studies.
AI Model Used: Three regression-based Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) with different backbone architectures: (1) Channel Shuffle (172 layers), (2) Depthwise Separable Convolutions (154 layers), and (3) Compound Scaling with EfficientNet (290 layers)
Data Types: Two annotated image libraries of earthmoving equipment: one published dataset of 1,281 images by Luo et al. and another created from video footage by Roberts and Golparvar-Fard. Both datasets include images with annotated keypoints (cabin, boom, arm, bucket joints) and geometric information, augmented fivefold through data augmentation techniques.

Title: Artificial intelligence- and blockchain-enabled carbon emissions ledger system (AB-CELS) for sustainable construction processes
Abstract: Material transportation and on-site assembly are the building lifecycle phases that produce significant carbon emissions. However, traditional methods for capturing and recording these emissions lack automation, traceability, and immutability. This limitation hinders project stakeholders from data-driven decision-makings to promote sustainable construction practices and effectively implement regulations aimed at reducing carbon emissions. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a proof of concept for a transformational emissions ledger system that integrates an AI-powered large multimodal model for automatic parsing of emission-relevant data and a blockchain-enabled smart contract for a traceable and immutable emissions ledger. The proposed solution enables project stakeholders to automatically generate an immutable emissions ledger recorded on blockchain during the material transportation and on-site assembly phases, thereby enhancing their ability to make informed decisions regarding carbon emissions management. Additionally, this system enables regulatory approaches, including subsidies and tax incentives, all anchored in an immutable emissions ledger based on blockchain.
Authors: Aurangzeb, I.; Yoon, J.H.
Construction Application Area: Carbon emissions tracking and accounting during material transportation (A4) and on-site assembly (A5) phases of construction processes
Methodology: The paper develops AB-CELS (Artificial Intelligence- and Blockchain-enabled Carbon Emissions Ledger System) that integrates a Large Multimodal Model (LMM) for automated parsing of fuel and electricity receipts into structured JSON data, and a blockchain-enabled smart contract for calculating and recording carbon emissions in an immutable ledger. The system was validated through a proof-of-concept pilot test using real construction receipts.
Research Goal: To facilitate automated carbon emission accounting in construction processes and enable a transparent, traceable, and tamper-proof ledger of carbon emissions, empowering stakeholders to digitally monitor and document emissions throughout material transportation and on-site assembly phases.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of automation, traceability, and immutability in traditional carbon emissions tracking methods, which hinders data-driven decision-making and effective implementation of emissions regulations. It also tackles the issue of potential data manipulation and falsification in centralized carbon accounting systems.
Future Research: Future research should conduct additional case studies with longer data collection periods in real construction projects, integrate the system with regulatory frameworks (carbon credits, taxes, certifications, subsidies), and expand emissions tracking to other building lifecycle phases (A1-A3, B1-B7, C1-C4) by developing stage-specific calculation functions and emission indexes.
Results: The proof-of-concept successfully demonstrated that the AB-CELS framework can automatically extract emissions-relevant data from fuel and electricity receipts using GPT-4o (LMM), calculate carbon emissions, and record them on an immutable blockchain ledger. The system provides transparent, traceable carbon accounting through a responsive dashboard interface that displays emissions data for A4 and A5 phases.
AI Model Used: GPT-4o (Large Multimodal Model)
Data Types: Fuel and electricity consumption receipts from material transportation (A4) and on-site construction assembly (A5) phases, including receipt images containing information such as receipt number, date, time, fuel type, quantity purchased (gallons or kWh), and location details. Carbon emission factors from UK government sources (DESNZ) were also used for calculations.

Title: IRAF-BRB: An explainable AI framework for enhanced interpretability in project risk assessment
Abstract: In high-stakes project risk assessment, balancing predictive accuracy with interpretability is critical to fostering stakeholder trust and supporting well-informed decision-making. This study presents the Interpretable Risk Assessment Framework with Belief Rule-Based Systems (IRAF-BRB), an Explainable AI (XAI) framework specifically designed to improve transparency, accountability, and accuracy in risk assessment. IRAF-BRB combines Interpretive Structural Modeling (ISM) to map and analyze interdependencies among risk factors with an optimized Belief Rule-Based (BRB) model. A modified Differential Evolution Covariance Matrix Self-Adaptation (DECMSA) algorithm is employed to enhance the predictive power of the BRB model while preserving interpretability, ensuring that stakeholders can both trust and understand the model's outputs. By transforming complex risk data into intuitive visualizations, the IRAF-BRB framework enables project managers to identify key risk drivers and anticipate cascading effects, leading to proactive risk mitigation. Experimental results demonstrate that IRAF-BRB reduces Mean Squared Error (MSE) to 4.09e−4 in predicting risk levels for high-rise construction projects, outperforming traditional BRB models such as Differential Evolution-based BRB (DE-BRB) (8.29e−4) and Particle Swarm Optimization-based BRB (PSO-BRB) (2.53e−3). The statistical significance of these results was confirmed via a two-sample t-test (p<0.05), establishing IRAF-BRB as a reliable and effective tool for accurate and interpretable risk assessment.
Authors: Badhon, B.; Chakrabortty, R.K.; Anavatti, S.G.; Vanhoucke, M.
Construction Application Area: Project risk assessment in high-rise building construction, including factors like skilled labor shortages, cost overruns, schedule delays, structural design complexity, inflation, budget constraints, incorrect time estimation, and material supply chain disruptions
Methodology: The paper proposes the IRAF-BRB framework which combines Interpretive Structural Modeling (ISM) to map interdependencies among risk factors with an optimized Belief Rule-Based (BRB) system. A modified Differential Evolution Covariance Matrix Self-Adaptation (DECMSA) algorithm is employed to optimize the BRB model while preserving interpretability through four interpretability constraints.
Research Goal: To develop an explainable AI framework (IRAF-BRB) that balances predictive accuracy with interpretability in project risk assessment, enabling stakeholders to understand and trust AI-driven risk predictions while capturing complex interdependencies among risk factors.
Gaps Addressed: Traditional risk assessment methods fail to capture evolving interdependencies among risk factors and lack accuracy, while existing AI models (including conventional BRB systems) struggle with interpretability, transparency, and the ability to explain complex risk relationships, limiting stakeholder trust and practical adoption.
Future Research: Future work could integrate data-driven counterfactual explanations for "what-if" scenario analyses, explore rule reduction techniques for improved scalability in high-dimensional datasets, and validate IRAF-BRB in alternative domains beyond construction such as cybersecurity, healthcare diagnostics, and predictive maintenance.
Results: IRAF-BRB achieved the lowest Mean Squared Error (MSE) of 4.09e-4 in predicting risk levels for high-rise construction projects, significantly outperforming baseline models including DE-BRB (8.29e-4), PSO-BRB (2.53e-3), and original DECMSA-BRB, with statistical significance confirmed via t-test (p < 0.05). The framework successfully maintains interpretability while improving predictive accuracy.
AI Model Used: Belief Rule-Based (BRB) system optimized with modified Differential Evolution Covariance Matrix Self-Adaptation (DECMSA) algorithm, Interpretive Structural Modeling (ISM)
Data Types: The study uses expert knowledge-based data for initialization of belief distributions and interdependency mapping, along with test data from high-rise construction projects. Risk factors are categorized into linguistic levels (low, medium, high) across eight identified critical risk factors from literature review of 54 papers.

Title: Design of prefabricated wall-floor building systems using meta-heuristic optimization algorithms
Abstract: This paper proposes a method for the optimum design of a new industrialized precast 'Wall-Floor' building system using the Artificial Intelligence (AI), in which collaboration between the Engineer and the Architect concludes to the optimum wall configurations. The method provides an easy application of AI to precast construction industry. Conventionally, structural systems do not involve the application of AI to the overall process of construction of prefabricated structural parts despite its gained efficiency in the entire design and construction stages. MATLAB and the Finite Element software together with Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm provide the means for design, analysis and optimization of an illustrative example house having a rectangular plan. The example, under free and irregular wall arrangements, aims at achieving the optimal beam layout together with their connections. It is verified that, the proposed technique can optimize the structural performance as well as satisfying the architectural parameters and allowing easy fabrication process with minimum efforts.
Authors: Baghdadi, A.; Heristchian, M.; Kloft, H.
Construction Application Area: Prefabricated building design, structural optimization, topology optimization, wall and beam layout configuration, precast construction systems
Methodology: The paper uses meta-heuristic optimization algorithms (PSO, ICA, GA-binary) integrated with MATLAB and ETABS finite element software to optimize the design of prefabricated wall-floor building systems. The methodology involves finding optimal wall configurations, beam layouts, and connections through a multi-step process including wall dimensioning, segmentation, distance constraints, stiffness calculations, and symmetry optimization.
Research Goal: To propose a method for the optimum design of industrialized precast 'Wall-Floor' building systems using Artificial Intelligence that enables collaboration between engineers and architects to achieve optimal wall configurations with efficient structural performance, minimal fabrication effort, and architectural satisfaction.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of AI application in the overall process of construction of prefabricated structural parts and the need for a more straightforward approach in precast building construction that provides flexibility in element arrangement while minimizing material use, construction time, and the need for specialized equipment and skilled labor.
Future Research: Future studies should focus on improving the algorithms by utilizing multi-optimization algorithms and/or applying semi-supervision to enhance performance. Additionally, the method should be expanded to address complex geometries, different load combinations, and the possibility of wall rotation optimization.
Results: The proposed technique successfully optimized the structural performance of a Domino-House example with a 6×12m rectangular plan, achieving optimal wall configurations and beam layouts that satisfied both architectural parameters and structural requirements. The algorithm converged on average in 149 iterations out of 500, with architects selecting 20% of the optimum wall configurations for final architectural space design.
AI Model Used: Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), Imperialist Competitive Algorithm (ICA), Genetic Algorithm (GA-binary)
Data Types: The paper uses structural analysis data from ETABS software including wall dimensions (6×0.1m), stiffness parameters, load combinations, plan dimensions, wall-to-wall distances, and geometric constraints. The example building is based on Le Corbusier's Domino-House with 18 walls and 50 connections.

Title: Productivity analysis of trailing suction hopper dredgers using stacking strategy
Abstract: Trailing Suction Hopper Dredger (TSHD) is commonly used in dredging construction operations. Accurate productivity estimation and optimization are important for an efficient and smooth operation of TSHDs. An intelligent approach using stacking strategy for estimating TSHD productivity is proposed in this study. The proposed method involves two major modeling stages. First, the ReliefF-Granger algorithm is used to analyze data collected during real-world operation in order to extract the key factors influencing productivity from a vast amount of monitoring data. Several Artificial Intelligence (AI) algorithms were subsequently applied to fuse the extracted factors in order to achieve better generalization capability. In the second stage, a variety of heterogeneous AI models were adopted for mixed feature training prediction, and an optimal combined model was obtained through a grid search algorithm. To verify its accuracy and applicability, the proposed method was applied to a channel deepening project as a case study.
Authors: Bai, S.; Li, M.; Song, L.; Ren, Q.; Qin, L.; Fu, J.
Construction Application Area: Dredging construction operations, specifically productivity prediction and optimization of Trailing Suction Hopper Dredgers (TSHD)
Methodology: The paper proposes an intelligent approach using stacking strategy for TSHD productivity prediction. It involves two major stages: (1) using ReliefF-Granger algorithm to extract key factors from monitoring data, and (2) applying multiple heterogeneous AI models (XGBoost, SVR, LSTM, BAS-BP) for mixed feature training prediction with optimal combined model obtained through grid search algorithm.
Research Goal: To develop an accurate productivity estimation and prediction method for Trailing Suction Hopper Dredgers (TSHD) using artificial intelligence techniques to improve operational efficiency and enable real-time productivity monitoring without time lag issues inherent in traditional densitometer measurements.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the limitation that most AI-based productivity prediction studies use single models which lack generalization ability, and the challenge that traditional densitometer measurements have time lag issues that lead to errors in real-time productivity control during dredging operations.
Future Research: The paper suggests that the productivity estimation strategy can provide prediction reference for other types of construction equipment beyond dredging. Future work could extend the stacking strategy approach to other construction machinery systems and explore additional AI algorithms for feature fusion and model integration.
Results: The proposed stacking strategy achieved a determination coefficient (R²) of 0.88 on test data, significantly outperforming single models (R² ranging from 0.805 to 0.865). The method successfully identified 11 key features affecting productivity and demonstrated progressive improvement in prediction accuracy through feature fusion and model integration.
AI Model Used: XGBoost, Support Vector Regression (SVR), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Beetle Antennae Search-Back Propagation (BAS-BP), Isolation Forest, ReliefF algorithm
Data Types: Real-world operational monitoring data from Tongtu TSHD dredging project at Tianjin Port, consisting of 14,387 data points across 339 monitoring parameters from five subsystems (power system, ship motion, drag head, dredge pump-pipeline, and spoil hopper). Data includes speed over ground, flow, vacuum suction, drag angle, dredge pump power, and other operational parameters.

Title: AI-based prediction of independent construction safety outcomes from universal attributes
Abstract: This paper significantly improves on, and finishes to validate, an approach proposed in previous research in which safety outcomes were predicted from attributes with machine learning. Like in the original study, we use Natural Language Processing (NLP) to extract fundamental attributes from raw incident reports and machine learning models are trained to predict safety outcomes. The outcomes predicted here are injury severity, injury type, body part impacted, and incident type. However, unlike in the original study, safety outcomes were not extracted via NLP but were provided by independent human annotations, eliminating any potential source of artificial correlation between predictors and predictands. Results show that attributes are still highly predictive, confirming the validity of the original approach. Other improvements brought by the current study include the use of (1) a much larger dataset featuring more than 90,000 reports, (2) two new models, XGBoost and linear SVM (Support Vector Machines), (3) model stacking, (4) a more straightforward experimental setup with more appropriate performance metrics, and (5) an analysis of per-category attribute importance scores. Finally, the injury severity outcome is well predicted, which was not the case in the original study. This is a significant advancement.
Authors: Baker, H.; Hallowell, M.R.; Tixier, A.J.-P.
Construction Application Area: Construction safety - predicting safety outcomes including injury severity, injury type, body part impacted, and incident type
Methodology: The paper uses Natural Language Processing (NLP) to extract 80 fundamental attributes from raw incident reports. Three machine learning models (Random Forest, XGBoost, and linear SVM) are trained to predict safety outcomes from these attributes. Model stacking is employed to combine predictions. The study uses independently annotated safety outcomes (not NLP-extracted) to validate the approach, addressing a key limitation of prior work.
Research Goal: To validate the NLP + machine learning approach for predicting construction safety outcomes from universal attributes, demonstrating that attributes extracted by NLP can predict independently annotated safety outcomes with high accuracy. The paper also aims to improve upon previous research by predicting injury severity successfully.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the limitation of previous research where both attributes and outcomes were extracted using the same NLP tool, potentially creating artificial correlations. It also addresses the gap that injury severity was previously found to be unpredictable from attributes alone.
Future Research: Future research should test whether this method enables improved human decision-making during work planning. Researchers should also explore predicting incident occurrence (success vs. failure) using non-injury reports or success cases from site diaries and plan-of-day documents.
Results: All three machine learning models (XGBoost, RF, SVM) demonstrated high predictive skill for all four safety outcomes, significantly outperforming random baselines. Injury severity was successfully predicted, which was not achieved in previous research. Model stacking improved performance for three out of four outcomes. The NLP tool proved robust when applied outside its original domain (oil and gas vs. industrial/energy/infrastructure/mining).
AI Model Used: Random Forest, XGBoost (Extreme Gradient Boosting), Linear Support Vector Machine (SVM), and model stacking using logistic regression as meta-model. Natural Language Processing using rule-based NLP system for attribute extraction.
Data Types: More than 90,000 incident reports from a global industrial partner in the oil and gas sector, spanning work from five continents from early 2000s to 2018. The reports contained text narratives and independently annotated safety outcome fields (severity, injury type, body part, incident type).

Title: Optimized scheduling and buffering of repetitive construction projects under uncertainty
Abstract: Purpose: Construction projects are complex projects taking place in dynamic environments, which necessitates accounting for different uncertainties during the planning stage. There is a significant lack of management tools for repetitive projects accounting for uncertainties in the construction environment. The purpose of this paper is to present an algorithm for the optimized scheduling of repetitive construction projects under uncertainty. Design/methodology/approach: Fuzzy set theory is utilized to model uncertainties associated with various input parameters. The developed algorithm has two main components: optimization component and buffering component. The optimization component presents a dynamic programming approach that processes fuzzy numbers. The buffering component converts the optimized fuzzy schedule into a deterministic schedule and inserts time buffers to protect the schedule against anticipated delays. Agreement Index (AI) is used to capture the user's desired level of confidence in the produced schedule while sizing buffers. The algorithm is capable of optimizing for cost or time objectives. An example project drawn from literature is analysed to demonstrate the capabilities of the developed algorithm and to allow comparison of results to those previously generated. Findings: Testing the algorithm revealed several findings. Fuzzy numbers can be utilized to capture uncertainty in various inputs without the need for historical data. The modified algorithm is capable of optimizing schedules, for different objectives, under uncertainty. Finally AI can be used to capture users' desired confidence in the final schedule. Originality/value: Project planners can utilize this algorithm to optimize repetitive projects schedules, while modelling uncertainty in different input parameters, without the need for relevant historical data.
Authors: Bakry, I.; Moselhi, O.; Zayed, T.
Construction Application Area: Scheduling and buffering of repetitive construction projects
Methodology: The paper develops an algorithm utilizing fuzzy set theory to model uncertainties in input parameters (quantities, productivity rates, costs) and dynamic programming for optimization. The algorithm has two components: optimization (finding optimal crew formations for least cost or least duration) and buffering (converting fuzzy schedules to deterministic schedules with time buffers). Agreement Index is used to capture user confidence levels for buffer sizing.
Research Goal: To present an algorithm for optimized scheduling of repetitive construction projects under uncertainty that can model uncertainties without historical data and insert time buffers to protect schedules against anticipated delays.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of management tools for repetitive projects that account for uncertainties in the construction environment and the absence of comprehensive methodological buffer sizing techniques that do not depend on historical data.
Future Research: Future work should expand the algorithm by experimenting with fuzzy distributions other than triangular, allowing multiple objective optimization, and presenting methodologies for monitoring buffer consumption as a means of tracking schedule performance during construction.
Results: The algorithm successfully replicates previous deterministic results, demonstrates the impact of accounting for uncertainty on project cost and duration, and shows that fuzzy numbers can model uncertainties without historical data. The buffering component effectively sizes and inserts time buffers based on user-specified confidence levels using Agreement Index.
AI Model Used: Fuzzy set theory (triangular fuzzy numbers), Dynamic programming
Data Types: Project data including crew formations, productivity rates, work quantities, direct and indirect costs, and precedence relations for activities. The case study uses data from a three-span concrete bridge project with five sequential activities across four repetitive units.

Title: Feasibility of VR-generated synthetic data for automated productivity monitoring in modular construction
Abstract: This paper examines the feasibility of using VR-generated synthetic data for automated productivity monitoring in modular integrated construction. Site conditions including module shape, color, and occlusion were analyzed to assess their impact on object detection models, and models trained on real world, synthetic, and hybrid datasets were compared. Results showed that the hybrid dataset (real + synthetic) improved detection accuracy, with a 1:3 real to synthetic data ratio yielding the highest performance in this experiment (mean precision = 0.846, recall = 0.88, mAP = 89.7%). While synthetic data enhanced data diversity and detection performance, excessive reliance introduced domain gaps, highlighting the need for a balanced dataset. This paper demonstrates that VR-generated synthetic data can complement real world data, addressing data scarcity in construction site monitoring. The findings contribute to improving AI-driven productivity analysis by optimizing dataset composition and enhancing object detection accuracy in construction automation.
Authors: Ban, J.; Kim, D.; Kim, T.W.; Choi, B.
Construction Application Area: Productivity monitoring, modular integrated construction (MiC), off-site construction monitoring, automated object detection
Methodology: The paper uses computer vision-based object detection (YOLOv7) to monitor modular construction processes. Three experiments were conducted: (1) analyzing how site conditions (module shape, color, occlusion) affect detection performance, (2) comparing models trained on real, synthetic, and hybrid datasets, and (3) testing different ratios of real-to-synthetic data (1:0.25 to 1:9) to determine optimal dataset composition.
Research Goal: The paper examines the feasibility of using VR-generated synthetic data to complement real-world data for training object detection models in automated productivity monitoring of modular integrated construction processes, addressing data scarcity challenges in construction site monitoring.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the limited availability of high-quality training data in construction, particularly for off-site construction methods like modular construction. It also addresses the inability of existing CV-based monitoring approaches to capture dynamic workflows, object interactions, and complex site conditions due to insufficient data diversity and manual labeling errors.
Future Research: Future research should explore the generalizability of the hybrid training framework across different deep learning architectures and vision tasks (e.g., semantic segmentation, activity recognition, pose estimation). Additionally, domain adaptation techniques and lightweight fine-tuning strategies should be investigated to enhance model robustness and facilitate scalable deployment across diverse construction contexts.
Results: The hybrid dataset (combining real and VR-generated synthetic data at a 1:3 ratio) achieved the highest performance with mean precision of 0.846, recall of 0.88, F1-score of 0.931, and mAP of 89.7%, outperforming models trained solely on real data (F1-score 0.84, mAP 77.3%) or synthetic data alone (F1-score 0.58-0.69). However, excessive reliance on synthetic data introduced domain gaps and reduced performance.
AI Model Used: YOLOv7 (YOLOv7-s)
Data Types: Real-world data consisted of high-resolution video footage (3840×2160 pixels, 24 fps) collected from four modular construction sites. VR-generated synthetic data were created using Blender with Cycles rendering engine, simulating modular installation processes with physically based rendering (PBR), incorporating diverse site backgrounds, lighting conditions, and object interactions with automatic labeling.

Title: APPLICATION OF MACHINE LEARNING TO LIMITED DATASETS: PREDICTION OF PROJECT SUCCESS
Abstract: Much research is conducted on the importance of success factors. This study contributes to the body of knowledge by using artificial intelligence (AI), specifically machine learning (ML), to analyse success factors through data from construction projects. Previously conducted studies have explored the use of AI to predict project success and identify important success factors in projects; however, to the extent of the authors' knowledge, no studies have implemented the same method as this study. This study conducts quantitative analysis on a sample of 160 Norwegian construction projects, with data obtained from a detailed questionnaire delivered to relevant project team members. The method utilises ML through a Random Forest Classifier (RFC). The findings obtained from the analysis show that it is possible to use AI and ML on a limited dataset. Furthermore, the findings show that it is possible to identify the most important success factors for the projects in question with the developed model. The findings suggest that a group of selected processes is more important than others to achieve success. The identified success factors support the theoretically acknowledged importance of thorough and early planning and analysis, complexity throughout the project, leadership involvement, and processes supporting project success.
Authors: Bang, S.; Aarvold, M.O.; Hartvig, W.J.; Olsson, N.O.E.; Rauzy, A.
Journal: Journal of Information Technology in Construction
Construction Application Area: Project success prediction and identification of critical success factors in construction projects
Methodology: The study applies machine learning using a Random Forest Classifier (RFC) on a dataset of 160 Norwegian construction projects obtained from CII Nordic 10-10 questionnaires. The data was cleaned, split into multiple dataframes by sector and phase, labeled using a 2-out-of-3 voting system based on time, cost, and quality dimensions, and analyzed through Monte Carlo Simulations with 10,000 iterations to identify the most important success factors.
Research Goal: To explore how AI and machine learning can be applied to analyze limited datasets from project evaluations to predict project success and identify the most important factors contributing to project success in construction projects.
Gaps Addressed: The study addresses the gap in applying machine learning techniques to limited datasets in construction project management, and fills the knowledge gap by providing a method to identify critical success factors using AI when data availability is constrained.
Future Research: Future research should explore different methods of handling missing values (such as filling NaN values or discarding polluted projects), investigate manual tuning of hyper parameters for individual dataframes, explore additional classification categories beyond binary success/failure, and test the model on larger datasets with more consistent data registration across projects.
Results: The study successfully demonstrated that ML can be applied to limited datasets to predict project success with reasonable accuracy (mean f1-scores ranging from 0.62 to 0.81 for best performing dataframes). The identified critical success factors include complexity management, early planning, uncertainty analysis, leadership involvement, training, constructability, and project processes that support success, which align with established project management theory.
AI Model Used: Random Forest Classifier (RFC) with 100 decision trees per iteration, implemented through Monte Carlo Simulations with 10,000 iterations
Data Types: The data consists of qualitative and quantitative evaluations from 160 Norwegian construction and infrastructure projects collected through CII Nordic 10-10 questionnaires. The dataset includes project descriptive data, output ratings, question scores (binary and 1-5 scale), and project ratings covering various aspects of project management including complexity, planning, leadership, training, and customer satisfaction.

Title: The state of applications of quantitative analysis techniques to construction economics and management (1983 to 2006)
Abstract: With increasing complexity of construction industry problems, researchers are experimenting with computationally rigorous techniques with the aim of seeking innovative solutions. In order to trace the applications of quantitative analysis techniques to research in the two fields of construction economics and construction management for both conventional and AI techniques, the methodology involves compiling all the relevant papers from the top two ranking construction management journals, namely, Construction Management and Economics and ASCE's Journal of Construction Engineering and Management. The period is from 1983 to 2006. The compiled papers are classified by field, area (or topic), technique applied and year of publication to enable time series and cross-sectional analyses of the data. Mainly, the results are depicted as trends when the patterns of distribution of the papers are plotted over time. The three findings are: (1) for construction economics, the overall increasing trend is higher for papers that have applied conventional techniques; (2) for construction management, there is a clear positive trend for papers that have applied AI techniques which starts from 1995; and (3) the areas (or topics) of construction management that have increasingly higher growth in the application of AI techniques are optimization of site operations and optimization of project time, cost and resources allocation. Two broad recommendations are made that relate to advancing the fields of construction economics and construction management with the view that researchers must better enable themselves to build tools that incorporate intelligence as innovative solutions for increasingly complex problems.
Authors: Bee-Hua, G.B.
Construction Application Area: Construction economics (tender bid estimation, construction demand forecasting, building cost modeling) and construction management (site operations optimization, resource allocation, equipment selection, project budget/cash-flow, site productivity estimation, time-cost optimization)
Methodology: This is a bibliometric study that compiled and analyzed 138 papers from two top-ranked construction management journals (Construction Management and Economics and ASCE's Journal of Construction Engineering and Management) from 1983 to 2006. Papers were classified by field (construction economics vs. management), area/topic, technique applied (conventional vs. AI), and year of publication to enable time series and cross-sectional analyses. The patterns of distribution were plotted over time to depict trends.
Research Goal: The paper aims to trace the applications of quantitative analysis techniques (both conventional and AI) to research in construction economics and construction management over 24 years (1983-2006), and to verify the hypothesis that there is a growing trend of AI techniques being applied to construction management research due to its increasingly complex nature.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the need to understand the state and trends of applying both conventional and AI techniques across construction economics and construction management fields, extending beyond previous reviews that focused only on ANNs in construction management. It also examines whether AI adoption is growing in response to increasing problem complexity.
Future Research: The paper recommends that construction economists need to be more aware that AI techniques can extend representations of economic behavior beyond neoclassical constrained optimization models. For construction management, it recommends formalizing the process of innovation, research and training to advance AI to the mainstream of the field, particularly ANNs and GAs as promising techniques that can drive this paradigm shift.
Results: The study found three main findings: (1) for construction economics, papers applying conventional techniques show a higher overall increasing trend; (2) for construction management, there is a clear positive trend for papers applying AI techniques starting from 1995; and (3) the areas of construction management with increasingly higher growth in AI applications are optimization of site operations and optimization of project time, cost and resources allocation.
AI Model Used: No Model - This is a review/bibliometric study analyzing papers that used various AI models including ANNs, GAs, fuzzy logic, case-based reasoning, knowledge-based systems, and expert systems
Data Types: The data consists of published academic papers from two peer-reviewed construction management journals spanning 24 years (1983-2006). A total of 138 papers were compiled that applied quantitative analysis techniques to construction economics (41 papers) and construction management (97 papers) topics.

Title: Clustering Information Types for Semantic Enrichment of Building Information Models to Support Automated Code Compliance Checking
Abstract: Missing, incomplete, implicit, and/or incorrect information are major obstacles to automated code compliance checking in the construction industry. All existing platforms for automated code checking require users to extensively preprocess their input models to supplement missing information before checking can begin. Semantic enrichment using artificial intelligence (AI) can automate much of this normalization process. Progress in the field of semantic enrichment, in turn, requires identification and specification of the information types that must be made explicit, and of the procedures appropriate for each type. After characterizing a broad set of clauses from five diverse building codes, a two-stage clustering process with the k-means algorithm was used to derive a hierarchical classification of semantic enrichment task types. The resulting classification defines 10 tasks that are typically needed for automated code compliance checking. Future research can build on the classification to formalize a knowledge base to inform selection of appropriate approaches for semantic enrichment tasks.
Authors: Bloch, T.; Sacks, R.
Journal: Journal of Computing in Civil Engineering
Construction Application Area: Automated code compliance checking, semantic enrichment of Building Information Models (BIM)
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (BIM and semantic enrichment for automated code compliance)
Research Method: Mixed method (descriptive research with quantitative clustering analysis)
Methodology: The paper analyzes five building codes to identify concepts and properties requiring semantic enrichment. Features are defined for concepts and properties, then k-means clustering algorithm is applied to classify semantic enrichment tasks into hierarchical categories. The clustering uses parameter sweeping and silhouette method for evaluation.
Research Goal: To identify and classify semantic enrichment task types needed for automated code compliance checking, and to match each class of concepts with the most appropriate enrichment technique, thereby formalizing knowledge for selecting appropriate AI approaches for semantic enrichment.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of a knowledge base and systematic framework for semantic enrichment to automate the normalization process of BIM models. Current automated code checking platforms require extensive manual preprocessing of models, which is time-consuming and error-prone.
Future Research: The next step involves validation through experimentation to refine classifications and match each cluster with appropriate semantic enrichment approaches. Additional research includes developing and testing generic SE routines for different task types, and exploring machine learning approaches for code compliance checking that may reduce the need for normalization.
Results: The clustering analysis identified a hierarchical classification with 10 subtypes of semantic enrichment tasks across three levels. Level 1 distinguishes concepts from properties. Level 2 identifies creation and association tasks for concepts, and calculation and classification tasks for properties. Level 3 further divides these into specific subtypes based on characteristics like spatial dependency, composition type, and association type.
AI Model Used: k-means clustering algorithm (unsupervised machine learning)
Data Types: Concepts and properties extracted from five building codes (International Fire Code, International Building Code, Israel code for security rooms, American National Standard for Accessible and Usable Buildings, and International Property Maintenance Code), characterized by feature vectors including element type, dependency type, composition type, association type, property type, parent object type, and value type.

Title: AUTONOMOUS MIXED REALITY FRAMEWORK FOR REAL-TIME CONSTRUCTION INSPECTION
Abstract: The increasing complexity in construction projects necessitates advancements in the precision and efficiency of inspection processes. In response to this challenge, the present study explores the feasibility of a framework for autonomous inspection using Mixed Reality (MR), Building Information Modelling (BIM) and Artificial Intelligence (AI).The proposed framework encompasses techniques for: object detection in images taken through an MR headset; matching to the object instance in the digital twin; and visualisation of detection results in the MR headset to enable real-time human-in-the-loop decision making, thereby optimising the inspection workflow. The framework's efficacy is evaluated with two datasets representing diverse construction settings, including residential and office environments, focusing on the checking of the presence of ubiquitous elements like electrical sockets and switches. These tests illustrate the practical applicability and limitations of the proposed method.
Authors: Boan, T.; Jiajun, L.; Bosché, F.
Journal: Journal of Information Technology in Construction
Construction Application Area: Construction inspection, progress monitoring, quality control, and MEP (Mechanical, Electrical, and Plumbing) installations verification
Methodology: The paper develops an autonomous Mixed Reality (MR) framework that integrates BIM-based Digital Twin, AI-powered object detection (YOLOv5m), and real-time pose tracking using HoloLens 2. The system continuously captures RGB and depth images, detects building elements, projects BIM objects onto image planes, matches detections with the digital twin, validates positional accuracy, and provides real-time visual feedback to inspectors through color-coded bounding boxes.
Research Goal: The goal is to explore the feasibility of an autonomous MR-based construction inspection framework that integrates BIM, AI, and Mixed Reality to enable real-time, hands-free verification of installed components (focusing on electrical sockets and switches) without requiring manual user intervention, thereby optimizing inspection workflows.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the limitation that existing MR-based inspection solutions require significant manual user intervention and act mainly as enhanced viewing platforms rather than autonomous verification systems.
