It aims to automate routine inspection tasks by continuously detecting and verifying components as the user navigates the site, reducing human oversight and improving efficiency.
Future Research: Future research should focus on enhancing camera pose tracking accuracy through integration of external sensors (e.g., beacons, GNSS, RTK) and improving object detection capabilities by training on more diverse datasets to recognize complex MEP elements. Additional work is needed to expand applications to other use cases such as construction safety compliance monitoring and to conduct comprehensive user experience studies.
Results: Under optimal conditions (incidence angle <10°, alignment angle <12°, distance <1.5m), the system achieved a mean positional deviation of approximately 16.1 cm. However, performance degraded significantly when these constraints were relaxed, with deviations increasing to around 50 cm in natural standing positions, limiting the system's applicability to positioning conformance control but demonstrating feasibility for element detection and matching.
AI Model Used: YOLOv5m (You Only Look Once version 5 medium)
Data Types: The paper uses RGB images and depth images captured from HoloLens 2 cameras, real-time spatial data (camera position and orientation from IMU sensors), BIM model data containing 3D locations and properties of building elements, and a training dataset of 2,026 indoor images featuring electrical sockets and switches with various augmentations.

Title: Hybrid ANN-CBR model for disputed change orders in construction projects
Abstract: The purpose of this paper is to provide a method that can be used to solve potential lawsuit problems caused by change orders in construction projects. A hybrid Artificial Intelligence (AI) model, the Hybrid ANN-CBR Model (HACM), is developed utilizing the AI branches of Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) and Case Based Reasoning (CBR). The research is based on the litigation archives collected by the Supreme Courts and appellate courts in 48 states and one district of the USA. The accuracy of the HACM prediction rate reaches 84.61%, not only for predicting litigation likelihood by using the ANN approach but also utilizing the CBR approach to yield warnings and display litigation information related to past cases. After evaluating 31 cases it is confirmed that the model HACM performs well especially for those medium sized construction projects. It can be concluded that it is feasible to link ANN and CBR together to provide a tool with a relatively high rate of prediction accuracy and a conceptual model to solve potential severe disputes cased by change orders.
Authors: Chen, J.-H.; Hsu, S.C.
Construction Application Area: Dispute management and litigation prediction caused by change orders in construction projects
Methodology: The paper develops a Hybrid ANN-CBR Model (HACM) that combines Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) with Back-propagation algorithm for classification and Case Based Reasoning (CBR) for similarity calculation. The model uses 340 litigation archives from U.S. Supreme Courts and appellate courts across 48 states, analyzing 11 factors reduced from an initial 23 through feature transformation and dimension reduction. The ANN classifier predicts litigation likelihood while the CBR component retrieves similar historical cases with similarity above 95%.
Research Goal: To develop an early warning system that predicts potential litigation caused by disputed change orders in construction projects and provides information from similar past cases to help prevent severe disputes from escalating to litigation.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of preventive tools for construction litigation, noting that while many studies discuss dispute resolution methods, few focus on early warning systems to prevent litigation. Previous studies have not integrated AI approaches like ANN and CBR to predict and warn about potential litigation triggered by change orders.
Future Research: Future research should focus on making the user interface more user-friendly and improving database server administration for systematic data arrangement. Additionally, implementing knowledge management concepts into the framework is recommended, as comprehensive data collection positions the system close to knowledge management application readiness.
Results: The HACM achieved an accuracy prediction rate of 84.61% for classifying projects with litigation potential. Evaluation with 31 electrical projects showed an accuracy classification rate of 80.65%, with misclassifications predominantly occurring in relatively small projects (less than $1 million), which comprised 72.70% of the database.
AI Model Used: Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) with Back-propagation algorithm (3-layer feed-forward perceptron network with 3 neurons in hidden layer) and Case Based Reasoning (CBR) with similarity calculation algorithm
Data Types: The research uses 340 litigation archive entries from U.S. Supreme Courts and appellate courts (1972-2002) containing project data (owner type, project type, plaintiff-defendant role, contract type, project size, percentage of change) and dispute data (17 change-order related factors including additions, deletions, design changes, scheduling, etc.). The data was collected from electronic text format and paper archives.

Title: Evolutionary multivariate adaptive regression splines for estimating shear strength in reinforced-concrete deep beams
Abstract: This study proposes a novel artificial intelligence (AI) model to estimate the shear strength of reinforced-concrete (RC) deep beams. The proposed evolutionary multivariate adaptive regression splines (EMARS) model is a hybrid of multivariate adaptive regression splines (MARS) and artificial bee colony (ABC). In EMARS, MARS addresses learning and curve fitting and ABC implements optimization to determine the optimal parameter settings with minimal estimation errors. The proposed model was constructed using 106 experimental datasets from the literature. EMARS performance was compared with three other data-mining techniques, including back-propagation neural network (BPNN), radial basis function neural network (RBFNN), and support vector machine (SVM). EMARS estimation accuracy was benchmarked against four prevalent mathematical methods, including ACI-318 (2011), CSA, CEB-FIP MC90, and Tang's Method. Benchmark results identified EMARS as the best model and, thus, an efficient alternative approach to estimating RC deep beam shear strength.
Authors: Cheng, M.-Y.; Cao, M.-T.
Construction Application Area: Structural engineering - estimating shear strength of reinforced-concrete deep beams
Methodology: The paper proposes EMARS (Evolutionary Multivariate Adaptive Regression Splines), which combines MARS for learning and curve fitting with ABC (Artificial Bee Colony) algorithm for optimization. MARS handles the nonlinear relationships between input parameters and shear strength, while ABC optimizes MARS parameters (Mmax, d, Imax) to minimize estimation errors. The model was constructed using 106 experimental datasets and validated using 10-fold cross-validation.
Research Goal: To develop and test a novel artificial intelligence model (EMARS) that can accurately estimate the shear strength of reinforced-concrete deep beams under various parameter settings. The model aims to provide an efficient alternative to existing mathematical methods and AI techniques.
Gaps Addressed: Existing mathematical methods (ACI-318, CSA, CEB-FIP) provide conservative estimates, and previous AI approaches (ANN, GP, SVM) have limitations such as requiring extensive parameter tuning, not providing explicit relationships between input and output, or inability to indicate relative importance of input variables. MARS has not been applied to structural engineering problems, and manual selection of optimal MARS parameters is challenging.
Future Research: The paper does not explicitly mention specific future research directions in a dedicated section. However, it suggests that EMARS could be applied to other structural engineering problems beyond shear strength estimation.
Results: EMARS achieved excellent performance with R² values of 0.99 for training and 0.97 for testing, average RMSE values of 13.1 kN (training) and 13.47 kN (testing), and MAPE values of 5.67% (training) and 5.89% (testing). EMARS outperformed BPNN, RBFNN, SVM, and four mathematical approaches (ACI-318, CSA, CEB-FIP, Tang's Method), providing an observed-to-estimated ratio close to 1.00 with the smallest standard deviation (0.08).
AI Model Used: EMARS (Evolutionary Multivariate Adaptive Regression Splines) - a hybrid of MARS and ABC (Artificial Bee Colony). Comparison models include BPNN (Back-Propagation Neural Network), RBFNN (Radial Basis Function Neural Network), and SVM (Support Vector Machine)
Data Types: The dataset consists of 106 experimental test results of RC deep beams from literature, including 35 patterns of normal strength RC from Kong et al. (1970), 52 patterns from Smith and Vantsiotis (1982), and 19 patterns of high strength RC from Tan et al. (1995). Nine input parameters were used: L/d, fc, d/bw, a/d, fyh, fyv, ρh, ρs, ρv, with shear strength (V) as the output.

Title: LEAST SQUARE MOMENT BALANCED MACHINE: A NEW APPROACH TO ESTIMATING COST TO COMPLETION FOR CONSTRUCTION PROJECTS
Abstract: In the construction industry, traditional methods of cost estimation are inefficient and cannot reflect real-time changes. Modern techniques are essential to create new tools that outperform current cost estimation. This study introduced the Least Square Moment Balanced Machine (LSMBM), AI-based inference engine, to improve construction cost prediction accuracy. LSMBM considers moments to determine the optimal hyperplane and uses the Backpropagation Neural Network (BPNN) to assign weights to each data point. The effectiveness of LSMBM was tested by predicting the construction costs of residential and reinforced concrete buildings. Correlation analysis, PCA, and LASSO were used for feature selection to identify the most relevant variables, with the combination of LSMBM-PCA giving the best performance. When compared to other machine learning models, the LSMBM model achieved the lowest error values, with an RMSE of 0.016, MAE of 0.010, and MAPE of 4.569%. The overall performance measurement reference index (RI) further confirmed the superiority of LSMBM. Furthermore, LSMBM performed better than the Earned Value Management (EVM) method. LSMBM model has proven to enhanced the precision in predicting cost estimates, facilitating project managers to anticipate potential cost overruns and optimize resource allocation, provide information for strategic and operational decision-making processes in construction projects.
Authors: Cheng, M.-Y.; Khasani, R.R.
Journal: Journal of Information Technology in Construction
Construction Application Area: Cost estimation, cost to completion prediction for construction projects
Methodology: The paper introduces Least Square Moment Balanced Machine (LSMBM), which combines principles of LSSVM with moment balance concepts and uses BPNN to assign weights to datapoints. The model was validated using two case studies: residential buildings (372 instances) and reinforced concrete buildings (10 projects, 225 periods). Three feature selection techniques were employed: Correlation Analysis, PCA, and LASSO. A 10-fold cross-validation method was used with grid search for hyperparameter optimization.
Research Goal: To develop and validate LSMBM, an advanced AI-based inference engine that improves construction cost prediction accuracy by considering moments to determine the optimal hyperplane and using BPNN to assign weights to each datapoint, thereby enhancing precision in predicting cost estimates and facilitating better resource allocation and decision-making in construction projects.
Gaps Addressed: Traditional cost estimation methods are inefficient, cannot reflect real-time changes, require significant human input and time, and are affected by factors like labor productivity, market fluctuations, and weather conditions that EVM cannot adequately address. LSSVM treats all datapoints equally without weighting, creating problems when some datapoints are more reliable than others, potentially reducing prediction accuracy.
Future Research: Future research should expand the scope to other types of construction projects beyond residential and RC buildings, such as infrastructure projects. More sophisticated methods of hyperparameter optimization should be explored to improve model stability and effectiveness. Additionally, enhancing model interpretability through methods such as SHAP analysis, ICE plots, and PDP plots could provide deeper insights into factors influencing cost estimations.
Results: The LSMBM model achieved the lowest error values with RMSE of 0.016, MAE of 0.010, and MAPE of 4.569% when combined with PCA feature selection. It outperformed other machine learning models (LSSVM, BPNN, RF, DT, KNN, SVM, LR) and traditional EVM methods across all evaluation metrics. The overall performance measurement reference index (RI) further confirmed the superiority of LSMBM, achieving the highest accuracy of 95.43% compared to previous methods (89.9%-95.08%).
AI Model Used: Least Square Moment Balanced Machine (LSMBM), Backpropagation Neural Network (BPNN), Least Square Support Vector Machine (LSSVM), Random Forests (RF), Decision Trees (DT), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Linear Regression (LR)
Data Types: The study used two datasets: (1) 372 instances of residential condominium buildings (3-9 stories) constructed in Iran between 1993-2008 with 7 input factors, and (2) 10 RC construction projects in Taiwan with 225 periods, including 18 input variables covering structural components, financial metrics, progress and duration measures, and performance and environment factors. Input variables included superstructure floors, basement floors, total floor area, contract price, actual duration, EVM metrics (SPI, CPI, critical ratio), weather conditions, and productivity indexes.

Title: Artificial intelligence approaches to achieve strategic control over project cash flows
Abstract: The ability over the course of a construction project to make reliable predictions regarding cash flows enhances project cost management. This paper uses artificial intelligence (AI) approaches to predict cash flow trends for such projects in order to develop appropriate strategies that apply factors such as float, process execution time, construction rate and resource demand to project cash flow control. AI approaches involved in this paper include K-means clustering, genetic algorithm (GA), fuzzy logic (FL), and neural network (NN). K-means clustering is employed to categorize similar projects, while the other approaches are used to develop the Evolutionary Fuzzy Neural Inference Model (EFNIM), a knowledge learning model. FL and NN are employed in the EFNIM to develop a neural-fuzzy model that can deal with uncertainties and knowledge mapping. GA is used to optimize the membership functions of FL and NN parameters globally. The major target of this AI learning is to address sequential cash flow trends. This trained result is furthermore applied to a strategic project cash flow control. This cash flow control affects project performance within the banana envelope of the S-curve for project management. Crown Copyright © 2008.
Authors: Cheng, M.-Y.; Tsai, H.-C.; Liu, C.-L.
Construction Application Area: Project cash flow management and control, cost management
Methodology: The paper employs a hybrid AI approach combining K-means clustering for project categorization and the Evolutionary Fuzzy Neural Inference Model (EFNIM) that integrates Genetic Algorithm (GA), Fuzzy Logic (FL), and Neural Networks (NN). Historical data from 52 construction projects are clustered, with 13 similar projects selected for training and testing the model to predict expenditure cash flow trends.
Research Goal: To develop an AI-based approach for predicting project cash flow trends and to introduce a strategic project cash flow control methodology that ensures project performance remains within the banana envelope of the S-curve.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the difficulty in predicting expenditure cash flow due to various impacting factors and the lack of strategic approaches for controlling project cash flows proactively. It also tackles the challenge of determining optimal parameters for fuzzy logic membership functions and neural network configurations.
Future Research: The paper suggests that the strategic financial cash flow control concept needs more support and promotion for direct application in real construction projects. More academic reviews and validation in actual construction scenarios are recommended.
Results: The trained EFNIM model achieved a root mean square error (RMSE) of 2.873% for training data and an average error of 2.601% for testing data, which falls within the acceptable forecast error range (±3%) for the construction industry. The validation case showed an average prediction error of 1.35%, demonstrating the model's accuracy in predicting expenditure cash flows.
AI Model Used: Evolutionary Fuzzy Neural Inference Model (EFNIM) - a hybrid model combining Genetic Algorithm (GA), Fuzzy Logic (FL), Neural Networks (NN), and K-means clustering
Data Types: Historical data from 52 condominium high-rise construction projects executed between 1996 and 2006 in Taipei, including 9 significant project factors (floor, contract cost, area, duration, and cost ratios for various engineering components). Each project was divided into 20 periods representing cumulative cash flow ratios.

Title: Novel Approach to Estimating Schedule to Completion in Construction Projects Using Sequence and Nonsequence Learning
Abstract: Estimate schedule to completion (ESTC) is a difficult variable to determine accurately during the various phases of construction projects due to the complex, uncertain, and limited nature of currently available information. Currently, most construction managers estimate this variable using their subjective prior experience with budget planning. However, this method is costly and highly prone to inaccuracy. The study developed a novel inference model, the neural network-long short-term memory (NN-LSTM) model, to estimate ESTC accurately by factoring in the sequence and nonsequence factors that significantly influence project duration and that capture the inherent uncertainties in the construction field. NN-LSTM fuses two characteristics of artificial neural networks (ANNs): feedforward neural networks (FNNs) for nonsequential issue, and recurrent neural networks (RNNs) for sequential issue. After a systematic review of the literature, brainstorming, and in-depth interviews with domain experts, a database of 226 historical cases collected from 11 construction projects with 14 influencing factors was created for learning purposes, with 10-fold cross validation used to partition the training and testing data sets. NN-LSTM was then applied to estimate the schedule to completion for the historical cases, with FNN capturing the impact of time-independent factors on project duration and LSTM capturing the long temporal dependency for sequential inputs. The learning results indicated good performance, with a mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of less than 5% and a mean absolute error (MAE) of 2%, proving the NN-LSTM model to be more reliable than the currently prevailing earned value management (EVM) formulas. Moreover, in subsequent comparison testing NN-LSTM proved to be superior to other artificial intelligence (AI) prediction models. The model has the ability to generate reliable scheduling estimates for project managers in order to facilitate their accurate planning and the monitoring of the time performance of projects, allowing timely action to be taken to remedy foreseen delays and facilitating informed decision-making.
Authors: Cheng, M.-Y.; Chang, Y.-H.; Korir, D.
Journal: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Construction Application Area: Schedule estimation and project duration forecasting
Methodology: The paper develops a novel NN-LSTM model that combines feedforward neural networks (FNNs) for time-independent factors and long short-term memory (LSTM) networks for time-dependent factors. The model was trained and validated using 10-fold cross validation on a database of 226 historical cases from 11 construction projects with 14 influencing factors.
Research Goal: The study aims to develop a novel inference model (NN-LSTM) to accurately estimate the schedule to completion (ESTC) for construction projects by considering both time-dependent and time-independent factors that influence project duration.
Gaps Addressed: Existing earned value management (EVM) methods cannot react timely to factors affecting project completion and lack the ability to handle sequential data. Previous artificial intelligence models typically used FNNs which assume time independence and cannot capture sequencing information during project duration.
Future Research: The paper suggests that the NN-LSTM model could be enhanced through the collection of more historical cases to further improve its reliability and accuracy. The model could potentially be applied to other types of construction projects beyond reinforced concrete buildings.
Results: The NN-LSTM model achieved high accuracy with MAPE less than 5% and MAE of 2% for both training and testing datasets. The model proved superior to nine EVM formulas and five other AI techniques (SVM, LSSVM, BPNN, ESIM, ELSIM) in predicting schedule to completion.
AI Model Used: Neural Network-Long Short-Term Memory (NN-LSTM) combining Feedforward Neural Networks (FNN) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks. Comparison models include SVM, LSSVM, BPNN, ESIM, and ELSIM.
Data Types: Historical data from 11 reinforced concrete construction projects in Taiwan completed between 2000-2007, including 226 periods with 14 variables such as total floor area, number of floors, contract amount, labor market data, productivity index, material prices, weather data, and earned value management metrics (PV, EV, AC, SPI, CPI).

Title: Text mining-based construction site accident classification using hybrid supervised machine learning
Abstract: Safety is one key consideration in the monitoring of construction projects by engineers. Accidents in the project can potentially cause issues, such as workers' injury and progress delay, which lead to financial losses. Generally, accident narratives store all summaries and causes of the related events. Since documentations rapidly use large quantities of resources, the implementation of Artificial Intelligence (AI) begins to seek attention. Nevertheless, in current models, there are still drawbacks, such as weak learning performance and substantial error rate. In this regard, this study develops a hybrid model incorporating Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) and Symbiotic Organisms Search (SOS), named Symbiotic Gated Recurrent Unit (SGRU). SOS searches the best parameters of GRU to ensure optimal performance. Furthermore, Natural Language Processing is applied to pre-process the text data prior classification process. The experimental result in this study showcases SGRU as the best classification model among other AI models. Therefore, SGRU shares the capability to aid the safety assessments of construction projects.
Authors: Cheng, M.-Y.; Kusoemo, D.; Gosno, R.A.
Construction Application Area: Construction site safety - accident classification and cause analysis
Methodology: The paper develops a hybrid supervised machine learning model called SGRU (Symbiotic Gated Recurrent Unit) that integrates GRU neural networks with Symbiotic Organisms Search (SOS) optimization algorithm. Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques including text cleaning, stop words removal, tokenization, and GloVe embedding are applied to pre-process construction accident narrative text data before classification.
Research Goal: To develop an effective AI-based text classification model for automatically categorizing construction site accident narratives by their causes, thereby improving safety assessment efficiency and reducing human error and bias in accident report analysis.
Gaps Addressed: Current machine learning models for construction accident classification have weak learning performance and substantial error rates. Traditional manual assessment methods are time-consuming, costly, and subject to human bias, making them ineffective for processing large volumes of accident reports.
Future Research: Future work should explore data balancing techniques such as over-sampling or under-sampling to address imbalanced class distribution issues in the dataset. Further investigation of newly developed deep learning models and their comparison with the proposed SGRU algorithm is also recommended.
Results: The SGRU model achieved an average weighted F1-score of 0.69, outperforming other AI techniques including Decision Tree (0.52), KNN (0.53), Naïve Bayesian (0.44), Logistic Regression (0.50), SVM (0.58), LSTM (0.57), and GRU (0.56). SGRU successfully predicted all 11 accident cause categories while other algorithms failed to predict some categories.
AI Model Used: Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU), Symbiotic Organisms Search (SOS), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Decision Tree, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Naïve Bayesian, Logistic Regression, Natural Language Processing (NLP) with GloVe embedding
Data Types: Text data consisting of 1000 construction site accident narratives with 11 unique labeled causes, collected from the US Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) database. The dataset includes detailed descriptions of incidents with causal factors and events leading to accidents.

Title: Time-dependent rebar price prediction for procurement decision-making using bio-optimized deep machine learning
Abstract: Purpose: This study aims to (1) develop an artificial intelligence (AI)-based model to accurately forecast rebar prices and (2) propose procurement strategies to reduce the subjectivity involved in rebar price trend forecasting and minimize procurement costs for construction project general contractors. Design/methodology/approach: Correlation analysis was used to identify the key factors influencing changes in rebar prices over time. An AI-based inference model, symbiotic bidirectional gated recurrent unit (SBiGRU), was developed for rebar price forecasting. The performance of SBiGRU was compared with other AI techniques, and procurement strategies based on the SBiGRU model were proposed. Findings: The SBiGRU model outperformed the other AI techniques in terms of rebar price forecasting accuracy. The proposed rebar price forecasting model (RPFM) and procurement patterns, which integrate inventory management principles and rebar price forecasts, were demonstrated to effectively optimize procurement costs, realizing a remarkable 6.13% reduction in procurement expenses compared to the conventional monthly procurement approach. Research limitations/implications: The accuracy of AI models may be impacted by disparities in the data used for model training. Future research should explore approaches incorporating price predictions and order factors. Originality/value: This study significantly extends the bounds of traditional rebar price prediction by integrating AI-driven forecasting with inventory management principles, highlighting the potential of AI-based models to improve construction industry procurement practices, reduce related risks and costs, optimize project operations and maximize project outcomes.
Authors: Cheng, M.-Y.; Vu, Q.-T.; Dessalegn, M.; Chen, J.-H.
Construction Application Area: Rebar price forecasting and procurement decision-making for construction project cost management
Methodology: The paper uses correlation analysis to identify key factors influencing rebar prices, then develops a hybrid deep learning model called SBiGRU (Symbiotic Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit) that combines BiGRU with Symbiotic Organisms Search (SOS) optimization algorithm for hyperparameter tuning. The model is validated using historical data from 2007-2016 and compared with eight other AI techniques. Based on the predictions, eight procurement strategy patterns are proposed integrating inventory management principles.
Research Goal: The study aims to (1) develop an AI-based model to accurately forecast rebar prices and (2) propose procurement strategies to reduce subjectivity in rebar price trend forecasting and minimize procurement costs for construction project general contractors.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the limitation of traditional ML models in handling time-series data, the difficulty of DL models in identifying optimal parameters, and the absence of management strategy recommendations based on steel price predictions. It also extends traditional rebar price prediction by integrating AI-driven forecasting with inventory management principles.
Future Research: Future research should explore approaches incorporating price predictions and order factors. Additionally, comprehensive assessments should be conducted before and after global crises to understand how global disruptions alter supply chains and rebar pricing dynamics, integrating factors such as shifts in global logistics, changes in international trade policies, and fluctuations in labor availability into predictive models.
Results: The SBiGRU model outperformed eight other AI techniques with MAPE values of 3.53% for training and 2.26% for testing. The proposed procurement strategy achieved a remarkable 6.13% reduction in procurement expenses compared to the conventional monthly procurement approach, demonstrating effective cost optimization in a one-year case verification.
AI Model Used: Symbiotic Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit (SBiGRU) - a hybrid model combining BiGRU with Symbiotic Organisms Search (SOS) optimization. Other models compared include SVM, LSSVM, ESIM, ELSIM, NNLSTM, SOS-LSSVM, LSTM, and BiGRU.
Data Types: The study uses monthly time-series data from January 2007 to December 2016 (120 months) including ten factors: domestic rebar apparent consumption, domestic scrap supply, domestic billet supply, domestic rebar price, iron ore import price, coke import price, scrap steel import price, billet import price, crude oil price, and exchange rate. Data was sourced from Taiwan Steel and Iron Industries Association, Central Bank of Taiwan, and Ministry of Economic Affairs.

Title: Optimized artificial intelligence models for predicting project award price
Abstract: Abstract Bridges are essential components of transportation systems. The bidding process is the main determinant of whether a contractor is commissioned to complete a construction project. Therefore, contractors must rapidly and precisely estimate construction costs and the bid award amount. This study involved optimizing artificial intelligence models to forecast bid award amounts for bridge construction projects. A genetic algorithm is used in several forecasting models, including models based on multiple regression analysis, artificial neural networks (ANNs), and case-based reasoning (CBR). Data for public bridge construction projects were collected from the Taiwan government e-procurement system. The cross-validation results show that the mathematical model for the ANNs provides more reliable simulations and has a superior fit compared with the regression methods, CBR, and the conventional approach. This study provides an optimization process for estimating project award prices that improves construction and evaluations of AI-based models as well as an auxiliary tool that contractors can use to make bidding decisions.
Authors: Chou, J.-S.; Lin, C.-W.; Pham, A.-D.; Shao, J.-Y.
Construction Application Area: Cost estimation, bidding, bid award price prediction for bridge construction projects
Methodology: The study uses genetic algorithms (GA) to optimize multiple artificial intelligence models including multiple regression analysis, artificial neural networks (ANNs), and case-based reasoning (CBR). Data from 98 bridge construction projects were collected from Taiwan's government e-procurement system. A 10-fold cross-validation algorithm was employed to validate model performance, and nine additional cases were collected for final testing.
Research Goal: To develop and optimize artificial intelligence models that can accurately forecast bid award amounts for bridge construction projects based on limited available information, providing contractors with an auxiliary tool for making bidding decisions.
Gaps Addressed: Most existing bidding models require sensitive competitor information and are unsuitable for the bidding stage. Previous cost estimation methods are more appropriate for early project stages rather than bidding. There is a lack of effective forecasting techniques for bridge projects, which are complex to classify and difficult to estimate.
Future Research: Future studies should collect data on macroeconomic factors such as economic situation, expected inflation, and interest rates to enhance prediction results. Other optimization algorithms can be evaluated for verification and improvements. Further work should identify the lowest p value in F test regression analysis and investigate GA-ANN models with different alpha values and fitness functions.
Results: The GA-ANN model exhibited optimal performance with an average MAPE of 7.526% in cross-validation testing and 13.09% in final validation with nine additional cases, showing approximately 6.3% improvement over the conventional method. The model demonstrated superior accuracy compared to GA-NLR (7.574%), GA-CBR (8.83%), and generalized linear regression (9.149%).
AI Model Used: Genetic Algorithm (GA), Artificial Neural Network (ANN), Case-Based Reasoning (CBR), Multiple Regression Analysis, Nonlinear Regression (NLR)
Data Types: Historical data from 98 bridge construction projects collected from Taiwan's government e-procurement system (June 2008 to May 2009), including bid information, budget amounts, compliance periods, detailed estimates, construction specifications, and categorical variables such as bid authority and construction type. Nine additional validation cases were collected from June to August 2009.

Title: Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy inference system for Egyptian residential construction waste prediction
Abstract: Construction waste (CW) significantly impacts environmental degradation, resource depletion, and construction costs, especially in rapidly developing regions. However, data on CW generation remain scarce in many developing countries, hindering effective waste management. This study presents an artificial intelligence (AI)-based predictive model to estimate CW quantities—specifically, concrete, bricks, and steel—using a case study in Egypt. Data were collected from 25 construction sites, incorporating variables such as total area, design consistency, worker experience, and waste reuse. The model employs an Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System (ANFIS) to analyze the dynamic nature of construction activities and predict waste generation. Model accuracy was evaluated using Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), and the a20-index. The concrete model achieved R2 = 0.95, RMSE = 0.077, MAE = 0.071, a20 = 0.500; the bricks model showed R2 = 0.96, RMSE = 0.063, MAE = 0.043, a20 = 0.400; and the steel model resulted in R2 = 0.93, RMSE = 0.044, MAE = 0.042, a20 = 0.500. The results demonstrate the model's potential to support data-driven waste estimation where data is limited. These findings can inform strategies and policies for CW management by enabling the forecasting of waste volumes during construction phases.
Authors: Daoud, A.O.; KhairEldin, M.; Ibrahim, A.H.; Toma, H.M.
Construction Application Area: Construction waste management and prediction
Methodology: The paper develops ANFIS (Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System) models to predict construction waste quantities for concrete, bricks, and steel in Egyptian residential projects. Data were collected from 25 construction sites incorporating design and construction-related variables. The study uses grid partitioning with different membership functions (triangular, generalized bell, and trapezoidal) and evaluates model performance using R², RMSE, MAE, and a20-index metrics.
Research Goal: To develop an AI-based predictive model to estimate construction waste quantities (concrete, bricks, and steel) in Egyptian residential projects, enabling better waste management planning and supporting sustainable construction practices in developing countries with limited data availability.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of accurate construction waste data in Egypt and developing countries, which hinders effective waste management and recycling initiatives. It also responds to the limited application of ANFIS models specifically for construction waste prediction, particularly in contexts with scarce data availability.
Future Research: The study suggests developing hybrid models combining ANFIS with genetic algorithms (GA) to improve predictive performance. Future research should include wider datasets with economic factors like material costs, comparative analysis with other machine learning approaches, and exploration of different normalization methods (Z-score, decimal scaling) to enhance ANFIS model accuracy.
Results: The ANFIS models achieved high predictive accuracy: concrete model (R²=0.95, RMSE=0.077, MAE=0.071), bricks model (R²=0.96, RMSE=0.063, MAE=0.043), and steel model (R²=0.93, RMSE=0.044, MAE=0.042). The models demonstrate superior performance compared to existing approaches and provide practical waste prediction capabilities for early project planning phases.
AI Model Used: ANFIS (Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System) with different membership functions: triangular for concrete model, generalized bell for bricks model, and trapezoidal for steel model
Data Types: The data includes quantitative and qualitative variables from 25 Egyptian residential construction sites. Design-related variables (total floor area, number of floors, design consistency, wall density) and construction-related variables (site organization, workers' experience, material storage, waste reuse) were collected through site inspections, project manager interviews, and construction document reviews.

Title: ControlNet-based domain adaptation for synthetic construction images via graphical simulation and generative AI
Abstract: Data scarcity in construction hinders deep neural network training for computer vision applications. While synthetic data generators provide annotated images, they lack realism, creating a reality gap that leads to suboptimal real-world performance. This paper introduces BCon, a framework that integrates BlendCon, a construction data generation engine, with ControlNet, a generative architecture featuring conditioning controls, to enhance the realism and diversity of synthetic images while preserving annotations. Through hyperparameter tuning and post-processing, a dataset of 25,600 enhanced images is created. Quantitative evaluations demonstrate significant improvements in realism metrics: DreamSim (+9.5%), VIEScore (+114.3%), CLIPScore (+14.7%), and FID-5k (+22.6%), indicating closer alignment with real images. Moreover, YOLOv10 models trained on enhanced images achieve an AP<inf>50–95</inf>of 0.66 on worker detection, outperforming those trained on original synthetic data by 7.9% and slightly surpassing models trained on equivalently sized real data. This framework offers cost-effective, high-quality dataset generation for visual AI applications in construction.
Authors: Davari, S.; Kim, D.; TohidiFar, A.
Construction Application Area: Worker detection, safety monitoring, hazard detection, progress monitoring, synthetic data generation for construction computer vision applications
Methodology: The paper introduces BCon, a framework that integrates BlendCon (a Blender-based graphical simulation engine) with ControlNet (a conditional diffusion-based generative AI architecture). The framework generates fully annotated synthetic construction images using BlendCon, then enhances their realism using ControlNet with Stable Diffusion XL while preserving spatial structure and annotations through depth maps and semantic masks as conditioning inputs. Hyperparameter tuning is conducted using GPT-4o for automated realism assessment, and YOLOv10 models are trained on the enhanced dataset to evaluate downstream worker detection performance.
Research Goal: The goal is to develop and validate a fully automated synthetic data generation framework that combines graphical simulation with generative AI to produce realistic yet fully annotated synthetic construction images for training deep neural networks, thereby bridging the reality gap and addressing data scarcity in construction applications.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the reality gap in synthetic construction data, where graphical simulation engines produce annotated images that lack realism leading to poor real-world performance, and generative AI models produce photorealistic images but lack essential annotations for supervised learning tasks.
Future Research: Future research directions include extending the framework to other computer vision tasks (semantic segmentation, action recognition, pose estimation), improving computational efficiency through model distillation and optimization, developing real-time and 3D-aware extensions for sequence synthesis, implementing model-in-the-loop synthesis paradigms with active learning, and conducting cost-benefit analyses for industrial deployment.
Results: The BCon framework achieved significant improvements in realism metrics: DreamSim (+9.5%), VIEScore (+114.3%), CLIPScore (+14.7%), and FID-5k (+22.6%). YOLOv10 models trained on BCon-enhanced images achieved an AP50-95 of 65.7% on worker detection, outperforming models trained on original BlendCon synthetic data (60.9%) by 7.9% and slightly surpassing models trained on equivalently sized real data (65.6%).
AI Model Used: ControlNet with Stable Diffusion XL (SD-XL 1.0), SDXL-VAE, GPT-4o for automated evaluation, YOLOv10-M for object detection, CLIP for semantic alignment evaluation
Data Types: The paper uses synthetic construction site images generated by BlendCon containing 43,000 worker instances with full annotations (2D/3D bounding boxes, semantic segmentation masks, depth maps, human poses), enhanced versions of these images using BCon, and real-world construction datasets including SODA, MOCS (combined 43,000 instances for training), and 1,300 scraped real construction images (4,000 instances for testing).

Title: Integrating IFC and NLP for automating change request validations
Abstract: SUMMARY: The management and the identification of design changes constitute an essential part of the of a design flow within the architecture, engineering and construction (AEC) industry, requiring the formalisation of a multi-disciplinary collaborative information modelling environment. Construction projects generate substantial amount of change information, which needs to be updated continuously throughout the process, from initial feasibility study to the decommissioning of facilities. Complications arise from the information storage in multiple incompatible file formats that can lead to the loss or omission of details. In addition to any unexpected changes, the mismanagement of information is another factor leading to delays and costly errors. In order to mitigate such issues, this paper proposes to integrate the Industry Foundation Classes (IFC) data model and Natural Language Processing (NLP) to validate and visually identify the result of change requests. The system is developed using C# by 1) integrating angular framework with ASP.NET (Active Server Pages) to create a dynamic single web page and 2). Using X-BIM toolkit that supports IFC format to read, create and visualise the BuildingSmart Data Models (aka IFC Models). This approach enables a web-based platform capable of generating reports and visual previews, highlighting the differences between IFC files throughout the design processes. A web-based system prototype allows users to compare subsequent versions of IFC design models in terms of additions, modifications and deletions. The prototype uses NLP to intelligently identify the changes that have been made as it compares newer and older versions of the same model, making this information available to designers and 3D modellers. Prospective work will focus on the application of artificial intelligence (AI) to automate the implementation of changes within the construction models.
Authors: Dawood, H.; Siddle, J.; Dawood, N.
Journal: Journal of Information Technology in Construction
Construction Application Area: Design change management, change request validation, BIM model comparison, and automated tracking of design changes throughout construction project lifecycles
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (BIM, drones, laser scanning, robotics, digital twins, sensor networks)
Methodology: The paper develops a web-based prototype system called ArchiTrack that integrates Industry Foundation Classes (IFC) data models with Natural Language Processing (NLP) to validate design change requests. The system uses C# with Angular framework and ASP.NET, leveraging the X-BIM toolkit to read and visualize IFC models. Finite State Transition Networks (FSTN) and CoreNLP are applied to extract domain-specific information from natural language input, enabling automated comparison of IFC file versions to identify additions, modifications, and deletions.
Research Goal: The paper aims to develop an automated web-based platform for validating and visually identifying design change requests in construction projects by comparing different versions of IFC building models, reducing errors and rework costs while enabling participation of all stakeholders including non-experts.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of awareness of domino effects from design changes across interconnected building sections, the costly errors and time overruns from mismanaged design changes, and the limitation of existing BIM collaboration tools that are expensive, require expert operation, and cannot be accessed by all stakeholders.
Future Research: Future work will expand the change request system to incorporate more general criteria (e.g., room size requirements, furniture specifications) and develop the NLP approach to automatically extract rules from input specifications using deontic verbs. The long-term goal is to apply artificial intelligence to automate the implementation of changes within construction models.
Results: The prototype successfully validates change requests with zero errors in testing, comparing IFC models to identify additions, modifications, and deletions. The system provides visual 3D comparison models highlighting changes with color coding and detailed reports of individual object changes, enabling automated validation without requiring expensive proprietary software.
AI Model Used: Natural Language Processing (NLP) using Finite State Transition Networks (FSTN) and Stanford CoreNLP with TokensRegex feature for information extraction from natural language text
Data Types: The system uses IFC (Industry Foundation Classes) building model files as primary data input, along with natural language text input for change requests. The data includes semantic information from IFC objects such as unique identifiers, object properties, spatial coordinates, associated spaces, and building levels.

Title: A virtual construction vehicles and workers dataset with three-dimensional annotations
Abstract: Most current artificial intelligence (AI) studies based on computer vision (CV) in construction are almost based on pure two-dimensional (2D) data, limiting their effectiveness to scenarios that only require 2D information. However, advanced AI systems often require the ability to perceive three-dimensional (3D) spatial information, which constrain their application scope in construction. To address this limitation, this study presents a Virtual Construction Vehicles and Workers Dataset with three-dimensional Annotations (VCVW-3D). The dataset covers 15 construction scenes and includes ten categories of construction vehicles and workers. The VCVW-3D dataset is characterized by its multi-scene, multi-category, multi-randomness, multi-viewpoint, multi-annotation, and binocular vision features. We trained and evaluated several 2D and monocular 3D object detection models, such as You Only Look Once (YOLO) and Fully Convolutional One-Stage Monocular 3D Object Detection (FCOS3D), using the VCVW-3D to establish benchmarks. The VCVW-3D dataset aims to promote the development of 3D computer vision in the construction industry by reducing the costs associated with data acquisition, prototype development, and exploration of space-awareness applications.
Authors: Ding, Y.; Luo, X.
Construction Application Area: Construction safety management, progress monitoring, quality control, productivity tracking, and spatial awareness applications including hazardous proximity detection, high-altitude operations, and spatial distance judgment
Methodology: The researchers developed a virtual construction dataset (VCVW-3D) using Unity 3D platform with 274 prefabricated assets across 15 construction scenes. They implemented automatic data synthesis with randomizers for position, angle, color, and lighting, capturing binocular stereo images with multiple 2D and 3D annotations including bounding boxes, semantic/instance segmentation, and depth maps. They then trained and evaluated various 2D and 3D object detection models to establish benchmarks.
Research Goal: To create and publicly release a large-scale virtual construction dataset with 3D annotations (VCVW-3D) that promotes the development of 3D computer vision in construction by reducing costs associated with data acquisition, prototype development, and enabling space-awareness applications that require 3D spatial information perception.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the limitation that most current AI studies in construction are based on pure 2D data, which restricts their effectiveness to scenarios requiring only 2D information. Additionally, it addresses the lack of publicly available 3D annotated datasets in construction, which is a main reason for limited 3D CV research in the field, as 3D data acquisition and annotation are extremely time-consuming and costly.
Future Research: Future work includes adding more 3D annotation information such as virtual LiDAR point clouds and 3D key point annotations for worker posture and equipment orientation, expanding object categories to include construction materials, conducting more experiments on multimodal tasks like 3D reconstruction and depth estimation, and exploring practical applications for spatial distance judgments in construction management such as high-altitude operations and safety distance estimation.
Results: The YOLOv5-x model achieved the best 2D detection performance with mAP of 0.681, while FCOS3D achieved the best monocular 3D detection with mAP of 0.465 and NDS of 0.439. Experiments showed that combining virtual synthetic data with a small amount of real-world data (MOCS validation set) achieved performance comparable to or exceeding models trained on large real-world datasets alone, demonstrating that virtual data can significantly reduce labeling costs while maintaining high accuracy.
AI Model Used: 2D object detection models: Faster R-CNN, SSD, YOLOv3, YOLOv5-x, YOLOX-s; Monocular 3D object detection models: SMOKE, FCOS3D, PGD, MonoFlex, ImVoxelNet; Binocular stereo 3D models: Disp R-CNN, DSGN, YoLoStereo3D, DSGN++
Data Types: The dataset consists of 300,000 synthetic images (20,000 training/validation and 5,000 test images per scene across 15 scenes) of 1920×1080 resolution, covering 10 categories of construction vehicles and workers. The data includes multiple annotation types: 2D bounding boxes, 3D bounding boxes with center point position, size and orientation, 2D semantic/instance segmentation masks, depth maps, binocular stereo vision pairs, and camera intrinsic/extrinsic parameters.

Title: Investigating the impact of emerging technologies on construction safety performance
Abstract: Purpose: Construction safety is heavily affected by using new technologies in this growing trend of technology adoption. Especially, safety performance is enhanced through the utilization of some effective technologies such as artificial intelligence, virtual reality, BIM and wearable devices. Therefore, the main purpose of this study is to investigate the influence of emerging technologies on construction safety performance and quantify the relationship between those. The proposed components of emerging technologies are BIM, GIS, VR, RFID, AI, ML, eye tracking and serious games and wearable devices, whereas the dimensions of construction safety performance are safety planning, safety training, safety inspection and monitoring, safety audits and reviews and safety leadership. Design/methodology/approach: A structural model was composed consisting of emerging technologies and safety performance indicators. Then, a questionnaire was designed and administered to construction professionals, and data from 167 projects were analyzed using structural equation modeling. The data were analyzed by using software, called SPSS AMOS. Findings: The analysis of the structural model proves that there is a positive and significant relationship between emerging technologies and construction safety performance. Moreover, the factor loadings for each factor were found to be high indicating a good representation of the construct by the components developed. Among the technologies, BIM, robotics and automation, AI and wearable devices were detected to be the most significant technologies in terms of impacting safety performance. Originality/value: The study contributes to the body of knowledge in that it develops a conceptual framework consisting of specific technologies in terms of emerging technologies, reveals the impact of such technologies on safety performance and proposes several tools and strategies for enabling effective safety management along the project lifecycle. Industry practitioners may benefit from the framework developed by adopting such technologies to enhance their safety performance on construction projects.
Authors: Dobrucali, E.; Demirkesen, S.; Sadikoglu, E.; Zhang, C.; Damci, A.
Construction Application Area: Construction safety performance, including safety planning, safety training, safety inspection and monitoring, safety auditing and review, and safety leadership
Methodology: The study developed a structural equation model (SEM) consisting of emerging technologies and safety performance indicators. A questionnaire was designed and administered to construction professionals, collecting data from 167 projects. The data were analyzed using SPSS AMOS software to test the relationship between emerging technologies and construction safety performance through confirmatory factor analysis and structural model assessment.
Research Goal: The main purpose of this study is to investigate the influence of emerging technologies (including BIM, GIS, VR, RFID, AI, ML, eye tracking, wearable devices, robotics and automation, AR, laser scanning and LiDAR, and IoT) on construction safety performance and quantify the relationship between them.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the gap that despite growing interest in emerging technologies for construction safety, the relationship between specific technologies and safety performance has not been thoroughly assessed. The study aims to better understand how emerging technologies impact construction safety and develop a comprehensive framework to measure this impact.
Future Research: The study recommends exploration of new models to investigate relationships between specific emerging technologies and individual safety components (e.g., BIM and safety training). Future research should compare different methodologies to analyze such relationships and develop strategies for best applying technologies in construction organizations.
Results: The analysis proved a positive and significant relationship between emerging technologies and construction safety performance with a path coefficient of 0.846. Among the technologies, BIM (0.986), robotics and automation (0.892), AI (0.875), and wearable devices (0.858) were detected as the most significant technologies impacting safety performance.
AI Model Used: No Model (The paper discusses AI as one of the emerging technologies but does not implement a specific AI model. The study uses Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) as the analytical approach.)
Data Types: The data consists of survey responses from 167 construction projects collected through questionnaires administered to construction professionals from large-scale US construction firms. Respondents rated their performance in implementing emerging technologies and achieving safety performance indicators on a 1-5 Likert scale.

Title: A framework of pavement management system based on IoT and big data
Abstract: With the expansion of urban road network, the importance of road maintenance is increasing, which guarantee the operation efficiency of transportation infrastructure. Compare to road construction, road maintenance is more sophisticated. Even though invested a lot of manpower and financial resources, management departments are troubled by insufficient and lagging road maintenance. The development and widely application of technologies including IoT, Big Data and Artificial Intelligence(AI) in various industries offer possible solutions to this issue. However, how to utilize these technologies is a challenge for related administration department because of their weak technical force. To address the problem, a pavement management system(PMC) is developed in this research. Combined with IoT and big data, the PMS provide an overall management structure of road maintenance. Composed of three subsections: Pavement detection and 3D modeling, Data analysis and Decision support, the PMS offer an automated and intelligent solution to related administrative departments and firms. Besides, two road maintenance related firms, one is a road maintenance company and the other is a technical firm that offer smart solutions to road maintenance, are selected as cases to illustrate how PMS are applied to support the daily operations and road maintenance.
Authors: Dong, J.; Meng, W.; Liu, Y.; Ti, J.
Construction Application Area: Road pavement maintenance, infrastructure management, road condition detection and evaluation, maintenance planning and scheduling
Methodology: The paper develops a conceptual framework for a Pavement Management System (PMS) based on IoT and Big Data technologies. The methodology includes literature review, framework development with three subsections (Pavement Detection and 3D modeling, Data analysis, and Decision support), and case study validation using two firms in Beijing: Beijing Municipal Bridge Maintenance Management Group Co.Ltd and Beijing Truelion Technology Co., Ltd. Data collection involved semi-structured interviews, internal document review, and on-site observations.
Research Goal: The goal is to develop a comprehensive Pavement Management System (PMS) framework that integrates IoT and Big Data technologies to provide automated and intelligent solutions for road maintenance, addressing inefficiencies in traditional manual inspection and improving resource allocation and decision-making for road maintenance departments.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the gap between existing research on specific road maintenance problems and real-world application by providing an overall systematic workflow. Previous studies focused on isolated technical solutions without explaining how to embed these techniques into the complete road maintenance system, lacking an integrated perspective on coordination between different modules and adaptation to whole operation processes.
Future Research: The paper acknowledges that while it focuses on functional and conceptual description of PMS, the application details of different sections are insufficient and need to be discussed in future research. Future work should provide more detailed implementation guidelines for each subsection of the system.
Results: The proposed PMS framework successfully integrates automated road detection, 3D modeling, data analysis, and decision support modules. The case studies demonstrate that the framework can replace inefficient manual inspection, enable automated road distress classification and assessment, provide intuitive visualization of road conditions, and improve management performance through data-driven decision-making.
AI Model Used: Image recognition algorithms for automated road distress classification, 3D laser point cloud technology for modeling
Data Types: The paper uses 3D laser point cloud data, high-definition camera images, GPS data, road surface parameters (evenness, rut depth, texture depth), historical road distress records, maintenance records, operational life data, and roadside facility information collected from IoT sensors mounted on detection vehicles.

Title: AI BIM coordinator for non-expert interaction in building design using LLM-driven multi-agent systems
Abstract: The building construction domain relies heavily on human experts for specialized tasks, often leading to excessive workload and inefficiency. For example, building design coordination typically requires BIM coordinators to operate BIM tools on participants' behalf, with limited ability to capture procedural knowledge. To address this gap, this paper introduces AI-based BIM coordinators to enable non-BIM experts to seamlessly interact with BIM tools while automatically documenting valuable procedural information. The approach customizes five LLM-based agents equipped with fundamental building knowledge and skills. Through user prompts, the collaboration of "bim_assistant" and "checker" agents ensures the accuracy and consistency of generated code, while the "sender" and "executor" agents facilitate direct execution in BIM tools beyond text-based responses, with the "terminator" ending the process. An AutoGen-Revit prototype demonstrates feasibility, flexibility, robustness, and time-cost effectiveness for common BIM-based design coordination tasks, including space reasoning, 3D element creation, clash detection, informative 3D view saving, documentation, and retrieval. This paper enables specialist-free workflows and generates procedural logs and images that serve as datasets for future analysis.
Authors: Dong, Y.; Zhan, Z.; Hu, Y.; Doe, D.M.; Han, Z.
Construction Application Area: Building design coordination, BIM tool interaction, 3D space reasoning, building element creation, clash detection, procedural knowledge documentation
Methodology: The paper develops an AI BIM Coordination Workflow by customizing five LLM-based agents (bim_assistant, checker, sender, executor, terminator) with building knowledge and skills. The agents collaborate through AutoGen's GroupChat pattern to enable non-BIM experts to interact with BIM tools using natural language. A prototype system was developed integrating AutoGen with Revit, validated through experimental testing on a campus dormitory construction project with 30 tests per interaction type.
Research Goal: To develop an AI-based BIM coordinator that enables non-BIM experts to seamlessly interact with BIM tools using natural language while automatically documenting valuable procedural information, thereby reducing reliance on human BIM coordinators and improving design coordination efficiency.
Gaps Addressed: Current building design coordination heavily depends on human BIM coordinators to manually operate BIM tools on behalf of participants, creating excessive workload and time/place limitations. Additionally, there is a lack of effective methods to capture and document procedural knowledge from BIM tool interactions for future reuse.
Future Research: Future research should focus on optimizing agents' collaboration strategies and automating different communication mechanisms based on task complexity to improve time efficiency. Further work is needed on efficiently structuring system messages for agents, exploring model selection for diverse AEC tasks, reducing token usage through algorithm optimization, and applying data-driven methods like computer vision techniques to documented procedural knowledge for better design issue identification and resolution.
Results: The prototype system demonstrated an execution success rate of 93.3% and accuracy of 86.7% for building element creation, and 93.3% for both metrics in clash detection tasks. The system consumed an average of 87.3 seconds and $0.017 USD for element creation tasks, and 131.8 seconds and $0.03 USD for clash detection tasks, proving feasibility, flexibility, robustness, and cost-effectiveness compared to traditional meeting time and labor costs.
AI Model Used: OpenAI o1-mini (primary model used), gpt-4-1106-preview (for text-based retrieval tasks). Comparison testing also conducted with claude-sonnet-4-20,250,514, gemini-2.5-flash, mistral-medium-latest, and codestral-latest
Data Types: The paper uses BIM model data from a campus dormitory construction project (3681.1m² area, 16.2m height, 3 levels). Data includes building element semantic, geometric, and topological information from Revit/IFC files, along with clash detection results, 3D views, logged procedural information (timestamps, viewpoints, durations, zoom scales), and images captured during design coordination interactions.

Title: AI-driven enhancement of customer-centric design for improved satisfaction and decision-making
Abstract: Customer-centric design improves satisfaction by actively involving clients in the construction decision-making process. However, current Virtual Reality (VR) methods rely on manual processes, extensive human resources, and costly high-end hardware, restricting accessibility. This study proposes an AI-driven enhancement of customer-centric design in construction through an accessible smartphone-based VR platform integrated with an Intelligent Support System (ISS). The ISS employs an artificial neural network to predict customers' preferred design styles from user-uploaded images and utilizes the TOPSIS algorithm to rank design alternatives based on customer evaluations regarding cost, time, risk, and aesthetics. Selected designs are automatically communicated to the design team. A validation case study involving 30 construction industry participants indicated a satisfaction rate of 79 %, overall approval rating of 69 %, and an 80 % match between user-selected designs and ISS recommendations. The developed AI-driven VR platform effectively improves decision-making, customer satisfaction, and accessibility of customer-centric participation in construction.
Authors: Dorri, M.; Hoseinpour, S.; Maghrebi, M.
Construction Application Area: Customer-centric design, interior design material selection, architectural design
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (AI-driven VR and intelligent support systems for customer-centric design)
Methodology: The paper proposes an AI-driven VR platform integrated with an Intelligent Support System (ISS) for customer-centric design in construction. The ISS employs a CNN for design style recognition to predict customers' preferred design styles from uploaded images, and uses the TOPSIS algorithm for multi-criteria decision-making to rank design alternatives based on cost, time, risk, and aesthetics. The system was validated through a case study with 30 construction industry participants who interacted with a smartphone-based VR prototype.
Research Goal: The aim of this study is to develop and evaluate a VR platform equipped with AI-driven modules, including customer style preference detection and multi-criteria optimization, to overcome the limitations of traditional customer-centric design methods and improve customer satisfaction and decision-making in the construction industry.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses gaps in existing VR customer-centric design systems including: absence of integrated decision-making tools, inaccessibility of VR equipment requiring high-end hardware, technical complexity for non-technical clients, dependency on human resources (designers), and limited application of AI/ML for decision support in evaluating client preferences and ranking design solutions.
Future Research: Future work should focus on better capturing nuanced user preferences that are difficult to address with existing AI algorithms, optimization for better user experience on older smartphone models, and expanding the system to include additional architectural features and construction elements beyond wall surfaces.
Results: The validation case study with 30 participants indicated a satisfaction rate of 79%, overall approval rating of 69%, and an 80% match between user-selected designs and ISS recommendations. The proposed method reduced design process time by approximately 50% (from 60 to 30 minutes), achieved 80% realism compared to 65% for benchmark methods, and demonstrated cost efficiency by eliminating the need for expensive VR hardware and reducing reliance on design experts.
AI Model Used: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on VGG16 architecture for design style recognition; TOPSIS (Technique for Order of Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution) algorithm for multi-criteria decision making
Data Types: The data includes 900 interior design images (300 each for modern, minimalist, and classical styles) augmented to 10,000 images for training the CNN model. Additionally, material property data including cost, execution time, risk factors, and design style ratings were collected from RS Means database, subject matter experts, and previous studies, stored in CSV format.

Title: Collision Hazard Detection for Construction Worker Safety Using Audio Surveillance
Abstract: The ability to hear auditory safety cues of mobile equipment while wearing hearing protection equipment (HPE) is critical to preventing injuries and deaths in construction. Existing collision hazard detection models using proximity technologies have limited applicability due to the need for an expensive and complex deployment of sensing devices on every piece of construction equipment. This study proposes a more affordable collision prevention technology that uses audio signals to detect the presence of mobile equipment. The study addresses the problem by improving the auditory situational awareness for construction workers exposed to loud noises with a novel sound detection model that uses artificial intelligence (AI) to detect the sound of collision hazards buried in a great deal of ambient noises. This study included three phases: (1) collecting audio data of construction equipment, (2) developing a novel audio-based machine learning model for automated detection of collision hazards, and (3) conducting field experiments to investigate the system's efficiency and latency. The outcomes showed that the proposed model detects equipment correctly and can timely notify the workers of hazardous situations.
Authors: Elelu, K.; Le, T.; Le, C.
Journal: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Construction Application Area: Construction safety, specifically collision hazard detection for workers near mobile equipment
Methodology: The paper develops a CNN-based audio surveillance system for detecting mobile construction equipment sounds. The methodology includes three phases: (1) collecting and annotating audio data of construction equipment into normal (stationary) and abnormal (mobile) categories, (2) extracting acoustic features using mel-frequency cepstral coefficients (MFCCs) and training a 2D CNN model to classify sounds, and (3) implementing the model in a mobile Android application and conducting both controlled laboratory tests and field experiments to validate performance and latency.
Research Goal: To develop an affordable and effective collision hazard detection technology using audio signals and artificial intelligence that can augment construction workers' auditory situational awareness by detecting the presence of mobile equipment sounds buried in background noise, thereby helping prevent struck-by-equipment fatalities.
Gaps Addressed: Existing collision hazard detection methods using proximity technologies (GPS, RFID, Bluetooth, cameras) require expensive and complex deployment of sensors on every piece of equipment and have limitations in low-visibility conditions. Current audio-based methods focus on activity tracking rather than hazard detection, and previous sound classification models use single non-overlapping sounds rather than realistic soundscapes with multiple simultaneous sources.
Future Research: Future work should focus on sound source localization to estimate the location and direction of mobile equipment relative to workers, which would reduce false alarms. Additional research is needed to incorporate other background noise types beyond stationary equipment (e.g., natural sounds, transportation sounds), implement advanced denoising and signal enhancement techniques, develop ensemble models that adapt to different noise levels, and expand the training dataset with more equipment types and metadata about equipment characteristics.
Results: The best CNN model achieved a recall of 99.33% for detecting mobile equipment in non-overlapping sounds and 90.13% in mixed sounds at 10 dB SNR, demonstrating reliable performance in low-background-noise environments. Field experiments showed the mobile application could detect hazards with varying latency (first alerts ranging from 3-20 seconds depending on equipment type and distance), with faster detection in controlled environments and longer delays in real construction sites due to complex background noises, though the system maintained low false-alert rates.
AI Model Used: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) - specifically a 2D CNN architecture with four convolutional layers (with 16, 32, 64, and 128 filters), max-pooling, dropout regularization, two fully connected dense layers (128 and 64 nodes), ReLU activation functions, and softmax output layer for binary classification
Data Types: Audio recordings of construction equipment sounds collected from construction sites and supplemented by videos from YouTube and Freesound repositories. The dataset includes 5,400 seconds of abnormal sounds (9 types of mobile equipment: bulldozer, compactor roller, crane, excavator, forklift, front-end loader, grader, scraper, water truck) and 4,200 seconds of normal sounds (7 types of stationary equipment: concrete pumper, hammer, pile driver, pneumatic breaker, pneumatic tamper, saw, steel welding). Synthetic mixed sound data was generated at five different signal-to-noise ratios (10, 5, 0, -5, -10 dB) to simulate realistic construction soundscapes.

Title: Predictive digital monitoring of construction resources: an integrated digital twin solution
Abstract: Purpose Optimising resource utilisation on construction sites is essential for achieving key performance indicators related to cost, time and sustainability. While recent advances have explored the integration of 4D building information modelling (BIM) with technologies such as the Internet of things (IoT) and immersive tools, widespread adoption remains limited due to system complexity, scalability and integration challenges. This paper introduces the digital twins–based site resource monitoring (DTSRM) system as an integrated solution to support efficient and sustainable construction resource management. Design/methodology/approach A research gap was identified through a critical literature review. A predictive DTSRM system was then developed in multiple phases, including the creation of digital twin (DT) models and components such as ontology, IoT networks and machine learning (ML) algorithms. The system was tested using IoT simulators and C# scripts to ensure sensor data integration and functional validity. Synthetic datasets, designed to realistically simulate construction site conditions, were generated using Python to evaluate the system's performance. Findings DTSRM offers a real-time, integrated approach by collecting data from construction equipment and material storage through IoT sensors visualised within the BIM model. ML techniques enable the prediction of equipment productivity and the tracking of material consumption and inventory levels. This allows for timely, data-driven decisions that minimise delays and excess inventory costs. Practical implications DTSRM enables project stakeholders to prioritise resource allocation across multiple sites based on live productivity and inventory data. By reducing waste and improving efficiency, it directly supports circular economy principles in construction. The DTSRM ontology, though currently focused on equipment and materials, can be extended to include human resources for productivity and health and safety monitoring. Originality/value This research offers a practical, scalable solution that integrates DT, BIM, IoT and AI technologies to monitor construction resources in real time. Unlike many existing frameworks, DTSRM explicitly contributes to the circular economy by promoting resource efficiency, minimising waste and supporting informed decision-making across the construction lifecycle.
Authors: Elghaish, F.; Mohandes, S.R.; Rahimian, F.; Abrishami, S.; Hosseini, M.R.
Research Location: United Kingdom (Queen's University Belfast), though the system is designed to be applicable internationally
Construction Application Area: Resource monitoring and management (equipment productivity monitoring, material inventory tracking, construction site resource utilization)
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (BIM, digital twins, IoT/sensor networks, AI for resource monitoring)
Methodology: The paper employs Design Science Research (DSR) approach. A Digital Twins-Based Site Resource Monitoring (DTSRM) system was developed using Digital Twin Definition Language (DTDL), Azure Digital Twins platform, IoT sensors/simulators, and machine learning algorithms. The system was tested using IoT simulators and C# scripts to validate sensor data integration. Synthetic datasets were generated using Python to evaluate system performance, simulating realistic construction site conditions including equipment productivity and material consumption patterns.
Research Goal: The paper aims to present a new Digital Twins-Based Site Resource Monitoring (DTSRM) system that integrates BIM, IoT, and AI technologies to enable real-time monitoring and predictive management of construction resources (equipment and materials) on construction sites, addressing the inefficiencies of traditional manual resource monitoring methods.
