Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of integration between IoT sensors and BIM for real-time resource visualization, the absence of predictive tools using machine learning for equipment productivity and material consumption forecasting, and the limited use of bespoke digital twin ontologies specifically designed for construction site resource monitoring that can support simulation and AI-driven predictions.
Future Research: Future research will expand the testing scope to include a broader range of materials and various types of equipment to support development of additional machine learning models. The DTDL-based DTSRM ontology can be extended to include human resources for productivity and health and safety monitoring. Future implementations will incorporate mechanisms to automatically assess sensor accuracy and integrate materials management systems such as Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) calculations into an interactive PMO dashboard.
Results: The DTSRM system successfully demonstrates real-time integrated resource monitoring by collecting data from construction equipment and material storage through IoT sensors, visualized within BIM models. Machine learning techniques enable prediction of equipment productivity under various site conditions and tracking of material consumption and inventory levels, allowing for timely, data-driven decisions that minimize delays and excess inventory costs.
AI Model Used: Linear Regression (for predicting excavator productivity based on site complexity and soil hardness)
Data Types: Synthetic IoT sensor data simulating construction site conditions including equipment productivity metrics (excavator productivity measured in cubic meters per hour), material inventory levels (container height measurements), site complexity ratings, and soil hardness indicators. The synthetic datasets were generated using Python with 50,000 records to realistically represent actual equipment performance and parameter ranges across varying site conditions.

Title: Assessing Trust in Construction AI-Powered Collaborative Robots Using Structural Equation Modeling
Abstract: This study aimed to investigate the key technical and psychological factors that impact the architecture, engineering, and construction (AEC) professionals' trust in collaborative robots (cobots) powered by artificial intelligence (AI). This study seeks to address the critical knowledge gaps surrounding the establishment and reinforcement of trust among AEC professionals in their collaboration with AI-powered cobots. In the context of the construction industry, where the complexities of tasks often necessitate human-robot teamwork, understanding the technical and psychological factors influencing trust is paramount. Such trust dynamics play a pivotal role in determining the effectiveness of human-robot collaboration on construction sites. This research employed a nationwide survey of 600 AEC industry practitioners to shed light on these influential factors, providing valuable insights to calibrate trust levels and facilitate the seamless integration of AI-powered cobots into the AEC industry. Additionally, it aimed to gather insights into opportunities for promoting the adoption, cultivation, and training of a skilled workforce to effectively leverage this technology. A structural equation modeling (SEM) analysis revealed that safety and reliability are significant factors for the adoption of AI-powered cobots in construction. Fear of being replaced resulting from the use of cobots can have a substantial effect on the mental health of the affected workers. A lower error rate in jobs involving cobots, safety measurements, and security of data collected by cobots from jobsites significantly impact reliability, and the transparency of cobots' inner workings can benefit accuracy, robustness, security, privacy, and communication and result in higher levels of automation, all of which demonstrated as contributors to trust. The study's findings provide critical insights into the perceptions and experiences of AEC professionals toward adoption of cobots in construction and help project teams determine the adoption approach that aligns with the company's goals workers' welfare.
Authors: Emaminejad, N.; Kath, L.; Akhavian, R.
Journal: Journal of Computing in Civil Engineering
Construction Application Area: Human-robot collaboration, collaborative robots (cobots), construction automation, robotics integration, worker trust and acceptance
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (BIM, drones, laser scanning, robotics, digital twins, sensor networks)
Methodology: The paper employed a nationwide survey of 600 AEC industry practitioners distributed through LinkedIn and Prolific survey panel. Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) was used to analyze the relationships among technical and psychological factors influencing trust in AI-powered cobots. The study also incorporated confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) and tested multiple hypotheses about trust factors. The research built upon prior literature review and interviews with AEC practitioners to identify 13 key trust factors.
Research Goal: The study aimed to investigate the key technical and psychological factors that impact AEC professionals' trust in collaborative robots (cobots) powered by artificial intelligence, and to provide insights for calibrating trust levels and facilitating seamless integration of AI-powered cobots into the AEC industry.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses critical knowledge gaps surrounding the establishment and reinforcement of trust among AEC professionals in their collaboration with AI-powered cobots. It recognizes that while cobots are gaining popularity, their widespread adoption has been limited due to factors including lack of research on trust dynamics, and the unique complexities of construction tasks that require human-robot teamwork.
Future Research: Future research directions include conducting longitudinal experimental studies with real or simulated cobots to better understand trust calibration over time, measuring specific trust factors using physiological data from workers, exploring factors affecting overtrust and undertrust, examining trust development separately among different professional roles (laborers, managers, leadership), and investigating how trust varies across different project types and sizes.
Results: The SEM analysis revealed that safety and reliability are significant factors for adoption of AI-powered cobots in construction. Training and robustness directly contribute to safety perceptions. Communication between workers and cobots positively affects accuracy, robustness, security, privacy, and level of automation. The study found that transparency, though significant, varies in importance across different cobot applications. Direct hands-on experience and comprehensive training positively influence trust, while participants showed hesitancy in fully trusting autonomous cobots, preferring them under human supervision.
AI Model Used: No Model
Data Types: The study used survey data collected from 600 AEC industry practitioners through a Qualtrics survey. Data included demographic information (job position, education level, years of experience, technology familiarity), Likert-scale responses (28 questions on trust factors), rank-order questions (3 questions on preferences), and ratings of seven demonstrated cobot applications across multiple attributes (safety, reliability, transparency, security, privacy, robustness, accuracy). The survey also included converted categorical variables for education level, years of experience, and technology familiarity.

Title: Automating data integration for construction Life Cycle Assessment Using fuzzy matching and supervised learning
Abstract: Automating data-driven processes in construction is critical for enhancing efficiency and reducing human intervention. Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) in construction relies on the seamless integration of construction and environmental databases; however, manual processes are time-consuming and error-prone. This paper presents a methodology that fully automates data integration using fuzzy string matching and Random Forest (RF)-based supervised learning. The approach ensures accuracy and efficiency while eliminating manual effort. When applied to the Andalusia Construction Cost Database (BCCA) and ecoinvent databases, the methodology achieved high accuracy and reduced manual intervention time by 90%. This scalable solution advances automation in construction by facilitating real-time decision-making, improving digital workflows, and supporting sustainable construction practices through AI-driven data management.
Authors: Gachkar, D.; Gachkar, S.; Ghofrani, E.; Garcia-Martinez, A.; Angulo Bahón, C.
Construction Application Area: Life Cycle Assessment (LCA), environmental impact assessment, construction database integration, sustainable construction practices, material classification
Methodology: The paper proposes a four-step methodology combining fuzzy string matching and Random Forest supervised learning to automate the integration of construction and environmental databases. First, both databases are cleaned and standardized. Second, a mapping dictionary categorizes construction materials. Third, fuzzy matching identifies textually similar entries, followed by Random Forest classification to detect semantic matches beyond text similarity. Finally, unit conversions are performed using density values to align measurement units.
Research Goal: The goal is to fully automate the integration of construction and environmental databases for Life Cycle Assessment applications, enhancing efficiency and accuracy while eliminating manual effort and reducing time required for database correlation by 80-90%.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the labor-intensive, inconsistent, and error-prone nature of manual database integration for LCA in construction. It tackles challenges including data interoperability, lack of standardization, inconsistent naming conventions, and unit incompatibility between construction and environmental databases.
Future Research: Future research should explore expanding the methodology to composite materials and operational impacts, testing adaptability across diverse regional datasets and languages, refining unit conversion algorithms with uncertainty quantification, developing standalone software tools for practitioners, and integrating advanced AI methods such as domain-specific language models and transformer architectures.
Results: The methodology achieved 90% accuracy when applied to the Andalusia Construction Cost Database (BCCA) and ecoinvent databases, reducing manual intervention time by 80-90%. The Random Forest classifier reached 95% accuracy after iterative training with 2,500 labeled matches, demonstrating scalability and effectiveness in automating material matching despite textual and unit discrepancies.
AI Model Used: Random Forest (RF) classifier, fuzzy string matching algorithms (fuzzywuzzy package)
Data Types: The paper uses structured construction database data (Andalusia Construction Cost Database - BCCA) containing material codes, titles in Spanish/English, and units, as well as environmental database data (ecoinvent) with life cycle inventory information including material names, codes, units, and environmental impact factors for construction materials.

Title: ADVANCES IN HUMAN-ROBOT COLLABORATION (HRC) IN CONSTRUCTION 5.0 FOR BUILDING CONSTRUCTION: A BIBLIOMETRIC AND SYSTEMATIC LITERATURE REVIEW
Abstract: Construction, characterized by its inherent risks, demands innovative solutions to ensure the safety and efficiency of processes. Human-robot collaboration (HRC) emerges as a promising alternative in this context, especially within the framework of Construction 5.0. However, existing research presents gaps in the comprehensive understanding of the advances and future potential of this synergy. This study addresses this gap through a systematic review of 181 articles published between 2013 and 2024, using a methodology that combines quantitative and qualitative analysis. The results, obtained through an exhaustive literature search and data visualization tools, reveal emerging trends in HRC research within the building sector. In addition, it delves into three key areas of progress: the development of AI-assisted classification systems to improve efficiency in specific tasks, the exploration of robots with emotional capabilities for more flexible adaptation to work environments, and virtual reality training to optimize human-robot collaboration. In addition, three future lines of research are proposed to further explore HRC in the building sector. This research significantly expands the existing knowledge on this topic, placing it at the forefront of the discipline. It also provides practical guidelines for construction professionals to adopt the principles of Construction 5.0, enabling them to address the complex challenges of construction more effectively.
Authors: Garcés, G.
Journal: Journal of Information Technology in Construction
Construction Application Area: Human-robot collaboration (HRC) in building construction, including automation of construction tasks, material sorting, waste management, robotic assembly, inspection, demolition, and construction safety
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (BIM, drones, laser scanning, robotics, digital twins, sensor networks)
Methodology: This study employs a three-stage methodology combining bibliometric analysis with systematic literature review. Stage 1 involves a systematic review using PRISMA methodology to identify interactions in HRC. Stage 2 conducts bibliometric analysis and social network analysis using VOSviewer to assess publication trends, co-citation patterns, and keyword co-occurrence from 181 articles (2013-2024) retrieved from Web of Science. Stage 3 performs qualitative analysis of the discussions to identify key conceptual connections and technological trends in Construction 5.0.
Research Goal: The goal is to provide a comprehensive overview of advances in human-robot collaboration (HRC) in building construction within the Construction 5.0 framework, identify current research trends, cutting-edge practices, and propose future research directions to address existing knowledge gaps.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of comprehensive studies exploring the integration of HRC in building construction under Construction 5.0 principles. Specifically, it fills gaps regarding current trends, best practices, effectiveness of HRC protocols, and the challenges and opportunities of implementing HRC in the building sector.
Future Research: Future research should focus on three main directions: (1) ethical considerations and mitigation of algorithmic biases in HRC; (2) development of standards and regulations for HRC implementation in construction; and (3) exploring neurofeedback-enhanced HRC to optimize robot performance and human-robot collaboration in building construction environments.
Results: The bibliometric analysis reveals exponential growth in HRC research since 2020, with the United States, China, and Germany leading publications. Three key cutting-edge practices were identified: immersive virtual reality training for HRC, AI-powered classification systems for collaborative construction, and adaptive socio-emotional systems for collaborative robots. The study also identified key research trends including construction robotics, automation, system development, and safety as the most prominent themes.
AI Model Used: Deep learning, machine learning, artificial neural networks, convolutional neural networks (CNNs), reinforcement learning, hybrid AI systems combining symbolic reasoning with deep learning, spiking neural networks, and brain-computer interfaces (neurofeedback systems using EEG)
Data Types: Bibliometric data from 181 academic articles retrieved from Web of Science database (2013-2024), including publication metadata, citation networks, keyword co-occurrence data, author collaboration patterns, and institutional affiliations. Qualitative data extracted from systematic review of literature discussing HRC applications, technologies, and methodologies in construction.

Title: Optimizing Modularization of Residential Housing Designs for Rapid Postdisaster Mass Production of Housing
Abstract: Postdisaster housing reconstruction (PDHR) is a highly complex process because of the large number of recovery projects for affected communities and the shortage of resources after a disastrous event. PDHR also needs a strategy that reconsiders it as a large-scale integrated portfolio of projects instead of individual building reconstruction projects. However, this complexity and the lack of a holistic, systematic approach for planning frequently lead to an ad-hoc or case-by-case decision-making process. To resolve this critical challenge in postdisaster housing mass production, this study investigates and develops a systematic approach that optimizes the design modularization of housing recovery projects considering manufacturing, transportation, and assembly factors for a cost-efficient and sustainable implication of modular construction (MC) in PDHR. Using the genetic algorithm-based optimization method, the proposed method addresses the possible trade-offs between the commonality and suitability of the module configurations for PDHR projects. In addition, the authors used a set of feasible configurations of a variety of modular housing designs created from the AI-based generative design system and conducted the mass production scenarios after a disaster to validate the accuracy and robustness of the proposed methodology. The results clearly show that the proposed method significantly improved optimization and decision-making of MC design and construction processes and considerably enhanced rapid and logical responses to the demands of the postdisaster recovery process. The newly developed method is expected to assist the planners in formalizing the commonality concept in the PDHR process and achieving an optimal level of modularity and commonality that meet the required variation while maintaining the advantages of mass production.
Authors: Ghannad, P.; Lee, Y.-C.
Journal: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Construction Application Area: Post-disaster housing reconstruction, modular construction design optimization, design modularization, manufacturing, transportation, and assembly planning
Methodology: The paper develops a systematic optimization approach using genetic algorithm-based optimization (NSGA-II) to optimize modular housing designs for post-disaster housing reconstruction. It incorporates a module configurator that generates feasible modular configurations, modularity assessment (MSI index) for evaluating individual project suitability, commonality assessment (TCCI index) for portfolio-level optimization, and platform modules concept to balance standardization and customization needs.
Research Goal: To develop a systematic approach that optimizes the design modularization of housing recovery projects considering manufacturing, transportation, and assembly factors for cost-efficient and sustainable implementation of modular construction in post-disaster housing reconstruction, addressing both project-level and portfolio-level optimization needs.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses two main gaps: (1) existing literature on mass customization in MC neglects the effects of product modularization on downstream processes such as logistics and assembly, and (2) existing modular design optimization studies primarily focus on single projects rather than multiproject portfolios like post-disaster housing reconstruction, lacking systematic frameworks for addressing both commonality and individual project requirements.
Future Research: The paper suggests expanding the scope to include structural design aspects beyond architectural design, as the current work is limited to the early design phase. Additionally, incorporating more detailed factors from the detailed design phase and considering different platform module types with varying contributions to commonality would enhance the comprehensiveness of the optimization framework.
Results: The proposed method successfully produced Pareto optimal solutions that balance modular suitability (MSI) and commonality (TCCI). Results showed that 3-4 modules per configuration are often optimal, that platform modules with appropriate tolerance significantly increase commonality, and that the number of platform modules and tolerance levels have significant effects on achievable commonality levels in large-scale portfolios.
AI Model Used: Genetic Algorithm (NSGA-II - Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II)
Data Types: The paper uses hypothetical housing project data including floor plans with varying numbers of bedrooms, bathrooms, and footage areas. It also uses modular configuration data generated by a rule-based module configurator, connection libraries, transportation constraints, and platform module specifications with parametric definitions and tolerances.

Title: Enhanced understanding of bond strength of fiber-reinforcement-polymer bars reinforced concrete subjected to elevated temperature for interfacial failure prevention: Explainable artificial intelligence method
Abstract: Fiber-reinforcement-polymer (FRP) bars, as alternatives to traditional steel bars, effectively enhance the durability of concrete structures in extreme environments. However, the interfacial mechanical performance of FRP bars reinforced concrete degrades under elevated temperatures or fire, leading to bonded interface failure. The bond strength between FRP bars and concrete is critical to structural performance, but existing studies lack accurate prediction of its evolution under multi-factor coupling and clear mechanistic explanations. In order to accurately characterize the interfacial bond performance of FRP-reinforced concrete under elevated temperatures, three machine learning models, namely Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest (RF) and eXterme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), are established to predict bond strength using a database of 289 experimental results. These models are selected for their fitting capabilities matching the data's complexity (nonlinear coupling of multiple features): SVM handles moderate nonlinearity, RF adapts to mid-to-high complexity with anti-overfitting, and XGBoost captures intricate interactions for high complexity. Comparing them identifies the best-fitted model for bond strength prediction. In the established models based on the machine learning algorithms, the significant features are selected such as experimental temperature (T), diameter of FRP bar (d), anchorage length (l<inf>a</inf>), concrete compressive strength (f<inf>c</inf>), and concrete cover thickness to diameter of FRP bar ratio (c/d) considering the credibility of black-box models and the rationality of input features. In addition, the SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) method is employed to enhance interpretability and overcome the "black box" problem of the machine learning (ML) based prediction models. Beyond explaining model behavior, its application here provides actionable insights for fire-resistant design: it quantifies feature contributions and interactive effects, addressing the lack of mechanistic guidance in previous artificial intelligence (AI) based studies. This paper provides meaningful guidelines for the interfacial failure prevention and safe service of FRP bars reinforced concrete structures in the elevated temperature environment.
Authors: Hao, J.; Zhu, S.; J.; Yao, J.; Zhang, X.; Li, X.
Construction Application Area: Structural engineering - bond strength prediction of fiber-reinforcement-polymer (FRP) bars reinforced concrete under elevated temperatures for interfacial failure prevention
Methodology: The paper employs three machine learning models (SVM, Random Forest, and XGBoost) trained on a database of 289 experimental pull-out test records. Five input features are selected: experimental temperature, FRP bar diameter, anchorage length, concrete compressive strength, and concrete cover thickness to diameter ratio. The models predict bond strength, and the SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) method is employed to enhance interpretability by quantifying feature contributions and interactive effects.
Research Goal: To accurately characterize the interfacial bond performance of FRP-reinforced concrete under elevated temperatures through machine learning models and provide mechanistic insights for fire-resistant design by overcoming the 'black box' problem of AI models.
Gaps Addressed: Existing studies lack accurate prediction of bond strength evolution under multi-factor coupling and clear mechanistic explanations. Previous AI models have limited interpretability, small datasets lacking samples for extreme conditions, and a mismatch between model complexity and data characteristics.
Future Research: Future work should focus on systematic hyperparameter tuning to maximize model accuracy for practical engineering applications. Additionally, expanding samples of high-temperature (>500°C) pull-out tests is needed to enhance model generalization to extreme thermal conditions.
Results: The XGBoost model achieved the best performance with R² of 0.968 (training) and 0.905 (testing). SHAP analysis revealed that temperature exerts dominant influence on bond strength (five times that of FRP bar diameter), 300°C is a critical threshold for accelerated degradation, and smaller FRP bars (6-10 mm) mitigated high-temperature bond loss by 18-25%.
AI Model Used: Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest (RF), eXtreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost)
Data Types: The dataset comprises 289 experimental pull-out test records compiled from literature review, covering temperatures from 20 to 600°C, FRP bar diameters from 6 to 16 mm, anchorage lengths from 20 to 160 mm, concrete compressive strengths from 21.83 to 68.23 MPa, and c/d ratios from 3.15 to 12.5.

Title: Potential AI-Driven Algorithmic Collusion and Influential Factors in Construction Bidding
Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly aiding human decision makers in construction bidding processes by analyzing competitors' bidding patterns. However, concerns are emerging about the potential for AI-driven algorithmic collusion, which might inflate prices and disrupt fair competition in various sectors. Given the unique dynamics of the construction sector and its growing reliance on AI, understanding the impact of these algorithms on the bidding landscape is essential, both academically and practically. Thus, this study investigates the impact of AI in the construction bidding market on bid pricing patterns to predict how the landscape of the market might change as AI starts to play a more prominent role. We subjected AI bidding agents to repeated competitions with each other in computer-simulated construction bidding marketplaces. We focused on the markup decisions made by the AI bidders. Our findings indicate that AI bidders tend to develop cooperative strategies over time, leading to higher bids overall compared to lower, competitive bids. This collusive behavior was facilitated by frequent interactions (previous bidding competitions over time) between AI bidders. This collusive behavior was enabled by algorithms that aimed to maximize the profit and was hindered by algorithms that aimed to maximize the number of project wins. These findings highlight potential fairness and competitiveness issues in construction bidding with dominant AI bidders.
Authors: Heo, C.; Park, M.; Ahn, C.R.
Journal: Journal of Computing in Civil Engineering
Construction Application Area: Construction bidding, specifically markup decision strategies and bid pricing in competitive bidding processes
Methodology: The paper employs computer simulation using reinforcement learning (Q-learning algorithm) to model AI bidding agents competing in repeated construction bidding competitions. The agents learn optimal markup strategies through trial and error, receiving rewards based on different bidding objectives. Experiments varied key factors including number of bidders (2-5), bidding objectives (maximize wins vs. maximize profit), and frequency of interaction, with 900 experiments conducted across different parameter settings.
Research Goal: The study aims to predict how the growing integration of AI into the construction bidding market might influence bidders' markup strategies and market trends, specifically investigating whether AI bidders develop collusive behavior and identifying factors that facilitate or hinder such algorithmic collusion.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of research investigating how the introduction and integration of AI might impact the construction bidding market, particularly regarding potential algorithmic collusion. While previous studies focused on developing AI algorithms for bidding effectiveness, there was limited research on anticipating how the construction bidding market landscape might change due to ongoing competition among AI bidders.
Future Research: Future research should incorporate bid/no-bid decision processes, modify reward functions to include penalties for unsuccessful bids, explore faster learning algorithms (e.g., DQN, actor-critic), and expand experiments to include multiple winner determination methods beyond lowest-bid criteria. Additionally, research should investigate whether pre-trained agents and offline training can reduce the number of iterations needed for collusion, and examine how knowledge transfers across different bidding algorithms and market conditions.
Results: AI bidders following profit-maximization objectives exhibited collusive behavior by maintaining inflated bid prices (MUCR of 1.198) significantly higher than human bidders (MUCR of 0.943) and competitive AI bidders (MUCR of 1.05). Collusive behavior was facilitated by frequent interactions between AI bidders and profit-maximization algorithms, while it was hindered by increased number of competitors and win-maximization algorithms. The collusive pricing pattern persisted across 900 different parameter configurations, demonstrating robustness of the findings.
AI Model Used: Q-learning algorithm (reinforcement learning)
Data Types: Historical bidding data from Washington State Department of Transportation (WSDOT) comprising 1,244 public construction projects valued between 1-5 million USD, including project descriptions, submitted bids, winning bids, and contractor names. The data was preprocessed using the Interquartile Range (IQR) method to remove outliers, resulting in MUCR values ranging from 0.7 to 1.2.

Title: Algorithmic Collusion in Construction Bidding: Risks, Dynamics, and Regulatory Implications
Abstract: The rise of algorithmic decision-making across various industries has introduced both opportunities and challenges, particularly in markets in which competitive pricing is crucial. This study investigated the potential for algorithm-induced collusion in the construction bidding market, a sector that increasingly is adopting artificial intelligence (AI) for bid pricing. Using a reinforcement learning-based simulation framework, we investigated how algorithms interact under various market conditions and bid evaluation methods. Our findings indicate that algorithmic bidders gradually can converge to collusive strategies, consistently leading to supracompetitive prices across different market scenarios. Remarkably, they exhibited collusive coordination even when matched with a new opponent. We also found that different bid evaluation methods influence the speed and stability of this collusive behavior. These results highlight the need for regulatory attention to algorithmic collusion risks in construction bidding markets, particularly in scenarios with limited competition and price-focused evaluations.
Authors: Heo, C.; Ahn, C.R.; Oh, M.; Park, M.
Construction Application Area: Construction bidding, bid pricing, procurement
Methodology: The paper employs a reinforcement learning-based simulation framework using multiagent systems (MAS) to model algorithmic bidding behavior. Specifically, it implements Q-learning (Tabular Q-learning and Deep Q-Networks) to simulate how algorithmic agents interact and learn bidding strategies under various market conditions, bid evaluation methods, and levels of competition.
Research Goal: To investigate the potential for algorithm-induced collusion in construction bidding markets by examining how AI-driven pricing algorithms interact under different market scenarios and to understand the dynamics through which algorithmic collusion may emerge, stabilize, or be disrupted.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the knowledge gap regarding how algorithmic interactions in construction bidding lead to collusive behavior under varied market conditions. While previous studies have shown algorithmic collusion is possible, the robustness and transferability of these behaviors across different bidding environments, evaluation methods, and competitive scenarios remained underexplored.
Future Research: Future research should explore heterogeneous agent environments to assess how differences in algorithmic architecture, information access, and strategic objectives influence collusion likelihood. Additionally, studies should incorporate more complex bidding rules, multiproject portfolios, dynamic market entry/exit, and investigate the impact of regulatory constraints, transparency levels, and algorithmic diversity.
Results: Algorithmic bidders gradually converged to collusive strategies, consistently leading to supracompetitive prices across different market scenarios. Agents exhibited collusive coordination even when matched with new opponents, and different bid evaluation methods influenced the speed and stability of this collusive behavior, with mean bid methods leading to fastest convergence.
AI Model Used: Reinforcement Learning: Q-learning (Tabular Q-learning - TQL), Deep Q-Networks (DQN)
Data Types: Historical bid data from Washington State Department of Transportation (WSDOT) containing 1,244 public construction projects valued between USD 1-5 million, including project descriptions, submitted bids, winning bids, and contractor names. The data was preprocessed using the interquartile range (IQR) method to remove outliers.

Title: Hybrid artificial intelligence approach based on metaheuristic and machine learning for slope stability assessment: A multinational data analysis
Abstract: Slope stability assessment is a critical research area in civil engineering. Disastrous consequences of slope collapse necessitate better tools for predicting their occurrences. This research proposes a hybrid Artificial Intelligence (AI) for slope stability assessment based on metaheuristic and machine learning. The contribution of this study to the body of knowledge is multifold. First, advantages of the Firefly Algorithm (FA) and the Least Squares Support Vector Classification (LS-SVC) are combined to establish an integrated slope prediction model. Second, an inner cross-validation with the operating characteristic curve computation is embedded in the training process to reliably construct the machine learning model. Third, the FA, an effective and easily implemented metaheuristic, is employed to optimize the model construction process by appropriately selecting the LS-SVM's hyper-parameters. Finally, a dataset that contains 168 real cases of slope evaluation, recorded in various countries, is used to establish and confirm the proposed hybrid approach. Experimental results demonstrate that the new hybrid AI model has achieved roughly 4% improvement in classification accuracy compared with other benchmark methods.
Authors: Hoang, N.-D.; Pham, A.-D.
Construction Application Area: Slope stability assessment in civil engineering and geotechnical engineering
Methodology: The paper proposes a hybrid AI framework combining Least Squares Support Vector Classification (LS-SVC) with the Firefly Algorithm (FA) for slope stability assessment. The FA is used to optimize the LS-SVC's hyperparameters (regularization parameter γ and RBF kernel parameter σ). An inner five-fold cross-validation with area under the ROC curve (AUC) computation is embedded in the training process to reliably construct the model.
Research Goal: The goal is to develop a novel hybrid artificial intelligence model for predicting slope stability conditions (stable or unstable) that achieves better accuracy than existing methods. The model aims to provide civil engineers with an effective tool to prevent and mitigate damages from slope collapses.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the limited investigation of LS-SVC capability in slope failure prediction despite its success in other domains. It also tackles the challenge of appropriately selecting LS-SVC's hyperparameters and the need for reliable model construction through embedded cross-validation and metaheuristic optimization.
Future Research: Future research should incorporate probabilistic and fuzzy theories to assess uncertainty in classification outputs, investigate other efficient machine learning algorithms and metaheuristics, study the effects of different kernel functions, apply data transformation methods for preprocessing, and include more relevant factors like slope aspect, vegetation coverage, and effects of typhoons and earthquakes to enhance predictive capability.
Results: The proposed MO-LSVC achieved the best prediction performance with an AUC of 0.86 and classification accuracy of 86.31%, representing roughly 4% improvement over the second-best model (RBP-ANN at 82.12%). On average, the model committed only 2.3 false classifications, with 0.8 false stable and 1.5 false unstable cases.
AI Model Used: Least Squares Support Vector Classification (LS-SVC), Firefly Algorithm (FA), Support Vector Classifier (SVC), Artificial Neural Network (ANN) with Levenberg-Marquardt (LM-ANN), Scaled Conjugate Gradient (SCG-ANN), and Resilient Back-Propagation (RBP-ANN), Relevance Vector Machine (RVM), Bayes Discriminant Analysis (BDA)
Data Types: The dataset contains 168 real cases of slope evaluation from various countries including India, UK, China, and Vietnam, collected from literature. Six influencing factors characterize each slope: unit weight (kN/m³), soil cohesion (kPa), internal friction angle (°), slope angle (°), slope height (m), and pore pressure ratio, with binary outputs indicating stable (1) or unstable (0) conditions.

Title: Sequence image layout generation for construction accident simulation using domain-tuned NER by ZSL-PLM and scene graph learning
Abstract: Safety in the construction industry remains a critical concern, with accident analysis often relying on time-intensive, error-prone manual processes. The unstructured nature of accident reporting text complicates the extraction and interpretation of sequence information essential for understanding accident dynamics. This study addresses these challenges by proposing a domain-specific AI framework that automates the extraction, structuring, predicting, and visualization of sequence information to generate interpretable 2D image layouts. A key achievement of the framework is its ability to infer accident sequences and generate layouts without relying on large-scale or ground truth image datasets for training. By integrating domain-tuned named entity recognition (NER), scene graph learning, and graph convolutional networks (GCNs), the framework achieves a highly robust entity diversity and demonstrates accurate entity recognition. These metrics, alongside notable improvements in spatial alignment (+88.89 %) and temporal consistency (+68.75 %) over the text-based model using DALL-E API, laying a foundation for robust framework advancing domain-specific deep learning applications and enhancing sequential analysis of construction accidents.
Authors: Hong, E.; Yi, J.-S.
Construction Application Area: Construction safety management, accident analysis, and simulation
Methodology: The paper proposes a domain-specific AI framework integrating zero-shot learning (ZSL)-based named entity recognition (NER), scene graph construction, and graph convolutional networks (GCNs) to automatically extract, structure, and visualize accident sequences from unstructured construction accident reports. The framework uses BART-based entity extraction, GPT-3.5-turbo for chunk extraction, dependency parsing for relation extraction, GCN-based scene graph embedding, and constraint-based layout generation to produce 2D sequential image layouts representing accident scenarios.
Research Goal: The study aims to automate the extraction and visualization of sequence information from construction accident reports to enhance the accuracy and scalability of accident analysis. By transforming unstructured text into structured scene graphs and interpretable 2D image layouts, the framework seeks to provide comprehensive understanding of accident scenarios without requiring large-scale annotated image datasets.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the limitations of manual, time-intensive, and error-prone accident analysis processes that rely on unstructured textual reports. It also fills the gap in applying structured sequence image layouts to construction accident scenarios, where existing general-purpose text-to-image models fail to capture precise spatial, causal, and temporal relationships essential for domain-specific safety analysis.
Future Research: Future work should incorporate human-centered evaluation methods such as expert reviews and user studies to validate the interpretability and practical utility of the generated layouts. Additionally, extending the framework to time-series analysis and dynamic visualizations, addressing data imbalances through augmentation techniques, and applying the methodology to other high-risk domains like healthcare and aviation are recommended directions.
Results: The framework achieved strong performance with domain-tuned NER metrics including F1-score of 0.78, Shannon Entropy of 6.07, and Simpson's Diversity Index of 0.97. Scene graph construction achieved structural similarity of 0.87 and coverage of 0.79. The sequence image layout generation module demonstrated superior performance over DALL-E API, with improvements of +88.89% in spatial alignment, +68.75% in temporal consistency, and +66.04% in semantic accuracy, along with a Fréchet Inception Distance of 17.6 and temporal consistency of 0.81.
AI Model Used: BART (facebook/bart-large-mnli) for zero-shot entity recognition, GPT-3.5-turbo for chunk extraction, Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) for scene graph embedding and learning, FAISS for vector database storage and retrieval
Data Types: The study uses 11,513 construction accident reports collected from the OSHA (Occupational Safety and Health Administration) Fatality and Catastrophe Investigation Summaries database, covering the period from September 21, 1972, to March 27, 2024. The unstructured textual data includes accident descriptions with entities such as workers, equipment, hazards, injuries, and temporal/spatial/causal relationships, which are preprocessed through tokenization, part-of-speech tagging, and text normalization.

Title: Comparing Natural Language Processing Methods to Cluster Construction Schedules
Abstract: The names of construction activities are the only unstructured data attribute in construction schedules, and they often guide construction execution. Activity names are devised to communicate between stakeholders, and therefore often are written using inconsistent terminologies across repetitive activities with omitted contextual information. This presents a challenge for machine learning systems when learning patterns from construction schedules. This paper compared the performance of state-of-the-art text-related clustering methods in identifying repetitive activities. This was achieved by creating a ground truth data set on the basis of the standard construction work classification, and then comparing the precision, recall, and F1 score of latent semantic analysis (LSA), latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA), word2vec, and fastText algorithms to group activity names in 27 construction schedules. Results indicated that the F1 score of LSA outperformed LDA (0.84% versus 0.88%), whereas the results of language models-based clustering depended on the quality of word embedding and the paired clustering method. This study provides insight into how to preprocess activity names of construction schedules for further artificial intelligence (AI)-based quantitative analysis. Methodologies described in this study will help researchers who work on natural language-related research in construction (e.g., safety and contract management) to better capture the feature of words, rather than only counting the word frequencies.
Authors: Hong, Y.; Xie, H.; Bhumbra, G.; Brilakis, I.
Journal: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Construction Application Area: Construction schedule clustering and activity name classification
Methodology: The paper compares natural language processing (NLP) methods to cluster construction schedule activities. It tests topic modeling methods (LSA and LDA) and language model-based clustering (word2vec and fastText paired with k-means and Gaussian mixture clustering). A ground-truth dataset was created using British Standard Method of Measurement (SMM7) to label 17,665 activities from 27 construction schedules, and performance was evaluated using precision, recall, and F1 scores.
Research Goal: The goal is to identify the most effective NLP method for clustering construction activity names with inconsistent terminologies, enabling better comparison of schedules across projects and learning from historic project data.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the challenge of inconsistent naming conventions in construction activities that limit machine learning systems' ability to learn patterns from schedules. Current research focuses on numerical schedule data while neglecting the valuable text descriptions in activity names.
Future Research: Future research can explore sentence embedding applications for differentiating construction activities and graph clustering methods. The methodology can be applied to other construction NLP areas like safety management, contract management, risk management, and maintenance management. Researchers can also investigate whether a bottom-up classification approach can design more practical construction work taxonomies.
Results: LSA outperformed LDA with F1 scores of 0.84-0.88 versus 0.78-0.84. Language model-based clustering (word2vec and fastText) achieved F1 scores of 0.77-0.79, which was less accurate than topic modeling methods. Self-trained language models performed similarly to pretrained models, and Gaussian mixture clustering showed consistent results across different language models.
AI Model Used: Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA), Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), word2vec (CBOW model), fastText, k-means clustering, Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) clustering
Data Types: The study used 27 construction project schedules with 17,665 activities from a UK Tier-1 contractor, including projects across various construction areas (national laboratories, nuclear power stations, roads, water treatment plants, bridges, docks, car parks, waste treatment plants, and harbors). Activities were manually labeled using the British Standard Method of Measurement (SMM7) classification system to create a ground-truth dataset.

Title: Massive-Scale construction dataset synthesis through Stable Diffusion for Machine learning training
Abstract: Advancements of artificial intelligence (AI)-driven image generation provide opportunities to address a problem in machine learning applications that have suffered from a lack of domain-specific training data. This study explores the feasibility of employing synthesized images (SIs) generated through Stable Diffusion as training data for construction. This study aims to examine the potential of Stable Diffusion in construction, and the performance of convolutional neural network (CNN) models trained exclusively on SIs. A total of 82.01% of images synthesized are suitable for representing construction tasks. The CNN model trained on preprocessed SIs (with context-based labeling results) exhibited a classification accuracy of 89.09%. The CNN model trained solely on raw SIs (synthesized images without context-based labeling results) achieved a successful classification rate of 86.51% for the images. This study presents the viability of SIs as a training dataset and introduces context-based labeling through object detection techniques, enhancing the performance of estimation models.
Authors: Hong, S.; Choi, B.; Ham, Y.; Jeon, J.; Kim, H.
Construction Application Area: Vision-based activity recognition, task estimation, construction worker monitoring, productivity analysis
Methodology: The paper employs Stable Diffusion to generate 150,000 synthetic images (SIs) of five construction tasks. Two CNN models are trained: CNN-PSI using pre-processed SIs with context-based labeling (using OpenPose for skeleton extraction, YOLOv6 for object detection within ROI, and distance-weighted labeling), and CNN-RSI using raw SIs with prompt-based labels. Both models are evaluated on 15,000 real-world construction site images.
Research Goal: To explore the feasibility of using AI-generated synthetic images from Stable Diffusion as training data for construction task estimation, and to assess the performance of CNN models trained exclusively on synthetic images when applied to real-world construction scenarios.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the scarcity of domain-specific training data in construction ML applications and the labor-intensive nature of manual data collection and labeling. It proposes using synthetic image generation and automated context-based labeling to overcome these limitations.
Future Research: Future research should focus on integrating synthetic images with real-world data, mitigating biases in synthetic image generation, conducting large-scale field trials, addressing occlusions in construction sites, and exploring temporal aspects of image synthesis to capture changes over project timelines.
Results: Stable Diffusion generated 82.01% appropriate construction task images. CNN-PSI achieved 89.09% classification accuracy on real-world images, while CNN-RSI achieved 86.51% accuracy. Context-based labeling showed 94.41% accuracy with an F1-score of 0.9660, significantly outperforming CLIP (64.25% accuracy) while requiring substantially less time than human labeling.
AI Model Used: Stable Diffusion SDXL 1.0, EfficientNet-B0 (CNN), OpenPose (VGG-19 based), YOLOv6, CLIP (for comparison)
Data Types: The paper uses 150,000 synthetic images generated by Stable Diffusion for five construction tasks (masonry, tile, rebar, formwork, support) with 30,000 images per task. For validation, 15,000 real-world construction site photographs were collected over 20 months from three apartment construction sites in South Korea (3,000 images per task). Additionally, 6,529 manually labeled object images were used to train the YOLOv6 object detection model.

Title: Conversational AI-based VR system to improve construction safety training of migrant workers
Abstract: Inadequate communication during construction safety training can hinder knowledge transfer to workers, particularly migrant workers who face linguistic and literacy barriers, resulting in higher rates of fatal injuries. Additionally, limited abilities of trainers can further exacerbate this problem. To overcome these gaps, this paper presents a virtual reality-based knowledge delivery approach by integrating artificial Intelligence (AI) agent using ChatGPT as a live instructor. The system's effectiveness is assessed with participants from five countries. Results show a significant increase of 23% in scores between pre- and post-tests, indicating improved knowledge. The system performs uniformly across diverse language and educational backgrounds particularly benefiting those with prior experience. This research significantly contributes to the global construction community by improving safety training, promotes safer practices, and ultimately reduces accidents in diversified construction sites. It also opens avenues for research in immersive technologies and AI techniques, advancing in training methodologies and enhancing safety outcomes.
Authors: Hussain, R.; Sabir, A.; Lee, D.-Y.; Zaidi, S.F.A.; Pedro, A.; Abbas, M.S.; Park, C.
Construction Application Area: Construction safety training
Methodology: The study employed an experimental approach with a pre-post test design to evaluate the effectiveness of iSafeCom, a VR-based training system integrated with conversational AI (ChatGPT). The system was developed using Unity game engine, incorporating Whisper for speech recognition, ChatGPT for conversational interaction, and Amazon Polly for text-to-speech conversion. Experiments were conducted with 52 participants from five countries to assess knowledge acquisition and the influence of personal attributes (language diversity, prior knowledge, education level).
Research Goal: The primary objective is to develop and evaluate iSafeCom, a conversational AI-based VR system that enhances construction safety training for migrant workers by overcoming language barriers and communication challenges. The system aims to improve knowledge transfer through interactive, personalized training experiences with a virtual instructor.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the inadequate communication during construction safety training that hinders knowledge transfer to migrant workers who face linguistic and literacy barriers. It also addresses the limited effectiveness of traditional training methods and the lack of tailored safety training systems that cater to the specific needs of diverse migrant worker populations.
Future Research: Future research directions include developing a construction industry-specific language model, expanding iSafeCom into a multi-user metaverse platform with VR and AR technologies, integrating blockchain for incentivization and reward systems, and incorporating wearable technologies with AI predictive models for real-time safety feedback and alerts.
Results: The study found a significant 23% increase in knowledge scores between pre- and post-tests (from 4.58 to 6.81 mean score), with a large effect size (Cohen's d = 1.925). The system performed uniformly across diverse language and educational backgrounds, with experienced participants benefiting more than novices (40% vs 25% knowledge acquisition index).
AI Model Used: ChatGPT (Large Language Model), Whisper (speech recognition), Amazon Polly (text-to-speech), Oculus Lip Sync
Data Types: The data included pre- and post-test quiz scores from 52 participants across five countries, demographic information (age, gender, language, education level, domain experience), and conversational interaction logs. Multiple-response questions were randomly selected from a database of 50 questions for each hazard scenario (working on elevated platforms, climbing ladders, using scaffolding).

Title: ACCEPTANCE OF CONTEMPORARY TECHNOLOGIES FOR COST MANAGEMENT OF CONSTRUCTION PROJECTS
Abstract: The construction industry has become more digital and the traditional methods of construction activities are gradually becoming outdated. In this era of digital construction, various information and communication technologies have been developed and deployed to the site for the management and control of construction activities including cost management. Irrespective of the benefits of adopting these technologies, most of them are still not readily accepted for use for construction management. This study articulated Seven (7) recent technologies driving the industry and evaluated their acceptance for cost management of construction projects. The technologies include mobile technology, Augmented/Virtual Reality (AR/VR), Building Information Modeling (BIM), Internet of Things (IoT), Autonomous Equipment (Drones and Robotics), Artificial Intelligence (AI), and Predictive Analytics (PA). Data was gathered using a restructured questionnaire and technology acceptance model analysis was performed to identify which of the technologies have higher acceptance for cost management based on the criteria of availability, affordability, frequency of use, usefulness for cost management, and acceptance in the industry. Test statistics using Spearman's correlations and Kendall's correlations for each of the technologies and Spearman's Correlations of Technology acceptance with other variables in the TAM Model were performed. The results showed that mobile technology has higher correlation values than other technologies, and therefore has a higher acceptance for cost management. Kendall's coefficient of concordance values and Spearman's correlation values for Mobile technology were all above 0.6 which indicates a high level of agreement among the raters and strong relationships between the compared TAM variables.
Authors: Igwe, U.S.; Mohamed, S.F.; Mat Dzahir, M.B.M.; Ugulu, R.A.; Ajayi, O.
Journal: Journal of Information Technology in Construction
Construction Application Area: Cost management of construction projects, including cost estimation, cost planning, cost control, budgeting, forecasting, quantity measurement, valuations, and resource management
Methodology: The research used a structured questionnaire distributed online via Google Forms to 349 Quantity Surveyors in Nigeria. The Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) framework was applied with extended criteria including technology availability, affordability, and frequency of use. Data analysis employed non-parametric statistical tests including Spearman's correlations and Kendall's coefficient of concordance to evaluate relationships between TAM variables and determine technology acceptance levels.
Research Goal: To identify and evaluate the acceptance of seven contemporary technologies (Mobile Technology, AR/VR, BIM, IoT, Drones/Robotics, AI/ML, and Predictive Analytics) for cost management of construction projects, determining which technologies are most readily accepted in the construction industry.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the gap between the potential benefits of contemporary ICT technologies for construction cost management and their actual adoption and acceptance in practice. Despite technological advances, cost overruns remain inevitable, suggesting insufficient integration and acceptance of available technologies for effective cost control.
Future Research: The paper does not explicitly mention specific future research directions. However, it implies the need for further investigation into maximizing the use of all identified technologies beyond mobile technology for effective cost management.
Results: Mobile technology demonstrated the highest acceptance among all seven technologies, with Kendall's tau_b and Spearman's rho correlation values all above 0.6, indicating strong relationships between TAM variables. Mobile technology showed correlation values of 0.614 (availability), 0.676 (ease of use), 0.564 (frequency of use), 0.498 (affordability), and 0.853 (usefulness for cost control) with technology acceptance, all higher than other technologies evaluated.
AI Model Used: No Model
Data Types: Primary data collected through Likert-scale questionnaire responses from 349 Quantity Surveyors in Nigeria, assessing their perceptions of technology availability, affordability, ease of use, frequency of use, usefulness for cost management, and acceptance level for seven contemporary technologies.

Title: Question-Answering System Powered by Knowledge Graph and Generative Pretrained Transformer to Support Risk Identification in Tunnel Projects
Abstract: Risk identification is fundamental to effective risk management in any construction project. It is especially true for tunnel projects where technicality and complexity increase the risks, uncertainties, and challenges of realistic risk identification. However, two important issues need to be addressed in the current risk identification practice. First is the organization of previous project risk information (data) in unstructured and semistructured formats which hinders the use of emerging AI-based techniques and digitization of the risk management process. Second, overreliance on manual efforts and human experience, which is labor-intensive, error-prone, and time-consuming. To address these issues, this study proposes an intelligent question-answering system based on a knowledge graph and generative pretrained transformers (GPT) model. This study developed a tunnel risk knowledge graph (TRisKG) and then integrated the TRisKG with a GPT model to develop a question-answering system for tunnel project risks (QASTRisk). The proposed QASTRisk yielded a precision value of 97%, recall of 94%, and an F-1 score of 95%, indicating excellent performance compared to the existing studies. The proposed approach is appropriate for quick, effective, and intuitive risk identification at the early stage of tunnel projects as it can facilitate timely and reliable decision-making to prevent safety accidents and reduce project delays and cost overruns. Also, this work contributes to the body of knowledge by providing a framework for developing intelligent systems using artificial intelligence (AI) techniques to assist project managers in automatic and efficient risk identification at the preconstruction phase of the project. Thus, this enhances decision-making, improves project performance and the efficiency of project risk management practices, and increases productivity in the construction industry.
Authors: Isah, M.A.; Kim, B.-S.
Journal: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Construction Application Area: Risk management and risk identification in tunnel construction projects
Methodology: The paper develops a tunnel risk knowledge graph (TRisKG) from 142 risks and 369 risk factors validated by experts, then integrates it with OpenAI's GPT-3.5 model to create a question-answering system (QASTRisk). The system uses few-shot prompting to train GPT-3.5 to convert natural language questions into Cypher queries that retrieve risk information from the Neo4j graph database.
Research Goal: To develop an intelligent question-answering system that can facilitate automated, quick, and accurate risk identification in tunnel projects by integrating knowledge graph technology with GPT models, thereby reducing manual efforts and improving efficiency in the risk management process.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses two main gaps: (1) lack of structured knowledge bases for construction project risks that hinder integration with AI-based models and risk knowledge reuse, and (2) overreliance on manual, labor-intensive, error-prone, and time-consuming methods for risk identification that lack robust AI-based automation frameworks.
Future Research: Future research should develop more robust knowledge graphs incorporating risks for other tunnel types and construction project categories (highways, bridges, buildings, etc.), construct more complex ontologies to support complex question-answering beyond factoid questions, and integrate other risk management processes (assessment, response, monitoring) into the system for holistic risk management.
Results: The proposed QASTRisk system achieved excellent performance with 97% precision, 94% recall, and 95% F-1 score on the test set, comparable to or better than existing question-answering systems. The system successfully retrieves risk information from natural language queries without requiring predefined question templates, demonstrating flexibility and robust support for risk identification.
AI Model Used: OpenAI's GPT-3.5 model (gpt-3.5-turbo-instruct), Knowledge Graph, Neo4j graph database
Data Types: Risk data for NATM tunnel projects including 142 risks and 369 risk factors collected from 42 published research articles and validated by 8 experts with 10-25 years of experience. Data is organized into 15 work packages and 6 risk categories (design, construction, technical, safety, management, and environmental risks).

Title: AI-augmented construction cost estimation: an ensemble Natural Language Processing (NLP) model to align quantity take-offs with cost indexes
Abstract: Accurate construction cost estimation is crucial for the financial success of construction projects. Effective financial management in construction heavily relies on a robust and reliable estimation system. Building Information Modeling (BIM) has emerged as a powerful tool, providing precise quantities for various building elements through Quantity Take-Offs (QTOs). Traditionally, Quantity Surveyors (QSs) match these QTOs with cost indexes, a task that is both labor-intensive and prone to errors due to subjectivity and inconsistencies in classification systems and software structures. This paper presents an ensemble Natural Language Processing (NLP)-based method designed to automatically align QTOs with corresponding cost indexes across different building classifications and works. Our proposed method leverages advanced NLP techniques to analyze and align textual descriptions in QTOs with corresponding items in cost indexes. The system was rigorously tested on a high-rise residential building project, where it demonstrated an 82.96% agreement rate with QS-based estimations, highlighting its semantic alignment accuracy. The model also achieved only −2.05% total cost deviation from QS estimates. The proposed Artificial Intelligence (AI)-driven system complements QSs by serving as a notification tool, highlighting discrepancies between human and system-generated estimates. This approach aids QSs in refining their cost assessments, enhancing accuracy and mitigating potential financial risks.
Authors: Jafary, P.; Shojaei, D.; Rajabifard, A.; Ngo, T.
Construction Application Area: Construction cost estimation, specifically aligning Quantity Take-Offs (QTOs) with Construction Cost Indexes (CCIs)
Methodology: The paper develops an ensemble NLP-based framework using four base models (spaCy, BERT, Word2Vec, GloVe) and their adjusted variants to automatically match QTO work descriptions with corresponding cost items in the AIQS Building Cost Index (BCI). The system involves text preprocessing, similarity calculation using cosine similarity, and ensemble voting to achieve consensus-based cost assignment. The model performance is evaluated by comparing system predictions with Quantity Surveyor (QS) assignments on a real-world 13-level residential building project.
Research Goal: The goal is to develop an AI-driven NLP-based system that automatically aligns free-text work descriptions in Quantity Take-Offs with standardized entries in Construction Cost Indexes, thereby reducing manual effort, improving consistency, and enhancing accuracy in construction cost estimation workflows.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the challenge of semantic alignment between QTO descriptions and standardized cost database entries, a task traditionally handled manually by Quantity Surveyors that is time-consuming, subjective, and prone to inconsistencies. While prior research has explored NLP in cost management, limited studies have specifically tackled the alignment of QTOs with CCIs using NLP techniques.
Future Research: Future work should expand the case study to diverse construction projects and building types, incorporate feedback from multiple Quantity Surveyors through mixed-method approaches, integrate with additional CCIs like Rawlinsons and CoreLogic's CCCI, and develop domain-specific language models tailored to the construction industry. The system should also be extended to international classification standards to broaden its applicability.
Results: The ensemble model achieved an overall alignment accuracy of 82.96% with QS-assigned cost descriptions across 592 work items in 19 classifications. Despite this disagreement rate, the total cost deviation between the NLP-generated and QS-assigned Bill of Quantities was only -2.05%, demonstrating strong cost conformity and validating the effectiveness of the NLP-based approach.
AI Model Used: Ensemble model combining four NLP models: spaCy (rule-based), BERT (transformer-based contextual embeddings), Word2Vec (contextual static embeddings), and GloVe (count-based embeddings). The paper also developed adjusted variants of each model and a final ensemble model using majority voting and accuracy weighting.
Data Types: The data includes textual QTO descriptions extracted from CostX software following the Australian Standard Method of Measurement (ASMM) for a 13-level residential building with 592 work items across 19 classifications, and corresponding cost descriptions and unit rates from the AIQS Building Cost Index (BCI) for Melbourne, Australia.

Title: Identifying the Perception Differences of Emerging Construction-Related Technologies between Industry and Academia to Enable High Levels of Collaboration
Abstract: Emerging technologies play an essential role in improving overall productivity in the construction industry. However, this field has historically been criticized for the slow adoption of these innovative tools. To promote emerging technology adoption, exchanging knowledge and practices through academia and industry collaboration is essential. This paper analyzes the perception differences between academics and construction industry practitioners with regard to 17 emerging technologies through survey data and identifies potential technologies for high levels of collaboration from an academic perspective. The survey was designed to obtain responses about technological use, interest, maturity, implementations, applications, benefits, and barriers. Three technologies, three-dimensional (3D) printing; artificial intelligence (AI), neural networks (NNs), and deep learning (DL); and smart materials, were identified as having the highest potential for enabling collaboration. The findings from this study will allow academic researchers to strategically develop research directions that align with the needs reported by industry practitioners to sustain research funding and foster the adoption of innovative technologies in the construction fields.
Authors: Jang, Y.; Kim, K.; Leite, F.; Ayer, S.; Cho, Y.K.
Journal: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Construction Application Area: The paper examines emerging technologies across multiple construction areas including safety management, risk management, quality management, training and education, and various project phases (preplanning/design, material/supply chain, construction, and maintenance). AI/NN/DL is specifically identified as one of 17 emerging technologies evaluated.
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (BIM, drones, laser scanning, robotics, digital twins, sensor networks)
Methodology: The study deployed an online survey to 65 respondents (35 academics and 30 industry practitioners) in the US construction sector. The survey examined seven questions across three categories: acceptance of technology, technology life-cycle, and technology use strategy. Statistical analyses including chi-square tests, Fisher's exact tests, cross-tabulation analysis, and t-tests were used to compare perceptions between academia and industry. A collaboration potential score was developed using a formula combining industry interest, maturity stage, and implementation level metrics.
Research Goal: The study aims to analyze the perception differences of 17 emerging technologies in construction between academia and industry practitioners and identify potential technologies for high levels of collaboration from an academic perspective. This will allow academic researchers to strategically develop research directions that align with industry needs.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the gap in understanding how technological perceptions of practicing professionals and cutting-edge researchers compare and contrast. While previous studies examined either industry or academic perspectives separately, there is a need to understand perception differences between both groups regarding emerging technologies in construction to enable effective collaboration.
Future Research: Future research will focus on in-depth investigation of the identified high and medium potential technologies to provide detailed directions for industry-academia collaborations. The investigation will expand to examine a variety of applications of each technology so that a particular application does not represent the technology as a whole.
Results: Three technologies were identified as having the highest potential for collaboration: 3D printing (score: 3.93), AI/NN/DL (score: 5.44), and smart materials (score: 4.00). The main barriers to adopting emerging technologies were cost (3.80), practicability (3.70), and reliability (3.35) on a 5-point scale. Academia and industry had considerable perception differences in technology use and implementation level, while having fewer differences in technology interest and maturity stage.
AI Model Used: No Model
Data Types: The paper used survey data collected through an online questionnaire administered to 65 respondents (35 academics and 30 industry practitioners). The survey collected categorical and Likert-scale data on technology use, interest, maturity stage, implementation level, application phase, beneficial areas, and barriers for 17 emerging technologies.

Title: Standard terms as analytical variables for collective data sharing in construction management
Abstract: In order to facilitate data analysis techniques for solving construction management (CM) problems, especially in a collaborative manner, it is essential to define the data schemas of various construction information as the common variables. However, one of the critical barriers is that there is no standard set of variables addressed for collaborative research and practice. Therefore, defining standard variable terms is essential to share, utilize, and analyze construction data systematically and efficiently. In this context, the purpose of this paper is to propose a structured set of standard CM variable terms to enable the global collective analyses of CM data, not only for human managers but also for automated machine inferences. To address this issue, firstly, bibliographic data was extracted from Scopus, focusing on CM research, and the VOSviewer was used to analyze the bibliographic information. Proposed standard variable terms were then organized into a hierarchical structure with two levels, including eight variables in the first level and fifty-seven variables in the second level. Additionally, examples of utilizing standard variable terms were presented to the traditional analytical techniques, ontology, and artificial intelligence (AI) techniques to illustrate the viability of the proposed variable terms in CM. The proposed standard variable terms can be used as the basis for further detailed taxonomy toward collective advanced analytics implementation in CM. This approach will significantly enhance automated knowledge sharing between different applications and machines, resulting in global collective intelligence among unspecified CM professionals.
Authors: Jin, Z.; Kang, S.; Lee, Y.; Jung, Y.
Construction Application Area: Construction management, data sharing, cost estimation, scheduling, safety management, quality management, performance analysis, and various other construction management functions
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (BIM, drones, laser scanning, robotics, digital twins, sensor networks)
Methodology: The paper uses bibliometric analysis with VOSviewer software to analyze approximately 13,000 construction management papers from Scopus database. It establishes a hypothesis of eight first-level variables, validates them through keyword co-occurrence analysis, derives second-level variables, and performs a second round of bibliometric analysis for validation and complementation. The methodology includes both top-down variable definition and literature review.
Research Goal: The paper aims to propose a structured set of standard construction management variable terms to enable global collective analyses of CM data, facilitating automated knowledge sharing between different applications and machines for collaborative research and practice.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the critical barrier that there is no standard set of variables for collaborative construction management research and practice. It tackles the issue that the same variables are used in different terms depending on various stakeholders, preventing efficient data exchange and sharing among stakeholders and data analysis techniques.
Future Research: Future studies include creating structured metadata by defining classes and their relations based on ontology concepts and publishing the namespace on the web. Additionally, a quantitative evaluation methodology based on the proposed standard variables will be developed, with further detailed third-level taxonomy to be developed through collaborative efforts.
Results: The paper successfully proposed a hierarchical structure of standard variable terms with eight first-level variables (facility life-cycle, business function, facility type, performance, project delivery method, stakeholder, management method, and project outline) and fifty-seven second-level variables. The validation showed that 75% of 880 keywords from bibliometric analysis could be classified into the eight variables, demonstrating their comprehensiveness.
AI Model Used: Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), Genetic Algorithms (GA), Case-Based Reasoning (CBR), Rule-Based System (RBS), Support Vector Machine (SVM) - these are discussed as examples of techniques that can utilize the proposed standard variables
Data Types: Bibliographic data extracted from Scopus database including approximately 13,533 papers on construction management research. The data consists primarily of author-selected keywords, with 11,934 keywords analyzed from the literature to identify common construction management variable terms.

Title: AI-based risk assessment for construction site disaster preparedness through deep learning-based digital twinning
Abstract: Hurricanes are among the most devastating natural disasters in the United States, causing billions of dollars of property damage and insured losses. During extreme wind events, unsecured objects in jobsites can easily become airborne debris, which results in substantial loss to construction projects and neighboring communities. Towards a systematic disaster preparedness in construction jobsites, this paper presents a novel vision-based digital twinning and threat assessment framework. We encode the context of disaster risk into deep-learning architectures to identify and analyze the characteristics and impacts of potential wind-borne debris in construction site digital twin models. Case studies on nine piles of construction materials are presented to demonstrate and discuss the fidelity of the proposed computational modules.
