The proposed methods are expected to help provide heads up for practitioners to quickly recognize, localize, and assess potential wind-borne derbies in construction jobsites, and thereby implementing hurricane preparedness in an effective and timely manner.
Authors: Kamari, M.; Ham, Y.
Construction Application Area: Construction site disaster preparedness, hurricane risk assessment, and safety management focusing on potential wind-borne debris identification and threat assessment
Research Method: Mixed method (quantitative and qualitative evaluation through case studies)
Methodology: The paper proposes a vision-based framework using deep learning for digital twinning and risk assessment. It involves: (1) reconstructing 3D point clouds from UAV and ground-level images using Structure-from-Motion, (2) performing semantic segmentation using convolutional neural networks (ResNet50) to identify potential wind-borne debris, (3) projecting 2D semantic information onto 3D point clouds using a novel depth-aware projection framework, (4) conducting volumetric measurements of debris instances, and (5) assessing threats based on kinetic energy calculations with respect to wind intensity.
Research Goal: The paper aims to develop an automated, vision-based framework for construction site hurricane preparedness that can rapidly identify, localize, and assess potential wind-borne debris in construction jobsites, enabling risk-informed decision-making and prioritized preparedness actions.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the labor-intensive and error-prone nature of manual visual inspections for identifying potential wind-borne debris in construction sites. It also tackles the lack of systematic and automated approaches for assessing threats associated with unsecured construction materials during extreme wind events.
Future Research: Future research should focus on building robust machine vision systems that can handle suboptimal weather conditions (rainy, foggy, dim light), occlusions, and moving objects in jobsites. Additional studies are needed to calibrate volumetric measurements for unstacked and disorganized material piles by analyzing the level of disarrangement.
Results: The proposed depth-aware projection framework achieved 99.8% accuracy in 3D semantic segmentation (compared to 97.85% baseline). Volumetric measurements showed errors ranging from 2.4% to 9.2% across nine debris piles. The framework successfully generated site-specific risk heatmaps showing threat levels at different wind speeds (15-100 m/s), enabling prioritization of preparedness activities.
AI Model Used: Convolutional Neural Networks including AlexNet, VGG19, ResNet18, and ResNet50 (ResNet50 was selected for the case studies with 98.2% averaged accuracy in 2D semantic segmentation)
Data Types: Visual data including 360 images for Case #1 (180 aerial and 180 ground-level) and 32 aerial images for Case #2, collected from residential and commercial construction sites using UAVs and smartphones. The data includes images of various construction materials such as pine boards, plywood, PVC pipes, galvanized pipes, steel beams, and wooden boards.

Title: A review of LLMs and their applications in the architecture, engineering and construction industry
Abstract: During the past decade, there has been rapid emergence, continuous development and advancements in the field of Artificial Intelligence (AI), and a broad adaptation ofLarge Language Models (LLMs) in a wide variety of application domains transforming and streamlining industry practices. However, the construction industry has yet to fully incorporate these technologies, delaying their wide-scale adaptation. Only a limited number of recent studies have explored the opportunities, capabilities and potential of current LLM implementations in the broad domain of Architecture Engineering and Construction (AEC) industry, leaving a significant gap in this field of research. This study aims to address this gap and provide an extensive review of already established state-of-the-art applications and use case scenarios of LLMs in the AEC industry. Apart from that, by exploring the key contributions and limitations of these applications, and by considering relative reviews on this subject, it was possible to categorize them, to extract the emerging challenges and future directions of the field and propose actionable recommendations for industry stakeholders. This study also includes an introduction to important concepts and recent advancements of LLM technologies, focusing on transformer-based architectures and providing an extensive list of LLM families.
Authors: Kampelopoulos, D.; Tsanousa, A.; Vrochidis, S.; Kompatsiaris, I.
Construction Application Area: Training and education, planning and scheduling, safety analysis and hazard recognition, document generation and compliance check, virtual assistants and question answering, code and data generation/interpretation/visualization, BIM and BEM functionalities
Methodology: This is a systematic literature review that utilized Scopus and Web of Science databases to identify relevant studies. The methodology involved identification (927 Scopus, 437 WoS documents), screening by title and abstract (resulting in 95 documents), and full-text eligibility assessment (final 28 studies). The search focused on LLM-related keywords combined with AEC industry terms, limited to English articles and conference papers published from 2021-2024. The identified use cases were then categorized based on their associated tasks into seven main categories.
Research Goal: The goal is to provide an extensive review of state-of-the-art applications and use case scenarios of LLMs in the AEC industry, addressing the research gap in already established LLM implementations. The study aims to categorize applications, extract emerging challenges and future directions, and propose actionable recommendations for industry stakeholders.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the limited documentation of well-established case studies and applications of LLMs in the AEC industry. While previous reviews focused on opportunities and potential applications, there was a research gap in documenting already established use cases, particularly given the slow adaptation to new technologies in the construction industry.
Future Research: Future directions include developing smaller and more efficient LLM models, focusing on domain-specific fine-tuning with construction datasets, improving interoperability through standardized data formats and APIs, implementing robust verification frameworks with human oversight, enhancing model explainability and transparency, and investing in workforce training for human-AI collaboration. Research should also prioritize sustainability over performance improvement, develop cost-effective optimization techniques, and address cultural and regional construction variations.
Results: The review identified 28 established use cases of LLMs in the AEC industry across seven task categories. Key findings revealed accuracy issues due to limited domain-specific knowledge, data interoperability challenges, high computational costs, and concerns regarding regulatory compliance, safety, and ethics. The study also documented that most implementations showed prompt sensitivity, required significant fine-tuning, and exhibited occasional inaccuracies especially in complex construction-specific scenarios.
AI Model Used: Multiple LLM families including GPT (GPT-1, GPT-2, GPT-3, GPT-4, ChatGPT, InstructGPT, Codex), LLaMA (LLaMA-1, LLaMA-2, LLaMA-3, Alpaca, Vicuna, Code LLaMA), Mistral (Mistral 7B, Mixtral 8×7B, Mixtral 8×22B), Claude (Claude 1, 2, 3), PaLM (PaLM-1, PaLM-2, FLAN-PaLM, Med-PaLM), BERT (BERT variants, RoBERTa, ALBERT, DistilBERT, DeBERTa), T5 (T5 variants, mT5, FLAN-T5), BART (BART-Base, BART-Large, m-BART), and Falcon (Falcon-7B, Falcon-40B, Falcon-180B)
Data Types: The paper discusses various types of data used across reviewed studies including construction documentation (project reports, specifications, incident reports, contracts), building information (BIM models, IDF files, XML formats), sensor data and environmental traces, construction safety records (OSHA databases), energy consumption profiles, images from construction sites, academic papers and research databases, code repositories, and domain-specific datasets for fine-tuning.

Title: A genetic algorithm tool for conceptual structural design with cost and embodied carbon optimization
Abstract: The conceptual design decisions have the largest influence on a building project's safety, value, and environmental impact; hence they are commonly assigned to a "senior engineer" to make use of his/her experience. However, the senior engineers can be biased towards solutions inside their area of expertise, which often prevents them from finding the best solutions among alternatives that must consider complex inter-related, and multi-disciplinary parameters. The engineering community could benefit from a rapid and high-quality decision-making method or tool to increase the speed and quality of its high-impact design choices. There are valuable studies in the literature exploiting Artificial Intelligence (AI) to improve the structural design process; however, most of them focus on the final design stage (e.g., Building Information Modeling), and the rest requires an existing project database (e.g., architectural drawings, already decided material types) to propose a small number of initial design alternatives. In this article, we present the development and validation of a genetic algorithm tool based on Non-dominated Sorted Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II) that can be used to analyse a wide range of safe, economical and low-CO<inf>2</inf> options for the conceptual design of buildings. The design space starts from a design brief (with only the information about the site characteristics and project objectives). The solutions are explored with the material, grid size, floor type, lateral resistance, and foundation system variables. In a short computational time (< 2 min per run), users are provided with a Pareto graph of a large set of feasible solutions (in terms of cost, embodied CO<inf>2</inf> emissions and free space) that an engineer would not be typically able to evaluate within a traditional conceptual design process. For future applications, the methodology presented in this paper is flexible to include more engineering materials (e.g., timber, masonry, structural glass), complex architectural forms and merge other disciplines in decision making (e.g., building physics construction management, fire safety).
Authors: Kanyilmaz, A.; Tichell, P.R.N.; Loiacono, D.
Construction Application Area: Conceptual structural design, cost optimization, embodied carbon optimization, multi-objective decision making, preliminary structural design
Methodology: The paper develops a genetic algorithm tool based on Non-dominated Sorted Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II) for conceptual structural design optimization. The tool explores design space including material types (steel, reinforced concrete), grid sizes, floor types, lateral resistance systems, and foundation systems. It uses multi-objective optimization to minimize cost, maximize free space, and minimize embodied CO2 emissions. The algorithm includes genetic operators (selection, crossover, mutation), repair modules for handling incompatibilities, and a deterministic lateral system module for stability design.
Research Goal: To develop and validate an AI-based tool that assists structural engineers and architects in making rapid, high-quality conceptual design decisions by analyzing a wide range of safe, economical, and low-CO2 options for building structures, presenting multiple Pareto-optimal solutions rather than a single optimized design.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the gap that conceptual design decisions are typically biased towards the expertise of senior engineers and that existing AI tools either focus on later design stages (BIM) or require existing project databases. It also addresses the need to incorporate embodied carbon calculations as a key design parameter alongside traditional cost and structural performance metrics.
Future Research: Future applications can include more engineering materials (timber, masonry, structural glass), complex architectural forms, and merge other disciplines in decision making (building physics, construction management, fire safety). Investigation of more sophisticated multi-objective optimization algorithms beyond NSGA-II, especially if the number of design objectives and variables is increased.
Results: The tool successfully provided 15-40 feasible solutions in 1-2 minutes runtime for various building configurations. When tested against three benchmark problems from literature, it reproduced the original human-found solutions and identified alternative designs with lower cost and/or embodied CO2 emissions. The tool demonstrated effectiveness in exploring design space with steel and reinforced concrete options, optimizing for cost (ranging 2.5-7M€), span dimensions (3-18m grids), and CO2 emissions (1500-3500 tonnes).
AI Model Used: Non-dominated Sorted Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II)
Data Types: The paper uses design input parameters (building dimensions, loads, material properties, soil conditions), structural design reference tables (load-span tables for floor systems, steel profile databases, concrete design tables), and cost/embodied carbon factors from construction databases (CYPE price generator, LETI embodied carbon factors). Validation uses benchmark case studies from published literature.

Title: Intelligent designer: A computational approach to automating design of windows in buildings
Abstract: The process of building design is a means of meeting client expectations for a building, followed by a set of comprehensive standards and codes relating to the design, construction, and maintenance of buildings. The rapidly rising volume of data along with increasing client expectations inspired many researchers to develop new computerized techniques to automate building design process. These techniques can be broken down into two broad categories: automated computer-aided design tools (e.g., development of building information modeling or BIM) and measuring or modeling client expectations (e.g., use of facial expressions to find psychological expectations of the client). Advances in artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning have made possible new approaches to the automation of design. This represents a shift from a focus on client-designer or designer-technology relationships to a more intelligent and independent client-technology communication. In this paper, a novel AI system called intelligent designer is proposed to understand (or learn) the client's need and expectations and generate valid designs. The design environment (i.e., the interaction between the client and the design) is formulated as a Markov decision process (MDP) and a mathematical framework is provided for making design decisions in situations where new designs are partly random (as they are influenced by the client's feedback) and partly under the control of the computer (as they are influenced by the regulations, standards, and guidelines). The approach is demonstrated using a window design experiment.
Authors: Karan, E.; Asadi, S.
Construction Application Area: Building design automation, specifically architectural design of windows in buildings
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (BIM, drones, laser scanning, robotics, digital twins, sensor networks)
Research Method: Mixed method (quantitative modeling with experimental validation)
Methodology: The paper formulates the building design environment as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) to model the interaction between client and design. It uses the Bellman equation and value iteration algorithm to determine optimal design decisions. The approach is demonstrated through a window design experiment with 30 participants who evaluate design options on a scale from poor to excellent.
Research Goal: To propose a novel AI system called 'intelligent designer' that can understand and learn client needs and expectations, and generate valid building designs that maximize client satisfaction by making autonomous design decisions.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the gap that computers cannot currently understand clients' subjective needs and expectations to innovate building designs autonomously. It tackles the challenge of making design decisions in situations where new designs are partly random (influenced by client feedback) and partly controlled (influenced by regulations and standards).
Future Research: Future work should consider indirect measurement of people's physical, emotional, and behavioral characteristics using technologies like eye tracking, facial expressions, ECG, EEG, EMG, and GSR. Research is needed on problems with large numbers of information-processing activities and decision variables to better understand AI benefits in complex building design processes.
Results: The intelligent designer achieved 0.20 increase in average evaluation scores compared to 0.11 decrease for random design options. It improved evaluation scores in 58% of design attempts (11% more than random) and reduced design time by approximately 60%, taking an average of 7 epochs versus 17 epochs for random options to reach maximum participant satisfaction.
AI Model Used: Markov Decision Process (MDP) with value iteration algorithm using Bellman equation for reinforcement learning
Data Types: The paper uses subjective evaluation scores from participants rating window design options on a 6-point scale (poor, fair, satisfactory, good, very good, excellent). These scores are used to calculate transition probabilities and rewards in the MDP model. The experiment involves 15 design decision variables related to window grille patterns, glass colors, and frame colors.

Title: Artificial intelligence quality inspection of steel bars installation by integrating mask R-CNN and stereo vision
Abstract: Contractors should conduct strict quality inspection of the steel bars used in concrete structures and need to automate the process of quality inspection. The objective of this study is to develop an Artificial Intelligence Quality Inspection Model (AI-QIM) that can execute quality inspection on steel bars at the construction site. The proposed AI-QIM is built on the Mask Region-based Convolutional Neural Network (Mask R-CNN) technique, which can perform instance segmentation of steel bars. This object detection technique is integrated with a stereo vision camera to generate information on steel bar installation. A contractor can use the proposed AI-QIM to estimate the quantity, spacing, diameter, and length of steel bars during quality inspection. A sample case study indicated that the AI-QIM yielded a maximum relative error of 3% when measuring steel bar spacing and a maximum relative error of 8% when measuring steel bar lengths within a range of 1–2 m from a stereo camera.
Authors: Kardovskyi, Y.; Moon, S.
Construction Application Area: Quality inspection of steel bars installation in concrete structures
Methodology: The paper develops an AI-QIM (Artificial Intelligence Quality Inspection Model) by integrating Mask R-CNN for instance segmentation of steel bars with stereo vision technology for attribute measurement. The model was trained on 240 labeled images using transfer learning from the COCO dataset, with 70% for training and 30% for validation, achieving a mean Average Precision (mAP) of 0.75.
Research Goal: The objective is to develop an AI-QIM that can automatically execute quality inspection of steel bars at construction sites, enabling contractors to estimate quantity, spacing, diameter, and length of steel bars during installation.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the limitations of manual visual inspection which is time-consuming, error-prone, and requires physical access to temporary structures. It also addresses the need for automation to improve inspection quality, safety, and reduce manpower requirements for steel bar quality inspection.
Future Research: Future research should focus on improving object detection capability by using higher resolution depth images (beyond 1280×720 px) to enable precise detection at distances greater than 2m, improving object mask precision to reduce jagged edges, and using larger datasets for training to improve the mAP of the AI-QIM for practical implementation.
Results: The AI-QIM achieved accurate quantity detection (100% accuracy), maximum relative error of 3% for spacing measurement and 8% for length measurement within 1-2m range, and maximum relative error of 15.4% for diameter measurement. The model demonstrated better precision at shorter distances from the stereo camera.
AI Model Used: Mask R-CNN (Mask Region-based Convolutional Neural Network)
Data Types: The dataset consists of 240 RGB images of steel bar installations captured using a GoPro camera and stereo vision camera at two construction sites under different weather conditions. Images were manually labeled with polygon object masks stored in JSON and XML formats, then converted to TFRecord format for training.

Title: Quality Failures-Based Critical Cost Impact Factors: Logistic Regression Analysis
Abstract: Due to the various dynamic conditions of construction sites, quality failures have become part and parcel of the industry. Many studies have identified the causal factors of construction site quality failures and their cost impacts. However, limited studies have been made evaluating the domino effects of these on one another and the correlation between the cost impact and frequency of each attribute. In this study, made in the context of ongoing research into related artificial intelligence (AI)-based predictive models, a total of 2,527 nonconformance reports (NCRs) collected from 59 construction projects within the scope of a previous study were analyzed using the Delphi method and logistic regression analysis. According to the Delphi results, 25 critical cost impact factors were refined and categorized into five main groups: Materials, Design, Installation, Operation, and Process. Then, five main hypotheses were developed to test each attribute's cost impact and interaction by logistic regression. The results showed that although some attributes (from the Materials and Operation groups) have a significant impact on the cost of quality if observed in a failure report individually, others may become a critical cost-impact factor when interacting with other attributes. No significant correlation was observed between the frequency and cost impact of the attributes. Finally, a holistically based quality control system that considers the domino effects of causal factors from planning to operation was proposed for construction practitioners to reduce quality failures causing cost and time overruns.
Authors: Kazar, G.; Doǧan, N.B.; Ayhan, B.U.; Tokdemir, O.B.
Journal: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Research Location: Turkey (based on authors' affiliations with Turkish universities and data collection from Eastern European and Asian construction projects)
Construction Application Area: Quality management, cost overruns, quality failure analysis, construction defects and nonconformance management
Methodology: The study combines expert opinion using the Delphi method with quantitative analysis of 2,527 nonconformance reports (NCRs) from 59 construction projects. Twenty-five critical cost impact factors were identified and categorized into five groups (Materials, Design, Installation, Operation, and Process). Multinomial logistic regression analysis was performed to test five hypotheses about the cost impacts of quality failures and their interaction effects.
Research Goal: To investigate which construction attributes significantly affect construction costs based on quality failure reports and to evaluate the domino effects and interaction of causal factors on cost performance, using both expert opinion and site observation data.
Gaps Addressed: Prior studies using NCRs focused mainly on cost variations rather than defining causal parameters. There is limited research evaluating the domino effects of quality failure factors on one another and the correlation between cost impact and frequency of each attribute using actual site reports combined with expert opinion.
Future Research: Future research could include more NCRs from diverse project types and regions, develop AI-based predictive models for quality failures, apply different methods like AHP instead of Delphi, conduct similar studies in different regions and special projects (e.g., industrial) to compare results, and use Machine Learning algorithms to evaluate interaction effects.
Results: Materials and Design attributes significantly impact construction costs, while Installation, Operation, and Process groups alone did not have significant impacts but became critical when interacting with other attributes. No significant correlation was found between frequency and cost impact of attributes. Seven attributes were identified as having crucial impacts when occurring together with other factors, demonstrating domino effects.
AI Model Used: No Model (The paper mentions it is part of ongoing research into AI-based predictive models, but this particular study uses logistic regression analysis, which is a statistical method rather than an AI model)
Data Types: The study uses 2,527 nonconformance reports (NCRs) collected from 59 construction projects (mostly high-rise buildings and infrastructure) conducted between 2016-2020 in Eastern Europe and Asia. Data includes quality failures categorized as reworks, defects, design modifications, managerial warnings, and nonconformances, with each case rated for cost impact by three experts on a 1-5 Likert scale.

Title: AI, Agility, and Environmental Performance: A New Framework for Construction Project Managers
Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) increasingly is being used by businesses to improve capabilities and performance. Guided by the dynamic capabilities framework, this study investigated how AI assimilation affects environmental performance, with organizational agility serving as a mediating factor in construction organizations. A moderated mediation analysis was employed in this study to investigate the contingent effect of strategic flexibility in altering the connection between agility and environmental performance. Data were gathered from 308 project managers. The findings of structural equation modeling validated the arguments that AI assimilation increases organizational agility and that this translates to increased environmental performance. Furthermore, strategic flexibility moderates the direct relationship between agility and environmental performance, as well as the indirect relationship between AI assimilation and environmental performance via organizational agility, emphasizing the significance of aligning agile responses with the strategic objectives of the business. The relevance of this research to the field of construction engineering management is that it leads to the integration of AI, improving the environmental performance of the firm through organizational agility. This study shows the moderating function of strategic flexibility in the interrelation between agility and environmental performance. These consequences are aimed primarily at the construction project management sector and policymakers because they emphasize the importance of agile methods of progress of projects to serve the strategic objectives the success of which is vital for environmental sustainability.
Authors: Khan, A.N.; Kwan, H.K.
Journal: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Construction Application Area: Environmental performance, organizational agility, strategic flexibility, and construction project management processes. The study focuses on how AI assimilation improves environmental sustainability outcomes in construction organizations rather than specific technical construction tasks.
Methodology: The study employed structural equation modeling (SEM) to test hypotheses about relationships between AI assimilation, organizational agility, strategic flexibility, and environmental performance. Data were collected through surveys from 308 project managers in Chinese construction firms. The analysis included confirmatory factor analysis (CFA), moderated mediation analysis using Hayes' PROCESS macro with 5,000 bootstrap samples, and various validity and reliability tests.
Research Goal: The goal is to investigate how AI assimilation affects environmental performance in construction organizations, with organizational agility serving as a mediating factor and strategic flexibility as a moderator, guided by the dynamic capabilities framework.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of research on how organizational agility mediates the relationship between AI assimilation and environmental performance in construction engineering management organizations. It also fills the gap regarding how strategic flexibility moderates the relationship between organizational agility and environmental performance.
Future Research: Future research should expand to different industries and cultural contexts, employ longitudinal designs to establish causality, include external environmental factors (regulatory frameworks, market conditions), and broaden the scope to include social and economic performance aspects of sustainability beyond just environmental performance.
Results: AI assimilation positively impacts organizational agility (β=0.34, p<0.001), which in turn positively affects environmental performance (β=0.33, p<0.001). Organizational agility mediates the relationship between AI assimilation and environmental performance. Strategic flexibility significantly moderates both the direct relationship between agility and environmental performance and the indirect relationship between AI assimilation and environmental performance through organizational agility.
AI Model Used: No Model
Data Types: Primary quantitative data collected through self-reported surveys from 308 construction project managers in Chinese firms located in Shanghai, Guangzhou, Wuhan, and Hangzhou. The survey measured AI assimilation, organizational agility, strategic flexibility, and environmental performance using validated Likert-scale instruments.

Title: Enhanced project brief: Structured approach to client-designer interface
Abstract: Purpose - The focus of this work is on the client-designer interface where decisions have significant impact over the lifecycle of the project. Therefore, the briefing stage is examined in the context of clients needs which is divided into project-based strategy and broader clients strategy. The purpose of this paper is to address the pitfalls in the briefing process which has been attributed to the shortcomings in the client-designer communication interfaces. This will be achieved by developing an automated brief generation framework. The research examines the efficiency of standard approaches to modelling and design, and the benefits that these methodologies have offered to the computer industry. The work reviews the similarities between the two industries and argues in support of the potential benefits in adopting a standard methodology in the construction industry. The structure upon which the framework is developed is based on system analysis and design methodology (SSADM) which has proven to be an effective platform used within the software development industry. Design/methodology/approach - SSADM is an established methodology within the software development industry. The paper will demonstrate that due to fundamental similarities between the construction and software development industries, SSADM is likely to offer a viable platform upon which an automated enhanced brief generation model is developed for use in the construction industry. The construction design and construction process will be mapped on SSADM high-level definition before focusing and honing on the design phase. The methodology for the development of the framework will be based on the rationalist approach of generating knowledge through reasoning leading to model-building. Findings - A model that is based on SSADM is proposed for the design development phase of construction projects. In order to shape the project strategy, the model considers the combined role of clients requirements with organisation strategy and environmental factors. The paper has shown that it is feasible to increase the automation of the briefing process and enhanced the briefing output. The model here does not diminish the importance of direct communication between the client and the design team. It provides a more structured way of doing so, while taking advantage of vast array of data and technology in order to improve the brief outcome. Research limitations/implications - From practical perspective, the proposed framework is in its formative stage, thus requiring incremental refinement through several case studies. This is particularly true about the AI components of the system which typically rely on extensive data representing the real-case scenarios. Therefore, the work invites further research into the examination of various parts as well as the overall system. Practical implications - There are several ways by which construction projects are procured. There may be fluctuation in their rate of usage, but while there is no indication of any procurement option fading, new ones such as PPP and PFI are periodically introduced. The existence of this diversity is indicative of the fact that the industry tends to respond to problems rather than attempting to instigate a measured solution supported by theoretical underpinning. Subsequently, there have been suggestions of a communication and information discourse between actors and within processes involved in project lifecycle. This project is aimed at addressing the gap in the client-designer communication. The automated approach to brief generation will lead to better briefs while reducing ambiguities as well as the overhead associated with brief generation. Social implications - The quality of project brief has a significant impact on decisions at the design stage. In turn, these decisions will influence all phases of construction project lifecycle. The briefing session and requirement analysis of a construction project can be very difficult for inexperienced clients particularly for complex projects. Therefore, there is potential for the process of client-requirement-analysis to be optimised. The work promises to improve the quality of the briefing process, thus helping clients to realise their intended objectives and minimise resource waste. Originality/value - The work builds on the commonalities of the construction and software development industries and takes advantage of the advancements in the latter. In doing so, project quality is defined quantitatively which is used to develop project strategy in a three-dimensional space. The development of the model was also contingent upon enhancement of artificial neural network structure.
Authors: Khosrowshahi, F.
Construction Application Area: Project briefing, client-designer interface, design development, requirement analysis
Methodology: The research adopts a rationalist approach based on generating knowledge through reasoning leading to model-building. It adapts SSADM (Structured System Analysis and Design Methodology) from software development industry to construction industry, particularly focusing on the design brief phase. The framework integrates multiple components including strategy-making using artificial neural networks with enhanced learning capability, quality quantification in a three-dimensional cost-time-quality space, and feasibility checking through knowledge-based systems.
Research Goal: To develop an automated framework for enhanced project brief generation that addresses communication pitfalls between clients and designers in construction projects. The framework aims to improve the briefing process by providing a more structured approach while taking advantage of data and technology to reduce ambiguities and overhead costs.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the communication and information discourse between clients and designers during the briefing stage, which has been identified as a weak point causing project discontinuities. It also addresses the lack of standardized methodologies in construction briefing processes, contrasting with the structured approaches successfully used in software development.
Future Research: The proposed theoretical framework needs to be tested against real-case scenarios through several case studies. The AI components of the system require extensive data representing real case scenarios for training and validation. The work invites further research into examining various parts as well as the overall system through incremental refinement.
Results: The paper proposes a theoretical model based on SSADM for the design development phase of construction projects that considers combined roles of client requirements, organizational strategy, and environmental factors. The model demonstrates feasibility of increasing automation in the briefing process and enhancing briefing output through a more structured approach, though practical implementation and validation remain as future work.
AI Model Used: Artificial Neural Network (ANN) with enhanced architecture allowing 'teacher intervention' (Learn-On-Demand methodology)
Data Types: The paper is theoretical and does not use specific empirical data. It references the need for historical project data including cost, time, and quality metrics, building classifications (RIBA building types), and knowledge-based rules and conditions for feasibility checking in future implementation.

Title: Balancing AI generalization and specialization: Multi-domain learning for universal computer vision models in construction
Abstract: While model generalization and specialization are a critical concern in computer vision, balancing them in data-scarce construction settings remains challenging due to their unique nature. This paper proposes a multi-domain learning approach where a model acquires domain-generic visual knowledge from various domain datasets, while maintaining domain-specific predictabilities for each individual domain. Results show that the approach can train a more powerful model than traditional methods, regardless of training dataset size, evaluation metrics, and test domains. The model, trained on only half to one-eighth of the dataset size used in traditional methods, exhibited comparable or even superior performance while demonstrating greater robustness to challenging and diverse construction environments. These findings suggest that the approach can competitively balance model generalization and specialization, leading to improved performance across various aspects. This advance can optimize the use of given training datasets and facilitate the development of more universal computer vision models in construction.
Authors: Kim, J.
Construction Application Area: Worker detection, site monitoring, productivity evaluation, safety analysis, object detection, tracking, activity recognition, and ergonomic risk assessment
Methodology: The paper proposes a multi-domain learning approach using a Cascade R-CNN architecture with domain-specific detection heads and a domain-aware freeze-and-unfreeze training strategy. The model is trained on three construction benchmark datasets (MOCS, SODA, CIS) with varying dataset sizes (500 to 40,000 instances) and compared against three baseline approaches: all-in-one, one-by-one, and sequential fine-tuning methods.
Research Goal: To develop a computer vision model that balances domain generalization and domain specialization by learning domain-generic visual knowledge from multiple construction datasets while maintaining domain-specific predictive capabilities for individual datasets, ultimately achieving better performance with less training data.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses two critical gaps: (1) it is unclear which approach (all-in-one, one-by-one, fine-tuned, or in-between) offers better performance in cross-domain generalization and within-domain specialization in construction, and (2) how the size of training datasets influences model performance and comparative effectiveness across different approaches has been underexplored.
Future Research: Future research should extend this approach to multi-class object detection and other computer vision tasks (instance segmentation, pose estimation, action recognition), evaluate its efficacy in hybridizing real and synthetic images, integrate with multi-task learning and visual foundation models, and test validity using images from different domains (e.g., manufacturing and urban scenes) to explore cross-domain effectiveness.
Results: The in-between model consistently outperformed all baseline models across various metrics, achieving comparable or superior performance with only half to one-eighth of the training data. It showed 3.3% average improvement in AP50 over baselines and demonstrated greater robustness to challenging construction scenarios including small appearances, low illuminations, occlusions, and complex backgrounds.
AI Model Used: Cascade R-CNN (Region-based Convolutional Neural Network) with ResNet-50 backbone, enhanced with domain-specific detection heads and domain-aware freeze-and-unfreeze training strategy for multi-domain learning
Data Types: Construction site images from three public benchmark datasets: MOCS (174 construction sites, 11,658 test instances), SODA (10 construction sites, 14,171 test instances), and CIS (11,341 test instances), all focused on construction worker detection with varying training dataset sizes from 500 to 40,000 instances per dataset.

Title: Enhancing Worker Safety: Real-Time Automated Detection of Personal Protective Equipment to Prevent Falls from Heights at Construction Sites Using Improved YOLOv8 and Edge Devices
Abstract: Personal protective equipment (PPE), including helmets, harnesses, and lanyards, is pivotal in preventing falls from heights at construction sites. However, ensuring consistent and correct usage of PPE presents a significant challenge. To address this issue, this study introduces an enhanced You Only Look Once, version 8 model (YOLOv8), a computer-vision-based AI model tailored for real-time multiclass PPE monitoring on portable edge devices. A pioneering large-scale multiclass PPE data set is curated to facilitate model training. Balancing detection accuracy with a lightweight design, we augment YOLOv8 through the integration of the coordinate attention module, ghost convolution module, transfer learning, and merge-nonmaximum suppression. The proposed model surpasses the original YOLOv8 and state-of-the-art models, showcasing improved accuracy and reduced computational cost. Deployed on the edge device Jetson Xavier NX, the model achieves precise PPE detection (mAP50: 92.52%) in real-time, operating at 9.11 frames per second. These findings establish a robust foundation for the efficient and real-time automated safety monitoring of construction sites, promising substantial enhancements to worker safety and data privacy within the construction industry.
Authors: Kim, D.; Xiong, S.
Journal: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Construction Application Area: Construction safety, specifically personal protective equipment (PPE) detection to prevent falls from heights
Methodology: The paper develops an improved YOLOv8 model by integrating the coordinate attention module, ghost convolution module, transfer learning, and merge-NMS. A large-scale multiclass PPE dataset containing 11,620 images with five classes (helmet, no_helmet, harness, no_harness, lanyard) was constructed from various sources including public datasets, online repositories, field collection, and synthetic image generation. The model was trained and deployed on the Jetson Xavier NX edge device for real-time detection.
Research Goal: To develop an enhanced YOLOv8 model for real-time automated detection of personal protective equipment (PPE) compliance at construction sites using edge devices, specifically targeting fall prevention by detecting helmets, harnesses, and lanyards while balancing detection accuracy with lightweight design for portable deployment.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of research on real-time automated detection systems for PPE compliance, particularly for preventing falls from heights. It fills the gap in simultaneous detection of harnesses and lanyards, overcomes challenges of detecting thin objects like lanyards in complex construction backgrounds, and addresses the need for lightweight models deployable on edge devices while maintaining high accuracy.
Future Research: Future research directions include exploring synchronized multicamera systems to mitigate occlusion challenges, optimizing the model for various edge devices to ensure broader deployment capabilities in different hazardous occupational settings, comprehensive analysis and robustness testing under diverse environmental conditions, testing the system at actual construction sites, and extending the method to other hazardous environments beyond construction.
Results: The improved YOLOv8 achieved a mAP50 of 92.52% when deployed on the Jetson Xavier NX edge device, operating at 9.11 frames per second. Compared to the original YOLOv8s, the model showed improvements in AP50 for all classes (helmet: 0.52%, no_helmet: 0.66%, harness: 1.16%, no_harness: 2.18%, lanyard: 3.40%) while reducing model parameters by 18.96%, GFLOPs by 14.08%, and model size by 18.22%.
AI Model Used: YOLOv8 (improved version with coordinate attention module, GhostConv module, transfer learning, and merge-NMS); comparison models include VGG16-SSD, EfficientDet-d0, YOLOv3, YOLOv4, YOLOv5
Data Types: Image dataset containing 11,620 annotated images from multiple sources: 9,471 from public datasets (Roboflow hard hat workers dataset and AI-HUB construction site safety equipment dataset), 168 from online repositories, 385 from field data collection using mobile phone cameras, and 1,596 from synthetic image generation using chroma-key technique. Images were annotated with five classes: helmet, no_helmet, harness, no_harness, and lanyard.

Title: Image generation of hazardous situations in construction sites using text-to-image generative model for training deep neural networks
Abstract: There has been a persistent challenge in acquiring sufficient training image data for deep neural networks (DNNs) to enhance safety monitoring on construction sites. Given the prevalence of textual data in the construction sector and the capabilities of multi-modal AI systems, this paper presents the use of text-to-image models to generate training images that capture the relationships between objects involved in construction accidents. Through a systematic prompt design process, a synthetic dataset of 3585 images across 27 hazardous scenarios was generated. The efficacy of this method is demonstrated by the performance of DNN models trained on these virtual images, which achieved a mean Average Precision (mAP) of approximately 64% in object detection and 60% in segmentation tasks. This paper demonstrates the potential of text-to-image models in mitigating the scarcity of training data and enhancing the capability of DNNs to identify potential hazards.
Authors: Kim, H.; Yi, J.-S.
Construction Application Area: Construction safety monitoring and hazard identification
Methodology: The paper uses a text-to-image generative model (Stable Diffusion) to generate synthetic training images depicting hazardous construction scenarios. Subject terms and style modifiers are extracted from OSHA accident reports using NLP techniques, combined into prompts, and fed into the text-to-image model. The generated synthetic images are annotated with segmentation masks and used to train YOLOv5 models for object detection and segmentation tasks. The trained models are then tested on real-world construction images to evaluate their performance.
Research Goal: The primary goal is to demonstrate the feasibility of using text-to-image generative models to address the scarcity of training data for deep neural networks in construction safety applications. The study aims to generate synthetic images depicting hazardous situations on construction sites and validate their effectiveness as training data for DNN models to recognize workers and accident-related objects.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses three main gaps: (1) the lack of sufficient training image data for DNN models in construction safety due to dynamic site conditions and access limitations, (2) the limited application of text-to-image models in specialized fields like construction, and (3) the absence of training datasets that capture relationships between objects involved in construction accidents rather than just depicting individual objects.
Future Research: Future research directions include: (1) incorporating objective assessment methods or expert opinions for image quality evaluation, (2) automating the labeling process to leverage text-to-image models more effectively, (3) expanding the range of construction objects and accident scenarios to improve generalizability, (4) optimizing model parameters and settings for various construction site conditions, and (5) comparing the performance of DNN models trained exclusively with real-world images versus combinations of virtual and real-world images.
Results: The DNN models trained exclusively on synthetic images achieved a mean Average Precision (mAP) of approximately 64% for object detection and 60% for segmentation tasks when tested on real-world images. When using pre-trained YOLOv5 models with MS COCO dataset as a baseline, the accuracy improved by approximately 16%, demonstrating that synthetic images generated by text-to-image models can effectively serve as training data for construction safety applications.
AI Model Used: Stable Diffusion (text-to-image generative model), YOLOv5s (for object detection and segmentation), Natural Language Processing using NLTK WordNet (for text data extraction)
Data Types: The paper uses OSHA construction accident reports as textual data (703 fatal accident cases from 2013-2022) to extract hazardous scenario information through NLP. The study generated 5,400 synthetic images across 27 hazardous scenarios, with 4,041 images (75%) successfully annotated and used for training. Real-world construction images collected from various online sources, accident reports, and construction sites were used as test data for model evaluation.

Title: Case study on the determination of building materials using a support vector machine
Abstract: For any construction project to succeed, it is very important to select the materials accurately during the project's initial stage. Trying to choose the best-performing materials is a crucial task for the successful completion of a construction project. The material selection process typically is performed through the information received from a highly experienced decision maker and a purchasing agent without the logical decision making; thus, the construction field gains access to various artificial intelligence (AI) techniques to support decision models in their own selection method. Through a case study, this paper proposes the application of a systematic and efficient support vector machine (SVM) model to select suitable materials. The 120 data sets of the case study have completed building projects in South Korea. These data set were divided into three groups and constructed five binary classification models in the one-against-all (OAA) multiclassification method by data classification and normalization, resulting in the SVM model, based on the kernel polynominal, yielding a prediction accuracy rate of 87.5%. This case study indicates that the SVM model appears feasible to be the decision support model for selecting construction methods. © 2012 American Society of Civil Engineers.
Authors: Kim, J.; Kim, S.; Tang, L.
Journal: Journal of Computing in Civil Engineering
Construction Application Area: Material selection for building exterior materials
Methodology: The paper applies Support Vector Machine (SVM) with a one-against-all (OAA) multiclassification method to select exterior building materials. Using 120 completed building projects from South Korea, the data was divided into training (66%), validation (17%), and test sets (17%). The model employed polynomial kernel functions and tested three kernel types to determine optimal parameters for material selection.
Research Goal: To propose and demonstrate the application of a systematic and efficient support vector machine (SVM) model to select suitable exterior building materials during the preliminary design stage of construction projects.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the gap that traditional material selection processes are often intuitive, conducted without detailed research, and rely heavily on experienced decision makers without logical decision-making frameworks. Existing systematic methods are limited to configuration management based on physical features and fail to reflect changing construction site conditions.
Future Research: Future work should validate and compare the SVM model results with other AI techniques like Case-Based Reasoning (CBR) and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) for material selection. The model's reliability needs to be improved by increasing the number of input data cases, particularly for classes with fewer samples, to enhance overall accuracy.
Results: The SVM model achieved an overall prediction accuracy of 87.5% using the polynomial kernel function, with sixfold cross-validation showing average accuracy of 87.5% (ranging from 80% to 95%). This performance exceeded the Case-Based Reasoning model (83.5%) and matched the test set performance of ANN models (83%) in similar studies.
AI Model Used: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with polynomial kernel function, using one-against-all (OAA) multiclassification method
Data Types: The data consists of 120 completed building projects from South Korea (2000-2008) with nine input variables including location, site area, number of stories, structure system, gross area, duration, total cost, material cost, and material durability. The output data comprised five classes of exterior materials: water paint, aluminum panel, stainless panel, granite, and wood panel.

Title: Robust skeleton-based AI for automatic multi-person fall detection on construction sites with occlusions
Abstract: Rapid and accurate automatic fall detection is essential for improving worker safety and reducing the severity of fall-related incidents on construction sites. To address the challenges of real-time detection in complex and obstructed construction environments, this paper develops a specialized dataset for fall scenarios and introduces a skeleton-based AI model called YOSAP-LSTM. This model integrates YOLOv8 for human detection, SORT and AlphaPose for precise tracking of human keypoints, and a 1D CNN-LSTM for classifying falls versus non-falls. This approach achieves an impressive accuracy of 98.66 % (sensitivity: 97.32 %; specificity: 99.10 %), outperforming current fall detection algorithms while maintaining high accuracy under occlusions. Deployed on an edge device (NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX), the system runs at 6.44 fps, meeting real-time requirements for portable applications. The YOSAP-LSTM model is both robust and practical, offering significant potential for real-world use in construction by enhancing worker safety through timely fall detection in challenging environments.
Authors: Kim, D.; Yu, X.; Xiong, S.
Construction Application Area: Construction safety, specifically fall detection for worker safety monitoring
Methodology: The paper develops YOSAP-LSTM, a skeleton-based AI model integrating YOLOv8 for human detection, SORT for tracking, AlphaPose for pose estimation, and 1D CNN-LSTM for fall classification. The model was trained on a comprehensive dataset of 376,006 frames from multiple sources and deployed on an edge device (NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX) for real-time fall detection in construction sites.
Research Goal: To develop a robust, real-time, multi-person fall detection system specifically designed for construction site environments that can operate on edge devices, enabling immediate detection and response to fall incidents while addressing challenges of occlusions and complex environments.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of fall detection systems specifically designed for construction sites with complex, occluded environments and multi-person scenarios. Most existing research focuses on controlled settings like elderly care facilities rather than dynamic construction environments, and fails to track multiple individuals simultaneously or validate performance under varying occlusion levels.
Future Research: Future research should focus on validating the model in real-world construction environments, integrating additional sensors such as thermal imaging to reduce dependence on visual data, enriching datasets with broader fall scenarios, exploring multi-camera systems and multi-modal sensing approaches for proactive safety measures including near-miss detection, and investigating advanced architectures like attention-based models and Graph Neural Networks while maintaining efficiency for edge deployment.
Results: The YOSAP-LSTM model achieved impressive performance with 98.66% accuracy, 97.32% sensitivity, and 99.10% specificity for fall detection, operating at 6.44 FPS on the NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX edge device. The model maintained robust performance across varying occlusion levels and outperformed other baseline models including threshold-based, KNN, and ST-GCN approaches while using significantly fewer parameters (186,303 vs 6,141,758 for ST-GCN).
AI Model Used: YOLOv8 (for person detection), SORT (for tracking), AlphaPose (for pose estimation), 1D CNN-LSTM (for fall classification). The paper also compared against KNN, ST-GCN (Spatial-Temporal Graph Convolutional Network), and threshold-based models.
Data Types: Video datasets comprising 376,006 frames from three sources: KFall dataset (323,412 frames from indoor environments), Multiple camera dataset (14,678 frames from various viewpoints), and Ai-Hub dataset (35,385 frames from construction site simulations including falls from various heights). Additionally, 24,264 annotated images for training YOLOv8 person detection were collected from public datasets, field construction sites, and web crawling.

Title: Evaluating sub-contractors performance using EFNIM
Abstract: In the construction industry, sub-contractor's performance is a crucial factor in their awards of a new job by a general contractor. The objective of this study is to improve the current practices for evaluating sub-contractors performance. Drawbacks of current evaluation process are discussed firstly. The appropriateness for adopting the Evolutionary Fuzzy Neural Inference Model (EFNIM) for improving the drawbacks is studied. A Sub-contractor Performance Evaluation Model (SPEM) is then developed by employing the EFNIM. The effectiveness of the proposed SPEM is validated by performing case study of a real general contractor. Validation results show that the proposed method accurately measures sub-contractor's performance enhancing the current practice of evaluation.
Authors: Ko, C.-H.; Cheng, M.-Y.; Wu, T.-K.
Construction Application Area: Sub-contractor performance evaluation
Methodology: The paper develops a Sub-contractor Performance Evaluation Model (SPEM) using the Evolutionary Fuzzy Neural Inference Model (EFNIM), which integrates Genetic Algorithms (GAs), Fuzzy Logic (FL), and Neural Networks (NNs). The model is validated through a case study of a real general contractor in Taiwan, using 61 training patterns from 14 sub-contractors and 15 test patterns from one sub-contractor.
Research Goal: The objective is to improve current practices for evaluating sub-contractors' performance by developing an objective evaluation method that addresses the intrinsic ambiguity and difficult formalization inherent in subjective evaluations based on evaluators' experience and intuition.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses drawbacks of current evaluation processes including repeated evaluation stages, assumption of independent factors, equal weighting of all factors, and subjective judgment. It also tackles difficulties in identifying network topology, membership functions, and fuzzy rules that existed in previous AI-based contractor evaluation research.
Future Research: Methods for predicting sub-contractor's performance for future projects should be studied. Additionally, improving the quality and accuracy of training data is crucial for performance evaluation, as AI methods rely on patterns for searching solutions.
Results: The validation results show that the proposed SPEM accurately measures sub-contractor performance with a Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of 0.0082 for 61 training patterns and 0.0141 RMSE for 15 unknown test cases. The model successfully captures the mapping between evaluation factors and sub-contractor performance, eliminating subjective judgment and improving current evaluation practices.
AI Model Used: Evolutionary Fuzzy Neural Inference Model (EFNIM), which is a hybrid model combining Genetic Algorithms (GAs), Fuzzy Logic (FL), and Neural Networks (NNs)
Data Types: Historical performance data of labor-type sub-contractors, including 12 evaluation factors (construction technique, duration control, corporative manner, material wastage, services after completion, collaboration, safety, tool usage, workspace clearance, management ability, personality, and economic condition) scored on a 5-point scale, along with final scores provided by managers ranging from 56 to 88 points.

Title: Building applications for smart and safe construction with the DECENTER Fog Computing and Brokerage Platform
Abstract: Various smart applications are needed to address complex problems in construction falling under the broad categories of safety at work, construction site management, management of resources, waste and assets and construction progress monitoring. Fog computing emerges as a new computing paradigm for Edge-to-Cloud computing that integrates Internet of Things (IoT), Artificial Intelligence (AI), and Blockchain technologies to facilitate the development and operation of smart applications. However, a comprehensive methodology that applies Fog computing to construction projects is currently missing. In our work, we use the novel DECENTER Fog Computing and Brokerage Platform to address requirements for flexible use of AI methods in construction projects and develop a relevant methodology. Evaluation is performed through all application development phases at a real construction site in Ljubljana, Slovenia. Testing results show that the use of Fog computing contributes to high response rates, privacy and security when processing sensitive worker and company data.
Authors: Kochovski, P.; Stankovski, V.
Construction Application Area: Safety at work, construction site management, management of resources, waste and assets, construction progress monitoring, intruder detection, real-time equipment positioning, access-routes control, collision detection, waste mapping, and infrastructure monitoring
Methodology: The paper implements the DECENTER Fog Computing and Brokerage Platform for smart construction applications. It follows a component-based development approach using IoT sensors, video cameras, AI methods, and blockchain technology. The methodology involves setting up surveillance infrastructure at a construction site, deploying containerized AI services across Edge-to-Cloud continuum using Kubernetes orchestration, and implementing Smart Contracts for resource management and privacy preservation.
Research Goal: The goal is to develop and evaluate a comprehensive methodology for applying Fog computing to construction projects, addressing requirements for flexible use of AI methods while ensuring high Quality of Service, privacy, security, and cost-effectiveness in processing construction site data.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of a comprehensive methodology that applies Fog computing to construction projects, particularly the insufficient integration of AI methods in real-world construction applications. It also addresses the need for flexible AI method selection, fast response times in dynamic construction environments, end-to-end transparency for handling sensitive data, and reduction of operational costs.
Future Research: Future research includes applying European and Korean legislation for biometric AI certification and regulatory alignment for personal data processing. Additional work is needed on improving blockchain interoperability through intrachain operations and developing Smart Contracts that translate legislation into standardized code understandable by the blockchain.
Results: The testing results demonstrated that Fog computing contributes to high response rates (notifications received in less than 5 seconds), privacy and security when processing sensitive worker and company data, and operational cost reduction of up to 60% compared to infrastructure ownership. The QoS-aware orchestration successfully maintained high performance by redeploying applications when workload increased.
AI Model Used: YOLO (You Only Look Once) v3 for object detection and person identification, along with various other pre-trained AI models from the DECENTER AI repository (20 models total) for tasks such as vehicle detection, safety equipment detection (helmets, safety vests), and resource tracking
Data Types: The paper uses video surveillance stream data from IP and USB cameras deployed at the construction site, along with monitoring metrics data (network latency, bandwidth, packet loss, CPU usage, memory usage, video fps). Additionally, IoT sensor data for environmental conditions (weather, noise) is collected through the SensiNact platform.

Title: Carbon emission reduction in construction industry: qualitative insights on procurement, policies and artificial intelligence
Abstract: Purpose: The construction industry is a major contributor to global carbon emissions. This study investigates the role of procurement and contracting methods in carbon emission reduction (CER) in the construction industry. It also examines artificial intelligence's (AI's) potential to drive low-carbon practices, aiming to identify transformative policies and practices. Design/methodology/approach: This study employed a qualitative methodology, engaging in semi-structured interviews with nine industry professionals alongside an innovative engagement with Generative Pre-trained Transformer (GPT) technology to gather insights into procurement and project delivery methods (PDM) role in CER. The study involved identifying patterns, organizing themes, and analyzing data to extract meaningful insights on effective policies and strategies for CER in the construction industry. Findings: The results underscore the importance of early contractor involvement and integrated PDM for CER in construction. Results emphasize the pivotal role of project owners in directing projects toward sustainability, highlighting the need for client demand. The research identifies cost constraints, limited material availability, and human resource capacity as key barriers in the US. The study proposes innovative materials, financial incentives, education, and regulatory standards as effective interventions. It also explores the future use of AI in enhancing CER, suggesting new avenues for technological integration. Originality/value: The study provides empirical insights into the role of procurement and PDM in CER within the US construction industry by using qualitative approach and use of a GPT. It underscores the interplay between contracting methods, stakeholder engagement, and AI's emerging role, for enhancing policies and practices to decarbonize the US construction industry.
Authors: Kumar, D.; Zhang, C.
Journal: Built Environment Project and Asset Management
Construction Application Area: Carbon emission reduction, procurement, project delivery methods, sustainability practices
Methodology: The study employed semi-structured interviews with nine industry professionals (consultants, academia, contractors, and owners) and one GPT-4 chatbot. Thematic analysis was conducted using NVivo to categorize and compare data across cases, analyzing themes including effective contracting, barriers to carbon emission reduction, sustainable construction practices, stakeholder roles, policies and incentives, and future use of AI.
Research Goal: The study investigates the role of procurement and contracting methods in carbon emission reduction in the US construction industry and examines artificial intelligence's potential to drive low-carbon practices, aiming to identify transformative policies and practices for decarbonization.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the notable gap in research concerning the influence of procurement and project delivery methods on reducing construction emissions, particularly in how contracting methods impact energy and carbon reductions in public construction projects within the US context.
Future Research: Future research should expand the scope geographically and in participant diversity, including larger and more varied samples from different regions and sectors. Quantitative studies, comparative analysis across regions, and research focused on harnessing emerging digital capabilities like AI, simulation, and blockchain to accelerate sustainability are recommended. Studies evaluating the efficacy of policy interventions and incentives would inform evidence-based carbon governance.
Results: The results underscore the importance of early contractor involvement and integrated project delivery methods for carbon emission reduction. Project owners play a pivotal role in directing sustainability. Key barriers include cost constraints, limited low-carbon material availability, and human resource capacity gaps. The study proposes innovative materials, financial incentives, education, regulatory standards, and AI integration as effective interventions.
AI Model Used: GPT-4 (Generative Pre-trained Transformer)
Data Types: Qualitative interview data from nine semi-structured interviews with construction industry professionals (average 25-30 minutes each) covering topics on procurement strategies, project delivery methods, barriers to carbon emission reduction, and AI's role. One additional interview was conducted with a GPT-4 chatbot developed specifically for this study.

Title: Ontological inference process using AI-based object recognition for hazard awareness in construction sites
Abstract: Research on automating risk situation recognition using AI is being actively conducted. However, hazard situation recognition using AI has a limitation in securing a large amount of data for training of all hazard situations. This study proposes an approach that combines the conventional AI image recognition technology and relation-based reasoning to overcome the limitations of the method of sufficiently training each image regarding various hazard situations. To validate the proposed process, we constructed ontology by defining the relations between construction site objects and working situations, safety situations required for each working situation, and hazard situations based on the case of the work using mobile scaffolding. This approach will enhance the efficiency of using AI by inferring the current working situation based on the relations between recognized objects and determining whether it is a safe situation based on the inference on the standard safety situation for the corresponding working situation.
Authors: Lee, S.; Yu, J.
Construction Application Area: Construction site safety, hazard awareness, and safety management
Methodology: The paper proposes a convergence model combining AI-based image recognition technology with ontology-based reasoning. Individual construction site objects are recognized using AI (deep learning), then ontology is used to infer object relationships, working situations, and safety/hazard situations. The approach was validated using mobile scaffolding as a case study with Protégé v5.5 for ontology construction and HermiT Reasoner for validation.
Research Goal: To propose an automated hazard awareness system that combines AI image recognition with ontology-based reasoning to overcome the limitation of requiring large amounts of training data for all possible hazard situations in construction sites.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the practical limitation of conventional AI image recognition approaches that require collecting and training massive amounts of data for every possible hazard situation. It also addresses the difficulty of continuously monitoring construction sites manually through CCTV systems.
Future Research: Future research is needed to secure accuracy, sustainability, and usability of the proposed technology for practical use. This includes testing optimal factors and criteria for relationship recognition, developing automated ontology extraction from safety guidelines using NLP, creating information extraction and semantic reasoning modules, and comparing accuracy between AI-only and the proposed AI-ontology approach.
Results: The validation showed that the ontology was consistently defined without logical errors and properly inferred working situations, safety situations, and hazard situations according to design intent. The approach significantly reduced training data requirements from 32,768,000 images (for AI-only approach) to 10,000 images (for the proposed approach) for mobile scaffolding hazard recognition.
AI Model Used: Deep learning-based object detection (YOLO mentioned for bounding box extraction). Related works reviewed include: Mask R-CNN, Faster R-CNN, DCNN, CNN, Convolutional-LSTM Network, VGG-16, ResNet-50, Xception, and SVM classifiers.
Data Types: Video images from construction site CCTV cameras with bounding box coordinates of construction site objects (workers, scaffolding components like cross brace, wheel, ladder frame, guardrail, vertical frame, platform, outrigger). Object relationship data defined by coordinate comparison values and safety rules from Korea's 'Standard Safety Guidelines for Temporary Construction'.

Title: From design to operation: Multi-agent AI for virtual in-situ modeling of digital twins in BIM
Abstract: A virtual building model (VBM) is a mathematical representation that describes the behavior of a physical building. Accurate VBMs provide insightful information about the physical building within the digital twin (DT) framework. However, there is limited research on the autonomous construction of VBMs. To address this gap, this paper proposes a multi-agent artificial intelligence (AI) system that autonomously constructs VBM. The proposed system autonomously develops, calibrates, and manages virtual models that constitute the VBM by leveraging data and information generated throughout the building lifecycle within a BIM environment. The proposed method is validated on a real operating heating, ventilation, and air-conditioning (HVAC) system, autonomously developing a chilled water flow rate model (MAPE 2.27 %) and an evaporator inlet temperature model (RMSE 0.33°C). These results suggest the feasibility of autonomously constructing VBMs and contribute to shifting the DT paradigm from physical construction automation to the autonomous construction of VBM.
Authors: Lee, J.; Li, J.; Yoon, S.
Construction Application Area: Building information modeling (BIM), digital twin construction, virtual building model (VBM) development, HVAC system modeling, building operations and maintenance
Methodology: The paper proposes a multi-agent AI system that autonomously constructs virtual building models (VBMs) within a BIM environment. The system consists of four specialized AI agents: building metadata extraction agent, physics-based modeling agent, calibration agent, and ontology generation agent. These agents work collaboratively to extract modeling sources from BIM, develop physics-based virtual design models, calibrate them using operational data, and manage semantic relationships through ontologies.
Research Goal: The paper aims to enable autonomous construction of VBMs throughout the building lifecycle by developing a multi-agent AI system that leverages data and information from BIM environments. This shifts the digital twin paradigm from physical construction automation to autonomous VBM construction.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of clearly defined responsibility for VBM development and management in the construction industry, and the need for lifecycle-oriented approaches that integrate information from both design and operation phases. It also tackles the challenge of developing accurate VBMs that account for inherent uncertainties in physics-based models.
Future Research: Future research should focus on developing frameworks capable of constructing control-oriented VBMs that incorporate lifecycle uncertainties and cover various DT-based application domains such as lighting, structural performance, and disaster prevention. Advanced semantic alignment techniques and metadata schemas supporting integration of diverse heterogeneous data sources should be developed to enable fully autonomous virtual model development and calibration.
Results: The proposed system successfully developed and calibrated virtual models for a real HVAC system, achieving a chilled water flow rate model with 2.27% MAPE and an evaporator inlet temperature model with 0.33°C RMSE. The study demonstrated the feasibility of autonomously constructing VBMs and validated the effectiveness of multi-agent AI coordination and knowledge engineering strategies.
AI Model Used: GPT-4o (Large Language Model)
Data Types: The paper uses BIM data including equipment specifications and building metadata (IFC schema), operational data from building automation systems (BAS) collected at one-minute intervals, Brick schema-based ontologies describing sensing networks and building systems, and pump performance curve data. The operational data included some missing values and outliers.

Title: Generative AI-driven data augmentation for enhanced construction hazard detection
Abstract: The construction industry has long struggled with poor safety records. Traditional safety monitoring methods, reliant on manual observations, are often ineffective. To address these limitations, computer vision and generative artificial intelligence (AI) have been explored. While computer vision has shown promise in automating safety monitoring, its effectiveness is often hindered by the challenges of efficiently collecting diverse datasets. Generative AI offers a potential solution by augmenting image datasets, enabling more robust construction hazard detection.
