This paper investigates the use of generative AI for augmenting image data to improve hazard detection performance. Various combinations of generative AI tools and prompting strategies are tested. The results show that the combination of image-guided structured prompting with Stable Diffusion achieves the highest detection performance (mAP@50 of 92.5 %) using 150 augmented images. This represents a substantial improvement compared to the baseline mAP@50 of 51.6 % achieved with real images alone.
Authors: Lee, Y.; Kang, G.; Kim, J.; Yoon, S.; Jeon, J.
Construction Application Area: Construction safety, specifically fall hazard detection and monitoring of unprotected openings at construction sites
Methodology: The study utilized three generative AI tools (DALL-E, Midjourney, Stable Diffusion) combined with three prompt engineering techniques (zero-shot, structured, and image-guided structured prompting) to augment a baseline dataset of 200 real construction site images. The augmented datasets (varying from 50 to 300 images per combination) were used to train a YOLOv8n object detection model. The model's performance was evaluated using precision, recall, mAP@50, and mAP@50-95 metrics.
Research Goal: The paper aims to improve construction hazard detection performance by leveraging generative AI-based image augmentation to address the limitations of small real-world datasets. Specifically, it investigates how different combinations of generative AI tools and prompting strategies can enhance computer vision-based detection of unprotected openings at construction sites.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses three main gaps: (1) the unclear relationship between generative AI tools, prompt engineering strategies, and resulting hazard detection performance; (2) uncertainty about whether augmented imagery data can effectively enhance hazard detection; and (3) minimal research on using generative AI for detecting unprotected openings, which are a leading cause of construction accidents.
Future Research: Future research should include: (1) multiple training iterations with cross-validation techniques; (2) expanding hazard scope beyond unprotected openings; (3) direct comparison with traditional augmentation methods; (4) increasing dataset size and diversity from various construction contexts; (5) exploring alternative prompting strategies; (6) conducting real-world field validation studies; and (7) developing standardized evaluation frameworks combining quantitative metrics with expert-driven assessments for AI-generated data quality.
Results: The combination of image-guided structured prompting with Stable Diffusion achieved the highest detection performance with a mAP@50 of 92.5% using 150 augmented images, representing a substantial improvement from the baseline mAP@50 of 51.6% achieved with real images alone. Across all tools, image-guided structured prompting consistently delivered the best results, with optimal performance achieved when augmented images ranged between 100-150, after which performance plateaued or declined slightly.
AI Model Used: YOLOv8n (object detection model); Generative AI models: DALL-E, Midjourney, and Stable Diffusion (for image augmentation)
Data Types: The study used 200 real construction site images of unprotected openings collected via search engines and field capturing, plus 2,700 synthetically generated images (300 per combination of three generative AI tools and three prompting techniques). All images were labeled using polygon annotation method via Roboflow platform.

Title: Ontology-Based Framework for Context-Aware DfX Criteria Inference Using BIM
Abstract: Manual Design for X (DfX) processes in construction projects often suffer from inefficiency, inconsistency, and a high risk of errors. As construction projects increase in complexity, there is a greater need for a systematic and automated approach to DfX criteria inference. This study proposes an ontology-based framework for inferring DfX criteria using building information modeling (BIM) data. The framework systematically maps BIM element attributes - type, location, and structural features - to DfX criteria, including quality, safety for work, manufacturability, and assemblability, using description logic. Logical connections are established through object properties, and reasoning is performed using Protégé's HermiT reasoner. SPARQL queries are used to validate reasoning results by explicitly asserting inference outcomes and ensuring accurate mapping between BIM attributes, DfX criteria, and a checklist. This ontology-driven approach enhances automation, reduces reliance on subjective judgments, and improves the efficiency of construction design reviews. This study provides a scalable and reusable structure that supports dynamic project requirements. Future work will focus on broader validation across diverse construction scenarios, improving interoperability with other BIM tools, and integrating AI-based reasoning techniques for enhanced automation and performance.
Authors: Lee, S.-K.; Jeong, M.-S.; Cho, T.-Y.; Chung, B.-Y.; Yu, J.-H.
Construction Application Area: Design for X (DfX) criteria inference, including quality, safety for work, manufacturability, and assemblability in construction design reviews
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (BIM, drones, laser scanning, robotics, digital twins, sensor networks)
Methodology: The paper proposes an ontology-based framework that systematically maps BIM element attributes (type, location, structural features) to DfX criteria using description logic. Element information is extracted from BIM using Revit schedules, transformed into ontology data using Protégé, and validated using HermiT reasoner. SPARQL queries are used to validate reasoning results and ensure accurate mapping between BIM attributes, DfX criteria, and checklists. The framework is validated through a case study of a residential building model and a survey of OSC practitioners.
Research Goal: The goal is to automate and systematize the DfX review process by integrating BIM and ontology to automatically infer context-specific DfX review criteria based on element characteristics and relationships, thereby enhancing efficiency, reducing subjective judgments, and improving consistency in construction design reviews.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the inefficiency, inconsistency, and high risk of errors in manual DfX review processes that heavily rely on experts' experience and subjective judgment. It also addresses the limitation of current BIM practices that are primarily limited to static data representation and lack the flexibility and automation required for effective DfX reviews.
Future Research: Future work will focus on broader validation across diverse construction scenarios, improving interoperability with other BIM tools beyond Revit and Protégé, integrating AI-based reasoning techniques for enhanced automation and performance, and expanding the DfX checklist to encompass a wider range of construction contexts and trades.
Results: The validation using HermiT reasoner and SPARQL queries confirmed that the ontology model maintains logical consistency and accurately infers appropriate DfX criteria and checklist items based on BIM element attributes. A survey of OSC practitioners indicated positive expectations regarding time savings (4.4 points), error reduction (4.0 points), and improved component quality (4.0 points), with an average willingness to adopt the system of 4.2 points on a 5-point scale.
AI Model Used: No Model - The paper uses ontology-based reasoning (HermiT reasoner) and SPARQL queries, which are knowledge-based approaches rather than AI/machine learning models
Data Types: The data used includes BIM element attribute data extracted from Autodesk Revit 2022 (element type, location, size, strength, reinforcement, formwork, curing method, embedded items, opening details, finishing information) for a 28-story residential building. The data is transformed into RDF format and structured according to ontology classes and properties for automated DfX criteria inference.

Title: Technology opportunity discovery linking artificial intelligence and construction technologies: A graph convolution network-based approach
Abstract: The advancement of digital technology has driven innovation across industries. Artificial intelligence (AI) as a pivotal digital technology, has the potential to stimulate innovative outcomes when converged with construction technologies. However, current research has yet to accurately discover promising technology opportunities between AI and construction. To fill this gap, this study uses patent data to develop a holistic approach for precisely discovering technology opportunities. It focuses on analyzing technology convergence from two distinct perspectives: the "degree" and "value" of convergence. The technology convergence degree is measured based on the semantic network and graph convolution network (GCN) method. The technology convergence value is calculated according to patent attribute metrics. Analysis of the spatial-temporal evolution of technology convergence reveals the irreversible convergence trend between AI and construction. This study further identifies key opportunities, such as the convergence of computer vision and construction equipment technology, which have the potential to advance the construction industry. Technology opportunities are also identified both at global and national levels, providing strategic insights that could guide industry regulatory authorities.
Authors: Li, K.; Shan, T.; Wu, H.; Zou, Z.; Huang, R.; Chang, R.; Shrestha, A.
Construction Application Area: The paper examines AI applications across multiple construction domains including: construction materials, equipment, design, visualization, decision support, structure analysis, engineering management, building design, and environmental considerations. The research identifies technology convergence between AI subfields (machine learning, deep learning, computer vision, neural networks, natural language processing, augmented reality, data mining, robotics) and these construction technology areas.
Methodology: The paper employs a novel quantitative framework using patent data analysis from IncoPat database (2013-2022). The methodology combines Natural Language Processing (NLP) with Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN) to measure technology convergence degree through semantic similarity analysis of patent texts. Technology convergence value is assessed using patent attribute metrics (IPC classifications, citations, inventors, claims, families, etc.). The approach constructs semantic networks from patent titles and abstracts, trains keyword vectors using Word2Vec initialization and GCN, then identifies key convergence portfolios by analyzing both convergence degree and value at global and national levels.
Research Goal: The study aims to develop a holistic approach for precisely discovering technology opportunities between AI and construction technologies by analyzing technology convergence from two distinct perspectives: the 'degree' (extent of knowledge overlap) and 'value' (practical benefits and innovation impact) of convergence. The research seeks to identify promising convergence portfolios that have both high convergence degrees and positive innovation effects, providing strategic insights for industry regulatory authorities at global and national levels.
Gaps Addressed: Current research has not accurately discovered promising technology opportunities between AI and construction, focusing primarily on surface-level exploration that lists AI applications without precise identification of technology opportunities. Additionally, existing studies identify converging technologies through network analysis or link prediction without delving into the value and impact of these convergences, failing to recognize that not all technological convergences lead to beneficial innovations, which can result in technological 'bubbles' and resource waste.
Future Research: Future research should expand beyond the two-field limitation to compare AI integration in other industries beyond construction and examine convergence of other digital technologies for more comprehensive results. The study suggests improving keyword selection accuracy, incorporating complete patent texts and figures beyond titles and abstracts, conducting structural analysis of patent interrelationships within portfolios, and developing more sophisticated valuation methods that consider the interconnections among patents rather than treating them equally.
Results: The study reveals an irreversible convergence trend between AI and construction technology, with both convergence degree and value increasing over the past decade. Key technology opportunities were identified, particularly computer vision converging with construction equipment technology (CP64), which shows both high convergence degree and high value. However, the research found that high convergence degree does not necessarily generate corresponding value - only 5 of 15 high-degree convergence portfolios have high convergence value, indicating potential technological 'bubbles'. Country-level analysis shows China leads in convergence degree but the United States has higher convergence value, reflecting different AI development policies and patent quality strategies.
AI Model Used: Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN), Word2Vec
Data Types: Patent data from IncoPat database spanning 2013-2022, including 71,062 patents in AI and construction technology convergence fields. The data includes patent titles, abstracts, IPC/CPC classifications, citations, inventors, claims, patent families, assignments and licenses, and country information from 54 countries and regions with focus on top five countries (China, United States, Japan, South Korea, India).

Title: Data-driven lifting-centered construction site layout planning decision approach with BIM
Abstract: Construction site layout planning (CSLP) is essential for optimizing the placement of temporary facilities (TFs), yet it inadequately integrates tower crane characteristics, causing inefficient material transportation and safety risk. Current decision-making relies on labor-intensive data extraction, complex mathematical models, and fragmented workflows incompatible with specialized software. This paper proposes an automated data-driven lifting-centered CSLP decision approach with building information modeling (BIM) and AI to enhance TF placement efficiency. The approach incorporates three stages: automated data extraction from the BIM model with users' promotion, development of data-driven lifting-based multi-objectives CSLP decision engines, and evaluation of generated TFs placement through BIM-based simulations. Validation indicates that over 92 % of AI-generated CSLP outcomes outperform traditional methods (genetic algorithm (GA)). Experiments on a real-world project demonstrate that this approach reduces processing time to 7.93 % of GA and lowers functional costs by 11.60 %. This method assists designers in expediting the CSLP decision-making process with BIM models.
Authors: Li, R.; Chi, H.-L.; Hu, Z.; Li, D.; Yi, W.; Brilakis, I.
Construction Application Area: Construction site layout planning (CSLP), temporary facility placement, tower crane layout planning
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (BIM, drones, laser scanning, robotics, digital twins, sensor networks)
Methodology: The paper proposes a data-driven approach integrating BIM with generative adversarial networks (GANs) for automated construction site layout planning. It involves three stages: (1) automated data extraction from BIM models using Revit API and OpenCV to generate 2D conditional images, (2) development of CSLP-GAN using conditional GANs to predict optimal temporary facility locations based on multi-objective optimization considering construction cost, safety, and lifting efficiency, and (3) visualization and integration of generated layouts back into BIM models. A mathematical optimization model generates paired datasets for training, and the approach is validated against traditional methods (Branch and Bound Algorithm and Genetic Algorithm) using processing time and functional cost metrics.
Research Goal: The paper aims to develop an automated, data-driven approach for lifting-centered construction site layout planning integrated with BIM that efficiently generates reasonable temporary facility locations in real-time while considering tower crane characteristics, construction cost, safety, and lifting efficiency.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the inadequate integration of tower crane characteristics in CSLP, labor-intensive manual data extraction processes, computational complexity of traditional mathematical optimization methods in large-scale projects, and fragmented workflows incompatible with specialized software that create information silos between abstract numerical results and actual construction data.
Future Research: Future research should explore wider varieties of temporary facilities and their constraints, conduct comprehensive field validation studies to evaluate practical usability in real-world applications, integrate construction schedules and task dependencies to account for changing site layout requirements throughout the project lifecycle, and expand the system to accommodate different construction project types and dynamic changes in CSLP.
Results: The AI method achieved 99.04% reduction in computational time compared to BBA and 84.15% reduction compared to GA, with over 92% of AI-generated layouts outperforming GA results. In a real-world case study in Wuhan, the approach reduced processing time to 7.93% of GA and lowered functional costs by 11.60%, demonstrating effectiveness in generating practical layouts that consider spatial relationships, safety distances, and operational efficiency.
AI Model Used: Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (CGAN), specifically a U-Net architecture generator with ResNet blocks and a multi-scale discriminator
Data Types: The dataset consists of 1,616 samples (1,294 training, 161 validation, 161 testing) of paired 1024×1024 pixel images representing construction site layouts extracted from BIM models and 2D CAD drawings of various high-rise building projects. Input images include construction boundaries, building boundaries, roads, entrances, tower cranes, and potential temporary facility locations; output images show optimal temporary facility placements calculated using mathematical optimization models considering transportation cost, construction safety, and lifting efficiency.

Title: Generative design for modular construction structures based on expert knowledge-informed graph neural networks and meta-heuristics
Abstract: With the growing demands in the construction industry, coupled with cost pressures and environmental concerns, modular building (MB) solutions are proposed to address the challenges. However, the design process of MB is more fragmented and complex, especially the structural design. This requires a reconsideration of the automated approach for its layout design, incorporating structural design. This paper develops a generative AI-enabled framework, focusing on the structural design of reinforced concrete MB. The proposed hybrid approach integrates a graph neural network-based model in a genetic generative design framework to surrogate structure design. And multiple structural related objectives are optimized in this framework. It was tested in a real project in Hong Kong and compared with the engineer's design. The optimal Pareto-balanced compromise solution resulted in a 12 % increase in usable floor area, a 7 % increase in structural performance, and a 23 % reduction in construction cost.
Authors: Li, X.; Lu, W.; Peng, Z.
Construction Application Area: Structural design and layout optimization for modular buildings, specifically reinforced concrete modular buildings (RC-MB)
Methodology: The paper develops a hybrid framework (MoSDA) that integrates a Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based structural expert AI (MsE-AI) with a genetic generative design (GGD) framework. The MsE-AI uses GATConv and SAGEConv layers to predict structural wall designs from flat layouts. The GGD framework includes a rule-based generator, the MsE-AI surrogate model, a multi-objective evaluator (UFA utilization, structural performance, construction cost), and NSGA-II optimization algorithm.
Research Goal: To develop an AI-enabled generative design framework (MoSDA) that assists architects in automated structural design and optimization of reinforced concrete modular building flat layouts during the preliminary design stage, considering structural performance, usable floor area, and construction cost simultaneously.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of automated structural design tools for modular buildings, particularly RC-MB, and the difficulty of integrating structural engineering expertise into traditional rule-based generative design frameworks. It also addresses the challenge of optimizing multiple conflicting objectives (structural performance, usable floor area, cost) in early-stage modular building design.
Future Research: Future research should consider the role of walls in establishing center of rigidity and controlling structural rotation under lateral forces, incorporate overall building structure design including cast-in-situ core design and load transfer between modules and building core, and expand the framework to incorporate other design factors such as energy efficiency and daylighting.
Results: The MsE-AI model achieved 0.85 accuracy in predicting structural walls for RC-MB using the Flat-Con-WPID-Sw-UP representation method. When applied to a real Hong Kong project, the optimal Pareto-balanced solution achieved 12% improvement in usable floor area, 7% increase in structural performance, and 23% reduction in construction cost compared to the engineer's design.
AI Model Used: Graph Neural Network (GNN) combining Graph Attention Network (GATConv) and GraphSAGE (SAGEConv) layers
Data Types: The study used 98 real RC-MB flat layout data from Hong Kong Housing Development's open data, which were augmented 48-fold through flipping, rotating, and translating operations, resulting in 4,704 graphs. The data includes flat layouts with structural wall information, module configurations, and building element features represented as graph structural data.

Title: Heterogeneous structural mechanics mechanism in underground excavation for Intelligence construction based on hybrid physical-guided and data-driven method
Abstract: The structural mechanics mechanism research in underground excavation is a prominent challenge for the time varying scenarios, the uncertainty of the complex system and the environment. In this study, to explore the structural mechanics mechanism, virtual models are performed based on the physical data of the soil and structure, and are compared to the on-site photos of the same scenarios. The basic mechanics is inspected by three Generative AI models in 73 tests to explore the mechanisms by text outputs. The physical-based simplified mechanics models are calculated on the first two supports scenarios to offer some guides and knowledge to the following data mining work. The structural physical mechanics is also numerical simulated to offer some further physical guides. The structure monitoring data of the support axial force and the diaphragm wall deformation in the deep excavation is explored with the time and excavating depths varying, and the relationship of the dual heterogeneous mechanics performance are qualitative discussed by a three-dimension demonstration method. Furthermore, a novel index, the ratio of the force to the deformation, is proposed on the data-driven sight. The relationship is calculated quantitatively on the proposed index in different construction stages, and the probability distribution is inspected. In summary, three physical-based models are performed and investigated to offer physical guides for data-driven dual heterogeneous mechanics mechanism on an underground excavation case in Metro construction, and the qualitative and quantitative exploring are performed based on the three-dimension data demonstrations and on a proposed index. The mechanism exploring methods can be synthetical integrated for the future engineering expert systems in engineering, and for the coming intelligence construction.
Authors: Li, W.-M.; Dou, Y.-C.; Han, X.-R.; Yang, W.-J.; Lian, J.
Construction Application Area: Underground excavation structural mechanics, deformation monitoring, support structure analysis, and construction safety in Metro construction
Methodology: The paper employs a hybrid physical-guided and data-driven approach. Physical-based models include simplified mechanics models (calculated using Force Method and Moment Distribution Method), numerical finite element models, and virtual BIM models compared with on-site photos. Data-driven analysis explores monitoring data of support axial force and diaphragm wall deformation across different construction stages. The study also preliminarily tests three Generative AI models (ERNIE Bot, Baidu AI, and Doubao) with 73 tests on 8 structural mechanics scenarios to explore their potential for mechanism explanation.
Research Goal: The paper aims to explore the heterogeneous structural mechanics mechanism in underground excavation by integrating physical-guided models (simplified mechanics, numerical simulation, and virtual models) with data-driven analysis of monitoring data, and to investigate the relationship between support axial force and diaphragm wall deformation for intelligence construction applications.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the challenge that structural mechanics mechanisms in underground excavation are difficult to describe by single research methods due to time-varying scenarios, system complexity, and environmental uncertainty. It also addresses the limitation that data-driven approaches in civil engineering are constrained by data loss, noise, and errors, requiring integration of multiple methods for comprehensive understanding.
Future Research: Future work includes improving monitoring project design, enhancing calculation and simulation accuracy, further integration with AI science and technology progress, developing more efficient classification methods for construction stages, and advancing the integration of multiple methods to achieve better accuracy and efficiency for intelligent construction expert systems.
Results: The study found that support axial force and diaphragm wall deformation show qualitative associations across four construction stages, with force increasing rapidly before the 5th support installation and during bottom slab concreting. A novel quantitative index (ratio of force to deformation) was proposed, showing similar distribution patterns across different supports in corresponding stages, indicating potential for engineering decision support. The ratio distributions were primarily in intervals of [200,800) for early stages and [100,400) for later stages.
AI Model Used: Generative AI models: ERNIE Bot, Baidu AI (文心一言), and Doubao (豆包). Also tested DeepSeek AI for quantitative calculations.
Data Types: The paper uses multiple data types including: (1) structural monitoring data (support axial force and diaphragm wall deformation measured by sensors), (2) geotechnical exploration data (soil layer properties), (3) construction design data (support dimensions, depths, and schedules), (4) on-site construction photos, (5) virtual BIM model data created in Revit, and (6) numerical simulation data from finite element analysis across 24 construction scenarios over 205 days.

Title: Impacts of Collaborative Robots on Construction Work Performance and Worker Perception: Experimental Analysis of Human-Robot Collaborative Wood Assembly
Abstract: Collaborative robots are increasingly recognized as potential assistants to relieve workers from repetitive and physically demanding tasks on construction jobsites. Despite the great potential, most efforts have focused on developing various artificial intelligence (AI) and robotic technologies to achieve specific human-robot collaboration (HRC) functions. However, there is a significant lack of research regarding the impacts of such collaboration on construction work performance and workers' perception and acceptance of collaborative robots, which could be a critical influence factor on the feasibility and effectiveness of HRC on construction jobsites. To this end, this study aims to evaluate the multidimensional impacts of collaborative robots on work efficiency, quality, and workload as well as workers' perception and acceptance. HRC experiments on sample construction tasks (i.e., wood assembly) were conducted in conjunction with quantitative measurements and subject surveys. Through comparison between HRC experiments and human-human collaboration (HHC) experiments based on this case study, it was found that HRC could improve up to 29.3% and 88.6% in work efficiency and assembly accuracy, respectively, and reduce workers' workload by up to 20.3%. Furthermore, workers' perception of HRC is found to be positive overall with higher acceptance after HRC experience, characterized by questionnaires designed based on the technology acceptance model. Through physical experiments, this research is expected to produce more reliable results compared with conventional approaches where participants are simply provided with imaginary scenarios. The findings will also guide the development of robotic technologies to enhance the practical application of HRC in construction.
Authors: Liang, X.; Rasheed, U.; Cai, J.; Wibranek, B.; Awolusi, I.
Journal: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Construction Application Area: Human-robot collaboration in construction assembly tasks, specifically wood assembly and construction work performance evaluation
Methodology: The study conducted physical experiments with human-robot collaboration (HRC) and human-human collaboration (HHC) on wood assembly tasks at two difficulty levels. A total of 13 participants formed 9 groups performing both HRC and HHC experiments. Work performance was measured through completion time, accuracy, nailing quality, and workload (heart rate reserve). Worker perception was assessed using TAM-based surveys distributed before and after HRC experiments, combined with post-experiment interviews.
Research Goal: To evaluate the multidimensional impacts of collaborative robots on construction work performance (efficiency, quality, workload) and workers' perception and acceptance of collaborative robots through experimental analysis of human-robot collaborative wood assembly tasks.
Gaps Addressed: Most research has focused on developing AI and robotic technologies for specific HRC functions, but there is significant lack of research on the actual impacts of HRC on construction work performance and workers' perception. Previous studies used imaginary or simulated scenarios rather than physical experiments, which could be biased and not reflect reality.
Future Research: Future research should focus on large-scale structures to include broader real-world construction scenarios and enhance the intelligence of collaborative robots to enable autonomous physical interactions and proactive action planning. Studies should also include more experienced workers across different experience levels and trades to better understand how HRC impacts vary based on worker characteristics.
Results: HRC improved work efficiency by up to 29.3%, assembly accuracy by up to 88.6%, and reduced workers' workload by up to 20.3% compared to HHC. Workers showed overall positive perception of HRC with higher acceptance after experiencing actual collaboration, though they identified limitations including slow robot operating pace and lack of communication about robot intentions.
AI Model Used: No Model - The robot was preprogrammed using PolyScope interface for predetermined trajectories and movements
Data Types: Quantitative data including completion time, assembly accuracy measurements, nailing quality counts, and heart rate data from wearable Fitbit devices. Qualitative data from TAM-based 7-point Likert scale surveys (pre and post-experiment) and open-ended post-experiment interviews with participants.

Title: Impact of Digital Technology Adoption on the Comparative Advantage of Architectural, Engineering, and Construction Firms in Singapore
Abstract: With a plethora of digital technologies, and some having high capital and running costs, it is critical to know which of these give architectural, engineering, and construction (AEC) firms a comparative advantage. Comparative advantage is measured using these metrics: higher project quality (Y1), productivity (Y2), ability to win tenders (Y3), and reputation (Y4), compared to AEC firms' nearest competitors. This study investigated which specific digital technologies give rise to a comparative advantage for adopters in the AEC sector. The research design was an online survey using a structured questionnaire. Data were collected from AEC professionals in Singapore. The research found that the adoption of five digital technologies is significantly associated with higher project quality, productivity, and reputation compared to AEC firms' nearest competitors. These 'super technologies' are cloud-based technology, design for manufacturing and assembly (DfMA), Internet-of-Things (IoT), robotic technology, and artificial intelligence (AI). The study discovered some digital technologies that are not correlated with any comparative advantage metrics. The contribution to knowledge is the creation of the digital technology adoption model that potential adopters may use to help them decide which specific digital technologies to adopt to achieve comparative advantage. For firms that are already adopting digital technologies, the adoption model serves as a benchmarking tool to measure comparative advantage. The implication for practice is for potential adopters to zoom in on the 'super technologies' and eschew those that do not provide comparative advantages.
Authors: Ling, F.Y.Y.; Heng, G.T.H.; Chang-Richards, A.; Chen, X.; Yiu, T.W.
Journal: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Construction Application Area: Digital technology adoption for comparative advantage in project quality, productivity, ability to win tenders, and reputation
Methodology: The research used an online survey with a structured questionnaire distributed to 101 AEC professionals in Singapore. Statistical analyses including Wilcoxon signed ranks test, Mann-Whitney U test, Spearman's rank correlation, and multiple linear regression were conducted to examine the relationship between digital technology adoption levels and comparative advantage metrics.
Research Goal: To determine the impact of adopting specific digital technologies on AEC firms' comparative advantage measured by project quality, productivity, ability to win tenders, and reputation compared to their nearest competitors, and to develop a digital technology adoption model to guide firms.
Gaps Addressed: Previous studies on digital technology benefits focused on only one or two technologies or measured benefits at a macro level without considering comparative advantage. This study fills the gap by identifying which specific digital technologies bring about comparative advantage to adopters across multiple performance metrics.
Future Research: Future case studies may be conducted by analyzing specific AEC firms in different countries to objectively measure the extent to which each digital technology has been adopted and their comparative advantage, investigating the reasons for digital technology adoption and other confounding variables of comparative advantage.
Results: Five 'super technologies' (IoT, cloud-based technology, robotic technology, AI, and DfMA) were found to be significantly associated with higher project quality, productivity, and reputation. AI was identified as the 'supreme' technology, significantly correlated with all four comparative advantage metrics. The study created a digital technology adoption model showing which technologies provide comparative advantages.
AI Model Used: No specific AI model was developed in this paper. The paper studies AI/machine learning/deep learning as one of the digital technologies being adopted by construction firms, but does not develop or implement an AI model itself.
Data Types: Primary data collected through questionnaire survey using 5-point Likert scales. Data includes: respondents' interaction level with 16 digital technologies (9-point descriptive scale), and self-reported comparative advantage ratings on project quality, productivity, ability to win tenders, and reputation compared to nearest competitors.

Title: AI-assisted decision-making and dynamic trust in lean construction: synergy mechanisms and insights
Abstract: Purpose: This research investigates the influence of AI-assisted decision-making on dynamic trust in lean construction organizations. It examines how AI enhances collaboration efficiency, information transparency and trust stability in complex project environments. The moderating effects of decision chain length and the mediating roles of risk preference and task transfer resilience are also analyzed to provide actionable insights for trust management. Design/methodology/approach: Using a survey of 293 lean construction professionals, structural equation modeling (SEM) was applied to assess the relationships among AI-assisted decision-making, dynamic trust, risk preference, task transfer resilience and decision chain length. The sample reflects diverse project types and organizational contexts. Findings: AI-assisted decision-making significantly improves dynamic trust. Risk preference and task transfer resilience mediate this relationship, while decision chain length moderates the effects. Shorter decision chains amplify the mediating roles of risk preference and task transfer resilience, while longer chains reduce these effects. Notably, as decision chain length increases, the influence of task transfer resilience on dynamic trust weakens compared to risk preference. Originality/value: This research bridges gaps in understanding the mechanisms linking AI and dynamic trust in lean construction. By integrating decision chain length and mediating factors, it offers a novel framework for optimizing trust dynamics and collaboration through AI. These findings provide critical insights for advancing lean construction practices in the era of intelligent technologies.
Authors: Liu, L.; Liu, Y.
Construction Application Area: AI-assisted decision-making in lean construction, focusing on dynamic trust, collaboration efficiency, information transparency, and trust stability in complex project environments
Methodology: The paper employs Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) using survey data from 293 lean construction professionals to assess relationships among AI-assisted decision-making, dynamic trust, risk preference, task transfer resilience, and decision chain length. The questionnaire uses a seven-point Likert scale and undergoes rigorous reliability and validity testing, including Cronbach's α, KMO test, and confirmatory factor analysis. The study also includes a case study of a high-speed railway construction project to validate findings.
Research Goal: The research investigates how AI-assisted decision-making influences dynamic trust in lean construction organizations, examining the mediating roles of risk preference and task transfer resilience, and the moderating effects of decision chain length to provide actionable insights for trust management.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses gaps in understanding the mechanisms linking AI and dynamic trust in lean construction by introducing a dual-path mediation model and revealing the paradoxical moderating effect of decision chain length. It also accounts for asymmetric sensitivity in dynamic trust formation and the coupling relationship between decision chain length and risk preference.
Future Research: Future research could explore the applicability of different AI models and algorithms across organizations with varying lean construction maturity levels. Additionally, research should investigate how organizational culture, leadership styles, and employees' attitudes toward technology adoption interact with AI to influence trust and collaboration in lean construction.
Results: AI-assisted decision-making significantly enhances dynamic trust in lean construction, with risk preference and task transfer resilience serving as partial mediators. Shorter decision chains amplify these mediating effects, while longer chains strengthen the direct impact of AI on trust but weaken the mediating pathways. As decision chain length increases, task transfer resilience's influence on trust diminishes more slowly than risk preference.
AI Model Used: No specific AI model is used in the SEM analysis. The paper discusses AI applications conceptually, including machine learning, computer vision, natural language processing, optimization algorithms, IoT sensors, reinforcement learning, federated learning, digital twins, and hybrid LSTM-GAN models for geological hazard prediction in the case study.
Data Types: The paper uses primary survey data collected from 293 lean construction professionals across 23 representative lean construction projects in China, including residential, commercial, and infrastructure construction. Data collection occurred from February 1 to May 15, 2024, with respondents having direct involvement in lean construction projects and experience with AI-assisted decision-making technologies.

Title: Z -Number-Based Quantitative Expression of Activity Information in Uncertain Project Scheduling
Abstract: Uncertainty is the general state of modern project scheduling, and fuzzy numbers are the main theme to resolve uncertain project scheduling problems. Traditional fuzzy numbers can represent uncertain information values of construction projects, but cannot reflect the accuracy and reliability of such information. The Z-number can express the uncertain construction project information reasonably via the first component, and use the second component to describe objectively the accuracy and reliability of information values, thus effectively overcoming the existing disadvantages of traditional fuzzy numbers. This study used historical statistical data and expert opinions to construct the Z-number, which effectively overcomes the drawbacks of conventional fuzzy numbers in disposing the uncertain activity information. A Z-number quantization scheme based on the fuzzy expected value and agreement index algorithm effectively eliminates a large amount of information loss caused by the quantization or defuzzification of the fuzzy number, and achieves the evolution and optimization from Z-numbers to AI fuzzy numbers. The objectivity of the research was ensured by fully considering the probability of completion of the activity and the risk preference of the decision makers. Numerical experimental results verified the effectiveness of the proposed method. Taken together, this study provides a new framework for exploring fuzzy logic-based solutions to the uncertain project scheduling problem, and fills the gap of Z-number-based quantitative expression of activity information in uncertain project scheduling.
Authors: Liu, D.; Hu, C.; Guo, S.; Yu, J.
Journal: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Construction Application Area: Uncertain project scheduling, activity duration and resource estimation
Methodology: The paper proposes a Z-number-based quantitative expression system for activity information in uncertain project scheduling. Z-numbers are constructed using historical statistical data and expert opinions, then converted to conventional generalized trapezoidal fuzzy numbers (GTFNs) through a quantization scheme based on fuzzy expected value and agreement index algorithm. The methodology includes converting Z-numbers to GTFNs and using parameterized generalized trapezoidal fuzzy numbers with agreement index for activity information estimation.
Research Goal: The goal is to provide a new framework for expressing uncertain activity information in project scheduling that overcomes the limitations of traditional fuzzy numbers by using Z-numbers to represent both the uncertain information values and their reliability/accuracy. This aims to improve the objectivity and rationality of uncertain project scheduling.
Gaps Addressed: Traditional fuzzy numbers can represent uncertain information values but cannot reflect the accuracy and reliability of such information, leading to subjective and inaccurate project scheduling. The paper addresses this gap by using Z-numbers where the first component represents activity information and the second component describes the reliability and accuracy of that information.
Future Research: Future research should introduce programming languages such as Python and Java to develop an intelligent project information processing system to reduce operational difficulty and improve work efficiency. The scope needs to be expanded beyond theoretical applications to address limitations in applicable objects and computational efficiency.
Results: The proposed Z-number quantization scheme effectively eliminates information loss caused by fuzzy number quantization and achieves evolution from Z-numbers to AI fuzzy numbers. Numerical experiments verified the effectiveness of the method, showing that project duration and critical paths change dynamically with different α values, demonstrating that the Z-number construction and normalization are simple in reasoning while avoiding large information loss.
AI Model Used: No Model
Data Types: The paper uses historical statistical data from existing similar construction projects (activity durations, resource demands such as concrete volume) and expert opinions expressed as linguistic variables that are converted to fuzzy numbers using predefined measurement specifications.

Title: Autonomous intelligent control of earth pressure balance shield machine based on deep reinforcement learning
Abstract: In order to reduce the construction risk caused by human operation error and improve the geological adaptive ability of the shield machine, an autonomous intelligent control method is proposed for shield machine within the framework of interaction–judgment–decision based on Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) deep reinforcement learning in this study. Due to the strong nonlinear relationship between the shield machine's tunneling parameters, this research builds a deep reinforcement learning environment using mechanism model of sealed cabin pressure. DDPG agent model of the shield machine is established to replace the shield machine to interact and train with the geological environment. By minimizing the difference between the target pressure setting value and the sealed cabin pressure value, the dynamic balance between the sealed cabin pressure and the pressure on the excavation surface is realized, and the best strategy is obtained. Through real-time interaction with the geological environment, the method in this paper can dynamically adjust the tunneling parameters, accurately control the sealed cabin pressure, and has a strong geological adaptive ability. By realizing the intelligent decision-making of the tunneling parameters, it greatly improves the independent decision-making ability of the shield machine system, reduces the inaccuracy of human operation, and provides an effective guarantee for the efficient and safe operation of the shield machine. This study applies deep reinforcement learning technology to the control field of earth pressure balance shield machine, promotes AI technology, and provides a new idea for the development of AI construction technology in engineering field.
Authors: Liu, X.; Zhang, W.; Shao, C.; Wang, Y.; Cong, Q.
Construction Application Area: Shield machine tunneling control, specifically earth pressure balance control in the sealed cabin during tunnel boring operations
Methodology: The paper develops a deep reinforcement learning environment using a mechanism model of sealed cabin pressure. A DDPG (Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient) agent is trained to replace the shield machine operator by interacting with the geological environment. The agent learns optimal tunneling parameters (propulsion speed, screw conveyor speed, cutterhead speed) through continuous interaction to minimize the difference between target pressure and actual sealed cabin pressure.
Research Goal: To develop an autonomous intelligent control method for earth pressure balance shield machines that can dynamically adjust tunneling parameters in real-time, accurately control sealed cabin pressure, and improve geological adaptive ability while reducing construction risks caused by human operation errors.
Gaps Addressed: Traditional control methods based on mechanism models cannot interact with the geological environment in real-time and have poor geological adaptability. Data-driven methods rely too heavily on manually labeled training samples and cannot dynamically adjust tunneling parameters or handle uncertain events, limiting their autonomous decision-making capabilities.
Future Research: Future research should study the random mutation process of different geological conditions during shield tunneling to further enhance the independent decision-making ability of the shield machine and successfully complete the whole construction process under varying geological conditions.
Results: The DDPG agent autonomous control model achieved absolute error control between 0.006-0.007 MPa (less than the expected error of 0.01 MPa) between sealed cabin pressure and target pressure setting value. The method demonstrated superior performance compared to MPC, XGBoost, LSTM, and PPO control methods, with better stability and adaptability across different soil conditions (clay, silt, residual soil layer, fully weathered rock).
AI Model Used: Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG), Deep Q-learning (DQN), Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Gate Recurrent Unit (GRU), XGBoost, Support Vector Machine (SVM), Extreme Learning Machine (ELM)
Data Types: The study uses actual construction data from Beijing Metro Line 10, including tunneling parameters (propulsion speed, screw conveyor speed, cutterhead speed), sealed cabin earth pressure values, geological conditions (tunnel depth 12.6m, water level 7.1m, soil types including silty clay, silt, soft stone, fine sand), and simulated data from the mechanism model of sealed cabin pressure built in MATLAB-Simulink.

Title: Autonomous collaborative optimization control of earth pressure balance shield machine based on hierarchical control architecture
Abstract: Due to manual operations are unable to adjust parameters promptly to adapt to continuously changing geological conditions, which can easily lead to safety accidents. Therefore, this study aims to solve the real-time dynamic optimization problem of shield machines and achieve autonomous optimal control. Based on cyber-physical system (CPS), this paper proposes a Twin Double Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient-Model Predictive Control (TD3-MPC) hierarchical autonomous control scheme, which is divided into coordination level and execution level. Based on Twin Double Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3) algorithm, TD3 agent is designed. A virtual tunnel environment has been designed based on sealed cabin pressure mechanism model. TD3 agent and virtual environment are used as coordination level. A dynamic state-space model for sealed cabin pressure is established, incorporating shield machine dynamics model and mechanism of sealed cabin pressure. On this basis, model predictive controller is designed as execution level. TD3 agent interacts with virtual tunneling environment based on geological information parameters of real-time sampling. An optimal sealed cabin pressure curve under current sampling interval is found and transmitted to executive level as the control target. Execution level solves the quadratic programming through real-time feedback of sealed cabin pressure and continuous rolling optimizes screw conveyor speed and propulsion speed to achieve accurate tracking of pressure controlling target. Simulated results demonstrated that this control approach has a good pressure control effect and strong soil adaptive ability. From multiple perspectives, viability and efficiency are further verified for the proposed controlling scheme. This paper further promotes the application of AI technology in underground construction.
Authors: Liu, X.; Zhang, W.; Shao, C.; Wang, Y.; Cong, Q.; Ma, L.
Construction Application Area: Shield machine tunneling control, specifically sealed cabin pressure control and earth pressure balance during underground tunnel construction
Methodology: The paper proposes a hierarchical autonomous control scheme combining Twin Double Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3) and Model Predictive Control (MPC). The coordination level uses TD3 agent interacting with a virtual tunneling environment to find optimal sealed cabin pressure curves, while the execution level employs MPC to optimize screw conveyor speed and propulsion speed for tracking control.
Research Goal: To solve the real-time dynamic optimization problem of shield machines and achieve autonomous optimal control of sealed cabin pressure by addressing the limitations of manual operations that cannot promptly adjust parameters to adapt to continuously changing geological conditions.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the inefficiency of manual control modes that respond poorly to sudden geological changes, the poor geological adaptability of existing control methods, and the strategy fluctuation problem when single intelligent end-to-end control schemes complete soil transition tasks.
Future Research: Future research should focus on optimizing communication performance between shield machine subsystems based on intelligent methods. Additionally, establishing more accurate 'black box' models of excavation environments considering actual construction parameters, and developing multi-agent control systems for more tunneling parameters should be explored.
Results: The simulation results demonstrated that the TD3-MPC hierarchical control approach achieved good pressure control effect with strong soil adaptive ability, completing soil transition tasks with smaller pressure fluctuation ranges and better stability compared to other methods including DDPG, PPO, LSTM, and standalone MPC.
AI Model Used: Twin Double Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3), Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG), Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Gate Recurrent Unit (GRU)
Data Types: The paper uses construction data from Beijing Metro Line 10, including geological parameters (cohesive force, effective gravity, angle of internal friction, burial depth, groundwater level), tunneling parameters (screw conveyor speed, propulsion speed), and sealed cabin pressure measurements from actual shield machine operations.

Title: Geotechnical risk modeling using an explainable transfer learning model incorporating physical guidance
Abstract: While Artificial intelligence (AI) has been successfully applied in assessing geotechnical risk, such methods heavily rely on data quality to achieve satisfactory performance, and their results hardly can be interpreted due to their opaque design. With this in mind, this paper aims to address the following research gap: How can we accurately model geotechnical risks using limited data and domain knowledge, and efficiently explain the results of AI model? We develop a physics-guided transfer learning (PGTL) model to enhance the explainability and accuracy of geotechnical risk modeling. With the help of a physical model that simulates the tunnel excavation, a physics-guided dataset with 1000 samples is established and used to train a deep neural network. On these bases, transfer learning is adopted to fuse the features of physics mechanisms and monitoring data, constructing an explainable prediction model of geotechnical risk. To further support risk decision-making, feature relevance techniques are employed to assess the contribution of input parameters to risk. A shield tunnel construction in Wuhan is selected as a case to validate the effectiveness of the proposed method. The PGTL exhibits a more promising accuracy with R2 of 0.777 in contrast to three popular machine learning approaches, and provides insights into parameters that significantly induce risk, enhancing site managers' understanding of tunnel construction and being conducive to tunnel safety.
Authors: Liu, F.; Liu, W.; Li, A.; Cheng, J.C.P.
Construction Application Area: Geotechnical risk modeling, tunnel construction safety, ground settlement prediction
Methodology: The paper develops a physics-guided transfer learning (PGTL) model that combines finite element modeling (FEM) with deep neural networks. First, FEM simulates tunnel excavation to generate a physics-guided dataset of 1000 samples. Then, a DNN is pre-trained on this dataset and fine-tuned using limited monitoring data through transfer learning. Global sensitivity analysis (Sobol, Morris, Borgonovo methods) is applied for post-hoc explainability.
Research Goal: To accurately model geotechnical risks (ground settlement) using limited data and domain knowledge, while providing explainable AI predictions that enhance understanding of tunnel construction safety and support risk decision-making.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses two key gaps: (1) AI models' heavy reliance on high-quality data for satisfactory performance in geotechnical risk assessment, and (2) the lack of interpretability in AI models which undermines trust in using them for safety decisions in tunnel construction.
Future Research: Future work should focus on performing fine geotechnical tests and selecting more accurate soil constitutive models based on experimental results. Additionally, improving the scalability and generalizability of PGTL by building FEMs for multiple geological conditions and using clustering to automate the selection of appropriate physics-guided datasets.
Results: PGTL achieved superior predictive accuracy with R² of 0.777, RMSE of 1.925, MAE of 1.487, MAPE of 41.4%, and KGE of 0.693, outperforming baseline methods (DNN, RF, SVR). Global sensitivity analysis identified grout pressure, burial depth, and modulus of deformation as the most significant parameters affecting ground settlement.
AI Model Used: Deep Neural Network (DNN), Transfer Learning, Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Regression (SVR), Improved Gray Wolf Optimizer (IGWO)
Data Types: The data includes a physics-guided dataset of 1000 samples generated from finite element modeling, and 300 monitoring data samples from the Sanyang Road Highway-railway Tunnel in Wuhan. Input parameters include geological parameters (modulus of deformation, cohesive force, friction angle, Poisson ratio, volumetric weight), mechanical parameters (grout pressure, tunnel face pressure, jack force), and tunnel design parameters (burial depth).

Title: State-of-the-art review on the use of AI-enhanced computational mechanics in geotechnical engineering
Abstract: Significant uncertainties can be found in the modelling of geotechnical materials. This can be attributed to the complex behaviour of soils and rocks amidst construction processes. Over the past decades, the field has increasingly embraced the application of artificial intelligence methodologies, thus recognising their suitability in forecasting non-linear relationships intrinsic to materials. This review offers a critical evaluation AI methodologies incorporated in computational mechanics for geotechnical engineering. The analysis categorises four pivotal areas: physical properties, mechanical properties, constitutive models, and other characteristics relevant to geotechnical materials. Among the various methodologies analysed, ANNs stand out as the most commonly used strategy, while other methods such as SVMs, LSTMs, and CNNs also see a significant level of application. The most widely used AI algorithms are Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), Random Forest (RF), and Support Vector Machines (SVM), representing 35%, 19%, and 17% respectively. The most extensive AI application is in the domain of mechanical properties, accounting for 59%, followed by other applications at 16%. The efficacy of AI applications is intrinsically linked to the type of datasets employed, the selected model input. This study also outlines future research directions emphasising the need to integrate physically guided and adaptive learning mechanisms to enhance the reliability and adaptability in addressing multi-scale and multi-physics coupled mechanics problems in geotechnics.
Authors: Liu, H.; Su, H.; Sun, L.; Dias-da-Costa, D.
Construction Application Area: Geotechnical engineering, including physical properties, mechanical properties, constitutive models, and other characteristics (compression, consolidation, permeability, liquefaction) of geotechnical materials
Research Method: Systematic Literature Review and Bibliometric Analysis
Methodology: The paper conducts a systematic literature review (SLR) and bibliometric analysis using the Web of Science Core Collection database, covering papers from 2012 to 2022. It employs CiteSpace software for co-citation analysis and keyword clustering to identify research trends and applications of AI in geotechnical engineering computational mechanics.
Research Goal: To provide a comprehensive evaluation of AI methodologies incorporated in computational mechanics for geotechnical engineering, categorizing applications into four pivotal areas: physical properties, mechanical properties, constitutive models, and other characteristics relevant to geotechnical materials.
Gaps Addressed: Current research predominantly focuses on traditional AI techniques with limited exploration of advanced methods like deep learning and reinforcement learning. There is also a need for integrating AI models with physically-based principles to enhance reliability and interpretability in practical applications.
Future Research: Future research should focus on integrating physically guided and adaptive learning mechanisms to improve reliability and adaptability in addressing multi-scale and multi-physics coupled mechanics problems in geotechnics. Research should also explore advanced AI algorithms like transfer learning and meta-learning to handle out-of-range data and improve model generalization under uncertain conditions.
Results: The most widely used AI algorithms are Artificial Neural Networks (ANN, 35%), Random Forest (RF, 19%), and Support Vector Machines (SVM, 17%). The most extensive AI application is in the domain of mechanical properties (59%), followed by other applications (16%), physical properties (14%), and constitutive models (11%).
AI Model Used: ANN, CNN, RNN, LSTM, GAN, SVM, Random Forest, Bayesian Networks, and various hybrid models combining these approaches with optimization algorithms
Data Types: The paper discusses various data types used in geotechnical AI applications, including experimental data (laboratory tests like triaxial tests, direct shear tests), field measurement data, CT scan images, numerical simulation data, and sensor monitoring data for properties like particle size distribution, stress-strain relationships, strength parameters, and consolidation characteristics.

Title: A LLM-informed multi-agent AI system for drone-based visual inspection for infrastructure
Abstract: The development of large language models (LLMs) holds the potential to significantly advance automation in the Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AEC) industry. This paper explores the application of LLM-powered agent systems in drone-based visual inspection, focusing on three core aspects. First, a multi-agent framework is proposed, composed of five specialized sub-agents—Router, PathPlanner, Controller, Perceptioner, and Retriever—that collaboratively handle inspection subtasks such as 3D spatial reasoning, path planning, and UAV control. Second, a novel image-based pipeline is introduced to generate multi-criteria semantic point clouds and abstract them into 3D Scene Graphs (3DSGs), enabling spatial-semantic reasoning aligned with human intent. These 3DSGs act as both knowledge storage and reasoning engines. Third, the system is evaluated through simulations and laboratory experiments, demonstrating its ability to automate inspection workflows and provide a foundation for extensible AI-agent systems. The results highlight the advantages of LLM-based agents in flexible task delegation and high-level decision-making. While current implementations rely on general-purpose LLMs accessed via commercial APIs—introducing some latency and adaptation gaps—these aspects also point to promising directions for future optimization and domain-specific enhancement. Overall, the study presents an early yet promising step toward collaborative human–machine intelligence in infrastructure inspection, where autonomous agents augment human decision-making through interactive, context-aware support.
Authors: Liu, J.; Li, H.; Chai, C.; Chen, K.; Wang, D.
Construction Application Area: Infrastructure inspection, drone-based visual inspection, structural condition assessment, bridge inspection, building inspection
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (BIM, drones, laser scanning, robotics, digital twins, sensor networks)
Methodology: The paper develops a multi-agent AI system powered by LLMs for drone-based visual inspection, integrating five specialized sub-agents (Router, PathPlanner, Controller, Perceptioner, and Retriever). It introduces 3D Scene Graphs (3DSGs) for spatial-semantic reasoning and proposes an image-based pipeline to generate multi-criteria semantic point clouds. The system is validated through simulation experiments in Unreal Engine with AirSim and laboratory experiments using a DJI Tello drone.
Research Goal: To develop an LLM-informed multi-agent AI system that enhances automation in drone-based infrastructure visual inspection by enabling dynamic human-machine collaboration, 3D spatial reasoning, and intelligent task planning and execution.
Gaps Addressed: Current drone-based inspection systems rely heavily on human control or fully automated deep learning approaches with limited flexibility. There is a lack of meaningful human-machine collaboration, limited application of LLM-based agents in AEC robotics, and challenges in enabling LLMs to perceive and reason about 3D spatial data for large-scale infrastructure inspection.
Future Research: Future work will focus on real-time 3DSG construction methods, deploying lightweight or fine-tuned LLMs on UAV hardware to reduce latency, enhancing agent memory and modular architecture, integrating higher-resolution imaging systems with precise control capabilities, and improving perception module adaptability to domain-specific conditions for practical infrastructure inspection.
Results: The system successfully demonstrated automated inspection workflows in both simulation and laboratory environments. GPT-4o and DeepSeek-V3 showed strong performance across four core inspection tasks. The multi-agent system effectively performed information retrieval, path planning, drone control, and image analysis tasks, with GPT-4o achieving 100% success rate on all test requirements in simulation experiments.
AI Model Used: GPT-4o, GPT-4o mini, GPT-3.5 Turbo, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Claude 3.5 Haiku, DeepSeek-V3, Mask-RCNN, CLIP, LLaVA, InstructBLIP, SemanticSAM
Data Types: The paper uses RGB images, depth maps, point clouds, and thermal images collected from drones. Data includes multi-criteria semantic segmentation masks (structure-level, component-level, and damage-level), 3D reconstructed point clouds from Structure-from-Motion (SfM) and RGB-D cameras, and synthetic corrosion damage textures applied to simulated infrastructure models.

Title: Advances in artificial intelligence and digital twin for tunnel boring machines
Abstract: The deep integration of artificial intelligence (AI) and tunnel boring machine (TBM) has emerged as a critical research direction in intelligent tunnel construction. However, variable geological conditions, numerous electromechanical systems, and complex rock-machine interactions pose significant challenges to its implementation. This paper systematically reviews the latest research advances of AI in the TBM field, focusing on key technologies such as environmental perception, automated control, and predictive health management. Additionally, an intelligent TBM system architecture based on Digital Twin (DT) technology is proposed. The architecture integrates and coordinates a perception layer, an analysis layer, a decision-making layer, and an execution layer, enabling full-process autonomous control from environmental perception to intelligent decision-making. Finally, several critical issues and prospects in developing intelligent TBM, including data integration, model interpretability, and computational efficiency are discussed. This study aims to provide theoretical references and practical guidance for researchers in related fields, further promoting the in-depth application and development of digital twin technology in the intelligent TBM domain.
Authors: Liu, G.; Tang, Y.; Zhang, H.; Li, R.; Wang, H.; Liu, B.; Zhang, S.; Zhu, H.; Liu, D.; Ma, S.
Construction Application Area: Tunnel boring machine (TBM) operations including environmental perception (geological conditions, rockburst prediction, surface settlement), automation control (advance rate, attitude, energy consumption), and prognostics and health management (disc cutter wear, bearing faults)
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (BIM, drones, laser scanning, robotics, digital twins, sensor networks)
Methodology: This is a systematic literature review and bibliometric analysis paper that analyzed 337 publications from Web of Science and Scopus databases (2013-2025) using VOSviewer visualization software. The paper reviews AI applications in intelligent TBM across three main domains: environmental perception, automated control, and prognostics health management. It also proposes a novel digital twin-based intelligent TBM framework with four layers (perception, analysis, decision-making, and execution) and validates it on a laboratory testing platform.
Research Goal: The paper aims to systematically review the latest research advances of AI and digital twin technology in intelligent TBM operations, identify research hotspots and evolution trajectories through bibliometric analysis, and propose a comprehensive digital twin-based intelligent TBM system framework that integrates environmental perception, automated control, and health management for full-process autonomous control.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses three main research gaps: (1) existing studies focus on isolated technological breakthroughs rather than cross-modal fusion and system integration of AI-DT collaborative systems, (2) core challenges including multi-modal data fusion, real-time bidirectional mapping, and model self-adaptive updating under dynamic conditions remain unresolved, and (3) significant gaps exist in effectively integrating intelligent perception, autonomous control, and equipment health management into a high-efficiency intelligent TBM system.
Future Research: Future research should focus on three main directions: (1) developing dynamic fusion frameworks for multimodal heterogeneous data with physics-informed neural networks, (2) enhancing model interpretability through physics-embedded hybrid models and spatiotemporal decoupling, and (3) improving computational efficiency via lightweight edge-oriented architectures, quantum-inspired optimization, and RL-MPC co-design with hardware acceleration. Additionally, research should establish a four-layer enhancement framework integrating perception, analysis, decision, and execution layers with engineering semantic comprehension capabilities.
Results: The bibliometric analysis reveals exponential growth in publications and citations from 2021-2024, with China leading in publication volume (188 papers). The review demonstrates that AI technologies, particularly machine learning and deep learning, have achieved significant breakthroughs in TBM environmental perception (geological condition prediction with R²>0.98), automated control (comprehensive optimization rate >25%, disc cutter wear optimization 40.6%), and health management (bearing fault diagnosis accuracy 98.46%). However, challenges remain in data quality, model interpretability, and computational efficiency.
AI Model Used: Multiple AI models including: CNN, LSTM, RNN, GRU, Transformer, Random Forest, Support Vector Machine (SVM), Decision Trees, Neural Networks (BPNN, ANN), Reinforcement Learning (RL, DRL, MADRL), Attention Mechanisms, Autoencoders, GANs, Graph Neural Networks (GNN), Bayesian Networks, YOLO, ResNet, and various ensemble learning methods
Data Types: Multiple types of heterogeneous data including: (1) geological parameters from geotechnical surveys, (2) TBM operational parameters from sensors (thrust, torque, rotation speed, advance rate, vibration, temperature, pressure), (3) manual data from project logs and field measurements, (4) image data from cameras (muck images, tunnel face images), (5) microseismic monitoring data, (6) cutter wear measurements, (7) bearing vibration signals, and (8) BIM and point cloud data. Data sources include real-time sensor networks, historical construction records, and laboratory testing platforms.

Title: Automated PPE compliance monitoring in industrial environments using deep learning-based detection and pose estimation
Abstract: This paper presents an AI framework for automated detection of personal protective equipment (PPE) compliance in complex construction and industrial environments. Ensuring health and safety standards is essential for protecting workers engaged in construction, repair, or inspection activities. The framework leverages deep learning techniques for worker detection and pose estimation to enable accurate PPE identification under challenging conditions. The framework components are replaceable, and employ the InternImage-L detector for worker detection, ViTPose for pose estimation, and YOLOv7 for PPE recognition. A duplicate removal stage, combined with pose information, ensures PPE items are accurately assigned to individual workers. The approach addresses challenges like shadows, partial occlusions, or densely grouped workers. Evaluated on diverse datasets from real-world industrial settings, the framework achieves competitive precision and recall, particularly for critical PPE like helmets and vests, demonstrating robustness for safety monitoring and proactive risk management.
Authors: López, L.; Suárez-Ramírez, J.; Alemán-Flores, M.; Monzón, N.
Construction Application Area: Safety - specifically Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) compliance monitoring in construction and industrial environments
Methodology: The paper proposes a modular AI framework integrating deep learning for worker detection, pose estimation, and PPE recognition. It uses InternImage-L for worker detection, ViTPose for pose estimation, and YOLOv7 for PPE detection. The framework includes collision detection and duplicate removal stages. Worker-PPE matching is performed by calculating distances from PPE items to pose keypoints, ensuring accurate assignment even in challenging conditions with overlapping workers and occlusions.
Research Goal: The paper aims to develop a robust automated framework for real-time monitoring of PPE compliance in construction sites and shipyards using advanced computer vision and deep learning techniques, capable of handling challenging conditions like occlusions, shadows, varying distances, and densely grouped workers.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses limitations in existing PPE detection methods, particularly struggles with small PPE items, distant workers, cluttered environments with worker overlaps, variable lighting conditions, occlusions, and accurate worker-PPE matching in complex scenarios. It also addresses the dependency on perfect pose estimation in prior works and introduces collision detection and duplicate removal mechanisms.
Future Research: The paper suggests future work should focus on real-time alert mechanisms, compliance reporting, and risk analytics to support automated safety monitoring in high-risk industrial settings. The framework's modularity allows for future improvements by replacing components with more advanced alternatives.
Results: The framework achieves competitive precision and recall on diverse datasets, particularly for helmets (93.16% precision, 73.06% recall) and vests (82.27% precision, 68.84% recall) in the Zamakona evaluation dataset. It outperforms state-of-the-art methods (Nath and Xiong approaches) across multiple metrics, demonstrating robustness in challenging real-world construction environments with overlapping workers, occlusions, and varied lighting conditions.
AI Model Used: InternImage-L (worker detection), ViTPose (pose estimation), YOLOv7 (PPE detection). The paper also evaluated alternative models including DDETR, DINO, DDQ for detection and Hrnet, SimCC for pose estimation.
Data Types: High-resolution 1080p images from shipyards (Repnaval Zamakona dataset with 227 images containing 1277 workers) and publicly available PPE datasets from construction sites. The data includes challenging scenarios with occlusions, shadows, variable lighting, different camera distances, and densely populated work areas with multiple overlapping workers.

Title: AI-based multi-layer framework for monitoring productivity and coordinating subcontractors in construction projects
Abstract: The construction industry continues to face significant challenges in productivity management, subcontractor coordination and schedule adherence. Traditional monitoring methods are labor-intensive, error-prone and often fail to support proactive decision-making. This study proposes an integrated, AI-based framework to address these issues through a combination of real-time activity recognition, delay prediction and strategic subcontractor management. The proposed three-layer framework integrates: (1) a CNN-YOLOv8 model for automated detection and classification of on-site activities, workers and machinery, (2) a Bayesian Network for probabilistic modeling of productivity and delay risks based on real-time and contextual factors and (3) a Game Theory-based model to optimize subcontractor bidding strategies, resource allocation and incentive distribution using Nash Equilibrium dynamics. The proposed integrated system was evaluated using a simulated construction project, demonstrating a 50% reduction in project delays, a 13.3% increase in productivity and improved fairness in resource allocation among subcontractors.
