Statistical metrics and visualizations confirmed model effectiveness in real-world conditions. This study contributes a novel integration of AI-based visual monitoring, probabilistic forecasting and strategic modeling to support real-time, data-driven construction management. The approach enhances operational efficiency, reduces cost and promotes fair subcontractor collaboration—offering practical value for project managers in complex construction environments.
Authors: Malekaee Ashtiyani, F.; Karimi-Gavareshki, M.H.; Gheidar-Kheljani, J.
Construction Application Area: Construction productivity monitoring, delay prediction, subcontractor coordination, resource allocation, activity recognition, worker and machinery detection
Methodology: The paper proposes a three-layer AI-based framework integrating: (1) CNN-YOLOv8 for real-time detection and classification of construction activities, workers, and machinery from site imagery; (2) Bayesian Networks to model dependencies among project variables and predict productivity levels and delay risks; and (3) Game Theory to simulate subcontractor behavior, optimize bidding strategies, and adjust resource allocation based on real-time performance data. The framework operates in a continuous feedback loop where visual monitoring outputs feed into probabilistic prediction models, which then guide dynamic strategy selection.
Research Goal: The goal is to develop an integrated, AI-driven framework that combines real-time activity recognition, delay prediction, and strategic subcontractor management to enhance construction project efficiency, reduce delays, improve productivity, and promote fair subcontractor collaboration in complex construction environments.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of integration between AI-based monitoring tools and downstream planning or coordination models. Existing studies treat computer vision detection, Bayesian delay prediction, and game-theoretic subcontractor modeling as isolated tasks rather than as components of a unified decision-support system that links real-time monitoring with predictive analytics and strategic collaboration optimization.
Future Research: Future research should explore integration with IoT and sensor networks for multimodal input, use of blockchain-based smart contracts to automate payments and penalties, scalability testing across diverse project types and environments, incorporation of behavioral economics into the game theory layer, and connection with digital twin systems for live synchronization of physical and virtual construction progress.
Results: The framework achieved a 50% reduction in project delays (from 20 to 10 days), a 13.3% increase in productivity (from 60% to 68%), and improved fairness in resource allocation. The CNN-ResNet50 model achieved 90% classification accuracy, YOLOv8 achieved 85% mAP@50 for object detection, and the Bayesian Network achieved a log-likelihood score of -2.28, demonstrating strong alignment with real-world outcomes.
AI Model Used: CNN (ResNet50), YOLOv8, Bayesian Networks, Game Theory (Nash Equilibrium, Best Response Dynamics)
Data Types: The study used 1,030 daily site images collected over 10 days from a 48,000 m³ water reservoir construction project in Iran, representing 10 different construction activity types. Data augmentation expanded the dataset to 11,069 images. The dataset also included expert-elicited conditional probability tables for Bayesian Networks and simulated performance data (bids, productivity levels, delays, resource allocations) for 10 subcontractors over 10 rounds in the game theory module.

Title: Exploring Large Language Model AI tools in Construction Project Risk Assessment: Chat GPT Limitations in Risk Identification, Mitigation Strategies, and User Experience
Abstract: The last 3 years have witnessed an increasing awareness and consensus on using artificial intelligence (AI) to enhance decision-making in the construction sector. This study explores the integration of ChatGPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer) into traditional risk management frameworks within the construction industry, contributing to the ongoing discourse on AI's role in enhancing risk identification, analysis, and mitigation. Using a mixed-method approach comparing ChatGPT-assisted to human evaluations, interviews, and a case study, the research develops a better understanding of construction risk analysis processes and discusses decision-making errors of omission, over- and underestimation of probabilities and impacts, and treatment of the residual risk after proposed mitigation strategies. Results suggest that users' experience of ChatGPT is primarily favorable, characterized by quick responses and an intuitive interface that enhances decision-making efficiency. Findings indicate that GPT may be especially beneficial for less experienced practitioners since it provides comprehensive risk awareness. However, experienced professionals contend that the software lacks contextual depth. The study contributes a ChatGPT-4 prompt to evaluate infrastructure risk for a given project scope. An evidenced case study on a road upgrade project in Ireland demonstrates a lessened dependence on the quality of user prompting skills and emphasizes the quality of project scope data input. A collaborative approach, including Chat-GPT early involvement and human refinement, promises to enhance conventional risk management speed and efficiency and reduce bias and inflexibility while maintaining the adaptability and ethical rigour required in the industry's evolving risk landscape.
Authors: Martin, H.; James, J.; Chadee, A.
Journal: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Construction Application Area: Risk identification, risk analysis, risk mitigation strategies, project risk management
Methodology: The study employed a concurrent triangulation mixed-methods design combining quantitative and qualitative data. It involved three participant groups: Group 1 used ChatGPT for risk assessment, Group 2 used traditional methods, and Group 3 (expert evaluators) assessed both approaches. Data collection included structured risk management tasks, performance metrics (time efficiency, accuracy), questionnaires with 5-point Likert scales, and semi-structured interviews. A case study on an Irish road upgrade project validated a developed ChatGPT prompt.
Research Goal: The study explores the integration of ChatGPT into traditional risk management frameworks within the construction industry to evaluate AI's effectiveness in enhancing risk identification, analysis, and mitigation. It aims to develop a better understanding of construction risk analysis processes and provide practical guidance through a ChatGPT-4 prompt for infrastructure risk evaluation.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses gaps in understanding how AI tools like ChatGPT can be integrated into practical construction risk management contexts, including how to systematically evaluate residual risks and their treatment, and how to develop effective prompts to support practitioners in implementing AI tools. Previous studies lacked consideration of residual risks, practical implementation guidance, and integration with existing risk management frameworks.
Future Research: Future research should focus on longitudinal studies with larger and more diverse samples, real-world applications to better understand AI and human expertise complementarity, and refinement of the developed prompt to improve AI's capabilities with project-specific data. Additional investigations are needed into comparative evaluation of different generative AI models, understanding how ChatGPT accounts for cognitive biases, and developing scalable strategies for integrating AI into traditional risk management methodologies.
Results: Results suggest that ChatGPT's user experience is primarily favorable with quick responses and intuitive interface, though it may be especially beneficial for less experienced practitioners by providing comprehensive risk awareness while experienced professionals found it lacking contextual depth. ChatGPT demonstrated marked time efficiency (36 min vs. 157 min for traditional methods) but produced more general outputs lacking project-specific detail compared to expert-driven evaluations. A collaborative approach combining ChatGPT early involvement with human refinement promises to enhance conventional risk management speed and efficiency.
AI Model Used: ChatGPT-4 (GPT-4)
Data Types: The paper used mixed data types including quantitative performance metrics (task completion times, risk identification accuracy scores, Likert scale questionnaire responses), qualitative data from semi-structured interviews and open-ended questions, and document data from a real infrastructure project case study (N2 Ardee to Castleblayney Road Upgrade Project in Ireland, including project scope documents and option selection reports).

Title: Proximity Activity Intensity Identification System in Hot and Humid Weather Conditions: Development and Implementation
Abstract: Construction workers are exposed to heat stress risks due to the combined effects of hot and humid weather conditions (HHWCs) and physically demanding work. A real-time activity intensity identification (AII) is required to measure the impact of HHWCs using a nonintrusive approach. This research developed a real-time AII system based on computer vision analysis (CVA). It then combined the CVA system with real-time video recordings to approximate workers' activity intensity (AI) levels alongside HHWC records. A fundamental activities matrix was developed to build a list of measurable and identifiable features of site activities. These features were used to identify and link different postures to a crew's AI and safety status within a given context. In real-site conditions, the AII system instantly and unobtrusively approximated workers' AI and safety status under HHWCs. The system showed high detection performance with competitive deployment time, cost, and effort, outperforming previous related models. The results showed that formwork and steelwork are mostly moderate activities; however, moderate AI and HHWCs can create heat stress and fatigue and significantly affect workers' safety, resulting in heat-related injuries and accidents. This research gives researchers and practitioners insight into the challenges associated with measurement methods and solving practical site measurement issues. This research promotes innovative methods for real-site measurements and contributes to knowledge in the field of safety and productivity in the construction industry by employing new, innovative CVA technology. This technology has applications in the industry by deploying a practical tool that could support aligned improvement in the safety and productivity of construction workers working under HHWCs. Practical Applications The challenges of automated and real-time measurements have always been of great interest to construction safety and productivity practitioners, particularly measurements of nonintrusive systems and competitive deployment time, cost, and effort. Such problems have also resulted in a substantial delay in making safety- and productivity-related decisions, which are major reasons for the increasing number of hot and humid weather-related injuries and incidents, particularly with the growing threat of global warming. Furthermore, under HHWCs, construction companies have also incurred significant productivity losses. This study offers an automated, nonintrusive, real-time measuring system with competitive deployment time, cost, and effort to monitor activity intensity and weather-related risks. Hence, site decision makers can make timely safety- and productivity-related decisions to improve work safety and productivity.
Authors: Moohialdin, A.S.M.; Lamari, F.; Miska, M.; Trigunarsyah, B.
Journal: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Construction Application Area: Safety monitoring, specifically activity intensity identification and heat stress risk assessment under hot and humid weather conditions
Methodology: The paper developed a real-time activity intensity identification (AII) system using computer vision analysis (CVA) based on TensorFlow. The system detects PPE (helmets and vests), workers, and nine different postures through three CVA layers. It synchronizes video recordings with weather measurements (WBGT) to approximate workers' activity intensity levels and safety status in real-time at the crew level.
Research Goal: To develop a nonintrusive, real-time activity intensity identification system that measures the combined impact of hot and humid weather conditions (HHWCs) and physically demanding work on construction workers' health, safety, and productivity.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of nonintrusive, real-time site measurements for detecting potential risks associated with activity intensity and HHWCs, which has significantly impeded timely implementation of necessary preventions. Current practices fail to provide nonintrusive assessments or interconnect AI assessments with HHWCs in real time.
Future Research: Ongoing research is directed toward expanding the AII system to include engagement with materials, tools, and equipment to approximate AII, which has high potential to be used in real-time crew productivity measurements. The system can also be extended to include a broader range of activities and cold weather conditions for worldwide application at other sites.
Results: The system achieved high detection performance with identification rates (IR) up to 97.15% and recall rates (RR) up to 99.81%. Results showed that formwork and steelwork are mostly moderate activities (86% of work time), but moderate AI combined with HHWCs can create heat stress and significantly affect workers' safety, resulting in heat-related injuries and accidents.
AI Model Used: TensorFlow Object Detection System with Faster R-CNN (region-based convolutional neural network) and TensorFlow Posenet algorithm for posture detection
Data Types: Real-time video recordings from IP cameras (94,808 frames at 10 fps, 720p resolution), weather measurements (temperature, relative humidity, WBGT) collected every 60 seconds, and detection data including worker counts, PPE status, postures, and transaction counts between postures from two construction activities (formwork and steelwork) over 4 days.

Title: Large-Language model (LLM)-Powered system for Situated and Game-Based construction safety training
Abstract: Construction sites are inherently hazardous, with worker behavior being a major contributor to accidents. While safety training is crucial for reducing unsafe actions, traditional methods often lack situational learning, overload workers, use one-size-fits-all approaches, and struggle linking theory to practice. This study proposes an interactive construction safety training framework powered by large language models (LLMs) and built over the foundation theories of situated and game-based learning. The framework has four main components: (1) a knowledge database of verified safety data; (2) an LLM processor generating hazardous scenarios and feedback; (3) situated and game-based elements like challenges, responses, and rewards; and (4) a frontend interface. A proof-of-concept prototype was successfully implemented, showcasing the feasibility and applicability of the platform. The system's outputs were evaluated using an automatic side-by-side evaluation approach. Initial results validate our proposed solution's ability to simulate personalized and realistic hazardous situations that engage safety learners to decide based on their safety understanding and then learn safety regulations through feedback. This platform can enhance training engagement, retention, and real-world safety behaviors. Our solution introduces an extensible template integrating pedagogical theories and AI capabilities to advance contextualized learning beyond construction safety training.
Authors: Naderi, H.; Shojaei, A.
Construction Application Area: Construction safety training
Methodology: The paper adopts a Design Science Research (DSR) methodology with five steps: problem identification, defining solution objectives, framework design and development, proof-of-concept demonstration, and evaluation. The framework integrates Large Language Models (LLMs) with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) architecture, incorporating verified OSHA safety guidelines into a knowledge database. The system generates personalized hazardous scenarios based on worker tasks and provides feedback grounded in safety regulations.
Research Goal: The study aims to develop an interactive construction safety training platform powered by LLMs that provides personalized, contextualized, and game-based learning experiences. It seeks to address limitations of traditional safety training methods by leveraging situated learning and game-based learning theories to enhance worker engagement, knowledge retention, and real-world safety behaviors.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses several gaps in traditional construction safety training: lack of situational and contextualized learning, information overload, one-size-fits-all approaches, limited opportunities for practice and experimentation, and inability to bridge the gap between theoretical knowledge and practical application. It also addresses the limited exploration of LLMs specifically for construction safety training contexts.
Future Research: Future research should expand the knowledge database to cover a wider array of tasks and safety regulations, conduct comprehensive user studies with construction workers to evaluate real-world effectiveness, integrate multimedia elements (videos, images, sounds) into scenarios, explore severity detection mechanisms, incorporate real-time human oversight from safety trainers, and conduct naturalistic summative evaluations measuring actual safety outcomes such as accident reduction rates and behavioral changes.
Results: The proof-of-concept prototype was successfully implemented and evaluated using an automatic side-by-side evaluation method with four LLM judges across seven criteria. GPT-4 outperformed other models (Llama2-7B, GPT-3.5, Llama2-70B) across all criteria including relevance to situated learning, information validation, immersion level, personalization capability, comprehensibility, suitability for adult learning, and prompt accuracy. The system achieved scores above 0.8 in most criteria, validating its feasibility and applicability for generating realistic, personalized safety training scenarios.
AI Model Used: GPT-4, GPT-3.5, Llama2-7B, Llama2-70B, Gemini (for evaluation)
Data Types: The study uses verified safety documentation from OSHA including construction standards, guidelines, and non-construction standards/policies, specifically focusing on high-risk activities such as roof work, wall erection, leading-edge operations, and floor joist installation. The data is converted into embeddings and stored in a vector database for retrieval-augmented generation.

Title: AI-based cognitive framework for evaluating response of concrete structures in extreme conditions
Abstract: In order to highlight the potential of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in the field of engineering for extreme conditions, this study offers insights into developing an AI-based cognitive framework capable of accurately tracing response of concrete structures under elevated temperatures. This framework is utilized to derive simple expressions that allow evaluation of thermal and structural response of reinforced concrete (RC) beams and columns either; at a specific point in time or through tracing time–temperature/deformation history, for up to four hours of fire exposure. The developed AI-based framework successfully comprehends the naturally complex behavior of fire-exposed RC structural members and implicitly takes into account high temperature material properties of concrete and steel reinforcement, as well as associated phenomena; i.e. creep deformation and fire-induced spalling, and thus does not require input of temperature-dependent material properties nor need for distinct simulation/analysis software.
Authors: Naser, M.Z.
Construction Application Area: Structural fire engineering, evaluation of thermal and structural response of reinforced concrete (RC) beams and columns under extreme fire conditions
Methodology: The paper develops an AI-based cognitive framework using symbolic regression and genetic algorithms to derive expressions for evaluating thermal and structural response of RC beams and columns under fire. The framework was trained on databases compiled from published fire tests, containing over 25,500 data points. The AI model identifies patterns in the data to create simple expressions that predict temperature and deformation histories without requiring temperature-dependent material properties or specialized software.
Research Goal: The study aims to develop an AI-based cognitive framework capable of accurately tracing the thermal and structural response of concrete structures under elevated temperatures, providing simple expressions for evaluating RC beams and columns during fire exposure up to four hours.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the complexity and challenges of predicting fire response in concrete structures, which traditionally requires expensive fire tests, specialized finite element software, and detailed input of temperature-dependent material properties. It also addresses the lack of simple, accessible methods for fire resistance evaluation in structural fire engineering.
Future Research: Future research should develop more comprehensive AI models that accommodate advanced construction materials, complex geometries, different fire exposures (e.g., traveling/realistic fires), and complicated phenomena (e.g., debonding, restraint conditions). The author also suggests improving models to better capture fire-induced spalling, restraint conditions, and behavior of high-strength concrete columns, with potential applications in autonomous and cognitive infrastructure.
Results: The developed AI-derived expressions successfully predict thermal and structural response of RC beams and columns with high accuracy (R² exceeding 94% for most expressions, correlation coefficients exceeding 94%). The expressions implicitly account for high-temperature material properties, creep deformation, and fire-induced spalling, and can evaluate response at specific points in time or trace complete time-temperature/deformation histories.
AI Model Used: Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs), Symbolic Regression, Genetic Algorithms
Data Types: The paper uses experimental data from published fire tests on RC beams and columns, including geometric properties, material properties (concrete compressive strength, steel yield strength), fire exposure duration, temperature measurements, and deformation measurements. The database contains over 25,500 data points from multiple experimental studies covering a wide range of structural configurations and fire conditions.

Title: Investigation into Explainable Regression Trees for Construction Engineering Applications
Abstract: The logic of an artificial intelligence (AI) model derived from machine learning algorithms and domain-specific data is analogous to an expert's perception of a complex problem. Human insight based on know-how and experience also provides the best clue to verify such analytical models generalized from data. To facilitate the acceptance and implementation of AI by industry professionals, we explored the least complicated form of model that still is sufficient to represent the complexities of real-world problems. This research established a framework to apply the M5P model tree in the context of producing explainable AI for practical applications. The explanatory information derived from M5P (a decision tree with linear regressions at leaf nodes) is instrumental in explaining how the more complicated AI model reasons for the same problem, illuminating the sufficiency of problem definition and data quality, and distinguishing valid submodels from invalid ones in the obtained model tree. A steel fabrication labor cost-estimating case and a concrete strength development case were given for method validation and application demonstration.
Authors: Naumets, S.; Lu, M.
Journal: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Construction Application Area: Labor cost estimation for steel fabrication and concrete strength prediction
Methodology: The paper establishes a framework using M5P model tree algorithm for explainable AI. The methodology involves applying M5P along with three other AI algorithms (ANN, SVM, RF) to predict construction outcomes. A three-color enhancement scheme is developed to evaluate the performance of each leaf node regression, using leave-one-out cross-validation to assess individual regression quality independently.
Research Goal: To develop and validate an explainable AI framework using M5P model trees that provides transparent prediction logic for construction engineering applications while maintaining sufficient accuracy, enabling industry professionals to understand and trust AI-based decision support systems.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of explainability in AI models used for construction engineering, particularly for regression-type problems. Most XAI research focuses on classification problems in other domains, leaving a gap in developing interpretable regression models for construction applications where understanding model reasoning is crucial for professional acceptance.
Future Research: The paper does not explicitly mention specific future research directions. However, it implies potential extensions to other construction engineering applications and further validation of the three-color scheme framework across different problem domains.
Results: The M5P model tree provided an explainable AI solution with R²=74% for steel fabrication and R²=86% (improved to 98% with only green leaves) for concrete strength prediction. The three-color scheme successfully distinguished valid submodels from invalid ones, revealing that some leaf nodes performed poorly despite acceptable overall model accuracy.
AI Model Used: M5P model tree, Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest (RF)
Data Types: Two datasets were used: (1) Steel fabrication data from a Canadian company containing 218 pre-bid estimates with 38 attributes including material quantities, project characteristics, and labor hours; (2) Concrete compressive strength data from UCI Machine Learning Repository with 1,030 instances containing 8 input features (cement, water, aggregates, age, etc.) and compressive strength as output.

Title: Triad of three system: AI-driven hydraulics for autonomous electric excavators with sustainable energy savings
Abstract: This paper introduces the Triad of Three (T3), an AI-driven hydraulic system designed for autonomous electric excavators, aimed at advancing construction automation. The study addresses how to enhance energy efficiency and control accuracy in hydraulic excavators using components and AI-based energy management. The T3 system integrates a Three-Port Pump (TPP), Three-Chamber Cylinder (TCC), and Three-Chamber Accumulator (TCA) with a hierarchical Energy Management Strategy (EMS) combining Model Predictive Control (MPC) and hybrid reinforcement learning (DDPG–PPO), validated through AMESim simulations. Results show 1 mm motion tracking accuracy and 62.2% energy savings compared with conventional systems. These findings are significant for manufacturers and researchers developing zero-emission, autonomous construction machinery. The study motivates future work on real-world validation and integration with digital workflows such as Building Information Modeling (BIM).
Authors: Nguyen, V.H.; Ahn, K.K.
Construction Application Area: Energy management and automation of hydraulic excavators for construction machinery
Methodology: The paper develops a simulation-based approach using AMESim software to model the Triad of Three (T3) system, which integrates a Three-Port Pump, Three-Chamber Cylinder, and Three-Chamber Accumulator. An AI-driven Energy Management Strategy combining Model Predictive Control (MPC) at the lower level and hybrid reinforcement learning (DDPG-PPO) at the higher level is implemented to optimize energy distribution across seven operating modes for autonomous electric excavators.
Research Goal: The goal is to enhance energy efficiency and control accuracy in hydraulic excavators by integrating advanced hydraulic components with AI-based energy management, enabling zero-emission autonomous operation for construction applications with significant energy savings (over 60%) and high-precision motion control (1 mm accuracy).
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses inefficient energy recovery in traditional Hydraulic Energy Regeneration Systems (HERS), limitations of single-component hydraulic systems (TPP, TCC, TCA) when used independently, and the lack of integrated AI-driven control strategies for autonomous construction machinery. It also tackles the challenge of unequal volumetric flow in single-rod cylinders and the need for zero-emission, energy-efficient construction equipment.
Future Research: Future research should focus on large-scale experimental validation under diverse operating conditions, long-term durability testing covering hydraulic wear and battery degradation, and integration with Building Information Modeling (BIM) and digital twin platforms. Additional directions include incorporating emerging AI techniques like model-based reinforcement learning, extending applications to multi-actuator coordination and fleet-level energy optimization, and retrofitting legacy hydraulic machinery.
Results: The T3 system achieved 62.2% energy savings compared to conventional systems, 1 mm displacement control accuracy, and the highest battery State of Charge (69.97%) and State of Health (99.970%) among tested configurations. The proposed MPC-DDPG-PPO algorithm demonstrated superior performance with the lowest RMSE (0.7045) and MAE (0.6231), and dual-path energy recovery efficiency of 83-85%.
AI Model Used: Model Predictive Control (MPC), Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG), Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), Combined hierarchical MPC-DDPG-PPO strategy
Data Types: Simulation data generated from AMESim 2310 software including hydraulic system parameters (flow rates, pressures, displacements), electrical system data (battery SOC, SOH, EMG efficiency, power consumption), and component specifications (TPP displacement and port angles, TCC chamber dimensions, TCA pressure and volume characteristics). The simulations were validated over 14 representative cycles (251 seconds) and extended to 100,000 cycles for long-term performance evaluation.

Title: APPLYING MACHINE LEARNING FOR PREDICTIVE ANALYSIS IN PROJECT-BASED DATA: INSIGHTS INTO VARIATION ORDERS
Abstract: The complexity of the global supply chain and project execution necessitates advanced methodologies in project management. As industries are generating large amounts of project data, machine learning (ML) algorithms can be a viable tool for addressing predictive analytics and transforming this industry into more digitalization. This study examines the feasibility of leveraging ML models for predicting variation orders (VOs) in an energy construction project through the use of actual project management data. Using historical project data, this study presents the investigative analysis of applying six ML regression models to predict VOs and evaluates the performance of these models using the mean squared error metric. It is observed that various project activities are nonlinear in the impact of the order of variation, which indicates that advanced ML techniques are required when analyzing the order of variation rather than using linear model analysis. Thus, the results underscore the critical role of ML predictive model implementation in improving change management by enabling preemptive detection of potential problems, risk reduction, and more efficient project execution. Moreover, this study will also help to narrow the existing gap between ML-based theoretical applications and practical project management strategies while also demonstrating the efficacy of AI-based decision support systems for on-time project control. The contributions of this study provide a foundation for developing integrated ML models and project management software, fostering data-driven decision making in dynamic project scenarios.
Authors: Nishat, M.M.; Ahsan, A.; Olsson, N.O.E.
Journal: Journal of Information Technology in Construction
Construction Application Area: Change management, variation orders prediction, project planning and control
Methodology: The paper applies six machine learning regression models (LSTM, LGBM, Extra Trees Regressor, Gradient Boosting Regressor, Ada Boost Regressor, and Cat Boost Regressor) to predict variation orders in a construction project. Data was extracted from project management software, preprocessed through filtering and feature engineering, then split into training (80%) and testing (20%) sets. Models were trained with hyperparameter tuning and evaluated using Mean Squared Error (MSE) as the performance metric.
Research Goal: The study aims to investigate the feasibility of leveraging ML models for predicting variation orders (VOs) in an energy construction project using actual project management data. It seeks to bridge the gap between ML-based theoretical applications and practical project management strategies while demonstrating the efficacy of AI-based decision support systems for project control.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the limited integration of AI and ML into project management processes, particularly in predictive analytics for change management. It also fills the gap in using ML for predicting project-level variation orders rather than broader trend analysis, and addresses the underutilization of extensive project data generated by industries for strategic decision-making.
Future Research: Future research should explore multi-project datasets to increase model generalizability and robustness. Additionally, research could investigate integrating these ML models into project scheduling software for real-time change risk assessments, automated feature engineering techniques, and hybrid models using transfer learning to address covariate shift across different project types.
Results: The study found that LSTM outperformed other conventional ML algorithms with better generalization capability, though CatBoost was more suitable for model interpretation. Results demonstrate that various project activities have nonlinear impacts on variation orders, indicating that advanced ML techniques are required for analyzing variation orders rather than linear models. The models achieved varying MSE scores, with CBR showing the best test performance (MSE: 0.4003).
AI Model Used: LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory), LGBM (Light Gradient Boosting Machine), ETR (Extra Trees Regressor), GBR (Gradient Boosting Regressor), ABR (Ada Boost Regressor), CBR (Cat Boost Regressor)
Data Types: Historical project data extracted from project management software including period status tables for resources and activities, change register data, activity-level metrics (earned value, expenditure, planned quantities), temporal features (period start/end dates, early start/finish dates), and resource allocation data. The dataset consisted of 12,150 activities across 179 weeks, which was aggregated to weekly data with 147 weeks used for analysis after filtering.

Title: Can ChatGPT exceed humans in construction project risk management?
Abstract: Purpose: The objective of this research is to investigate the capabilities of the ChatGPT GPT-4 model, a form of artificial intelligence (AI), in comparison to human experts in the context of construction project risk management. Design/methodology/approach: Employing a mixed-methods approach, the study draws a qualitative and quantitative comparison between 16 human risk management experts from Finnish construction companies and the ChatGPT AI model utilizing anonymous peer reviews. It focuses primarily on the areas of risk identification, analysis, and control. Findings: ChatGPT has demonstrated a superior ability to generate comprehensive risk management plans, with its quantitative scores significantly surpassing the human average. Nonetheless, the AI model's strategies are found to lack practicality and specificity, areas where human expertise excels. Originality/value: This study marks a significant advancement in construction project risk management research by conducting a pioneering blind-review study that assesses the capabilities of the advanced AI model, GPT-4, against those of human experts. Emphasizing the evolution from earlier GPT models, this research not only underscores the innovative application of ChatGPT-4 but also the critical role of anonymized peer evaluations in enhancing the objectivity of findings. It illuminates the synergistic potential of AI and human expertise, advocating for a collaborative model where AI serves as an augmentative tool, thereby optimizing human performance in identifying and managing risks.
Authors: Nyqvist, R.; Peltokorpi, A.; Seppänen, O.
Construction Application Area: Construction project risk management (CPRM), including risk identification, risk analysis, and risk control
Methodology: The study used a mixed-methods approach where 16 human experts and ChatGPT GPT-4 completed a construction project risk management test based on a simulated case project. All responses were anonymized and subjected to blind peer review by 19 reviewers. Both quantitative scoring (1-10 scale) and qualitative feedback were collected and analyzed to compare AI and human performance.
Research Goal: To investigate the capabilities of ChatGPT GPT-4 in comparison to human experts in construction project risk management, specifically exploring whether ChatGPT can exceed human performance in risk identification, analysis, and control.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of comprehensive research on generative AI (specifically GPT-4) capabilities in construction project risk management using objective evaluation methods. Previous studies used less advanced models (GPT-3.5), lacked anonymous peer review, or relied on non-blind author evaluations, introducing potential bias.
Future Research: Future research should conduct similar studies in various countries and on different construction project management tasks to comprehensively compare AI and human capabilities. Additionally, research should explore practical real-world applications of AI solutions in project management scenarios and redesign existing processes to efficiently integrate AI.
Results: ChatGPT GPT-4 achieved significantly higher quantitative scores (average 8.6) compared to human experts (average 5.7) in the anonymous peer review. However, qualitative analysis revealed that while ChatGPT provided comprehensive risk assessments, its responses lacked specificity, practicality, and actionable strategies compared to human experts who demonstrated superior context-specific knowledge.
AI Model Used: ChatGPT GPT-4 (OpenAI)
Data Types: The data consisted of written responses from 16 human experts and ChatGPT GPT-4 to construction project risk management questions about a simulated renovation project case. Quantitative scores (1-10 scale) and qualitative written feedback from 19 blind peer reviewers were collected and analyzed.

Title: Efficacy of Annotated Video-Based Learning Environment for Drawing Students' Attention to Construction Practice Concepts
Abstract: To enhance students' learning of construction practice, instructors use videos as multimedia pedagogical tools to bring practical experience into the classroom. However, cognitive load levels, individual differences, and multimedia design principles are important considerations in the effective use of videos for instruction. Therefore, this study investigates the effectiveness of an artificial intelligence (AI)-annotated video in guiding students' attention to important construction practice concepts. Students were exposed to both annotated and unannotated videos illustrating construction safety practices. Eye tracking metrics and self-reported cognitive load were collected as students interacted with both learning environments. The effectiveness of the videos in drawing students' attention were compared. The AI-annotated video was considered effective and the variations across individual differences were reported. No significant difference was observed in the cognitive loads of both learning environments. This study provides an understanding of the extent to which learners of different demographic characteristics allocate attention to signaled practice concepts. This study also illustrates the impact of the signaled concepts on learners' cognitive loads. This study contributes to existing theories by elucidating how practice knowledge could be adapted to meet construction engineering students' learning needs.
Authors: Olayiwola, J.; Yusuf, A.; Akanmu, A.; Gonsalves, N.; Abraham, Y.
Journal: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Construction Application Area: Construction safety education, construction engineering education, practice concepts learning
Methodology: The study developed annotated and unannotated construction safety videos using AI-based object detection (Faster RCNN with VGG16) and interaction detection (VTransE). 35 undergraduate students were divided into two groups and exposed to both video types. Eye tracking metrics (dwell time, time to first fixate) and NASA-TLX cognitive load questionnaires were collected and analyzed using normality tests, Welch t-test, and Wilcoxon rank sum test to compare effectiveness across individual differences.
Research Goal: To investigate the effectiveness of AI-annotated videos in guiding students' attention to important construction practice concepts and to understand how learners of different demographic characteristics and cognitive loads allocate attention to signaled concepts in construction project videos.
Gaps Addressed: The application of signaling techniques to construction education domain has not been extensively investigated, and the role of learners' individual differences (gender, academic program, academic year, prior experience) in multimedia learning environments has not received considerable attention in construction-related studies.
Future Research: Future research will extend the annotated video to teach other practice concepts relating to emerging technologies like robots in construction. Machine learning models will be employed to map eye tracking metrics to learners' individual differences to facilitate adaptive annotations. A summative assessment will be conducted to understand the extent to which signals improve students' learning of practice concepts.
Results: The annotated video showed shorter time to first fixate for most signaled concepts across demographics, indicating effectiveness in drawing attention. Female participants and senior-level students dwelled longer on more annotated concepts than their counterparts. Students with less prior experience dwelled on more signaled concepts. No significant differences in cognitive load were observed between annotated and unannotated videos, though mental effort was slightly higher with annotated videos.
AI Model Used: Faster RCNN with VGG16 for object detection, Visual Translation Embedding Network (VTransE) for interaction detection
Data Types: Eye tracking data (fixation, dwell time, time to first fixate), demographic questionnaire responses (gender, ethnicity, academic program, academic year, prior experience), NASA-TLX cognitive load ratings (mental demand, effort, frustration), and video recordings of construction safety practices obtained from YouTube and self-captured from construction projects.

Title: Practical solutions for improving the suboptimal performance of construction projects using Dubai construction projects as an example
Abstract: Purpose: Poor performance remains a challenge for the construction industry worldwide. One of the key performance indicators of the construction industry is the timely delivery of projects. Despite the recent methodological and technological advances in the field, project-overrun remains a significant challenge for the industry. This paper seeks to propose practical solutions that allow overcoming the challenges and promote the opportunities for improving the performance of the construction projects in Dubai. Design/methodology/approach: This study focussed on the construction projects in Dubai; therefore, this research adopted a sequential mixed approach in two stages. The first stage involved face-to-face interviews with seven carefully selected construction professionals. Their answers were analysed to provide with the literature study "the informed-basis for the development of the online questionnaire". The second stage involved an online survey administrated to 425 carefully selected construction organisations working in Dubai. Accordingly, a meticulous analysis for the prime causes of project overruns has also been undertaken. This analysis assisted proposing the most suitable solutions-based technologies that enabled alleviating overruns in the construction projects. Findings: The findings revealed that, there was a consensus agreement on the formidable opportunities for improving the performance of the construction industry in general and in particular in Dubai. These opportunities are intrinsically linked with the adoption of the latest technologies such as building information modelling, augmented reality, virtual reality and the artificial intelligence (AI). Whereas, adopting AI has already assisted two public authorities to release No Objection Certificates and work permits effectively within one day instead of 14 working days, which has saved 90% of the time and cost. Likewise, adoption of the AI has assisted delivering the construction project with a 9% of time saving and a 6% of cost saving due to embracing an automated system that enabled them to instantly detect and report the delays, once occurred. Research limitations/implications: The main limitation of this study is that the study was limited to the construction industry in the Emirate of Dubai. Therefore, future research could target the whole United Arab Emirates construction industry to propose the practical solution on the country level. Practical implications: The literature study is replete with solutions, which tend to be theoretical more than practical. Therefore, the proposed practical recommendations will significantly assist the construction industry to improve its suboptimal performance to rescind the sovereignty of the irrelevant involvements. The research recommended establishing independent entity to lead the change in the construction industry; this entity will have the power of enacting rules and legislations. Furthermore, this independent entity will have the power and authority of dictations and impose sanctions on the non-committed organisations that are reluctant to adopt the recommended technologies and approaches. Originality/value: Based on the findings of the study, this paper draws a road map for the construction industry by determining practical solutions for improvements starting with an establishment of an independent authority that selects and tests the most appropriate technologies and approaches to contribute to performance improvements.
Authors: Omar, H.; Mahdjoubi, L.
Construction Application Area: Project monitoring and control, NOC and work permit processing, progress tracking and delay detection
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (BIM, drones, laser scanning, robotics, digital twins, sensor networks)
Methodology: The study adopted a sequential mixed approach in two stages. The first stage involved face-to-face semi-structured interviews with seven carefully selected construction professionals to explore causes of project overruns. The second stage involved an online questionnaire survey administered to 425 construction organizations in Dubai to validate and rank the challenges identified. Grounded theory approach was used to analyze qualitative data, and Relative Importance Index (RII) was used to rank quantitative responses.
Research Goal: The paper seeks to propose practical solutions for improving the suboptimal performance of construction projects in Dubai by identifying root causes of project overruns and recommending appropriate technologies and approaches, including establishing an independent authority to lead change in the construction industry.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the gap between theoretical solutions and practical implementation in addressing construction project overruns. It also addresses the absence of a centralized authority with power to mandate adoption of proven technologies and approaches that have demonstrated effectiveness in improving construction performance.
Future Research: Future research could target the whole United Arab Emirates construction industry to propose practical solutions on the country level, expanding beyond the Emirate of Dubai to achieve broader applicability and impact.
Results: The findings revealed that unrealistic contract duration was the most critical challenge causing poor performance. AI-enabled automation for NOC and work permit processing saved 90% of time and cost (reducing processing from 14 days to 1 day). An automated monitoring system using BIM, CV, and AI achieved 9% time savings and 6% cost savings by instantly detecting and reporting construction delays.
AI Model Used: Artificial Intelligence (AI) was mentioned as used in automated systems for NOC/work permit processing and construction progress monitoring, but specific AI model architectures were not detailed
Data Types: The paper used qualitative data from semi-structured interviews with seven construction professionals and quantitative data from an online survey of 51 complete responses from construction organizations. The study also references case examples of automated systems tested in Dubai construction projects.

Title: Usability of large language models for building construction safety risk assessment
Abstract: Purpose: Risk assessment is an approach that involves identifying potential workplace risks and determining the necessary precautions to reduce their impact on workers. The advent of artificial intelligence (AI) technology in recent years has greatly benefited safety experts in their assessments of risks. Large language models (LLMs), such as ChatGPT, may provide significant advantages in occupational safety professionals' risk assessment processes. LLMs enable them to quickly access information, generate reports, analyze data and provide recommendations thanks to their natural language processing capability. This study aims to evaluate the usability of LLMs as a decision-support tool for risk assessments in building construction. Design/methodology/approach: First, risks and precautions were defined for 12 work items in building construction. Subsequently, ten experts and ChatGPT were requested to evaluate the risks based on their level of importance using a five-point Likert scale. The similarity of the responses was calculated using the Modified Manhattan Distance. Next, the precautionary choices made by the experts and ChatGPT were compared. Findings: It was found that the LLM provided similar answers to the experts in terms of risk scores and precaution selection. Nevertheless, the similarity value of ChatGPT responses surpasses the similarity value of expert responses. Originality/value: This study enhances the existing body of knowledge and provides valuable insights to industry stakeholders by showcasing the effectiveness of LLMs in evaluating occupational health and safety hazards. Moreover, to the best of our knowledge, this study represents one of the initial attempts to evaluate occupational safety and health risks with ChatGPT.
Authors: Oral, M.; Alboga, Ö.; Aydınlı, S.; Erdiş, E.
Construction Application Area: Occupational health and safety risk assessment in building construction
Methodology: The study conducted risk assessments for 12 work items in building construction with 10 experts and ChatGPT (10 independent sessions). Risks were evaluated using a 5-point Likert scale for probability and multiple severity parameters. Similarity analysis was performed using Modified Manhattan Distance to compare expert and ChatGPT responses. Risk scores were calculated using Traditional Risk Score (TRS) and Total Risk Score (TotRS) methods, and statistical significance was tested using two-sample student t-tests.
Research Goal: The study aims to evaluate the usability of large language models (specifically ChatGPT) as a decision-support tool for occupational safety risk assessments in building construction by comparing its performance with expert assessments.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the gap in understanding how web-based open LLMs such as ChatGPT can aid in the risk assessment phase of construction safety management. Existing AI-based risk assessment models either rely on expert opinion (not eliminating subjectivity) or require large datasets (which may not be available for every project), while LLMs offer a potential solution without requiring extensive databases.
Future Research: Future research can compare outcomes by applying new developments in artificial intelligence techniques. Greater levels of success can be attained using project-based risk assessment with provision of project-specific data. It is worth exploring if LLMs are appropriate for evaluating risks in other forms of construction, such as tunnels, dams, and highways.
Results: ChatGPT provided similar answers to experts in terms of risk scores and precaution selection, with no statistically significant difference (p=0.54 for TRS, p=0.80 for TotRS). However, ChatGPT's responses showed higher consistency (minimum similarity 0.90) compared to experts (minimum similarity 0.34). There was a 7.04% difference between experts and ChatGPT in the choice of precautions for 71 risks, demonstrating ChatGPT's effectiveness in construction safety risk assessment.
AI Model Used: ChatGPT 4.5 (plus version)
Data Types: The data consists of risk assessment questionnaires with 178 questions covering 71 risks and 12 precautions across 12 work items in building construction. Each risk was evaluated using 7 parameters (probability, general severity, environmental impact severity, social impact severity, project duration impact severity, project cost impact severity, and company reputation impact severity) on a 5-point Likert scale. The study collected responses from 10 occupational safety experts and 10 independent ChatGPT sessions.

Title: Estimation of building project completion duration using a natural gradient boosting ensemble model and legal and institutional variables
Abstract: Purpose: Earned value management (EVM)–based models for estimating project actual duration (AD) and cost at completion using various methods are continuously developed to improve the accuracy and actualization of predicted values. This study primarily aimed to examine natural gradient boosting (NGBoost-2020) with the classification and regression trees (CART) base model (base learner). To the best of the authors' knowledge, this concept has never been applied to EVM AD forecasting problem. Consequently, the authors compared this method to the single K-nearest neighbor (KNN) method, the ensemble method of extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost-2016) with the CART base model and the optimal equation of EVM, the earned schedule (ES) equation with the performance factor equal to 1 (ES1). The paper also sought to determine the extent to which the World Bank's two legal factors affect countries and how the two legal causes of delay (related to institutional flaws) influence AD prediction models. Design/methodology/approach: In this paper, data from 30 construction projects of various building types in Iran, Pakistan, India, Turkey, Malaysia and Nigeria (due to the high number of delayed projects and the detrimental effects of these delays in these countries) were used to develop three models. The target variable of the models was a dimensionless output, the ratio of estimated duration to completion (ETC(t)) to planned duration (PD). Furthermore, 426 tracking periods were used to build the three models, with 353 samples and 23 projects in the training set, 73 patterns (17% of the total) and six projects (21% of the total) in the testing set. Furthermore, 17 dimensionless input variables were used, including ten variables based on the main variables and performance indices of EVM and several other variables detailed in the study. The three models were subsequently created using Python and several GitHub-hosted codes. Findings: For the testing set of the optimal model (NGBoost), the better percentage mean (better%) of the prediction error (based on projects with a lower error percentage) of the NGBoost compared to two KNN and ES1 single models, as well as the total mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) and mean lags (MeLa) (indicating model stability) were 100, 83.33, 5.62 and 3.17%, respectively. Notably, the total MAPE and MeLa for the NGBoost model testing set, which had ten EVM-based input variables, were 6.74 and 5.20%, respectively. The ensemble artificial intelligence (AI) models exhibited a much lower MAPE than ES1. Additionally, ES1 was less stable in prediction than NGBoost. The possibility of excessive and unusual MAPE and MeLa values occurred only in the two single models. However, on some data sets, ES1 outperformed AI models. NGBoost also outperformed other models, especially single models for most developing countries, and was more accurate than previously presented optimized models. In addition, sensitivity analysis was conducted on the NGBoost predicted outputs of 30 projects using the SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) method. All variables demonstrated an effect on ETC(t)/PD. The results revealed that the most influential input variables in order of importance were actual time (AT) to PD, regulatory quality (RQ), earned duration (ED) to PD, schedule cost index (SCI), planned complete percentage, rule of law (RL), actual complete percentage (ACP) and ETC(t) of the ES optimal equation to PD. The probabilistic hybrid model was selected based on the outputs predicted by the NGBoost and XGBoost models and the MAPE values from three AI models. The 95% prediction interval of the NGBoost–XGBoost model revealed that 96.10 and 98.60% of the actual output values of the testing and training sets are within this interval, respectively. Research limitations/implications: Due to the use of projects performed in different countries, it was not possible to distribute the questionnaire to the managers and stakeholders of 30 projects in six developing countries. Due to the low number of EVM-based projects in various references, it was unfeasible to utilize other types of projects. Future prospects include evaluating the accuracy and stability of NGBoost for timely and non-fluctuating projects (mostly in developed countries), considering a greater number of legal/institutional variables as input, using legal/institutional/internal/inflation inputs for complex projects with extremely high uncertainty (such as bridge and road construction) and integrating these inputs and NGBoost with new technologies (such as blockchain, radio frequency identification (RFID) systems, building information modeling (BIM) and Internet of things (IoT)). Practical implications: The legal/intuitive recommendations made to governments are strict control of prices, adequate supervision, removal of additional rules, removal of unfair regulations, clarification of the future trend of a law change, strict monitoring of property rights, simplification of the processes for obtaining permits and elimination of unnecessary changes particularly in developing countries and at the onset of irregular projects with limited information and numerous uncertainties. Furthermore, the managers and stakeholders of this group of projects were informed of the significance of seven construction variables (institutional/legal external risks, internal factors and inflation) at an early stage, using time series (dynamic) models to predict AD, accurate calculation of progress percentage variables, the effectiveness of building type in non-residential projects, regular updating inflation during implementation, effectiveness of employer type in the early stage of public projects in addition to the late stage of private projects, and allocating reserve duration (buffer) in order to respond to institutional/legal risks. Originality/value: Ensemble methods were optimized in 70% of references. To the authors' knowledge, NGBoost from the set of ensemble methods was not used to estimate construction project duration and delays. NGBoost is an effective method for considering uncertainties in irregular projects and is often implemented in developing countries. Furthermore, AD estimation models do fail to incorporate RQ and RL from the World Bank's worldwide governance indicators (WGI) as risk-based inputs. In addition, the various WGI, EVM and inflation variables are not combined with substantial degrees of delay institutional risks as inputs. Consequently, due to the existence of critical and complex risks in different countries, it is vital to consider legal and institutional factors. This is especially recommended if an in-depth, accurate and reality-based method like SHAP is used for analysis.
Authors: Peiman, F.; Khalilzadeh, M.; Shahsavari-Pour, N.; Ravanshadnia, M.
Research Location: Iran, Pakistan, India, Turkey, Malaysia, Nigeria
Construction Application Area: Project duration estimation, schedule forecasting, actual duration (AD) prediction for building construction projects
Methodology: The paper uses three AI models (NGBoost, XGBoost, and KNN) to predict actual duration (AD) of building projects. Data from 30 building projects across six countries (426 tracking periods) were used, with 353 samples for training and 73 for testing. The models used 17 dimensionless input variables including 10 EVM-based variables, legal/institutional factors (RQ, RL), project-specific variables (building type, employer type), inflation rates, and importance percentages of delay causes. SHAP method was used for sensitivity analysis to determine variable importance.
Research Goal: The primary goal is to examine Natural Gradient Boosting (NGBoost) with CART base model for EVM-based actual duration forecasting of building projects, and to determine how World Bank's legal factors (RQ and RL) and institutional causes of delay affect AD prediction accuracy in developing countries with delayed and irregular projects.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses three main gaps: (1) no probabilistic ensemble method like NGBoost has been used for AD estimation despite high uncertainties in developing country projects; (2) legal and institutional factors (RQ and RL from WGI) have been neglected in AD estimation models; (3) institutional risks and legal factors have not been combined with EVM variables and used in time series models to determine their impact timing.
Future Research: Future research includes: (1) evaluating NGBoost accuracy and stability for timely, non-fluctuating projects in developed countries; (2) incorporating more legal/institutional variables as inputs; (3) using these inputs for complex projects with high uncertainty like bridge and road construction; (4) integrating NGBoost with new technologies such as blockchain, RFID, BIM, and IoT for construction projects.
Results: NGBoost outperformed all comparison models with MAPE of 5.62% and MeLa of 3.17% for the testing set, and showed 100% and 83.33% better performance compared to KNN and ES1 models respectively. The most influential variables were AT/PD, RQ, ED/PD, SCI, PCP, RL, and ACP. The probabilistic hybrid NGBoost-XGBoost model showed that 96.10% and 98.60% of actual values fell within the 95% prediction interval for testing and training sets respectively.
AI Model Used: Natural Gradient Boosting (NGBoost) with CART base learner, Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) with CART base learner, K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN)
Data Types: Data from 30 completed building construction projects across six developing countries (Iran, Pakistan, India, Turkey, Malaysia, Nigeria) with 426 tracking periods. Input variables included EVM-based metrics (AT, PD, ACP, PCP, SPI, SCI, ED, ES, etc.), World Bank governance indicators (RQ and RL), project characteristics (building type, employer type), inflation rates, and importance percentages of institutional delay causes (SPGDA and CGRL).

Title: Construction path tracking and pose estimation of unmanned bulldozer
Abstract: The vigorous development of automatic control technology and AI has generated considerable interest in driverless systems. Presently, research on driverless systems is mainly focused on the automotive field, but it also has immense scope in engineering machinery. In the field of unmanned bulldozers, path tracking and pose estimation are the foundation of construction quality and safety. This article focuses on this field. A construction path tracking method was designed, wherein motion control commands were generated according to the course angle deviation combined with verification of steering gain and saturation characteristics. The proposed method can track the construction path of an unmanned bulldozer with high accuracy. The improved ORB-SLAM3 pose estimation method fused with GNSS and the local map reloading pose estimation method are used to estimate the bulldozer pose to ensure construction safety. The two methods are superior to the general SLAM system in terms of precision and dynamic performance.
Authors: Peng, G.; Duan, H.; Tan, Z.; Zhou, Y.; Li, J.; Hu, B.; Zhou, C.
Construction Application Area: Path tracking and pose estimation for unmanned bulldozers, construction machinery automation, autonomous construction operations
Methodology: The paper develops a construction path tracking method using PI controller with steering gain and saturation verification based on heading angle deviation. For pose estimation, it proposes an improved ORB-SLAM3 method fused with GNSS (GNSS-ORB-SLAM3) and a local map reloading method. Experiments were conducted using KITTI dataset and real-world unmanned bulldozer tests to evaluate accuracy and dynamic performance.
Research Goal: The goal is to enable unmanned bulldozers to work autonomously, efficiently, and safely by achieving high-precision construction path tracking and reliable pose estimation even when RTK signals are poor or lost.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the limitations of relying solely on RTK/GNSS signals for pose estimation in construction sites, which can be unstable or unavailable in areas far from cities. It also addresses the need for robust pose estimation methods that can ensure construction safety when GNSS signals fail.
Future Research: The paper notes that experiments were conducted in relatively flat terrain and suggests future work should test the unmanned path tracking under harsh terrain conditions such as muddy ground. It also suggests using binocular cameras with polarizing plates to improve data quality and reduce the influence of light variations.
Results: The construction path tracking method achieved average position deviation within 5 cm and heading angle error within 1°. The GNSS-ORB-SLAM3 pose estimation method reduced RMSE by 83.79% compared to original ORB-SLAM3 for sequence 08 and by 27.91% for sequence 10. The local map reloading method showed significant improvement in pose estimation accuracy with reduced computational requirements.
AI Model Used: ORB-SLAM3 (Oriented FAST and Rotated BRIEF - Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), PI controller for path tracking
Data Types: Visual data from depth cameras and binocular cameras, GNSS/RTK positioning data, IMU data, and odometry data from the KITTI dataset. Real-world data from unmanned bulldozer field tests including position coordinates, heading angles, and sensor measurements at 5 Hz frequency.

Title: Performance review of RTI IMS software for automatic road surface damages identification
Abstract: Nowadays, the rapid development of AI models (Artificial Intelligence) has significantly improved performance in addressing construction management challenges in daily life. Among the construction management tasks, the detection of road surface damage in the management of the transportation system has become more straightforward due to robust advancements in machine learning (ML) identification techniques. In this study, the innovative YOLO technology is utilized to create the RTI IMS software, capable of automatically and accurately detecting road surface damages. The research also used the Mosaic method to construct an image library of road surface damages, tripling the variations, markedly enhancing prediction accuracy and improving the software's detection performance. To illustrate the software's superiority, the study evaluates object detection performance and speed compared to previous models for detecting road damage. The research culminates in the successful development of comprehensive software capable of automatic, accurate and rapid detection of road surface damages. The RTI IMS software can identify road surface damages and provide defects images to traffic management agencies, contributing to the increased efficiency of these agencies in patrolling, detecting and maintaining surface road defects.
Authors: Pham, S.V.H.; Nguyen, K.V.T.
Construction Application Area: Road surface damage detection and traffic infrastructure management
Methodology: The paper develops RTI IMS software using YOLOv5 (2023 version) for automated road surface damage detection. The methodology involves training the model on 9,053 images (15,435 damage instances) using the Mosaic augmentation method to expand the dataset to over 30,000 cases. The model is evaluated using mean Average Precision (mAP) and processing speed metrics, and compared against eight other detection models including CNN and SSD architectures.
Research Goal: The goal is to develop an automated, accurate, and fast road surface damage detection software (RTI IMS) that can assist traffic management agencies in efficiently detecting and maintaining road defects, thereby improving safety and reducing maintenance costs.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the limitations of manual road damage detection which is subjective, time-consuming, and poses safety risks to patrol personnel. It also overcomes the performance limitations of previous machine learning models (CNN, SSD) that struggled with accuracy below 55% mAP, particularly for certain damage types.
Future Research: Future research directions include continuously expanding the machine learning image database with more diverse and up-to-date damage cases. Another direction is to increase detection speed and integrate the software with real-time GPS data to automatically map road damage locations onto traffic networks for fully automated damage reporting to management authorities.
Results: RTI IMS achieved outstanding performance with 81.81% mAP at IoU 0.5, significantly outperforming previous models (highest prior was 54.75%). The software demonstrated superior detection speed of 0.4-0.5ms for 600x600 images and 1.2s for 1920x1080 resolution, much faster than competing models. It consistently detected all five types of road damage with accuracy above 70%, with Type I damage achieving 79.76% accuracy.
AI Model Used: YOLOv5 (2023 version) with CSPdarknet53 backbone architecture, using Single Shot Detection (SSD) approach with 7.2 million parameters. The model incorporates Mosaic data augmentation method.
Data Types: The dataset consists of 9,053 road surface damage images provided by Maeda et al. (2018), containing 15,435 instances of road defects from Taiwan, Japan, and India. These were classified into five damage types: blurring, corruption, alligator crack, longitudinal crack, and lateral crack. Using the Mosaic method, the dataset was expanded to over 30,000 training instances.

Title: ANALYSIS OF 5D BIM FOR COST ESTIMATION, COST CONTROL, AND PAYMENTS
Abstract: Increasing expectancy for efficiency in the delivery of building projects and the adoption of lean production processes for construction has made the necessity for the development of an integrated system for cost estimating, cost monitoring, cost control, and payments in the construction lifecycle important. Existing 5D BIM tools are used to estimate the cost of projects during the preconstruction period. There is a lack of integration between the 5D BIM models, existing progress monitoring tools, and payment systems used in construction. Lack of standardization in the use of model elements through the project lifecycle has also been identified as one of the factors limiting automation in 5D BIM. Construction project monitoring can be automated by combining modern technologies that allow for visualization of building progress (Laser scanners, computer vision) with 5D BIM cost estimation tools. These project monitoring tools can be combined with Artificial Intelligence (AI), and Smart contracts to develop an integrated lifecycle system for cost management in construction. This paper examines existing systems used in 5D BIM to develop integrated practices and systems that will streamline the process of cost estimating, cost monitoring, cost control, and cash flow in the construction supply chain. This will reduce the inefficiency that exists today with traditional contracts and payment applications that do not interact with the 5D BIM application. By leveraging a standardized classification ID system throughout a project life cycle and applying AI and smart contract, features like cost estimation cost control, and payments can be fully streamlined, integrated, and automated. A case study of an existing construction project utilizing 5D BIM was examined. According to the study, 5D BIM is used in the pre-construction stage of a cost estimation project. It was also revealed that 5D BIM improves project cost visualization and budget control.
Authors: Pishdad-Bozorgi, P.; Onungwa, I.O.
Journal: Journal of Information Technology in Construction
Construction Application Area: Cost estimation, cost control, cost monitoring, payments, project progress monitoring
Methodology: The research employs a qualitative approach using literature review and case study analysis. A comprehensive review of 5D BIM literature was conducted examining current practices in cost estimation and monitoring. A case study of a higher education project utilizing 5D BIM was examined through semi-structured interviews with project consultants, cost managers, 5D BIM consultants, contractors' BIM managers, architects, and the client's project manager to analyze existing workflows and practices.
Research Goal: The main objective is to examine how 5D BIM is used for cost estimation, cost control, and payment, and to develop a workflow for integrated cost management through the project development lifecycle by leveraging standardized classification systems, work packages, and emerging technologies including AI and smart contracts.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of integration between 5D BIM models, existing progress monitoring tools, and payment systems used in construction. It also identifies the absence of standardization in the use of model elements through the project lifecycle as a factor limiting automation in 5D BIM, and the disconnect between preconstruction estimates and construction phase cost monitoring and payments.
Future Research: Future research should focus on developing and testing the proposed integrated workflow that combines standardized classification systems, AI, machine learning, laser scanning, and smart contracts for automated cost management. Further investigation is needed on how to fully automate the recognition of materials and quality assessment from point cloud data, and how to integrate payment systems with 5D BIM throughout the entire project lifecycle.
Results: The case study revealed that 5D BIM is primarily used in the pre-construction stage for cost estimation and improves project cost visualization and budget control. However, cost monitoring during construction and payment processing are done manually outside the 5D BIM system. The study found that different classification systems and work breakdown structures are used for preconstruction and construction stages, making cost comparisons challenging.
AI Model Used: Machine learning and artificial intelligence are proposed for future implementation but not specifically applied in the current study. The paper discusses using AI and ML to recognize building objects from imagery and match BIM objects with on-site construction, and mentions neural networks for automated cost estimation.
Data Types: The paper uses qualitative data from interviews with project stakeholders (5D BIM consultants, cost managers, contractors' BIM managers, architects, and project managers) and literature review data. The case study involved a $110M higher education project with 300,000 square feet campus center using design-build delivery method.

Title: Using Fuzzy Inference Systems for Lean Management Strategies in Construction Project Delivery
Abstract: When using lean waste management in construction project delivery, computational methodologies are currently an innovative technology for the implementation of efficient and effective improvement strategies in the development of Industry 4.0 in Chile. Lean models are able to manage data obtained from construction projects along with the data obtained from the knowledge base of professional experts (expert survey). The waste management of construction projects under the lean philosophy requires cooperative efforts, where the opinion of professional experts is completely paramount to analyze multidisciplinary knowledge. Therefore, new protocols and disruptive procedures based on artificial intelligence (AI) tools can help decision makers prioritize activities, minimize uncertainty, and avoid wasteful actions that add no value to the project and thus can be minimized or completely eliminated. The vagueness of subjective human judgment in the degree of application of lean waste management in project delivery is modeled by a fuzzy logic model that includes additional considerations related to the lean implementation. Moreover, multiple linear regression analysis has been implemented in order to verify and validate the previous digital fuzzy model. In this sense, the main aim of this study is to develop new approaches regarding AI systems, using fuzzy sets and multiple linear regression for managing waste in construction project delivery in the metropolitan area of Santiago, Chile.
