A theorized application of the models reveals that the sample (100 construction projects) can be classified into three lean waste condition levels: high, medium, or low waste effects. The outcomes of this research will contribute to the Chilean construction industry environment and will open new ways for harnessing AI-based technology in the construction industry to the fullest potential, to achieve better time and cost predictability with a client- and end-user-centered world view.
Authors: Prieto, A.J.; Alarcón, L.F.
Journal: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Construction Application Area: Lean waste management, construction project delivery, waste identification and reduction (overproduction, waiting, transportation, overprocessing, inventory, motion, defects)
Methodology: The paper develops a Fuzzy Inference System (FIS) with seven input variables representing lean waste categories (overproduction, waiting, transportation, overprocessing, inventory, motion, defects) to output a Waste Management Index (WMI). The fuzzy model includes fuzzification, knowledge base with 145 inference rules, fuzzy inference engine using Mamdani mechanism, and defuzzification using center of area method. Multiple Linear Regression (MLR) is additionally applied to verify and validate the fuzzy model, simplifying its complexity while maintaining predictive capability.
Research Goal: The main aim is to develop new AI-based approaches using fuzzy sets and multiple linear regression for managing waste in construction project delivery in Santiago, Chile, providing a digital tool to help decision makers prioritize activities, minimize uncertainty, and eliminate wasteful actions that add no value to projects.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the gap in dealing with uncertainty and subjective reasoning in construction waste management, as traditional methods require considerable numerical data that is often unavailable. It also addresses the need for computational methodologies and Industry 4.0 technologies to improve lean waste management implementation in the AEC sector, which has continued to underperform relative to expectations.
Future Research: The WMI fuzzy logic model could be readapted to new contexts with specific and detailed analyses of the framework. Future research should develop new guidelines offering different adaptation strategies for construction projects regarding waste management in different constructive, environmental, social, and cultural contexts.
Results: The fuzzy model achieved a Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.9938 and R² of 0.9877 in sensitivity analysis with 401 simulations. When applied to 100 construction projects in Santiago, 18% ranked high waste (Condition C), 43% medium waste (Condition B), and 39% low waste (Condition A). The MLR validation model achieved R² of 0.8548, showing that 85.5% of WMI variability can be explained by the seven input variables.
AI Model Used: Fuzzy Inference System (FIS) using Mamdani inference mechanism with triangular and Gaussian membership functions; Multiple Linear Regression (MLR) for validation
Data Types: The data consists of theorized evaluations of seven lean waste input variables (overproduction, waiting, transportation, overprocessing, inventory, motion, defects) for 100 housing construction projects in Santiago, Chile, rated on a 1-5 scale. The model was also tested with 401 simulated project scenarios for sensitivity analysis, and expert knowledge was incorporated through the fuzzy inference rules.

Title: Generative AI on innovation performance of construction enterprises: the role of knowledge-based dynamic capabilities and enterprise AI capabilities
Abstract: Purpose: The aim of this study was to investigate the associations among generative artificial intelligence (AI), knowledge-based dynamic capabilities, enterprise AI capabilities (EAIC) and innovation performance of the construction enterprises. Design/methodology/approach: The structural equation model was used in this study. First, the hypothesis of the relationship between generative AI, knowledge-based dynamic capabilities, EAIC and innovation performance was proposed based on the previous relevant literature; then, the research data were collected by 310 questionnaires; finally, these hypotheses were tested through data analysis. Findings: Generative AI positively influenced knowledge-based dynamic capabilities and innovation performance of the construction enterprises; knowledge-based dynamic capabilities had a mediating effect on the influence of generative AI on innovation performance of the construction enterprises; EAIC had a positive moderating effect on the influence of generative AI on innovation performance of the construction enterprises. Originality/value: In this study, knowledge-based dynamic capability and EAIC are introduced into the relationship model between generative AI and innovation performance of the construction enterprises, and an integrated model is proposed about the relationship between these factors. This study enriches the research content of AI application, dynamic capability and innovation management. The research results are conducive to generative AI in the innovation process and the formulation of innovation strategies.
Authors: Qiao, S.; Zhiwei, L.; Jie, W.; Yuxi, M.; Guo, Z.; Han, W.
Construction Application Area: Innovation management, knowledge management, digital transformation, organizational performance, and general innovation processes in construction enterprises
Methodology: The study uses Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) to test hypotheses about relationships among generative AI, knowledge-based dynamic capabilities (KDC), enterprise AI capabilities (EAIC), and innovation performance. Data were collected via questionnaires from 310 construction enterprises in China from November 2023 to March 2024. The research team conducted exploratory factor analysis (EFA), confirmatory factor analysis (CFA), and structural model analysis (SMA) using SPSS 21 and AMOS 24.
Research Goal: The study aims to investigate the associations among generative AI, knowledge-based dynamic capabilities, enterprise AI capabilities, and innovation performance of construction enterprises. It seeks to understand the internal mechanisms through which generative AI influences innovation performance.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of research on the internal mechanisms of how generative AI impacts innovation performance in construction enterprises. Most existing research focuses on technical applications of Gen AI, while few studies explore its role in innovation management and the mediating/moderating factors such as knowledge-based dynamic capabilities and enterprise AI capabilities.
Future Research: Future research should broaden the sample to multinational contexts to increase generalizability, implement longitudinal analyses to establish causality over time, and explore additional mediating variables beyond KDC such as leadership motive, innovation capability, and communication. The study also suggests refining the concepts of KDC and EAIC by adding more observation indicators to better understand the intrinsic mechanisms of Gen AI applications.
Results: The study found that generative AI positively influences innovation performance and knowledge-based dynamic capabilities of construction enterprises. Knowledge-based dynamic capabilities have a partial mediating effect on the relationship between Gen AI and innovation performance. Enterprise AI capabilities have a positive moderating effect, with Gen AI having a greater impact on innovation performance when EAIC is high.
AI Model Used: No Model
Data Types: The study uses survey data collected through questionnaires with 12 items measured on a five-point Likert scale. The questionnaire includes four constructs: generative AI (3 items), knowledge-based dynamic capabilities (3 items), enterprise AI capabilities (3 items), and innovation performance (3 items), along with control variables including firm age, number of employees, annual turnover, and total assets.

Title: ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE IN CONSTRUCTION ASSET MANAGEMENT: A REVIEW OF PRESENT STATUS, CHALLENGES AND FUTURE OPPORTUNITIES
Abstract: The built environment is responsible for roughly 40% of global greenhouse emissions, making the sector a crucial factor for climate change and sustainability. Meanwhile, other sectors (like manufacturing) adopted Artificial Intelligence (AI) to solve complex, non-linear problems to reduce waste, inefficiency, and pollution. Therefore, many research efforts in the Architecture, Engineering, and Construction community have recently tried introducing AI into building asset management (AM) processes. Since AM encompasses a broad set of disciplines, an overview of several AI applications, current research gaps, and trends is needed. In this context, this study conducted the first state-of-the-art research on AI for building asset management. A total of 578 papers were analyzed with bibliometric tools to identify prominent institutions, topics, and journals. The quantitative analysis helped determine the most researched areas of AM and which AI techniques are applied. The areas were furtherly investigated by reading in-depth the 83 most relevant studies selected by screening the articles' abstracts identified in the bibliometric analysis. The results reveal many applications for Energy Management, Condition assessment, Risk management, and Project management areas. Finally, the literature review identified three main trends that can be a reference point for future studies made by practitioners or researchers: Digital Twin, Generative Adversarial Networks (with synthetic images) for data augmentation, and Deep Reinforcement Learning.
Authors: Rampini, L.; Re Cecconi, F.
Journal: Journal of Information Technology in Construction
Construction Application Area: Asset Management (Energy Management, Condition Assessment and Operations, Risk Management, Project Management)
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (BIM, drones, laser scanning, robotics, digital twins, sensor networks)
Methodology: The paper employs a bibliometric analysis of 578 papers to identify research trends, followed by an in-depth qualitative review of 83 most relevant papers. The methodology includes keyword co-occurrence analysis, journal citation analysis, and co-authorship network analysis using VOSviewer software, combined with systematic literature review techniques to categorize AI applications across 14 asset management core areas.
Research Goal: To frame AI applications within the 14 disciplines that characterize Asset Management by identifying the most researched AM areas for AI applications and possible future research topics, providing a comprehensive state-of-the-art review of AI in construction asset management.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of a comprehensive review focusing specifically on AI applications in Asset Management areas, which encompass a broad list of disciplines. Previous reviews focused on AI in AECO generally but none specifically examined the 14 core functions of AM.
Future Research: Three main future trends are identified: Digital Twin technology for monitoring and controlling assets throughout their lifecycle; GANs and synthetic images for data augmentation to overcome limited dataset availability; and Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) for improving energy management efficiency and customization in buildings.
Results: The review reveals that Energy Management, Condition assessment and operations, Risk management, and Project management are the four most researched areas. Energy management applications focus on DL for consumption prediction, RL for building control, and ML for post-occupancy evaluation. The research shows increasing publication trends from 2012-2022, with the US and China as top contributors.
AI Model Used: Multiple models reviewed including: ANNs, CNNs, RNNs, LSTM, GRU, SVM, Random Forest, Decision Trees, Reinforcement Learning, Deep Learning models (DNN, FCN), GANs, XGBoost, Gaussian Process, Fuzzy Logic, ARIMA, k-NN, and ensemble methods
Data Types: Bibliometric metadata from Scopus and Web of Science databases (578 papers), including keywords, citations, journal information, co-authorship data, and full-text content from 83 selected papers. Application domains include sensor data, images, videos, point clouds, thermal images, energy consumption data, building information models, and textual documents.

Title: Developing preliminary cost estimates for foundation systems of high-rise buildings
Abstract: This study provided a unique Artificial intelligence (AI) model for the cost estimation of the foundation system for high-rise buildings. First, parametric data research was conducted, including 180 examples of foundation systems for high-rise building models related to criteria such as average column spacing, floor height, slap load, overall weight, base shear, and over. In addition, moment, gravity load, seismic load, design load, various columns (mid-edge-corner), soil profile, bearing capacity, GWT, excavation depth, and foundation systems are all factors to consider (shallow and deep footing). The research encompassed column spacing (5.0, 7.0, and 9.0 m) and the number of stories from 15 to 35, with an average cost decrease of 15%. The AI model utilizes extensive machine learning algorithms to analyze structural and geotechnical parameters. It provides a predictive tool that improves decision-making in the preliminary design. The methodology includes collecting data, training models utilizing innovative neural network techniques and validating them against current cost estimation models. The findings indicate that the AI model significantly improves the accuracy of cost estimations, with a precision rate of 95%, compared to traditional methods. Moreover, the model presents a potential cost savings of up to 15%. Construction designers and decision-makers may utilize the study's findings to increase the accuracy of foundation system selection, lower costs and construction length, and improve overall project performance. Future work may involve applying this concept to infrastructure and substructure projects and collecting more data for benchmarking and empirical study.
Authors: Rashad, A.; Hegazy, H.; Zhang, J.; Mahdi, I.; Abdul-Rashied, I.; Ebid, A.
Construction Application Area: Cost estimation for foundation systems of high-rise buildings
Methodology: The study collected parametric data from 180 foundation system examples for high-rise buildings. The data included structural parameters (column spacing, floor height, loads) and geotechnical parameters (soil profile, bearing capacity, GWT, excavation depth). The researchers designed both shallow and deep foundation alternatives for each case, calculated costs and construction time, applied value engineering to select optimal solutions, and then trained three ANN models using SPSS to predict time, total cost, and foundation type.
Research Goal: To develop an AI-based model that provides accurate preliminary cost estimates for foundation systems of high-rise buildings and assists decision-makers in selecting the optimal foundation system by analyzing structural and geotechnical parameters.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the research gap related to using AI in developing precise preliminary cost estimates for high-rise construction foundation systems. Although AI and machine learning are increasingly popular in many industries, their use in geotechnical engineering, particularly for estimating costs in high-rise buildings, has not been thoroughly investigated.
Future Research: Future work may involve applying this concept to infrastructure and substructure projects and collecting more data for benchmarking and empirical study. The study should be extended to medium and low-rise buildings, different pile cap systems (combined pile caps, raft pile caps), and comparison with other AI techniques beyond ANNs.
Results: The AI model achieved a precision rate of 95% in cost estimations compared to traditional methods, with potential cost savings of up to 15%. The model successfully identified that average spacing is the most critical factor affecting project time, while the number of floors is the most important factor affecting total cost and foundation type selection.
AI Model Used: Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) with different configurations: (1) aNN-8-Hyptan-Time model with hyperbolic tangent activation function for time prediction, (2) aNN-8-sigmoid-TC model with sigmoid activation function for total cost prediction, and (3) aNN-8-sigmoid-Type model with sigmoid activation function for foundation type prediction. All models used 8 hidden layer units.
Data Types: The study used parametric data from 180 foundation system cases for high-rise buildings, including structural data (column spacing of 5/7/9m, 15-35 floors, floor heights, slab loads, total weight, base shear, overturning moment, gravity loads, seismic loads), geotechnical data (10 soil profile cases with sand/clay combinations, bearing capacity, groundwater table, excavation depth), and foundation design data (shallow footings: isolated, combined, raft; deep footings: pile diameters of 80/100/120/150/180cm), resulting in 1,080 total cases with associated costs and construction time.

Title: Enhanced identification of equipment failures from descriptive accident reports using language generative model
Abstract: Purpose: Equipment failure is a critical factor in construction accidents, often leading to severe consequences. Therefore, this study addresses two significant gaps in construction safety research: (1) effectively using historical data to investigate equipment failure and (2) understanding the classification of equipment failure according to Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) standards. Design/methodology/approach: Our research utilized a multi-stage methodology. We curated data from the OSHA database, distinguishing accidents involving equipment failures. Then we developed a framework using generative artificial intelligence (AI) and large language models (LLMs) to minimize manual processing. This framework employed a two-step prompting strategy: (1) classifying narratives that describe equipment failures and (2) analyzing these cases to extract specific failure details (e.g. names, types, categories). To ensure accuracy, we conducted a manual analysis of a subset of reports to establish ground truth and tested two different LLMs within our approach, comparing their performance against this ground truth. Findings: The tested LLMs demonstrated 95% accuracy in determining if narratives describe equipment failures and 73% accuracy in extracting equipment names, enabling automated categorical identifications. These findings highlight LLMs' promising identification accuracy compared to manual methods. Research limitations/implications: The research's focus on equipment data not only validates the research framework but also highlights its potential for broader application across various accident categories beyond construction, extending into any domain with accessible accident narratives. Given that such data are essential for regulatory bodies like OSHA, the framework's adoption could significantly enhance safety analysis and reporting, contributing to more robust safety protocols industry-wide. Practical implications: Using the developed approach, the research enables us to use accident narratives, a reliable source of accident data, in accident analysis. It provides deeper insights than traditional data types, enabling a more detailed understanding of accidents at an unprecedented level. This enhanced understanding can significantly inform and improve worker safety training, education and safety policies, with the potential for broader applications across various safety-critical domains. Originality/value: This research presents a novel approach to analyzing construction accident reports using AI and LLMs, significantly reducing manual processing time while maintaining high accuracy. By identifying equipment failures more efficiently, our work lays the groundwork for developing targeted safety protocols, contributing to overall safety improvements in construction practices and advancing data-driven analysis processes.
Authors: Ray, U.; Arteaga, C.; Ahn, Y.; Park, J.
Construction Application Area: Construction safety, specifically equipment failure identification and accident analysis
Methodology: The research utilized a multi-stage methodology involving: (1) data collection of 800 OSHA construction accident reports (300 with equipment failures, 500 without), (2) development of a two-stage prompting framework using LLMs - first stage classifies narratives as describing equipment failures or not, second stage extracts specific equipment details, (3) manual validation by three researchers to establish ground truth, (4) testing two LLMs (ChatGPT-3.5 and InstructGPT) with various prompt formulations and parameters, and (5) categorization of equipment failures according to OSHA standards (29 CFR Part 1926).
Research Goal: The study aims to develop an automated framework using Large Language Models to identify and categorize equipment failures from unstructured OSHA accident narratives, significantly reducing manual processing time while maintaining high accuracy and aligning findings with OSHA standards.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses two significant gaps: (1) effectively using historical accident narrative data to investigate equipment failure when manual analysis is time-consuming and error-prone, and (2) understanding the classification of equipment failure according to OSHA standards, which previous studies have not systematically aligned with regulatory frameworks.
Future Research: Future work should focus on integrating this framework into real-time safety monitoring systems for dynamic hazard detection, expanding the dataset to include accident narratives from diverse industries (manufacturing, mining), and incorporating clustering algorithms with semantic similarity models to automate the categorization of similar equipment failures and refine accuracy.
Results: The LLMs demonstrated 95.13% accuracy in classifying whether narratives describe equipment failures and 73% accuracy (substring match) in extracting equipment names. The analysis revealed that heavy and mechanized equipment (22.1%) and scaffolds (15.2%) were the most frequent categories of equipment failures in construction accidents.
AI Model Used: ChatGPT-3.5 and InstructGPT (Large Language Models from OpenAI)
Data Types: Unstructured textual data consisting of 800 construction accident narrative reports from the OSHA database spanning 2011 to 2023, including descriptive accident reports with information about equipment failures, accident precursors, worker actions, and accident causes.

Title: Personalized construction safety training system using conversational AI in virtual reality
Abstract: Training workers in safety protocols is crucial for mitigating job site hazards, yet traditional methods often fall short. This paper explores integrating virtual reality (VR) and large language models (LLMs) into iSafeTrainer, an AI-powered safety training system. The system allows trainees to engage with trade-specific content tailored to their expertise level in a third-person perspective in a non-immersive desktop virtual environment, eliminating the need for head-mounted displays. An experimental study evaluated the system through qualitative, survey-based assessments, focusing on user satisfaction, experience, engagement, guidance, and confidence. Results showed high satisfaction rates (>85 %) among novice users, with improved safety knowledge. Expert users suggested advanced scenarios, highlighting the system's potential for expansion. The modular architecture supports customization across various construction settings, ensuring adaptability for future improvements.
Authors: Sabir, A.; Hussain, R.; Pedro, A.; Park, C.
Construction Application Area: Construction safety training
Methodology: The paper develops iSafeTrainer, an LLM-integrated VR safety training system that uses conversational AI (ChatGPT, Whisper for voice transcription) within a non-immersive desktop VR environment. The system employs prompt engineering to personalize training based on users' trade (electrical, scaffolding, welding) and knowledge level (novice, beginner, expert). Evaluation was conducted through questionnaire-based surveys (structured and unstructured questions) with 31 participants to assess user satisfaction, engagement, safety knowledge improvement, and system usability.
Research Goal: The goal is to develop and evaluate an AI-powered VR safety training system that provides personalized, trade-specific, and adaptive construction safety training to address the limitations of traditional one-size-fits-all training methods.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the limitations of traditional static, pre-scripted VR training scenarios that lack adaptability to different trade specializations and fail to provide immediate, personalized feedback tailored to users' expertise levels and individual learning needs.
Future Research: Future research will incorporate comparative and statistical analyses with quantitative metrics like task completion rates, hazard recognition scores, and learning retention. The system will be enhanced to develop LLM-integrated virtual instructors capable of demonstrating safety tasks and autonomously recommending video-based training content from web databases, with potential for multilingual support and trade-specific AI-driven scenario generation.
Results: The system demonstrated high satisfaction rates (>85%) among novice users with improved safety knowledge. Overall, 81% of participants were satisfied, 85% found voice interaction easy, and 85% reported improved understanding of safety hazards and mitigation strategies. However, expert users (15-16% satisfaction) suggested more advanced scenarios, indicating the need for system refinement for experienced users.
AI Model Used: ChatGPT (GPT-4), Whisper (OpenAI speech recognition and translation), Text-to-Speech (TTS)
Data Types: Survey data from questionnaires (structured Likert-scale responses and unstructured open-ended feedback) collected from 31 participants including undergraduate and graduate students and post-doctoral candidates. The system also uses 3D mesh data from Gaussian Splatting and Sketchfab, along with virtual construction site objects and hazard simulations.

Title: Internet of Things in construction: trends and adoption insights from a scientometric perspective
Abstract: The adoption of the Internet of Things (IoT) in the construction industry has garnered significant academic interest, with a notable increase in research over the past decade. This study conducts a scientometric review of 428 papers published between 2010 and 2024, aiming to identify key trends, highlight emerging themes, and offer directions for future research to avoid redundancy. Through bibliographic coupling and scientific network analysis, the review uncovers prominent co-occurring keywords and themes, such as the integration of IoT with artificial intelligence, blockchain, cloud and edge computing, Building Information Modelling (BIM), and Digital Twins. A major finding is that improving safety, particularly in worker health and underground construction, is the most frequently addressed objective. Other focal areas include post-occupancy analysis, site management, infrastructure development, life cycle management, and prefabrication. However, the research highlights significant gaps. While IoT and BIM integration are well-explored, their application to worker safety and underground construction remains limited. The combination of Digital Twins and IoT is also underutilized in design evaluation, prefabrication, and life cycle management. This review underscores the complementary roles of various technologies and calls for interdisciplinary collaboration to fully realize the potential of IoT in advancing safety and efficiency in the construction industry.
Authors: Sadeghi, J.; Ahmadi, A.; Phipps, R.
Research Location: New Zealand (Massey University, Auckland; Victoria University of Wellington, Wellington)
Construction Application Area: IoT integration with AI for safety management, worker health monitoring, site management, post-occupancy analysis, infrastructure development, life cycle management, prefabrication, Building Information Modeling (BIM), digital twins, underground construction safety, and structural analysis
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (BIM, drones, laser scanning, robotics, digital twins, sensor networks)
Methodology: The paper conducts a scientometric analysis of 428 documents published between 2011 and 2024 using bibliometric methods. Data were collected from Scopus and Google Scholar, then analyzed using VOSviewer 1.6.18 for network visualization including keyword co-occurrence and bibliographic coupling. A Python-based text mining tool was developed to process cluster data and identify dominant themes. The analysis includes publication trends, author co-authorship networks, country collaboration networks, keyword density visualization, and citation burst analysis.
Research Goal: The paper aims to provide insights into key focus areas of IoT research in construction, recognize the complementary roles of technologies such as AI, BIM, blockchain, digital twins, and cloud/edge computing, highlight the main goals of IoT adoption in construction, and identify research gaps to guide future research and avoid redundancy in the field.
Gaps Addressed: The paper identifies significant research gaps including: (1) limited exploration of IoT-BIM integration for worker safety and underground construction despite their potential, (2) underutilization of digital twin and IoT integration in design evaluation, prefabrication, and life cycle management, (3) the overlooked area of IoT application in prefabrication when examining safety promotion, and (4) the absence of research on IoT integration with Lean construction methods despite potential benefits in reducing waste and improving efficiency.
Future Research: Future research directions include investigating IoT-BIM integration for enhanced safety management through real-time monitoring and predictive analytics, exploring IoT optimization of safety protocols in prefabrication processes, assessing the role of IoT and digital twins in life cycle management from design through prefabrication, evaluating the synergy between IoT and lean construction principles, and analyzing how digital twin and IoT integration enhances construction logistics tracking and building condition monitoring.
Results: The analysis revealed that 'Building Information Modelling (BIM)' and 'artificial intelligence/machine learning' are the most frequently occurring keywords, with safety (particularly worker safety and underground construction) being the most frequently addressed objective. China, India, and the USA collectively account for 50% of the publications, with the year 2023 having the highest number of articles. The review identifies BIM as the primary complementary technology with IoT, while also highlighting the roles of digital twins, AI, blockchain, and edge/cloud computing in enhancing IoT applications in construction.
AI Model Used: No Model (This is a review paper that analyzes existing literature rather than implementing specific AI models, though it discusses AI and machine learning integration with IoT in the reviewed studies)
Data Types: Bibliometric data from 428 articles published between 2011 and 2024, collected from Scopus and Google Scholar databases. The data includes journal articles, conference papers, book chapters, titles, abstracts, keywords, author information, citations, publication years, and countries of origin. The analysis focused on metadata such as co-authorship patterns, keyword frequencies, citation bursts, and bibliographic coupling relationships.

Title: AI model for analyzing construction litigation precedents to support decision-making
Abstract: Litigation among stakeholders in construction projects has a significantly negative impact on successful project completion and overall performance. Prompt decision-making in relation to litigation is crucial, but the manual review of extensive document sets is time-consuming. In this paper, the natural language processing (NLP) technique was applied to litigation data to develop a model for case summarization and winner prediction. By automatically summarizing the data and predicting litigation outcomes, the proposed model aids practitioners in making timely decisions and enhances document management during disputes. This paper contributes to existing knowledge in two ways. Firstly, the model aids practitioners in making timely decisions about proceeding with litigation. Secondly, unlike previous studies that manually processed raw data such as contracts and specifications, this study utilized NLP to process raw litigation case data automatically. As big data becomes increasingly common, the methodology employed in this study holds academic significance.
Authors: Seo, W.; Kang, Y.
Construction Application Area: Construction litigation and dispute management, claims and dispute resolution, contract compliance
Methodology: The paper applies Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques to construction litigation precedent data. It uses BERT and RoBERTa models for auto-summarization (using TextRank, LexRank, and BERT extractive summarization) and classification prediction to determine winning stakeholders (owner vs. contractor). The dataset consists of 300 federal court cases from the Westlaw database (2004-2023), split 7:3 for training and testing.
Research Goal: The paper aims to develop an AI-based model that automatically summarizes construction dispute litigation cases and predicts litigation outcomes to support timely decision-making by practitioners during dispute management.
Gaps Addressed: Previous construction dispute prediction studies manually labeled feature attributes from raw data, which is time-consuming and omits contextual information. Existing NLP models from general legal fields do not consider the unique characteristics of construction contracts and stakeholder relationships.
Future Research: Future research should increase sample size to improve accuracy, incorporate arbitration data in addition to litigation cases, diversify output labels to include multiple stakeholders (subcontractors, engineers, suppliers), integrate various data types (numerical, drawings, photos), and develop systematic validation methods for auto-summarization quality.
Results: The RoBERTa model achieved the highest classification accuracy of 66% (F1 score of 0.655) for predicting litigation outcomes. BERT-based extractive summarization was found most appropriate for condensing lengthy legal texts. The contractor category showed higher F1 score (0.696) than the owner category (0.614).
AI Model Used: BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers), RoBERTa (A Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach), TextRank, LexRank, Word2vec, SVM, Random Forest, K-Nearest Neighbor, XGBoost
Data Types: The paper uses litigation case text data from the Westlaw database, specifically 300 federal court construction dispute cases from 2004-2023. The data includes synopsis, factual background, and procedural history sections, with court decisions labeled by winning stakeholder (owner or contractor).

Title: Leveraging data-driven machine learning techniques to enhance bearing capacity estimation in prebored and precast piles
Abstract: The rapid pace of urbanization and the increasing demand for reliable deep foundation systems in metropolitan areas necessitate advanced methodologies for accurately predicting the bearing capacity of pile foundations. This study presents a novel application of machine learning (ML) techniques to estimate the pile bearing capacity (PBC) of prebored and precast piles (PPP) constructed with pretensioned prestressed high-strength concrete (PHC). Unlike traditional approaches that rely on empirical dynamic driving formulas, this research explores the potential of ML models to provide more accurate and practical solutions for construction quality control. Three ML algorithms—K-Nearest Neighbor Regression (KNR), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGB), and Deep Neural Networks (DNN)—were employed to predict PBC at two critical stages of PPP installation: the End of Initial Driving (EOID) and Restrike phases. A dataset comprising 217 dynamic load tests was used for model development and validation. The results demonstrate that the ML models, particularly the DNN, significantly outperform traditional dynamic driving formulas such as the Hiley, Danish, and Gates methods in terms of both prediction accuracy and generalization capability. The DNN model achieved R2 values of 0.960 for EOID and 0.915 for Restrike phase predictions, with minimal prediction errors across varying site conditions. Importantly, this study introduces a direct comparison between AI-driven models and conventional formulas, providing empirical evidence that ML-based predictions are less susceptible to the overestimations and underestimations often observed in traditional methods. Furthermore, interpretability was enhanced through SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), which identified key influencing factors, such as pile diameter and ram weight, offering valuable insights for on-site decision-making and adaptive control during pile driving operations. By validating ML predictions against field-tested PDA results and widely used empirical formulas, this research establishes the practical feasibility of integrating AI into existing construction workflows. The proposed ML approach not only enhances prediction reliability but also streamlines quality control processes, reducing dependence on empirical judgments and contributing to more efficient and cost-effective project delivery.
Authors: Seo, S.; Kim, G.; Park, J.-B.; Kim, J.; Park, Y.-B.; Chung, M.
Construction Application Area: Pile foundation bearing capacity estimation, construction quality control
Methodology: The paper employs three machine learning algorithms (K-Nearest Neighbor Regression, Extreme Gradient Boosting, and Deep Neural Networks) to predict pile bearing capacity of prebored and precast piles at two critical installation stages (EOID and Restrike). A dataset of 217 dynamic load tests was used for model development and validation, with 10-fold cross-validation applied to evaluate generalization performance, and SHAP analysis used for model interpretability.
Research Goal: The study aims to develop and validate machine learning models for predicting the pile bearing capacity of prebored and precast piles (PPP) at both End of Initial Driving (EOID) and Restrike phases, providing a more accurate alternative to traditional empirical dynamic driving formulas for construction quality control.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the limitations of traditional dynamic driving formulas that oversimplify complex pile-soil interactions and are insufficient for PPP, particularly in differentiating between EOID and Restrike phases. Existing ML research has primarily focused on driven piles and drilled shafts, leaving a significant gap in understanding and predicting PPP behavior across its unique two-stage construction process.
Future Research: Future research will focus on integrating additional geotechnical parameters such as soil classification, SPT-N values, and time-dependent properties to improve prediction accuracy and model generalization. The study also suggests further refinement of models through additional training to enhance predictive accuracy in Restrike conditions.
Results: The DNN model achieved the highest prediction accuracy with R² values of 0.960 for EOID and 0.915 for Restrike phase predictions, significantly outperforming traditional dynamic driving formulas (Hiley, Danish, Gates) and showing minimal prediction errors across varying site conditions. All ML models demonstrated superior performance compared to traditional formulas, with predictions requiring no dynamic reduction factor (DRF) adjustments and maintaining consistent accuracy across different energy transfer ratios (ETR) and set values.
AI Model Used: K-Nearest Neighbor Regression (KNR), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGB), Deep Neural Networks (DNN)
Data Types: The study uses a dataset of 217 dynamic load tests from prebored and precast piles (PPP) constructed with pretensioned prestressed high-strength concrete (PHC). Input variables include pile diameter, pile length, drop height, ram weight, set values, and elapsed time, with pile bearing capacity as the output variable measured through CAPWAP analysis from field PDA tests.

Title: Neural network cost estimating model for utility rehabilitation projects
Abstract: Purpose – The purpose of this paper is to present an artificial intelligent (AI) system for estimating the construction cost of water and sewer rehabilitation projects. Design/methodology/approach – To develop the proposed system, data pertaining to 54 sewer and water rehabilitation projects was collected. The collected data were analyzed using Pareto analysis technique to identify the most important factors that contribute positively to the cost estimation process. These factors were then utilized to develop a neural network (NN) model that estimates the construction cost of this class of projects. Findings – The study reveals a set of 23 factors that highly impact the construction cost of water and sewer network rehabilitation projects and presents a NN model that predicts the cost of these projects with high accuracy. Research limitations/implications – The proposed system was developed using information obtained from the city of San Diego, California, USA. The cost of these projects ranged from $800,000 to $7 million. The diameter of pipes installed in these projects ranged from 1 in. to 36 in. and their length was up to about 2.7 miles. Originality/value – The developed system saves time, improves the accuracy of the estimates and prevents problems that are usually associated with inaccurate estimates. The system will not only help funding authorities to ensure maximum utilization of resources, but will also help cities to manage their expenditures in a manner that assures satisfactory performance of their buried assets. Furthermore, the developed system is also believed to assist cities in comparing alternatives and the go/no-go decision making process. © 2013, Emerald Group Publishing Limited.
Authors: Shehab, T.; Farooq, M.
Construction Application Area: Cost estimating for utility rehabilitation projects (water and sewer pipeline rehabilitation)
Methodology: The study collected data from 54 water and sewer rehabilitation projects built in California (2000-2004). Pareto analysis was performed to identify the most important cost factors (23 bid items from up to 180 total). A back-propagation neural network with one hidden layer was developed using 60% training data, 20% testing data, and 20% production data. The network was trained to predict construction costs based on the identified factors.
Research Goal: To develop an artificial neural network model that accurately estimates the construction cost of water and sewer rehabilitation projects at the conceptual stage using minimum input information and to improve upon existing city estimating practices.
Gaps Addressed: Current city practices use limited parameters (mainly pipe diameter and length) which may not produce accurate cost estimates, leading to problems such as withholding projects for future fiscal years and/or constructing projects below optimum standards. The paper addresses the need for reliable conceptual cost estimates that reflect lessons learned from previous projects.
Future Research: A larger set of training data is required to further improve the system's performance, particularly for projects with unique characteristics such as rare cost ranges or unusual diameter specifications that are underrepresented in the current dataset.
Results: The developed neural network model predicted costs with an average accuracy of 0.32% compared to actual costs, significantly outperforming the owner's in-house estimating system which had an average accuracy of 25%. The model predicted 80% of the production set projects within 20% accuracy using only 23 bid items instead of up to 180.
AI Model Used: Back-propagation neural network with one hidden layer
Data Types: Historical project data including bid items from 54 projects costing $800,000 to $7 million, with pipe diameters from 1 to 36 inches and lengths up to 2.7 miles. Data included 23 key bid items covering pipes and laterals, manholes, sidewalks, pavement, soil, and services/assemblies. All costs were indexed to 2005.

Title: Digital workflow for printability and prefabrication checking in robotic construction 3D printing based on Artificial Intelligence planning
Abstract: This paper introduces an innovative digital workflow for printability and prefabrication checking in robotic construction 3D printing based on Artificial Intelligence (AI) planning techniques. The proposed method generates operational sequences for a robotic manipulator tasked with concrete 3D printing by utilizing Planning Domain Definition Language (PDDL) 2.1-based AI planners. The planned sequences consider diverse geometry specifications, aligning with printability checks and prefabrication requisites. The sequences can then be executed in a robotic simulation environment, allowing operators to assess the 3D printing process in every aspect before real-world deployment, and potentially avoiding costly mistakes and the waste of resources. Compared to existing methods, the use of AI planning makes our approach more modular and easy to adapt across a variety of robotic platforms, diverse designs, materials, and printing conditions; and it is released as open-source software to the benefit of the community. The proposed method was tested by processing objects with multiple geometries and a varying number of printing layers, and by employing two different PDDL planners. Results prove the viability of the approach but also hint at possible scalability limitations for complex geometries that involve a large number of edges to be printed.
Authors: Shojaei Barjuei, E.S.; Capitanelli, A.; Bertolucci, R.; Courteille, E.; Mastrogiovanni, F.; Maratea, M.
Construction Application Area: 3D printing, robotic construction, printability checking, prefabrication, process planning
Methodology: The paper develops an AI planning framework using Planning Domain Definition Language (PDDL) 2.1 to generate operational sequences for robotic concrete 3D printing. The approach employs PDDL planners (POPF and LPG) to create high-level task sequences, which are then executed in a robotic simulation environment (CoppeliaSim) using ROS, ROSPlan, and MoveIt for motion planning and control of a UR10 robot manipulator.
Research Goal: To introduce an innovative digital workflow for printability checking and prefabrication in robotic construction 3D printing using AI planning techniques. The system allows operators to assess the 3D printing process in a virtual environment before real-world deployment, potentially avoiding costly mistakes and waste of resources.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the non-adaptive nature of current additive manufacturing methods and the challenges in printability assessment and prefabrication for robotic construction 3D printing. It tackles the need for a systematic, modular, and easily adaptable approach across various robotic platforms, designs, materials, and printing conditions.
Future Research: Future work will advance the AI planning framework by integrating additional extensions for real-time adaptation and implementing it in real-world concrete 3D printing operations using industrial robotic manipulators. The framework will be expanded to incorporate considerations for filament geometry, temperature specifications, and materials compatibility.
Results: The proposed method successfully generated valid plans for 3D printing operations in simulation, meeting essential requirements for printability checking and prefabrication. However, scalability analysis revealed that runtime scales non-linearly with the number of edges and layers, with the LPG planner showing better performance than POPF for complex geometries.
AI Model Used: PDDL 2.1-based AI planners: POPF (Partial Order Planning Forwards) and LPG (Local Search for Planning Graphs)
Data Types: The paper uses geometric specifications of 3D objects including vertex coordinates, edge angles and lengths, printing velocities, layer thickness, nozzle dimensions, resting time between layers, and robot kinematic data. Test objects included various closed-shape and open-shape geometries with multiple layers.

Title: REAL-TIME PIPE SYSTEM INSTALLATION SCHEDULE GENERATION AND OPTIMIZATION USING ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND HEURISTIC TECHNIQUES
Abstract: Infrastructure systems in the United States are aging and considerable investment is needed to renew and replace a significant proportion of the existing systems. Piping systems, which are used in many infrastructure systems such as the distribution networks for utilities - water, sewage, gas, oil, etc., are very important in this regard. Real time scheduling is an important and necessary task in the planning and execution of construction projects. This is of particular importance in the installation of pipe systems, for which it is time consuming to plan and coordinate between team members the detailed requirements and information for the generation of practical installation schedules. During the installation stage, there can be delays or interference that could lead to the failure of the initial schedule plan. Current approaches are time-consuming, not automated and do not provide real-time schedules. Thus, the process is still fragmented and essentially manual, with inefficient information flow. To effectively improve the installation schedule, current knowledge of the installation site situation is important, with this knowledge being used to generate realistic schedules. Artificial intelligence (AI) maximizes the value of data by learning from previous cases and facilitates decision-making by making the process smarter and automatic. This paper proposes a new AI framework with machine learning (ML) and heuristic optimization techniques for automating practical pipe system installation schedule generation and optimization. A BIM model is used as reference to provide pipe system component information. A hybrid knowledge-based system is developed to integrate data-driven knowledge base and site-driven knowledge base on pipe system installation. K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) and Graph Neural Network (GNN) ML techniques are adapted to map extracted components with the installation activities and their requirements for installation based on knowledge obtained from industry experts and piping codes. In addition, a heuristic algorithm is adopted to optimize the installation schedule. Finally, an optimal installation schedule that minimizes overlapping activities, time and cost is suggested.
Authors: Singh, J.; Anumba, C.J.
Journal: Journal of Information Technology in Construction
Construction Application Area: Pipe system installation scheduling and planning
Methodology: The paper proposes an AI-based framework combining machine learning (K-Nearest Neighbor and Graph Neural Network) with heuristic optimization (Simulated Annealing) to automate pipe system installation schedule generation. A hybrid knowledge-based system integrates data-driven and site-driven knowledge bases, with BIM models providing component information for schedule optimization based on spatial constraints and resource availability.
Research Goal: The goal is to develop an AI-based framework to automate and optimize realistic installation schedules for pipe systems that minimizes overlapping activities, time, and cost while incorporating real-time site conditions and constraints.
Gaps Addressed: Current scheduling approaches are time-consuming, manual, not automated, and do not provide real-time schedules that account for on-site uncertainties and delays. The process is fragmented with inefficient information flow, and limited studies exist on applying schedule optimization specifically to pipe system installation.
Future Research: Future research will focus on detailed quantification of cost savings based on the framework and combining data mining techniques with schedule optimization to automatically create data-driven knowledge bases from historical data.
Results: The proposed framework achieved 97% time savings in generating optimal schedules compared to traditional approaches (3-4 minutes vs. 150 minutes for 13 pipe systems). GNN technique performed better than KNN, producing shorter schedule times (33.79h vs. 34.02h for one crew without delay).
AI Model Used: K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN), Graph Neural Network (GNN), Simulated Annealing (SA)
Data Types: The paper uses geometric and semantic information extracted from BIM models (pipe system locations, coordinates, dimensions, project type, system type, component parameters) and knowledge-based data from literature, industry experts, historical project documents, and real-time site observations (activity packages, productivity rates, delays).

Title: Leveraging large-scale vision foundation models for automated construction and demolition waste recognition
Abstract: Effective handling of construction and demolition waste (CDW) is crucial for sustainable resource recovery in industrial settings. This study introduces WasteXtract, a vision-based AI model for class-specific segmentation of CDW designed to automate waste monitoring and sorting at material recovery facilities (MRFs). The theoretical contribution of this research lies in adapting large-scale vision foundation models by incorporating a class-specific mask decoder and a lightweight adapter in a ViT-based image encoder. This approach enables efficient and scalable fine-tuning for the industrial application of CDW recognition, achieving reliable waste detection and classification performance. The engineering applications of WasteXtract are validated across two datasets representing real-world recognition of waste composition in skip-bins and conveyor-belt scenarios for intelligent automation and optimised resource recovery. The segmentation results show that WasteXtract achieves mean weighted intersection over union (WIoU) scores of 0.66 in skip-bin waste composition monitoring and 0.96 in the conveyor-belt scenario. Compared to baseline models such as DeepLabv3+ and U-Net, WasteXtract achieves significantly higher segmentation accuracy while reducing the number of trainable parameters, demonstrating its suitability for deployment in real-world, resource-constrained environments such as MRFs. This study highlights the transformative role of informatics in advancing automation and efficiency within sustainable waste management.
Authors: Sirimewan, D.; Kineber, A.F.; Raman, S.; Garcia, R.; Arashpour, M.
Construction Application Area: Construction and demolition waste management, waste recognition, waste monitoring and sorting at material recovery facilities
Methodology: The paper proposes WasteXtract, a vision-based AI model that adapts the Segment Anything Model (SAM) by incorporating a class-specific mask decoder and lightweight adapter modules in a ViT-based image encoder. The model uses parameter-efficient fine-tuning, training only 15.89 million of 105.57 million parameters. It employs weighted categorical cross-entropy loss to address class imbalance and is validated on two datasets representing skip-bin and conveyor-belt scenarios.
Research Goal: The goal is to develop an efficient and scalable AI model for automated class-specific segmentation of construction and demolition waste (CDW) to enhance waste monitoring and sorting processes at material recovery facilities, reducing reliance on manual labor and improving resource recovery.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the gap in leveraging large-scale vision foundation models (LVMs) for construction and demolition waste recognition. Specifically, it tackles the class-agnostic nature of LVMs like SAM, which cannot distinguish between different material types, and the need for parameter-efficient adaptation methods for domain-specific CDW segmentation tasks.
Future Research: Future research will explore semi-supervised, weakly supervised, or prompt-driven learning strategies to reduce reliance on exhaustive manual labeling. Additionally, the study will investigate integration of segmentation outputs into robotic picking and sorting systems, enabling evaluation through practical metrics such as object throughput and cycle time in real-world MRF environments.
Results: WasteXtract achieved mean weighted IoU (WIoU) scores of 0.66 on Dataset 1 (skip-bin scenario) and 0.96 on Dataset 2 (conveyor-belt scenario), significantly outperforming baseline models U-Net and DeepLabv3+. The model demonstrated strong performance while using only 15% of trainable parameters, reducing training time substantially and achieving precise segmentation even with imperfect annotations.
AI Model Used: Segment Anything Model (SAM) with Vision Transformer (ViT-base) encoder, adapted with lightweight adapter modules and a class-specific mask decoder. Baseline comparisons were made with U-Net and DeepLabv3+ with ResNet-101 backbone.
Data Types: Two image datasets of construction and demolition waste: Dataset 1 contains 5,277 annotated object masks from skip-bin perspectives with 9 waste classes (concrete, fill dirt, timber, hard plastic, soft plastic, steel, fabric, cardboard, plasterboard). Dataset 2 (CODD) contains 3,129 images with 16,545 annotated samples from conveyor-belt perspectives with 10 waste classes (concrete, brick, tile, plastic, wood, foam, stone, pipes, gypsum board, general waste).

Title: Codification Challenges for Data Science in Construction
Abstract: New forms of data science, including machine learning and data analytics, are enabled by machine-readable information but are not widely deployed in construction. A qualitative study of information flow in three projects using building information modeling (BIM) in the late design and construction phase is used to identify the challenges of codification that limit the application of data science. Despite substantial efforts to codify information with common data environment (CDE) platforms to structure and transfer digital information within and between teams, participants work across multiple media in both structured and unstructured ways. Challenges of codification identified in this paper relate to software usage (interoperability, information loss during conversion, multiple modelling techniques), information sharing (unstructured information sharing, drawing and file based sharing, document control bottlenecks, lack of process change), and construction process information (loss of constraints and low level of detail). This paper contributes to the current understanding of data science in construction by articulating the codification challenges and their implications for data quality dimensions, such as accuracy, completeness, accessibility, consistency, timeliness, and provenance. It concludes with practical implications for developing and using machine-readable information and directions for research to extract insight from data and support future automation.
Authors: Soman, R.K.; Whyte, J.K.
Journal: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Construction Application Area: Data science and machine learning applications in construction, including BIM (Building Information Modeling), information management, scheduling, resource management, and digital collaboration
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (BIM, drones, laser scanning, robotics, digital twins, sensor networks)
Methodology: The paper employs a qualitative case study approach examining three construction projects (a multistory residential building in the UK, a metro rail project in India, and a water infrastructure megaproject in the UK). Data was collected through site visits, document analysis, interviews (formal and informal), and observations of meetings. Within-case and cross-case analyses were conducted to identify codification challenges that limit machine readability and data quality.
Research Goal: The paper aims to identify codification challenges that limit the application of data science in construction by examining information sharing practices across BIM-enabled projects in the late design and construction phases, and to understand how these challenges impact data quality dimensions.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the gap in understanding why data science is not widely deployed in construction despite digitization efforts. While previous research has focused on BIM implementation and technical solutions for data management, this paper examines organizational and technical practices that prevent codification of information and result in poor data quality for data science applications.
Future Research: Future research should elaborate on organizational issues across different project phases and stakeholders, develop fundamental data science methods for integrating disconnected construction information, create algorithms for detecting data quality discrepancies, and develop workflows that ensure machine readability while addressing fragmentation, hybrid practices, and institutionalized practices in construction.
Results: The paper identifies codification challenges in three categories: software usage (interoperability, information loss during conversion, multiple modeling techniques), information sharing (unstructured sharing, drawing/file-based sharing, document control bottlenecks, lack of process change), and construction process information (loss of constraints, low level of detail). These challenges negatively impact data quality dimensions including accuracy, completeness, timeliness, consistency, accessibility, and provenance, limiting the uptake of data science in construction.
AI Model Used: No Model
Data Types: Qualitative data including interview transcripts (8 semi-structured interviews, 6 informal interviews), field notes from site visits and office observations, project documents (BIM models, construction programs, work package plans, BIM execution plans, design workflows), CDE access records, meeting observations, and workshop outputs. Data was collected across three construction projects over multiple site visits.

Title: Converting detailed estimates to primary estimates with data augmentation
Abstract: In general, preliminary or primary cost estimates are used to select contractors from among bidders in Japan. The primary cost estimate must be accurate, otherwise the contractor selected from the bidding process will lose profit. A general contractor in the world does not have a super-skilled engineer who can achieve the accurate primary cost estimates. The conventional primary estimate has a high error range and low reliability. An automated system converting detailed estimates to primary estimates has been highly demanded in the world. This paper presents a prototype AI converter that can accurately and automatically convert detailed cost estimates into primary estimates. Converting detailed cost estimates to primary estimates lies in a regression problem. This paper proposes a feature-elimination based data augmentation method for regression problems. The empirical experiment shows that the proposed data augmentation method is quite effective with an Extra-Trees ensemble method. The proposed method was empirically examined by using Colorado Department of Transportation (CDOT) dataset for accurately predicting constructions costs with the Extra-Trees algorithm and random forest algorithm respectively. The CDOT dataset is one and only one of the largest datasets available in public for constructions costs quotation/estimation of roads, bridges and buildings.
Authors: Takefuji, Y.
Construction Application Area: Cost estimation - specifically converting detailed cost estimates to primary/preliminary estimates for construction projects involving roads, bridges, and buildings
Methodology: The paper proposes a feature-elimination based data augmentation method for regression problems. The method computes feature importance of all input parameters, reorders them by importance values, extracts parameters based on feature-importance, and incrementally adds parameters to find the optimal subset. The approach was tested using Extra-Trees and Random Forest algorithms on the CDOT dataset.
Research Goal: To build an accurate primary cost estimator from detailed cost estimations and demonstrate the effectiveness of a proposed data augmentation method that can convert detailed estimates (with thousands of parameters) into accurate primary estimates (with minimal parameters) without requiring super-skilled human engineers.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of an accurate and efficient conversion method from detailed estimates to primary estimates. It also addresses the scarcity of super-skilled engineers (one in 5000 employees in Japan) who can produce accurate primary cost estimates, and the unreliability of conventional primary estimates which have large error ranges.
Future Research: The paper does not explicitly mention future research directions. The focus is on demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed method using the CDOT dataset.
Results: The proposed data augmentation method achieved prediction accuracy of 0.77743 using only 42 input parameters with Extra-Trees algorithm, compared to 0.71187 using all 8,747 parameters. With Random Forest, the method achieved prediction accuracy of 0.7648 with less than 100 parameters. The method successfully generated accurate primary cost estimates with significantly fewer parameters while improving prediction accuracy.
AI Model Used: Extra-Trees algorithm and Random Forest algorithm
Data Types: The Colorado Department of Transportation (CDOT) dataset containing 1,458 construction project instances with 8,751 parameters for construction of roads, bridges, and buildings. The dataset was divided into training data (1,093 instances) and test data (365 instances).

Title: Machine-learned kinetic Façade: Construction and artificial intelligence enabled predictive control for visual comfort
Abstract: The authors present the first on-site investigation of artificial intelligence (AI)-integrated three-dimensionally movable kinetic façade (KF). Despite continued architectural interest on the KF to improve indoor visual comfort, its in-situ operational strategy has been little addressed. To examine our primary hypothesis that the adaptive KF controlled by AI models improves indoor daylight probability (DGP) in real time, we developed an electromagnetic hexagonal KF mechanism, and three machine-learning (ML) regressors (eXtreme Gradient Boosting (XGB), Random Forest (RFR), Decision Tree) were implemented on a Raspberry Pi board to control the KF (width = 1.73 m, height = 1.1 m). 20,000 data from Radiance were used for model construction, and illuminance sensors were installed for on-site validation in a private office mockup. The façade shape was optimally morphed every 90s, using differential evolution. In the verification, XGB showed the greatest accuracy (R2 = 91.2%) with decent prediction time efficiency (μ = 0.58 s), but the RFR accuracy (R2 = 79.8%) slightly outperformed XGB in the field.
Authors: Takhmasib, M.; Lee, H.J.; Yi, H.
Construction Application Area: Kinetic facade design and operation, building envelope automation, visual comfort control, daylight glare optimization
Methodology: The paper develops an AI-integrated kinetic facade (KF) system by fabricating a full-scale hexagonal KF mockup with electromagnetic mechanisms. Three machine learning models (XGBoost, Random Forest Regressor, Decision Tree) were trained on 20,000 simulated data points from Radiance to predict Daylight Glare Probability (DGP). The models were implemented on a Raspberry Pi board to control facade shape morphing every 90 seconds using differential evolution optimization, with on-site validation through illuminance sensors in a private office mockup.
Research Goal: The paper aims to demonstrate and validate an AI-based predictive control method for kinetic facades that can optimize indoor daylight glare probability (DGP) in real-time through dynamic three-dimensional shape morphing, addressing the gap between simulation-based facade studies and actual field implementation.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses three main gaps: (i) insufficient evidence of field KF construction and on-site investigation of visual comfort, (ii) lack of data-driven AI-controlled KF operation beyond simulation-based methods, and (iii) inability to explain the contribution of individual KF module designs in terms of ML prediction performance.
Future Research: Future research should focus on full seasonal validation of the AI models across different climate conditions and occupancy scenarios, implementation of online learning through continuous data accumulation during actual operation, and exploration of the system's performance in different building contexts with evaluation of energy efficiency and occupant satisfaction.
Results: The XGB model showed the greatest accuracy (R²=91.2%) with efficient prediction time (μ=0.58s) in simulation verification, while RFR accuracy (R²=79.8%) slightly outperformed XGB in field validation. The AI-controlled KF successfully maintained appropriate indoor daylight levels (500-800 lx) and kept DGP values between 0.24-0.26, below the acceptable discomfort threshold, demonstrating the feasibility of real-time adaptive facade control.
AI Model Used: eXtreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), Random Forest Regressor (RFR), Decision Tree Regressor (DTR), with additional benchmarking of Gradient Boosting (GB), Histogram-based Gradient Boosting (HGB), Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), and Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)
Data Types: The study uses 20,000 simulated data points from Radiance daylight simulation software with Latin hypercube sampling, including features such as day (1-31), month (1-12), hour (9-18), modular KF panel opening angles (0-60 degrees), site altitude, azimuth, and global illuminance. Field validation data was collected from on-site illuminance sensors (TSL2591 and VEML7700) during May 11-19, 2023, along with EnergyPlus weather data for Suwon, South Korea.

Title: Dense 3D Reconstruction of Building Scenes by AI-Based Camera-Lidar Fusion and Odometry
Abstract: Scanning is a key element for many use cases in the architectural, engineering, construction, and operation industry. It provides point clouds used for construction quality assurance, scan-to-building information modelling (BIM) workflows, and construction surveys. However, data acquisition using static laser scanners or photogrammetry methods is labor-intensive during scanning and postprocessing. Mobile scanners are conceptually the solution to this problem, given their potential to dramatically reduce onsite scanning effort and eliminate postprocessing work. However, current mobile mapping devices are limited to producing point clouds of relatively low resolution. In this paper, we propose a dense three-dimensional (3D) reconstruction pipeline for improving the resolution of point clouds, suitable for handheld scanners comprised of a color camera and a lidar. We fuse time-synchronized and spatially registered images and lidar sweeps using a spatial artificial intelligence (AI) method into dense scans of higher resolution, which are then used for progressive reconstruction. The novelty of our approach is that we first increase the precision and density of a bunch of individual lidar scans by inferring additional geometric constraints coming from predicted feature maps in the corresponding images. Then, we automatically register these scans together, thus reconstructing the scene progressively in an odometric manner. We built a prototypic scanner, implemented our reconstruction pipeline as a software package, and tested the whole in both indoor and outdoor case studies. The results showed that our method provided an overall noise reduction in point clouds by 11% and increased their density around six times.
Authors: Trzeciak, M.; Brilakis, I.
Journal: Journal of Computing in Civil Engineering
Construction Application Area: 3D reconstruction, scanning, point cloud processing, quality assurance, scan-to-BIM workflows, construction surveys
Methodology: The paper proposes a dense 3D reconstruction pipeline that fuses camera images and lidar scans using spatial AI methods. First, they increase the resolution of individual lidar scans by inferring geometric constraints from images (surface normals and occlusion boundaries predicted by neural networks) and using these to guide depth optimization. Then, they register these enhanced scans together using an ICP-based odometric approach to progressively reconstruct the scene.
Research Goal: The goal is to improve the resolution (precision and density) of point clouds from handheld mobile scanners by combining lidar and camera data through AI-based fusion, enabling near-real-time dense 3D reconstruction suitable for construction applications.
Gaps Addressed: Current mobile scanners produce point clouds with insufficient resolution to recognize important features and meet AECO industry requirements. Existing methods either focus on trajectory accuracy (odometry) or single-view densification (IGDC), but not on progressive dense reconstruction with improved precision. The paper addresses the need for a near-real-time method that improves both precision and density of point clouds.
Future Research: Future work includes extending to full SLAM with loop closures to prevent drift accumulation over longer distances, investigating trajectory estimation performance compared to other odometry methods, improving camera-lidar extrinsic calibration and time synchronization, integrating hardware into a compact handheld device with mini-PC, implementing neural network pruning for real-time processing, and experimenting with supervised/self-supervised training techniques and different cost functions to overcome smarming effects on edges.
Results: The method achieved an 11% reduction in point cloud noise (at 95th percentile) and increased density approximately 6-7 times compared to A-LOAM. Indoor reconstruction showed clearer feature recognition, flatter surfaces, and better detail preservation. Outdoor results showed improved stair visibility though with some artifacts on vertical edges. Processing times were near real-time with GPU (2 seconds longer than A-LOAM).
AI Model Used: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for surface normal estimation and occlusion boundary detection, based on the Zhang and Funkhouser (2018) approach
Data Types: Time-synchronized streams of RGB images from a Ximea camera and 3D point clouds from a Velodyne VLP-16 lidar sensor, collected from indoor (room with furniture) and outdoor (college building) scenes. Ground truth data from FARO Focus 3D terrestrial laser scanner for outdoor validation.

Title: Harnessing the power of ChatGPT to promote Construction Hazard Prevention through Design (CHPtD)
Abstract: Purpose: Construction Hazard Prevention through Design (CHPtD) is recognized as a pivotal strategy for enhancing workplace safety. Despite its theoretical potential, practical implementation faces challenges, particularly regarding designers' familiarity with field-level operations and hazard recognition. This study aims to investigate whether ChatGPT can address these challenges by assisting in hazard recognition during CHPtD sessions. Design/methodology/approach: The research utilized a randomized controlled experiment to assess ChatGPT as an intervention in supporting hazard recognition during CHPtD sessions. The study involves 162 civil and construction engineering student participants, representing future professionals. Participants engaged in hazard recognition during CHPtD sessions either with or without the assistance of ChatGPT. Findings: Participants who utilized ChatGPT during CHPtD sessions demonstrated a significant improvement in hazard recognition, identifying approximately 40% more hazards compared to those who did not use ChatGPT. These findings underscore the efficacy of ChatGPT in supporting CHPtD efforts. Practical implications: The results highlight the practical utility of harnessing ChatGPT in CHPtD sessions to enhance hazard recognition and ultimately promote workplace safety. By leveraging ChatGPT, designers and engineers can better anticipate and mitigate potential hazards during the design phase, thus creating a safer working environment for field workers. Originality/value: This research contributes to addressing the challenges associated with implementing CHPtD by introducing ChatGPT as a valuable tool to support hazard recognition. By demonstrating the effectiveness of ChatGPT in enhancing hazard identification during CHPtD sessions, this study offers a novel approach to promoting the adoption of CHPtD and advancing workplace safety initiatives.
Authors: Uddin, S.M.J.; Albert, A.; Tamanna, M.
Construction Application Area: Construction safety, specifically Construction Hazard Prevention through Design (CHPtD) and hazard recognition
Methodology: The research utilized a randomized controlled experiment with 162 civil engineering students. Participants were randomly assigned to either a treatment group (ChatGPT usage) or control group (non-ChatGPT usage). Both groups performed simulated CHPtD sessions examining three building elements (rooftop skylight installation, structural steel girder installation, interior drywall installation) to identify safety hazards. Hazard recognition scores were calculated and compared using one-way ANOVA with blocking and two-way ANOVA.
Research Goal: The study aimed to investigate whether ChatGPT can address challenges in CHPtD implementation by assisting in hazard recognition during CHPtD sessions. The objective was to test the hypothesis that ChatGPT can be leveraged to achieve superior hazard recognition performance compared to when it is not utilized.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses two major barriers to CHPtD adoption: designers' lack of adequate training to anticipate potential hazards associated with their design choices, and their limited familiarity with construction operations required to bring their designs to life, which causes them to overlook hazards that could arise during field operations.
Future Research: Future research should validate findings across diverse samples and settings beyond a single university, encompass a broader spectrum of building elements, explore complementary tools to address remaining unrecognized hazards, and evaluate ChatGPT's ability to propose alternative design solutions to mitigate identified hazards effectively.
