Results: Participants who utilized ChatGPT during CHPtD sessions demonstrated significantly improved hazard recognition, identifying approximately 40% more hazards (64.25%) compared to those who did not use ChatGPT (24.49%). The performance improvement was consistent across all three building elements examined, with no significant variation based on the type of building element.
AI Model Used: ChatGPT (OpenAI's conversational AI language model based on GPT-3.5 architecture)
Data Types: The study used real design drawings and specifications from two local commercial construction projects (a 5-story building valued at over $150 million and a 4-story building valued at over $50 million). Field observations, pre-task safety meetings, job hazard analysis reports, interviews with workers, foremen, and supervisors were used to identify and validate hazards associated with selected building elements. Student responses identifying hazards during the experimental sessions were collected and analyzed.

Title: Strength and durability predictions of ternary blended nano-engineered high-performance concrete: Application of hybrid machine learning techniques with bio-inspired optimization
Abstract: Higher-strength, longer-lived, and environmentally friendly high-performance concrete (HPC) necessitates advanced formulation of materials and predictive modeling techniques. The traditional designs of high-performance concrete often fail to optimize the mix parameters suitably, mainly from the point of sustainability view, and lack the capacity to model complex nonlinear relationships among material properties and performance metrics. The current paper introduces the integrated framework of nano-engineered materials that comprises nano-silica, graphene oxide (GO), and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) along with advanced artificial intelligence (AI) methods in the predictive modeling and optimization of high-performance concrete (HPC). The Machine Learning (ML) models consisting of Random Forest (RF), Gradient Boosting Machines (GBM), and Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) were implemented to predict compressive and tensile strengths, elastic modulus, and durability metrics such as chloride penetration and sulfate resistance sets. Dimensionality reduction techniques, such as Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Partial Least Squares (PLS), improved model interpretability and performance sets. Optimization of mix designs was carried out using bio-inspired algorithms, namely Genetic Algorithms (GA) and Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), to balance mechanical performance, material efficiency, and environmental sustainability. The results suggest strength improvement up to 15% and durability up to 30% while reducing cement content and associated carbon emissions. This is the framework, which exploits artificial intelligence techniques in predictive modeling and optimization to achieve scalable and sustainable next-generation high-performance concrete designs. It is at the intersection of theoretical potential and practical application that the work is advancing the role of artificial intelligence in construction material science toward contributing to sustainable infrastructure development process.
Authors: Vairagade, V.S.; Bahoria, B.V.; Isleem, H.F.; Shelke, N.; Mungle, N.P.
Construction Application Area: High-performance concrete (HPC) material design, optimization of concrete mix proportions, prediction of mechanical properties (compressive strength, tensile strength, elastic modulus), and durability metrics (chloride penetration, sulfate resistance)
Methodology: The paper employs an integrated framework combining dimensionality reduction techniques (PCA and PLS), machine learning models (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting Machines, Deep Neural Networks), and bio-inspired optimization algorithms (Genetic Algorithms and Particle Swarm Optimization) to predict and optimize the performance of nano-engineered high-performance concrete. The framework uses synthetic and real datasets enriched with nano-material concentrations, curing conditions, and environmental exposure parameters.
Research Goal: The goal is to develop a comprehensive predictive and optimization framework for high-performance concrete incorporating nano-engineered materials (nano-silica, graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes) that maximizes mechanical strength and durability while minimizing cement content and environmental impact through advanced AI techniques.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses gaps in traditional HPC design methods that fail to optimize mix parameters for sustainability, lack capacity to model complex nonlinear relationships between material properties and performance metrics, and insufficient integration of nano-materials with advanced predictive modeling and optimization techniques.
Future Research: Future research directions include expanding datasets with more diverse environmental conditions and material properties, incorporating additional performance metrics (shrinkage, creep, thermal properties), implementing real-time data and IoT-based feedback mechanisms during concrete production, conducting life-cycle assessments for sustainability implications, and exploring hybrid dimensionality reduction approaches combining PCA and KPCA.
Results: The proposed model achieved up to 15% improvement in compressive strength (reaching 88.2-98.4 MPa) and up to 30% improvement in durability metrics compared to existing methods, while reducing cement content by approximately 5% and achieving 20-40% reductions in chloride penetration and sulfate attack, demonstrating superior optimization efficiency with an R² of 0.93 for DNNs.
AI Model Used: Random Forest (RF), Gradient Boosting Machines (GBM), Deep Neural Networks (DNNs), Genetic Algorithms (GA), Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), Principal Component Analysis (PCA), Partial Least Squares (PLS), Multivariate Regression (MVR)
Data Types: The study uses the UCI Concrete Compressive Strength Dataset (1030 samples) enriched with synthetic data representing nano-material concentrations (nano-silica, graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes), water-cement ratios, curing conditions, environmental exposure parameters, and performance metrics including compressive strength, tensile strength, elastic modulus, chloride penetration, and sulfate resistance measurements.

Title: Transfer learning-based query classification for intelligent building information spoken dialogue
Abstract: Retrieving queried information from building information models (BIM) requires experience in structured query languages and manipulation of BIM software. Artificial Intelligence (AI)-based spoken dialogue systems provide more opportunities for information retrieval from building information models via natural language queries. This research developed a transfer learning-based text classification (TC) method to classify different queries into pre-defined categories for an intelligent building information spoken dialogue system (iBISDS), a virtual assistant that provides information retrieval support for construction project team members. The architecture of the TC neural network (NN) was built based on the pre-trained Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach (RoBERTa). After the training process, the re-trained and fine-tuned RoBERTa NN achieved a precision of 99.76%, a recall of 99.76%, and an F1 score of 99.76% on the testing dataset. The experimental results indicated that the developed NN algorithm for TC can relatively accurately classify different building information-related queries into pre-defined TC categories.
Authors: Wang, N.; Issa, R.R.A.; Anumba, C.J.
Construction Application Area: Building information modeling (BIM) information retrieval, natural language understanding for spoken dialogue systems
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (BIM, drones, laser scanning, robotics, digital twins, sensor networks)
Methodology: The paper develops a transfer learning-based text classification method using the pre-trained RoBERTa neural network. A BINLQ dataset of 2,065 building information-related natural language queries was created and manually classified into 11 pre-defined categories. The dataset was split 80/20 into training-validation and testing sets. Ten independent experiments were conducted with different random splits to train and validate the model, with the best performing model evaluated on the test set.
Research Goal: To develop a transfer learning-based text classification method to classify natural language queries into pre-defined categories for an intelligent building information spoken dialogue system (iBISDS), enabling construction practitioners to retrieve information from BIM models via natural language queries.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the limited availability of labeled training data for building information-related natural language queries in the construction industry. It also addresses the need for more efficient information retrieval from BIM models without requiring expertise in structured query languages and BIM software manipulation.
Future Research: Future research should develop more diverse natural language queries for iBISDS, collect more building information models with different naming conventions, and develop queries for more building elements and information types. State-of-the-art deep learning models like DeBERTa and StructBERT + CLEVER should be explored to improve natural language understanding performance.
Results: The re-trained RoBERTa neural network achieved a precision of 99.76%, recall of 99.76%, and F1 score of 99.76% on the testing dataset. The model outperformed BERT (99.28% F1 score) and BiLSTM RNN (64.90% F1 score), demonstrating that the transfer learning-based approach can accurately classify building information-related queries.
AI Model Used: RoBERTa (Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach), BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers), BiLSTM RNN (Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory Recurrent Neural Network)
Data Types: The data consists of 2,065 building information-related natural language queries manually generated based on two architectural building information models (one academic building and one residential building in IFC4 ADD2 TC1 schema), plus 50 out-of-domain queries from the Stanford Question Answering Dataset (SQuAD). Queries were manually classified into 11 pre-defined categories related to attribute and quantity information of building elements.

Title: Time series prediction of tunnel boring machine (TBM) performance during excavation using causal explainable artificial intelligence (CX-AI)
Abstract: Since early warning is significant to ensure high-quality tunneling boring machine (TBM) construction, a real-time prediction method based on TBM data is proposed. To solve the "black box" problem of prediction by artificial intelligence (AI) methods, the causal explainable gated recurrent unit (CX-GRU) is developed to achieve real-time prediction for TBM parameters. The approach is implemented in a tunnel construction project in Singapore and the results indicate that CX-GRU performs well with the R square score are 0.9140 and 0.9184 in real-time prediction for thrust force and soil pressure. The causal discovery component can increase the computational efficiency of model training by 8.8% on average. According to the SHAP analysis of prediction results, the thrust force is more sensitive to the input TBM features, while the soil pressure is more sensitive to historical data. The CX-GRU is more reliable and efficient when applied to TBM projects than traditional methods.
Authors: Wang, K.; Zhang, L.; Fu, X.
Construction Application Area: Tunnel boring machine (TBM) performance prediction during excavation, specifically real-time prediction of thrust force and soil pressure
Methodology: The paper proposes CX-GRU (Causal Explainable Gated Recurrent Unit), which combines causal discovery using Direct-LiNGAM for feature selection, GRU neural networks for time series prediction, and SHAP (Shapley Additive Explanations) for model interpretability. The method performs global causal discovery to filter input features, local causal discovery to mine relationships within samples, and uses SHAP to explain prediction results at each time step.
Research Goal: To develop an interpretable real-time prediction method for TBM parameters that addresses the 'black box' problem of AI methods while maintaining high accuracy and computational efficiency. The goal is to enable reliable early warning and parameter adjustment during TBM construction.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses three main gaps: (1) traditional AI methods lack interpretability in TBM parameter prediction, (2) existing approaches do not fully utilize time-series characteristics of TBM data, and (3) current methods lack efficient feature selection mechanisms and cannot explain the contribution of individual features and time steps to predictions.
Future Research: Future work includes: (1) exploring real-time prediction results by incorporating different soil parameters as input indicators, and (2) implementing multi-objective optimization algorithms based on CX-GRU predictions to obtain optimal combinations of TBM parameters for better control.
Results: CX-GRU achieved R² scores of 0.9140 and 0.9184 for thrust force and soil pressure prediction respectively. The causal discovery component improved computational efficiency by 8.8% on average while maintaining or improving accuracy. The model outperformed traditional deep learning (LSTM, MLP) and machine learning methods (SVR, XGBoost), and demonstrated robust multi-step prediction capability (3 steps for thrust force, 8 steps for soil pressure).
AI Model Used: CX-GRU (Causal Explainable Gated Recurrent Unit), Direct-LiNGAM (for causal discovery), SHAP (for explainability). Comparison models include LSTM, MLP, SVR, and XGBoost
Data Types: Real-time TBM operational data collected from sensors during tunnel excavation, including 22 TBM parameters such as penetration, cutterhead torque, thrust cylinders data, articulation displacement, and deviation measurements. The study used 12,190 excavation data samples from 200 continuous tunnel rings from Singapore's T308 MRT tunnel construction project and validation data from Wuhan Metro Line 16.

Title: Global Sensitivity Analysis Methodology for Construction Simulation Models: Multiple Linear Regressions versus Multilayer Perceptions
Abstract: In this research, the multilayer perceptron (MLP), also known as error backpropagation neural networks, is made transparent and explainable by contrasting with the commonly applied multiple linear regression (MLR). A novel MLP-based method for performing global sensitivity analysis is formalized to tackle complicated, nonexplainable simulation models or artificial intelligence (AI) models, which were developed to support critical decisions in construction engineering. The sensitivity analysis results serve as further evidence to validate the decision support models and lend new insights into the problems under investigation. The proposed new method was applied in two case studies in construction engineering, they are: precast viaduct installation cycles and concrete strength development. In both applications, the results of sensitivity analysis were represented in straightforward forms and effectively cross-checked with the existing knowledge of the problem domain or the experiences of construction practitioners.
Authors: Wang, S.; Hasan, M.; Lu, M.
Journal: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Construction Application Area: Construction productivity modeling, precast viaduct installation cycles, concrete strength development, construction operations and logistics planning
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (AI and simulation models / sensitivity analysis)
Methodology: The paper proposes a novel MLP-based global sensitivity analysis method by making multilayer perceptron (MLP) transparent and explainable through comparison with multiple linear regression (MLR). The methodology involves calculating first-order derivatives of MLP to determine sensitivity measures for each input factor, then interpreting these sensitivities by relating them to MLR slopes and intercepts. This approach is demonstrated through two case studies: precast viaduct installation cycles and concrete strength development.
Research Goal: To develop a global sensitivity analysis methodology for construction simulation models and AI models that makes MLP as easily explainable as MLR, thereby promoting the application of explainable AI solutions in construction engineering and revealing the reasoning mechanism of complicated, nonexplainable numerical models.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the knowledge gap in how to enhance model sensitivity analysis methods to reveal the reasoning mechanism of complicated simulation or AI models in construction engineering. Specifically, it tackles the limitation that one-factor-at-a-time sensitivity analysis often leads to confusion and inconsistencies, and it is not straightforward to generalize underlying patterns to form a global view of multivariate sensitivities.
Future Research: While not explicitly stated as future research directions, the paper suggests that the proposed MLP-based approach holds potential to facilitate global sensitivity analysis on any complicated, nonexplainable numerical models such as agent-based simulations, continuous simulations, and large, complex optimization formulations in construction engineering.
Results: The MLP model achieved higher prediction accuracy than MLR in both case studies (R² of 0.98 vs 0.80 for viaduct case; 0.77 vs 0.61 for concrete case). The sensitivity measures derived from MLP were found to be consistent with MLR and validated against existing domain knowledge, with MLP providing sensitivity ranges rather than constant values, offering more sophisticated insights into input-output relationships while maintaining interpretability.
AI Model Used: Multilayer Perceptron (MLP), also known as error backpropagation neural networks or artificial neural networks (ANN); Multiple Linear Regression (MLR)
Data Types: Two datasets were used: (1) A simulation-generated dataset of 30 instances for precast viaduct installation cycles with four input factors (number of trucks, delivery mode, percentage of segments at remote storage, transport time) and one output (crew hours per cycle); (2) A large dataset of 1,030 instances from UCI Machine Learning Repository for concrete strength prediction with eight attributes (cement, blast furnace slag, fly ash, water, superplasticizer, coarse aggregate, fine aggregate, age) and compressive strength as output.

Title: AI-powered NUN-SEDFN framework for addressing sparse data challenges in geotechnical parameter prediction
Abstract: Accurate and comprehensive geological parameter acquisition is a persistent challenge in engineering geological mapping, particularly in construction environments with complex conditions where conventional drilling is impractical. Addressing the issue of sparse drilling data, this study introduces a novel prediction framework combining Non-Uniform Normalization (NUN) and a Spectral-Enhanced Deep Fusion Network (SEDFN). The proposed framework enhances the ability to predict geotechnical characteristic parameters critical for construction and infrastructure management. Specifically, the NUN-SEDFN framework transforms sparse textual drilling data into high-resolution geotechnical parameter maps by leveraging advanced AI techniques for data processing and prediction. The characterization stage employs NUN to ensure robust mapping between geotechnical data and image representations, addressing challenges in integrating large-span geological feature parameters. The prediction stage uses Frequency-Domain High Preservation Fast Fourier Convolution (FHP-FFC) and a Modified Super Resolution Convolutional Neural Network (mSRCNN) to learn and reconstruct high- and low-frequency geotechnical features, achieving over 80% prediction accuracy. This method enhances the reliability of geological mapping, offering significant potential for optimizing resource allocation, cost reduction, and safety in engineering and construction tasks. Furthermore, it demonstrates how AI can address data scarcity and improve decision-making in construction environments, aligning with current industry needs and technological trends.
Authors: Wang, Z.; Gao, R.; Hu, X.
Construction Application Area: Engineering geological mapping, geotechnical parameter prediction, foundation design, site investigation, geological surveying
Methodology: The paper proposes a NUN-SEDFN framework combining Non-Uniform Normalization (NUN) for data processing and a Spectral-Enhanced Deep Fusion Network (SEDFN) for prediction. NUN transforms sparse textual drilling data into image representations, while SEDFN uses modified SRCNN (mSRCNN) for low-frequency features and Frequency-domain High-preservation Fast Fourier Convolution (FHP-FFC) for high-frequency features, achieving super-resolution reconstruction of geotechnical parameters.
Research Goal: The paper aims to develop an AI-powered framework to predict geotechnical characteristic parameters from sparse drilling data, addressing challenges in engineering geological mapping where conventional drilling is impractical. The goal is to enhance accuracy and reliability of geological parameter acquisition for construction and infrastructure management.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the limitation of traditional methods that can only predict single parameters requiring multiple training processes, and their insufficient accuracy in complex geological conditions. It also tackles the challenge of handling large-span geotechnical feature parameters and sparse data (MUSIC data) in engineering geological surveys.
Future Research: Future work will focus on small-sample data prediction to address limited dataset sizes from actual engineering projects. The study also plans to explore strategies like data augmentation, regularization, and early stopping to mitigate potential overfitting, and to improve performance in areas with complex geological conditions such as lens-like bodies or karst caves.
Results: The NUN-SEDFN framework achieved over 80% prediction accuracy across three projects, with mean similarity indices of 89.0%, 86.4%, and 83.3% for Projects 1, 2, and 3 respectively. The method outperformed traditional linear interpolation and other CNN-based methods (SRCNN, FSRCNN, ESPCN) by approximately 10% in challenging geological scenarios, demonstrating superior capability in predicting geotechnical parameters from sparse data.
AI Model Used: Modified Super Resolution Convolutional Neural Network (mSRCNN), Frequency-domain High-preservation Fast Fourier Convolution (FHP-FFC), combined into Spectral-Enhanced Deep Fusion Network (SEDFN). Comparison models include SRCNN, FSRCNN, and ESPCN.
Data Types: The data consists of geotechnical characteristic parameters from three engineering projects in Wuhan, including foundation bearing capacity, compression modulus, and rock/soil state from drilling surveys. The original textual data from drilling sites is converted to image data using Non-Uniform Normalization (NUN) for training and prediction.

Title: The impact of AI on construction supply chain performance: a mediated moderation model
Abstract: Purpose – During the ongoing digital transformation, enhancing construction supply chain performance (CSCP) has become a central objective within the construction industry. This study aims to investigate how artificial intelligence (AI) enhances CSCP by examining the mediating role of supply chain integration (SCI) and the moderating role of supply chain dynamism (SCD). It addresses key research gaps in understanding the mechanisms through which AI impacts CSCP in the construction industry. Design/methodology/approach – A conceptual model was developed to explore the relationships among AI, SCI, SCD and CSCP. SCI was conceptualized through operational, informational and relational integration. A survey was conducted with 336 middle and senior managers from Chinese construction enterprises. Data were analyzed using partial least squares structural equation modeling to test the proposed hypotheses. Findings – The results demonstrate that AI significantly improves CSCP through its positive effects on operational, informational and relational integration. Furthermore, the positive impact of AI on SCI is amplified under conditions of high SCD. These findings confirm the mediating role of SCI and the moderating role of SCD, highlighting the conditions under which AI most effectively enhances CSCP. Originality/value – This study advances supply chain performance research by uncovering the mediating mechanism of SCI and identifying the contextual importance of SCD in the construction sector. It fills notable gaps in existing literature, which has predominantly focused on manufacturing contexts and offers actionable insights for practitioners aiming to leverage AI for supply chain optimization and digital transformation in construction.
Authors: Wang, L.; Zeng, Y.; Xu, Y.; Cheng, M.
Construction Application Area: Construction supply chain performance, supply chain integration (operational, informational, and relational integration), supply chain management, digital transformation
Methodology: A conceptual model was developed to explore the relationships among AI, supply chain integration (SCI), supply chain dynamism (SCD), and construction supply chain performance (CSCP). A survey was conducted with 336 middle and senior managers from Chinese construction enterprises. Data were analyzed using partial least squares structural equation modeling (PLS-SEM) to test the proposed hypotheses, including direct effects, mediating effects of SCI, and moderating effects of SCD.
Research Goal: The study aims to investigate how artificial intelligence (AI) enhances construction supply chain performance (CSCP) by examining the mediating role of supply chain integration (SCI) and the moderating role of supply chain dynamism (SCD). It addresses key research gaps in understanding the mechanisms through which AI impacts CSCP in the construction industry.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of empirical research on factors influencing CSCP, particularly the scarcity of studies that consider AI as a key factor. It fills gaps in understanding how AI impacts CSCP through SCI and how SCD moderates this process, as existing literature has predominantly focused on manufacturing contexts rather than construction.
Future Research: Future research should employ more rigorous sampling techniques to enhance sample representativeness, expand the sample size to include cross-regional and cross-national comparative analyses, and adopt longitudinal designs to systematically investigate long-term interaction mechanisms. A mixed-methods approach integrating quantitative surveys with in-depth qualitative interviews is also recommended.
Results: The results demonstrate that AI significantly improves CSCP through its positive effects on operational, informational, and relational integration. The positive impact of AI on SCI is amplified under conditions of high supply chain dynamism (SCD). Among the three dimensions, information integration has the most significant mediating effect, followed by relational and operational integration.
AI Model Used: No specific AI model. The paper studies AI as a general technology and its impact on construction supply chain performance.
Data Types: Survey data collected from 336 middle and senior managers from Chinese construction enterprises. The data includes responses on AI adoption, supply chain integration dimensions (operational, informational, and relational), supply chain dynamism, and construction supply chain performance, measured using 5-point Likert scales.

Title: Applications of AI in oil and gas projects towards sustainable development: a systematic literature review
Abstract: Oil and gas construction projects are critical for meeting global demand for fossil fuels, but they also present unique risks and challenges that require innovative construction approaches. Artificial Intelligence (AI) has emerged as a promising technology for tackling these challenges, and this study examines its applications for sustainable development in the oil and gas industry. Using a systematic literature review (SLR), this research evaluates research trends from 2011 to 2022. It provides a detailed analysis of how AI suits oil and gas construction. A total of 115 research articles were reviewed to identify original contributions, and the findings indicate a positive trend in AI research related to oil and gas construction projects, especially after 2016. The originality of this study lies in its comprehensive analysis of the latest research on AI applications in the oil and gas industry and its contribution to developing recommendations for improving the sustainability of oil and gas projects. This research's originality is in providing insight into the most promising AI applications and methodologies that can help drive sustainable development in the oil and gas industry.
Authors: Waqar, A.; Othman, I.; Shafiq, N.; Mansoor, M.S.
Construction Application Area: Safety management, resource management, sustainable management, risk management, project scheduling and monitoring, construction using robots, logistics management, supply chain management, financial analysis, scenario analysis, cost management, service life improvements, operations, drilling and completion, predictive maintenance, energy savings
Methodology: The paper employs a systematic literature review (SLR) methodology involving five stages: selection of objectives, selection of appropriate databases (Scopus, Google Scholar, ScienceDirect), keyword-based search using terms like Artificial Intelligence, Oil and Gas Projects, Sustainable Development, selection of compatible research papers based on inclusion criteria, and extraction and analysis of data from 115 research articles published between 2011 and 2022.
Research Goal: The goal is to examine AI applications for sustainable development in the oil and gas industry through a systematic literature review, evaluate research trends from 2011 to 2022, provide detailed analysis of how AI suits oil and gas construction, and develop recommendations for improving sustainability of oil and gas projects.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of comprehensive systematic literature reviews on practical applications of AI in oil and gas projects toward sustainable development, and the need for more information on AI techniques and realistic approaches that could improve outcomes in the oil and gas industry construction projects.
Future Research: Future research should focus on improving AI applications in safety management, resource management, risk analysis, and sustainability aspects of oil and gas projects. There is a need for research combining different databases to improve quantitative and qualitative understanding of AI implications for sustainable development, and to expand beyond journal articles to include other literature types.
Results: The findings indicate a positive trend in AI research related to oil and gas construction projects, especially after 2016, with maximum publications in 2020-2021. Developed countries (USA, UK, UAE, China) show higher research contributions. AI interventions have been successfully applied across construction, operation, and deconstruction phases of oil and gas projects, with significant applications in machine learning, neural networks, predictive maintenance, deep learning, big data analytics, and digital technologies.
AI Model Used: Machine Learning, Neural Networks, Deep Learning, Artificial Neural Networks, Big Data Analytics, Decision Support Systems, Predictive Modelling
Data Types: The paper uses bibliometric data from 115 research articles published between 2011-2022, extracted from Scopus, Google Scholar, and ScienceDirect databases. The data includes publication trends, journal contributions, geospatial distribution of research, and AI applications across different phases of oil and gas construction projects.

Title: A systematic review and network meta-analysis of the risks of artificial intelligence in construction projects
Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) technologies are deployed in construction projects to analyze a large corpus of data, identify patterns in the data, infer from the data, and provide relevant insights to inform decisions in construction projects. However, the scientific literature recognized multiple risks of AI in construction projects. Therefore, this study investigated the critical risk factors for implementing AI in construction projects. The findings revealed forty-nine (49) critical risk factors for AI in construction projects, which have data, technical, knowledge, management, financial, and legal dimensions. It is discovered that the risk factors are disproportionately discussed in theoretical studies and underrepresented in decision-making models/frameworks. It is also established that the existing studies failed to leverage the sociotechnical perspective to jointly optimize risk management for AI deployment in construction projects. Thus, the study provides better insights and establishes a fertile ground for future investigation into the critical risk factors for AI in construction projects.
Authors: Wuni, I.Y.
Construction Application Area: This is a systematic review covering multiple AI applications in construction including: automated planning and scheduling, construction cost prediction, automated site layout design, automated design review and code compliance checking, design optimization, building load prediction, construction defect detection, real-time construction site monitoring, detection of cracks in concrete structures, retrofitting adviser for energy savings, and leak detection in subsurface infrastructure
Methodology: The paper employs a systematic literature review (SLR) approach combined with meta-analysis and social network analysis (SNA). The study searched Scopus database using keywords related to risks and AI in construction, resulting in 59 articles reviewed. Reference matrices were created to map risk factors against theoretical discussions (Matrix T) and quantitative decision-making models (Matrix M). Normalized scores and degree centralities were calculated to identify gaps and interdependencies among critical risk factors.
Research Goal: The study aims to critically examine the scientific literature and extract critical risk factors for implementing AI in construction projects, categorize these risk factors into various dimensions, and conduct social network analysis to establish gaps in the literature.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the gap that AI risks in construction projects are implicitly discussed, scattered, fragmented, and buried in various literature, depriving stakeholders of a holistic framework of critical risk factors. It also reveals that existing studies predominantly view AI risks from a technical perspective rather than a sociotechnical perspective, and fail to jointly optimize risk management for AI deployment.
Future Research: Future research should incorporate all important critical risk factors into both theoretical discussions and decision-making models, particularly those with high normalized scores and degree centralities. Studies should adopt a sociotechnical perspective to investigate AI risks rather than purely technical perspectives. The significance of isolated risk factors (R7, R9, R17, R36, R39, R40, R42, R46) should be assessed based on actual data to confirm their relevance.
Results: The study identified 49 critical risk factors for AI implementation in construction projects, categorized into six dimensions: data (18 factors), technical (12 factors), knowledge (6 factors), management (7 factors), financial (3 factors), and legal (3 factors). The analysis revealed that risk factors are disproportionately discussed in theoretical studies and underrepresented in decision-making models, with data and technical risk factors receiving the most attention while management and legal risk factors only gained attention in recent years (2019-2023).
AI Model Used: No Model
Data Types: The study used bibliometric data extracted from 59 peer-reviewed journal articles published between 2017 and 2023 from Scopus database. Meta-data included publication year, listed critical risk factors, and type of article (theoretical discussion vs. quantitative modeling). The data was analyzed using reference matrices and social network analysis to examine co-occurrences and interdependencies among risk factors.

Title: Critical success factors for implementing artificial intelligence in construction projects: A systematic review and social network analysis
Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly deployed to automate routine tasks, generate accurate insights from big data, and build predictive models to inform better decision-making in construction projects. However, AI deployment in construction projects constitutes a sociotechnical process, such that adopting solely a technical approach becomes inadequate. This study investigated the critical success factors for implementing AI in construction projects. It combined a systematic literature review, meta-analysis, and social network analysis to evaluate the scientific evidence on the critical success factors, and quantitatively reveal the underrepresented factors. The meta-analysis identified 38 critical success factors, ranked according to normalized scores and degree centralities. The study derived four dimensions of the critical success factors, including organizational, technological, stakeholder, and data success factors. The social network analysis quantitatively revealed the strengths and existing gaps in the reviewed studies and provide insights into factors that need further investigation.
Authors: Wuni, I.Y.
Construction Application Area: The paper addresses AI implementation across the entire construction project lifecycle including planning, design, construction, and operation stages. It covers multiple areas such as cost prediction, scheduling, safety monitoring, design optimization, defect detection, waste management, energy consumption prediction, and asset management.
Methodology: The paper employs a systematic literature review (SLR) combined with meta-analysis and social network analysis (SNA). It reviewed 60 articles from Scopus, Web of Science, and ResearchGate databases (2008-2023), extracted 38 critical success factors (CSFs), classified them into four dimensions (organizational, technological, stakeholder, and data factors), and used simplified analysis and SNA to calculate normalized scores and degree centralities to reveal underrepresented factors and research gaps.
Research Goal: The study aims to investigate and comprehensively catalogue the critical success factors for implementing AI in construction projects, organize them into coherent dimensions, and quantitatively reveal the underrepresented factors in existing literature to provide guidance for effective AI deployment and future research directions.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses three main gaps: (1) lack of comprehensive knowledge about the spectrum of CSFs for AI deployment in construction projects, (2) absence of organized dimensions/categories of the CSFs, and (3) no study has specifically investigated the network of CSFs to reveal underrepresented factors in the literature.
Future Research: Future research should: (1) incorporate all identified CSFs in comprehensive computational models and decision-making frameworks, (2) investigate underrepresented factors such as CSF21 (strong expertise of AI developers), CSF3 (risk management), CSF18 (computing capabilities), CSF27 (familiarity with AI tools), and CSF34 (cybersecurity), (3) gather empirical data to assess the significance and weights of the 38 CSFs in specific contexts, and (4) use alternative network modeling techniques like Bayesian belief networks, interpretive structural modeling, or structural equation modeling to explore causal relationships between CSFs.
Results: The study identified 38 CSFs organized into four dimensions: organizational (15 factors), technological (11 factors), stakeholder (5 factors), and data (7 factors). The top five most documented CSFs are: top management commitment and support (65.0%), high quality data (55.0%), adequate financial resources (48.3%), conducive organizational culture (48.3%), and alignment with project strategy (46.7%). The SNA revealed significant gaps between theoretical discussions and computational modeling, with factors like strong AI developer expertise (CSF21) being well-discussed theoretically but never incorporated in mathematical models.
AI Model Used: No Model
Data Types: The data consists of secondary literature data extracted from 60 peer-reviewed journal articles published between 2008-2023, including metadata such as publication year, critical success factors mentioned, and article types (theoretical, mathematical/structural models, or mixed). The study analyzed citation frequencies, co-occurrences, and reference matrices to construct social network models.

Title: Effects of the Blockchain and Artificial Intelligence on Value Cocreation in Construction Projects: A Mixed Methods Study
Abstract: Emerging technologies - blockchain and artificial intelligence (AI) - have revolutionized the operational model of construction organizations by developing a new paradigm of value cocreation within construction projects. However, it remains unclear how blockchain and AI could foster value cocreation in construction projects. Dynamic capability theory (DCT) and task-technology fit theory (TTFT) were utilized to address this knowledge gap by constructing a theoretical model that elucidates the relationship among blockchain, AI, and value cocreation in construction projects. A mixed-method approach, combining quantitative and qualitative studies, was deployed to unveil the results. A total of 296 survey responses were collected from construction professionals, and partial least squares-structural equation modeling (PLS-SEM) was utilized to assess the model. Semi-structured interviews were conducted with 15 construction professionals to validate the study's findings, and the grounded theory method was employed to solidify the theoretical framework. The findings indicate that blockchain has a significant positive effect on AI, and both blockchain and AI have significant positive effects on value cocreation in construction projects. Moreover, government support and task-technology fit moderate the impact of blockchain and AI on value cocreation, thereby enhancing the value cocreation of construction projects. This study not only contributes to knowledge extension by clarifying the relationship among blockchain, AI, and value cocreation in construction projects but also provides valuable insights for engineering and construction firms seeking to effectively utilize blockchain and AI.
Authors: Xu, Y.; Chi, M.; Lee, C.-Y.; Chong, H.-Y.; Wu, H.
Journal: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Construction Application Area: Value cocreation in construction projects, project management, construction supply chain management, resource optimization, risk prediction, quality control, cost management, and collaborative decision-making
Methodology: The study employs a mixed-methods approach combining quantitative and qualitative methods. Quantitatively, 296 survey responses were collected from construction professionals and analyzed using partial least squares-structural equation modeling (PLS-SEM) to test hypotheses. Qualitatively, 15 semi-structured interviews were conducted with construction professionals, and grounded theory method was employed for thematic analysis through open, axial, and selective coding.
Research Goal: The study aims to empirically examine how blockchain and artificial intelligence (AI) technologies influence value cocreation in construction projects, and to explore the moderating roles of government support and task-technology fit in this relationship.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the limited empirical evidence on how blockchain and AI impact value cocreation in construction projects, and the lack of understanding about boundary conditions such as government support and task-technology fit that affect the successful application of these technologies.
Future Research: Future research should expand the geographic scope beyond China, examine the interactive effects of blockchain and AI integration on value cocreation, investigate the moderating role of project uncertainty or environmental uncertainty, and employ more diverse probabilistic sampling methods to improve generalizability.
Results: Blockchain has a significant positive effect on AI development, and both blockchain and AI have significant positive impacts on value cocreation in construction projects. Government support and task-technology fit positively moderate the relationships between blockchain/AI and value cocreation, with task-technology fit showing a stronger moderating effect than government support on the AI-value cocreation relationship.
AI Model Used: No specific AI model
Data Types: The study uses two types of data: (1) quantitative survey data collected from 296 construction professionals using a structured questionnaire with Likert scales measuring blockchain capabilities, AI capabilities, government support, task-technology fit, and value cocreation; and (2) qualitative data from 15 semi-structured interviews with construction experts having 6-16 years of experience.

Title: Advancing Bridge Construction Monitoring: AI-Based Building Information Modeling for Intelligent Structural Damage Recognition
Abstract: Building Information Modeling (BIM) has emerged as a transformative technology in the construction industry, revolutionizing various aspects of the field. The integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) techniques with BIM holds significant promise and is gaining momentum in interdisciplinary applications. In China, the construction industry has witnessed notable advancements through the convergence of BIM, AI, and cloud data. However, the current state of intelligent construction technology in China reveals certain limitations that hinder its comprehensive development. This study addresses these challenges by focusing on the design of intelligent recognition algorithms for monitoring structural damage during bridge construction. Previous research has primarily employed classical neural network algorithms, but these approaches have exhibited certain limitations. This paper proposes innovative improvement measures to overcome these limitations and demonstrates their effectiveness through practical arithmetic examples. Furthermore, to enhance the intelligence level of the BIM system, this study integrates the improved neural network recognition algorithm into the BIM framework. The integration enables the BIM system to recognize and assess bridge structure damage efficiently and accurately. The outcomes of this research provide valuable insights into advancing the field of intelligent construction technology, particularly in the context of bridge construction monitoring.
Authors: Yang, H.; Xia, M.
Construction Application Area: Bridge construction monitoring, structural damage recognition and identification
Methodology: The paper develops an improved neural network algorithm (combining BP neural network with additional momentum term and Levenberg-Marquardt acceleration algorithm) integrated with BIM technology for bridge structural damage identification. The system uses finite element analysis to generate damage sample libraries with different damage conditions (10%-50% at 10% intervals) at five control sections, extracting the first 10 orders of frequency as characterization parameters.
Research Goal: To design an intelligent recognition algorithm for monitoring structural damage during bridge construction by integrating AI techniques with BIM technology, improving upon classical neural network algorithms to enhance the accuracy and efficiency of bridge structure damage identification.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses limitations of classical neural network algorithms in bridge structural damage identification, including slow convergence speed, tendency to fall into local minima, and inability to fully utilize engineering data value. It also addresses the lack of intelligent construction technology development in China and insufficient integration of BIM with AI technologies.
Future Research: Future plans involve incorporating recurrent neural networks and knowledge graphs into the methodologies to bolster the capabilities of bridge construction monitoring and lead to further advancements in intelligent technology.
Results: The improved neural network algorithm achieved an average recognition error of 0.56% for identifying 10% damage and 1.21% for 80% damage, compared to the classical algorithm's errors of 0.77% and 1.66% respectively, demonstrating significant improvement in accuracy for bridge structural damage identification.
AI Model Used: BP neural network (Back Propagation neural network) with improvements including additional momentum term and Levenberg-Marquardt (LM) acceleration algorithm
Data Types: The data consists of structural frequency information (first 10 orders of self-oscillation frequencies) extracted from finite element analysis simulations of bridge structures under various damage conditions at five different locations (side span 1/4 L, side span 1/2, top of pier, mid-span 1/4 L, and mid-span 1/2) with damage levels ranging from 10% to 50%.

Title: Computational narrative mapping for the acquisition and representation of lessons learned knowledge
Abstract: Lessons learned knowledge is traditionally gained from trial and error or narratives describing past experiences. Learning from narratives is the preferred option to transfer lessons learned knowledge. However, learners with insufficient prior knowledge often experience difficulties in grasping the right information from narratives. This paper introduces an approach that uses narrative maps to represent lessons learned knowledge to help learners understand narratives. Since narrative mapping is a time-consuming, labor-intensive and knowledge-intensive process, the proposed approach is supported by a computational narrative mapping (CNM) method to automate the process. CNM incorporates advanced technologies, such as computational linguistics and artificial intelligence (AI), to identify and extract critical narrative elements from an unstructured, text-based narrative and organize them into a structured narrative map representation. This research uses a case study conducted in the construction industry to evaluate CNM performance in comparison with existing paragraph and concept mapping approaches. Among the results, over 90% of respondents asserted that CNM enhanced their understanding of the lessons learned. CNM's performance in identifying and extracting narrative elements was evaluated through an experiment using real-life narratives from a reminiscence study. The experiment recorded a precision and recall rate of over 75%.
Authors: Yeung, C.L.; Wang, W.M.; Cheung, C.F.; Tsui, E.; Setchi, R.; Lee, R.W.B.
Construction Application Area: Safety, knowledge management, lessons learned, incident narratives
Methodology: The paper develops a Computational Narrative Mapping (CNM) methodology that uses natural language processing (NLP) and named entity recognition (NER) to automatically convert narrative texts into structured narrative maps. Two algorithms are developed: Narrative Element Classification Algorithm (NECA) and Narrative Element Organization Algorithm (NEOA). NECA extracts narrative elements in SVORLT format (subject, verb, object, relevant information, location, time), while NEOA organizes these elements based on Labov's narrative model (abstract, orientation, complication, resolution, evaluation, coda).
Research Goal: The paper aims to develop an automated computational approach to construct narrative maps from lessons learned narratives to help learners better understand past experiences. It addresses the challenge that manual narrative mapping is time-consuming, labor-intensive, and knowledge-intensive by automating the process through AI technologies.
Gaps Addressed: Current lessons learned systems and narrative databases only provide basic search functions without facilitating user understanding and learning. Learners with insufficient prior knowledge experience difficulties grasping correct information from narratives. Manual narrative mapping by domain experts is inconsistent, time-consuming, and labor-intensive.
Future Research: Future work includes applying machine learning to enhance the expert rule-based approach, extending CNM as an evaluation tool for teachers to assess student understanding of narratives, and applying CNM to analyze and summarize narrative-based news or articles from various sources for learning and decision-making.
Results: Over 90% of respondents agreed that the CNM narrative map approach helped them better understand narratives and learn correct lessons compared to traditional paragraph and concept map formats. In the experiment-based evaluation, CNM achieved 75.7% precision and 75.4% recall in narrative element classification, outperforming the baseline GATE algorithm (55.8% precision, 48.3% recall).
AI Model Used: Natural Language Processing (NLP), Named Entity Recognition (NER), General Architecture for Text Engineering (GATE), Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging
Data Types: Text-based narratives from the construction industry describing incidents where workers fell from heights, and personal life story book texts from a reminiscence study. The data consists of unstructured narrative texts that are processed and converted into structured narrative maps.

Title: Embodied AI for dexterity-capable construction Robots: DEXBOT framework
Abstract: The construction industry is integrating robots into critical tasks at an accelerated pace, with the aim of enhancing efficiency, safety, and productivity. However, construction tasks requiring dexterity remain a challenge due to the need for precise movements, accurate perception, real-time decision-making, and a comprehensive understanding of the environment. To address these challenges, the introduction of embodied artificial intelligence (AI) represents a significant shift in robotic capabilities to enhance their alignment with the broader spectrum of construction settings. Rooted in cognitive science, embodied AI emphasizes the integration of an agent's physical form into its computational intelligence processes. It resembles how humans develop motor skills by interacting with physical world. This paper introduces DEXBOT, an exploratory framework for designing construction robots capable of high dexterity using embodied AI principles that mimics human strategies in complex tasks. The framework outlines six key perspectives for solving high-dexterity tasks with embodied AI: scene understanding, localization and motion planning, position-based control, force-based control, sequence planning, and correction decision-making. By presenting preliminary test cases for each perspective, the paper emphasizes the role of embodied AI in advancing dexterity level of construction robots. The DEXBOT framework is expected to encourage interdisciplinary collaboration of designing capable construction robots in the future.
Authors: You, H.; Zhou, T.; Zhu, Q.; Ye, Y.; Du, E.J.
Construction Application Area: Construction robotics, dexterous manipulation tasks, structural assembly, material handling, quality inspection, pipefitting, instrument installation, and electrical wiring
Methodology: The paper proposes the DEXBOT framework, which integrates embodied AI principles into construction robotics through six key perspectives: scene understanding, localization and motion planning, position-based control, force-based control, sequence planning, and correction decision-making. The framework is validated through preliminary test cases using physics-based simulations, reinforcement learning, and imitation learning in virtual environments with accurate physical properties.
Research Goal: To introduce a comprehensive framework (DEXBOT) for designing dexterity-capable construction robots using embodied AI principles that enhance robots' abilities to perform complex, high-precision tasks requiring fine motor skills, precise manipulation, and adaptive responses to unpredictable construction environments.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of a systematic framework for integrating embodied AI into construction robotics, which is needed to enable robots to perform high-dexterity tasks. It bridges the gap between existing robotics/AI methods and their application in diverse, dynamic, and unstructured construction environments where sophisticated human-robot collaboration is required.
Future Research: Future research should focus on translating theoretical advances to real-world field applications, conducting robust field testing and data collection, enhancing environmental robustness and cognitive adaptability of robots, developing seamless human-robot collaboration mechanisms, and exploring correction-rework-discard decision-making methodologies. Additional research is needed on developing efficient algorithms, specialized hardware, data augmentation techniques, and scalable modular robotic systems.
Results: The preliminary test cases demonstrated that embodied AI significantly improves construction robot capabilities across all six framework perspectives. For example, embodied reinforcement learning achieved 98.6% success rate in path planning (vs. 95.6% for visual SLAM), completed tasks in 475 frames (vs. 2134 for traditional methods), and showed higher success rates in position-based control (95.2-96.3% vs. 69.7-80.6%) and force-based control (92.5% vs. 77.9%).
AI Model Used: Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL), Deep Imitation Learning (DIL), PointNet++, Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), Behavioral Cloning, Generative Adversarial Imitation Learning, Large Language Models (LLMs including ChatGPT-4), Semantic Segmentation networks
Data Types: The paper uses synthetic data generated from physics-based simulations including point cloud data from LiDAR sensors, RGB-D camera data, force/torque sensor data, haptic feedback data, and object properties data (mass, friction, dimensions, materials). Virtual training environments were created using Universal Scene Description (USD) with prefab libraries containing physical properties of construction objects.

Title: Application of artificial intelligence in coal mine ultra-deep roadway engineering—a review
Abstract: The deep integration of computer field and coal mining field is the only way to coal mine intellectualization. A variety of artificial intelligence tools have been applied in open-pit and shallow coal mines. However, with the geometric increase of coal demand, the contradiction between supply and demand is becoming more and more serious, and the exploitation of resources from shallow layer (> 600 m) has become an inevitable trend. Well then, as a new engineering scene, the harsh conditions of "three high and one disturbance" seriously threaten the safety of personnel. The superposition of complex mining environment makes the number of input factors increase sharply, which leads to the application of artificial intelligence in deep coal mine roadway engineering. The guidance is not mature, the construction of various databases is missing, and there are still some problems in universality and applicability. To this end, this paper starts with the introduction of the operating characteristics of various artificial intelligence tools, and conducts a comprehensive study of the relevant high-level articles published in top journals. It systematically sorts out the research progress that has successfully solved the five directions of rock mechanics strength, surrounding rock stability, rock-burst, roof fall risks and micro-seismic events. While objectively evaluating the comprehensive performance of different tools, it also expounds its own views on key research and results. Literature review shows that whether as a development tool or a comparative model, the application of ANN in the field of coal mining is more than 98%, and it performs extremely well in the direction of surrounding rock stability and roof fall risk, with an accuracy rate of more than 90%. As the most mature direction of AI tools application, rock mechanical strength has experienced the development process from "SVM → ANN → DL → XGBoost → RF". The dataset is from small samples (< 100) to big samples (> 1000), and the R2 of tree-based models can be stabilized at more than 95%. The research on rock-burst prediction mainly focuses on field factors and micro-seismic monitoring data. Whether it is a small sample or a large-scale data model, the accuracy of BN remains above 85%. The prediction and evaluation of micro-seismic events is a new direction in recent years. The image processing and application of CNN is extremely important. The signal recognition and classification accounts for more than 90%, and the research potential of source location needs to be further explored. In general, the nature of the rock itself is the first choice for almost all influencing factors. At the same time, the update iteration of monitoring methods (micro-seismic, ground sound, separation, deformation, etc.) expands the development of the database, making it possible to obtain the data due to threat to life and cost of equipment, which is very difficult to obtain before. In the process of parameter selection at the input end, the method of combining lithology conditions, geological environment and monitoring data will gradually become the first choice for research. Finally, in the follow-up work collation and on-the-spot investigation, it mainly focuses on the existing problems of deep coal mines, explores the application potential of artificial intelligence in deep coal mine roadway engineering, puts forward the possible research focus and challenging problems in the future, and gives its own opinions.
Authors: Yu, B.; Wang, B.; Zhang, Y.
Construction Application Area: Ultra-deep coal mine roadway engineering, including rock mechanical strength prediction, surrounding rock stability evaluation, rock-burst prediction, roof fall risk assessment, and micro-seismic event monitoring
Methodology: This is a comprehensive literature review that systematically examines and categorizes the application of various AI tools (ANN, SVM, DT, RF, BN, etc.) in five key areas of ultra-deep coal mine roadway engineering. The paper analyzes high-level articles from top journals, evaluates the performance of different AI models, and discusses their applicability to complex mining conditions characterized by 'three highs and one disturbance' (high in-situ stress, high geothermal temperature, high karst water pressure, and strong disturbance phenomenon).
Research Goal: The paper aims to systematically review and evaluate the application of artificial intelligence tools in ultra-deep coal mine roadway engineering (>600m depth), focusing on five key directions: rock mechanical strength, surrounding rock stability, rock-burst phenomena, roof fall risks, and micro-seismic events, while identifying the most suitable AI tools for each application area.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of mature guidance for AI applications in deep coal mines due to missing database construction, complex mining environments with numerous input factors, and issues with universality and applicability of existing models. It also highlights the need for better integration of geological conditions, monitoring data, and field factors in predictive models.
Future Research: Future research should focus on: (1) developing databases combining geological conditions and real-time monitoring data (micro-seismic, deformation, ground sound) for more comprehensive parameter selection; (2) exploring applications in blasting operations (gangue block size prediction, blast-hole utilization rate), mechanical equipment (wear prediction, failure detection), and project management (personnel allocation, dynamic equipment management); and (3) improving model generalization and exploring highly-coupled machine learning models, particularly deep learning, for large-scale data processing in ultra-deep roadway engineering.
Results: The review found that ANN is the most widely applied tool (>98% of studies) with best performance in surrounding rock stability and roof fall risk (>90% accuracy). For rock mechanical strength prediction, tree-based models (RF, XGBoost) achieved R² >95% with large datasets (>1000 samples). Bayesian networks maintained >85% accuracy for rock-burst prediction across different sample sizes. CNN showed >90% accuracy in micro-seismic signal recognition and classification, though source location prediction requires further development.
AI Model Used: Multiple AI models including: Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), Support Vector Machines (SVM), Decision Trees (DT), Random Forest (RF), Bayesian Networks (BN), Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Deep Learning (LSTM, DNN), Gradient Boosting Machines (XGBoost, AdaBoost, CatBoost, GBDT, LightGBM), and various optimization algorithms (PSO, GA, GWO, WOA, TSA, etc.)
Data Types: The paper discusses various data types including: rock mechanical properties (UCS, BTS, density, porosity, wave velocity), geological parameters (RMR, in-situ stress, depth), micro-seismic monitoring data (waveforms, energy, magnitude, source parameters), field measurements (deformation, displacement, convergence), and image data (DIC images, waveform signals). Data volumes range from small samples (<100) to large-scale datasets (>10,000), with increasing emphasis on real-time monitoring data from multiple sources.

Title: Procedural lot generation for evolutionary urban layout optimization in urban regeneration decision support
Abstract: Decision support in urban regeneration planning involves fulfilment of complex design objectives that require a close collaboration between stakeholders, designers and planners. The objectives are to achieve computationally optimized design plans that are environmentally robust and sustainable. For the purpose of pre-planning analysis and optimization of such design models, the construction framework of the whole system development life cycle is simulated using specialist analysis tools. In the analysis and design phase of such a construction process, location allocation of built environment structures integrated with AI based urban assessment models remain a cumbersome task. With the advent of sophisticated computing hardware and 3D modelling techniques, it has become viable to visualize and develop simulation outcomes with ease over standard computers. However, creation of simulated outcomes with huge urban details still remains a daunting task. With the advent of knowledge-based data standards and mining techniques, possibilities have arisen to integrate procedural urban modelling frameworks with socio-economic deprivation assessment systems to create massive city models in order to improve sustainability and smart growth. The implementation of a procedural modelling framework offers a promising area of research in location allocation optimization of residential and public service structures for urban regeneration planning and collaboration purposes but is still largely stochastic in nature until now. The work presented in this article proposes an L-system-based automated urban layout generation module in addition to an evolutionary building placement optimization system for the purpose of urban regeneration decision support. The location allocation optimization of a highly accessible pedestrian and street network grid still remains a complex process especially with neighbourhoods suffering from severe socio-economic deprivation. Using an extension to 'L-systems', the module utilizes an 'mBPMOL' system to recursively subdivide an urban regeneration layout and later encode the layout to a genetic chromosome to iteratively place a range of urban structures to the lots. Moving further, the system implements a distance-based socio-economic deprivation minimization fitness function and assesses subsequent individual genetic solutions to search for an optimized layout. The development of such an online procedural framework is a very first attempt to employ the capabilities of procedural automation with evolutionary computation to automatically generate and optimize the placement of a large number of building models. The outcome achieved a set of relatively optimal solutions with evaluation based upon a distance-based fitness objective function of various public service structures. The solutions (layout plans) thus obtained offered a number of moderate to highly accessible alternatives. Development of such a system is meant to facilitate decision support and improve time efficiency among urban designers and planners. © 2011 The authors.
Authors: Yusuf, S.A.; Georgakis, P.; Nwagboso, C.
Journal: Journal of Information Technology in Construction
Construction Application Area: Urban regeneration planning, location allocation optimization of residential and public service structures, urban layout design
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (BIM, drones, laser scanning, robotics, digital twins, sensor networks)
Research Method: Mixed method (combining procedural modeling with quantitative optimization)
Methodology: The paper employs L-system-based procedural modeling (mBPMOL system) to recursively subdivide urban regeneration layouts into lots. These layouts are encoded as genetic chromosomes and optimized using genetic algorithms (GA) with a distance-based socio-economic deprivation minimization fitness function. The system uses multi-segment single-point crossover and mutation operators to evolve solutions across generations.
Research Goal: To develop an automated urban layout generation and optimization system that integrates procedural modeling with evolutionary computation to optimize the placement of residential units and public services in urban regeneration schemes while minimizing socio-economic deprivation.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the gap of integrating procedural urban modeling frameworks with AI-based optimization for decision support in urban regeneration. Current procedural systems are largely stochastic and lack integration with intelligent optimization methods for socio-economic factors.
Future Research: The work can be extended to incorporate multi-objective optimization with conflicting criteria (e.g., hospital placement near traffic hubs vs. industrial units near residential areas). Additional variables such as pollution, energy efficiency, and natural disaster impacts could also be integrated into the assessment framework.
Results: The system successfully generated optimized urban layouts over 100 generations with 30 individuals per generation, achieving fitness values up to 44,497. The optimized layouts showed well-organized placement of commercial and residential districts with efficient accessibility to public services, demonstrating superior performance compared to minimum fitness solutions.
AI Model Used: Genetic Algorithms (GA), L-systems (mBPMOL - modified Binary Propagating Map OL Systems with Markers), Neuro-fuzzy prediction model (ANFIS)
Data Types: Socio-economic deprivation data from UK National Neighbourhood Statistics (NeSS) covering crime, health, accessibility, and employment indicators measured on a scale of 0-32,482; urban regeneration grid layouts with lot assignments; network distance measurements between lots and center of population

Title: Artificial Intelligence-Driven Contractual Conflict Management in the AEC Industry: Mapping Benefits, Practice, Readiness, and Ethical Implementation Strategies
Abstract: Contracting parties in the architectural, engineering, and construction (AEC) industry often miss out on the benefits of constructive contractual conflicts and instead face adverse outcomes due to destructive ones. Studies suggest that embracing artificial intelligence (AI) can improve the management of destructive contractual conflicts. However, a gap remains in capturing a holistic view of applying an AI-driven contractual conflict management (CCM) system to manage these conflicts before they escalate into claims and disputes. Thus, this study aims to fill this research gap by answering three research questions: RQ1: What are the perceived benefits of using AI in managing contractual conflicts in the AEC industry? RQ2: What is the current state of the AEC industry regarding managing contractual conflicts and implementing an AI-driven CCM system to address these conflicts? RQ3: What core elements and strategies are needed to implement an AI-driven CCM system in the AEC industry? Data collected from 32 experts through semistructured interviews revealed that AI could aid in data analysis, conflict prediction, operational efficiency, decision analysis, negotiation, and collaboration processes within the industry. Despite facing several challenges in managing contractual conflicts, the industry is at a critical stage in implementing an AI-driven CCM system. This study identified and discussed the importance of implementing a legally framed, trustful, prevention-oriented, and human-centered AI-driven system. It also discussed the associated ethical issues and proposed frameworks to ensure a lawful, trustworthy, and human-centered AI-driven CCM system. Furthermore, it proposed an iterative implementation strategy, including formulation, context adaptation, piloting, optimization, and large-scale implementation. This study outlines theoretical and practical contributions and suggests potential research areas to address the study's limitations.
Authors: Zeberga, M.S.; Haaskjold, H.; Hussein, B.; Lædre, O.; Wondimu, P.A.
Construction Application Area: Contractual conflict management (CCM), including conflict prevention, mitigation, and resolution between contracting parties in construction projects
Methodology: The paper employed semistructured interviews with 32 experts from the AEC industry, AI sector, and construction law. Data was collected through digital interviews (mostly via Microsoft Teams), transcribed using speech-to-text software, and analyzed using an inductive coding approach with thematic analysis supported by NVivo 14 software.
Research Goal: The study aims to map the perceived benefits of AI in managing contractual conflicts in the AEC industry, assess the current state of conflict management practices and industry readiness for AI implementation, and identify core elements and strategies needed to implement an AI-driven CCM system.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the gap in capturing a holistic view of applying an AI-driven CCM system to manage contractual conflicts before they escalate into claims and disputes. It fills the need for understanding AI's benefits for conflict management, AEC's current practices, industry readiness, and implementation elements and strategies.
Future Research: The authors recommend extending the study by involving more informants and addressing enablers, barriers, competencies, and associated issues for AI-driven CCM implementation. Future research should also investigate scalability, interoperability with existing tools (BIM, DLT), cost-benefit analysis, and technical challenges related to data quality, algorithm transparency, and system functionality.
Results: The study identified that AI can benefit contractual conflict management through data analysis, conflict prediction, operational efficiency, decision support, negotiation facilitation, and collaboration enhancement. Despite challenges, the AEC industry is at a critical stage for implementing AI-driven CCM systems. Implementation should follow a legally framed, trustful, prevention-oriented, and human-centered approach with five iterative stages: formulation, context adaptation, piloting, optimization, and large-scale implementation.
AI Model Used: No Model
Data Types: The data consists of qualitative interview transcripts from 32 experts with diverse backgrounds including construction project management (22 experts), AI (6 experts), and construction law (4 experts). The experts represented various organization types (clients, contractors, consultants, academia) with experience ranging from 1 to over 20 years in the construction industry.

Title: Unified residential floor plan generation with multimodal inputs
Abstract: Residential design is a complex, open-ended task with low automation and limited collaboration between users and designers, creating a bottleneck in construction workflows. Existing models rely on only one or two input modalities, limiting flexibility and real-world applicability. To address this, the Multimodal Residential Plan Generation (M-RPG) framework is proposed, a unified generative framework supporting up to 11 multimodal inputs, including vector drawings in progress, graphs, room block images, and natural language. Trained on the large-scale multimodal residential plan dataset (Multi-RP) with over 20 million images and textual descriptions, M-RPG integrates domain-aware feature extraction and adaptive generation modules to produce high-quality, vectorized floor plans. It achieves strong performance across both single- and multi-modal settings. This work advances automation in construction by supporting flexible choices of modalities to meet diverse design needs and enhancing user-designer collaboration, thereby increasing design efficiency and promoting automation. It enables adaptable and practical AI-driven early-stage residential design, with outputs compatible with downstream simulation, evaluation, and BIM workflows.
Authors: Zeng, P.; Yin, J.; Zhang, M.; Li, J.; Zhang, Y.; Lu, S.
Construction Application Area: Residential floor plan design, architectural design automation, early-stage building design, BIM workflows
Construction Topic: Construction technologies (BIM, generative design / AI-driven floor plan generation)
Methodology: The paper proposes the M-RPG framework, a unified generative framework based on diffusion models (Stable Diffusion 2.1) with ControlNet architecture. It employs a two-stage generation strategy: first generating either residential layout or door-window-wall drawing adaptively based on contribution weights calculated from multimodal inputs, then generating the second component conditioned on the first. The framework includes domain-aware feature extraction modules (MFDA for vector drawings, deformable convolutions for N-graphs, 2D convolutions for spatial geometry) and a Channel Attention Mechanism for feature fusion.
