Research Goal: To develop a unified multimodal generative framework (M-RPG) that supports up to 11 different input modalities including vector drawings in progress, graphs, room blocks, and natural language to generate high-quality, vectorized residential floor plans, thereby advancing automation in construction and enhancing user-designer collaboration.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of comprehensive multimodal datasets for residential design (particularly vector drawings in progress) and the difficulty of integrating diverse data modalities within a single model. Existing models rely on only one or two input modalities, limiting flexibility and real-world applicability in personalized residential design.
Future Research: Future work includes generating floor plans directly in vector format without intermediate conversion, extending the framework beyond Manhattan-style layouts, incorporating real-world construction constraints (load-bearing walls, materials, cost, energy performance), developing multi-perspective evaluation protocols for layout feasibility and aesthetics, and creating interactive platforms for real-time user feedback and digital twin integration.
Results: The M-RPG model achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing models by an average of 105.4% across four metrics for graph input and showing improvements of 31.5%, 26.4%, 45.4%, and 27.7% for other input modalities. Expert evaluation confirmed minimal perceptual difference (46.2% selection rate vs. 50% ideal) between generated and real layouts, demonstrating the model's practical applicability.
AI Model Used: Stable Diffusion 2.1, ControlNet, Channel Attention Mechanism (CAM), Multi-Scale Fusion De-redundancy Attention (MFDA), Deformable Convolutions, T5 (for text encoding in comparison models), CLIP (for baseline comparisons), DALL-E 2 (for comparison), LayoutGPT (for comparison), Holodeck (for comparison)
Data Types: The Multi-RP dataset contains over 21 million images and textual descriptions from 96,760 residential floor plans, including vector drawings in progress (96,760×5×40 images), N-graphs (96,760×5 images), residential outlines (96,760×2 images), room blocks for five room types (96,760×5×2 images), and associated natural language descriptions covering completion rates, residential sizes, and room specifications.

Title: Extracting Worker Unsafe Behaviors from Construction Images Using Image Captioning with Deep Learning-Based Attention Mechanism
Abstract: Safety in the construction industry has always been a focus of attention. Existing methods of detecting unsafe behavior of workers relied primarily on manual detection. Not only did it consume significant time and money, but it also inevitably produced omissions. Currently, automated techniques for detecting unsafe behaviors rely only on the unsafe factors of workers' ontology to judge their behaviors, making it difficult to understand unsafe behaviors in complex scenes. To address the presented problems, this study proposed a method to automatically extract workers' unsafe behaviors by combining information from complex scenes - an image captioning based on an attention mechanism. First, three different sets of image captioning models were constructed using convolutional neural network (CNN), which are widely used in AI. These models could extract key information from complex scenes was constructed. Then, two datasets dedicated to the construction domain were created for method validation. Finally, three sets of experiments were conducted by combining the datasets and the three different sets of models. The results showed that the method could detect the worker's job type and output the interaction behavior between the worker and the target (unsafe behavior) based on the environmental information in the construction images. We introduced environmental information into the determination of workers' unsafe behaviors for the first time and not only output the worker's job type but also determine the worker's behavior. This allows the model output to be better for ergonomic analysis.
Authors: Zhai, P.; Wang, J.; Zhang, L.
Journal: Journal of Construction Engineering and Management
Construction Application Area: Construction safety - specifically detecting and identifying unsafe worker behaviors
Methodology: The paper develops an image captioning model using deep learning with attention mechanisms. Three CNN networks (ResNet101, ResNet152, and DenseNet161) are used as encoders for visual feature extraction, and LSTM networks are used as decoders to generate text descriptions. Two construction-specific datasets were created for training and validation, and the model performance was evaluated using BLEU scores, precision, and recall metrics.
Research Goal: To automatically extract workers' unsafe behaviors by combining key information from complex construction scenes using image captioning with attention mechanisms, enabling the identification of worker job types and their interactions with objects (unsafe behaviors) based on environmental information.
Gaps Addressed: Current automated techniques for detecting unsafe behaviors rely only on workers' ontology factors and fail to consider environmental information. Existing methods like target detection and image segmentation focus on specific target features while ignoring valuable contextual information about workers' relationships with tools and surrounding environment.
Future Research: Future work will focus on expanding the dataset to include more worker types and unsafe behaviors, improving model accuracy, and exploring practical applications by combining image captioning with target detection for video-based real-time safety monitoring. Integration with IoT systems, worker tagging, and sensors is also suggested for comprehensive spatial and temporal inference of unsafe behaviors.
Results: The proposed method achieved BLEU-4 scores of 0.628 (Dataset 1) and 0.6283 (Dataset 2), outperforming generic models by more than 0.1 on average. The model successfully identified worker job types and unsafe behaviors with an average accuracy of 0.7 or higher for unsafe behavior detection, with an average output time of 1.5 seconds per image.
AI Model Used: CNN (ResNet101, ResNet152, DenseNet161) for image encoding, LSTM for text generation/decoding, and Attention mechanism for feature weighting
Data Types: Two construction-specific image datasets were created: Dataset 1 contains 659 images with five worker behaviors and descriptions of worker-protective equipment relationships; Dataset 2 contains 5,000 images with five major worker job categories including unsafe behaviors. Images were collected through web crawling and live photos from construction sites.

Title: Intelligent real-time quality control for 3D-printed concrete with near-nozzle secondary mixing
Abstract: An intelligent monitoring method coupled with a feedback adjustment system was developed to accomplish real-time quality control of 3D printed concrete (3DPC). To achieve instant concrete rheology modification, a liquid accelerator was added in 3DPC before extrusion employing the near-nozzle secondary mixing strategy. Effects of accelerator contents on fresh and mechanical properties of 3DPC were explored, providing valuable insights for real-time regulation of material fluidity. Images of filaments captured during concrete 3D printing were categorized into 15 classes according to geometry and material characteristics. Subsequently, classification models were developed based on a lightweight modified Inception-ResNet, and its superiority was confirmed through a comparison with traditional VGG network. The classification ability, confusion harmful effects and the generalization performance of the models were evaluated. Eventually, guided by AI-aided quality assessment, real-time automated adjustments to extrusion speed and accelerator injection rate were realized, achieving in-situ quality control for automatic construction of 3DPC structures.
Authors: Zhang, H.; Tan, Y.; Hao, L.; Zhang, S.; Xiao, J.; Poon, C.S.
Construction Application Area: Quality control and monitoring in 3D concrete printing
Research Method: Mixed method (experimental testing and AI-based classification)
Methodology: The paper develops an intelligent real-time quality control system for 3D-printed concrete using near-nozzle secondary mixing with accelerators. Images of printed filaments are collected and classified into 15 classes based on geometry and material characteristics. A modified Inception-ResNet CNN model is developed and compared with VGG network for image classification. The system provides automated feedback to adjust extrusion speed and accelerator injection rate in real-time.
Research Goal: To develop an intelligent monitoring method coupled with a feedback adjustment system to accomplish real-time quality control of 3D printed concrete, enabling instant concrete rheology modification through near-nozzle secondary mixing with liquid accelerator.
Gaps Addressed: Previous research lacked real-time regulation for printing material through secondary near-nozzle mixing. There was insufficient research on intelligent feedback systems for adjusting printing parameters and material fluidity, particularly for 3DPC with secondary mixing processes.
Future Research: The paper does not explicitly mention specific future research directions in the conclusion section, but earlier sections suggest needs for implementing the system in larger-scale printing projects and potentially adapting the camera rotation system for non-linear printing paths.
Results: The modified Inception-ResNet achieved 93-94% classification accuracy across different target widths, outperforming VGG network by about 5%. The system successfully demonstrated real-time quality control in case studies, correcting geometry defects within 3 seconds for width adjustments and approximately 30 seconds for material fluidity adjustments through accelerator modification.
AI Model Used: Modified Inception-ResNet (Residual Inception Network) and VGG-16 network for comparison
Data Types: RGB images of 3D-printed concrete filaments captured during printing (3,354 total images), labeled into 15 classes based on width evaluation (extremely thin, thin, proposed, wide, extremely wide) and material fluidity (dry, proposed, fluid). Also includes experimental data on concrete fresh properties (penetration resistance, flowability, static yield stress) and mechanical properties (compressive strength).

Title: Intelligent decision-making of shield tunneling machine using deep reinforcement learning in complex geological environment
Abstract: To reduce constructional hazards of manual operation, the implementation an intelligent decision-making of the shield machine will be an inevitable development trend in the future. Hence, this paper suggests an intelligent decision-making approach using the Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3) agent, which is constructed under the Actor-Critic framework. A robust nonlinear correlation exists between sealed cabin pressure and tunneling parameters. Therefore, a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) training environment is established using the sealed cabin pressure mechanism model. TD3 agent is trained by exploring empirical data obtained from continuous interactions between TD3 agent and the environment. Critic networks calculate the evaluation value of the action. TD3 selects the minimum evaluation value to avoid overestimating the parameters. Actor network adopts delayed updating parameters to make control process more stable and accurate. Based on earth theory model, target pressure values under different earth conditions are calculated. An appropriate reward function is established based on the target pressure for current soil conditions. TD3 agent dynamically adjusts the propulsion speed and screw conveyor speed in real-time according to geological environment to achieve intelligent control of the sealed cabin earth pressure. The experimental results show that the decision-making error of this method is 0 Megapascal (MPa), and it better adapts to random soil variations and can complete nearly a hundred different soil transition tasks in complex environments. This study promotes technological advancements in tunnel engineering by introducing artificial intelligence (AI) technology into tunnel construction field, realizing deep integration of AI technology and civil engineering.
Authors: Zhang, W.; Liu, X.; Zhang, J.
Construction Application Area: Shield tunneling machine control, earth pressure balance management, sealed cabin pressure regulation, tunnel excavation safety
Methodology: The paper develops an intelligent decision-making approach using Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3) agent within an Actor-Critic framework. A deep reinforcement learning (DRL) training environment is established using sealed cabin pressure mechanism model. TD3 agent dynamically adjusts propulsion speed and screw conveyor speed through continuous environment interaction to maintain earth pressure balance.
Research Goal: To develop an intelligent decision-making system for shield tunneling machines that can autonomously control sealed cabin earth pressure by dynamically adjusting tunneling parameters in real-time according to changing geological conditions, thereby reducing construction hazards and achieving unattended operation.
Gaps Addressed: Traditional control methods rely on fixed parameters based on manual experience and cannot adapt to sudden soil changes, requiring manual intervention. Existing data-driven methods depend heavily on pre-labeled sample data and cannot account for real-time geological environment changes, limiting their applicability for intelligent decision-making.
Future Research: Future work could incorporate Kalman filtering and particle filtering algorithms to dynamically refine soil parameter estimates when precise geological data is unavailable. An LSTM/RNN-based delay compensation mechanism could be developed to handle delayed feedback in real-world conditions, and extended Kalman filtering with multi-sensor data fusion could enhance state parameter observability in complex construction environments.
Results: The method achieves zero error control (0.0008 MPa error in real test environments) with convergence within 20 seconds. It successfully completes nearly 100 different soil transition tasks in complex environments, demonstrating superior performance compared to LSTM and DDPG methods with 41.2% faster training time and 98.1% lower test error than DDPG.
AI Model Used: Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3), Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Deep Q-Network (DQN)
Data Types: Construction data from Beijing Subway Line 10 (23,316 rows, 227 columns) and Shanghai Subway Line 16 (1,200 rows, 257 columns) including operational parameters such as sealed cabin pressure, propulsion speed, screw conveyor speed, and geological parameters. Data collected by shield machine PLC systems during actual tunneling operations.

Title: Real-time and high-accuracy defect monitoring for 3D concrete printing using transformer networks
Abstract: Defects and anomalies during the 3D concrete printing (3DCP) process significantly affect final construction quality. This paper proposes a real-time, high-accuracy method for monitoring defects in the printing process using a transformer-based detector. Despite limited data availability, deep learning-based data augmentation and image processing techniques were employed to enable effective training of this complex transformer model. A range of enhancement strategies was applied to the RT-DETR, resulting in remarkable improvements, including a mAP50 of 98.1 %, mAP50–95 of 68.0 %, and a computation speed of 72 FPS. The enhanced RT-DETR outperformed state-of-the-art detectors such as YOLOv8 and YOLOv7 in detecting defects in 3DCP. Furthermore, the improved RT-DETR was used to analyze the relationships between defect count, size, and printer parameters, providing guidance for operators to fine-tune printer settings and promptly address defects. This monitoring method reduces material waste and minimizes the risk of structural collapse during the printing process.
Authors: Zhao, H.; Sun, J.; Wang, X.; Wang, Y.; Su, Y.; Wang, J.; Wang, L.
Construction Application Area: 3D concrete printing quality monitoring and defect detection
Methodology: The paper proposes a real-time defect monitoring system for 3D concrete printing using an enhanced RT-DETR (Real-Time Detection Transformer) model. The methodology involves data acquisition through industrial cameras, data augmentation using StyleGAN3 and image processing techniques (mosaic and mix-up), transfer learning, loss function optimization (F-EIoU), and training of the transformer-based detector to identify and measure breakpoint defects during the printing process.
Research Goal: The goal is to develop a real-time, high-accuracy automated defect detection and measurement system for 3D concrete printing that can identify breakpoint defects, provide quantitative measurements, and establish relationships between printer parameters and defect characteristics to enable timely corrective actions.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the limitations of manual visual inspection in 3D concrete printing, which suffers from low efficiency, subjectivity, inability to monitor areas beyond operator view, and lack of real-time quantitative analysis. It also addresses the challenge of limited dataset availability for training complex transformer models in the construction industry.
Future Research: Future work includes collecting data on other anomaly types (cracks, bulges, collapses, pits), evaluating detector adaptability to different printable concrete materials with various additives and mix designs, improving measurement accuracy for defects on inclined layers or with irregular geometries, increasing sample size to validate and enhance the relationship formula between printer parameters and defect lengths, and defining adjustable ranges of printer parameters to prevent other defects while reducing breakpoints.
Results: The improved RT-DETR achieved mAP50 of 98.1%, mAP50-95 of 68.0%, and 72 FPS, outperforming state-of-the-art detectors like YOLOv8 and YOLOv7. The defect measurement showed average relative errors of 2.2% for length and 2.5% for width. A predictive formula was developed relating printer parameters to breakpoint length with RMSE of 9.5 and MAE of 7.2.
AI Model Used: RT-DETR (Real-Time Detection Transformer), StyleGAN3, YOLOv8, YOLOv7, YOLOX, YOLOv5, DETR, ViTDet, Faster-RCNN, CenterNet
Data Types: The dataset consists of 1,000 images with 1,240 breakpoint defects and 300 negative samples (images without defects) captured during 3D concrete printing processes using a 90-frame industrial camera. Additionally, 12,000 virtual images (512×512 pixels) were generated using StyleGAN3 for data augmentation. Images were collected under controlled laboratory conditions with standardized concrete mix design and printing parameters.

Title: Decentralized artificial intelligence in construction using blockchain
Abstract: Alleviating cybersecurity risks associated with centralized AI training and implementation is a burgeoning challenge in the construction industry. This paper addresses two primary questions: (1) What is the knowledge of AI security vulnerability in construction, and (2) How can AI be decentralized using blockchain? To this end, this paper proposes a blockchain-AI integrated framework (BAII), enabling AI to be trained, verified, and applied on a decentralized blockchain. The framework has been successfully validated in an excavator pose recognition scenario, demonstrating acceptable latency and high performance with 95 % accuracy, 94 % precision, and 96 % recall. This research is pivotal for construction managers and IT security professionals, enhancing the reliability and safety of AI applications in construction. The decentralized AI (DAI) approach can also inspire further research into motivating constructors to contribute to AI modeling and training through incentive mechanisms in the blockchain.
Authors: Zheng, C.; Tao, X.; Liang, L.; Zukaib, U.; Tang, J.; Zhou, H.; Cheng, J.C.P.; Cui, X.; Shen, Z.
Construction Application Area: Construction site safety monitoring, excavator pose recognition, equipment operational safety, quality control, cost management, supply chain management, BIM-based applications
Methodology: The paper develops a blockchain-AI integrated (BAII) framework that enables AI to be trained, verified, and applied on a decentralized blockchain. It proposes a lifecycle security management mechanism for construction AI, develops an AI-enabled smart contract (AIESC) structure that embeds AI algorithms into blockchain smart contracts, and implements a reinforced signing strategy to ensure integrity of AI dependencies. The framework is validated through an excavator pose recognition scenario using IMU sensors.
Research Goal: The paper aims to address cybersecurity risks associated with centralized AI training and implementation in construction by proposing a blockchain-AI integrated framework that enables decentralized AI management throughout its lifecycle, enhancing reliability, safety, and transparency of AI applications in construction.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses two major gaps: (1) limited knowledge of AI security vulnerabilities in construction across different phases, and (2) insufficient integration of AI and blockchain where only partial AI data is managed by blockchain while AI models and training processes remain off-chain, risking data manipulation and lacking transparency.
Future Research: Future research will focus on addressing data governance challenges including enhancing data privacy and security through tiered access control and end-to-end encryption, improving data quality and standardization with better preprocessing capabilities, developing interoperability interfaces supporting open standards like openBIM and openGIS, and designing scalable multi-chain architectures to ensure system reliability and performance across diverse and large-scale construction projects.
Results: The BAII framework successfully validated in excavator pose recognition achieved 95% accuracy, 94% precision, and 96% recall with acceptable latency (average 1.186s for training, 1.193s for application, 0.088s for reading). The framework demonstrated that AI can be trained and deployed entirely on-chain while maintaining performance comparable to traditional centralized AI, with all training and application records stored immutably on blockchain for traceability and transparency.
AI Model Used: ROSE (Robust Online Self-Adjusting Ensemble) algorithm based on Hoeffding Tree algorithm for streaming data classification and excavator pose recognition
Data Types: The paper uses IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit) sensor data including acceleration (X, Y, Z axes) and angular rate collected from excavators at 100 Hz sampling frequency. A total of 86,994 data points were collected, including 52,233 stationary data points, 17,487 vibration data points, and 17,274 boom motion data points. The data underwent preprocessing including noise reduction, dimensionality reduction, and shuffling.

Title: Graph neural networks for classification and error detection in 2D architectural detail drawings
Abstract: The assessment and classification of architectural sectional drawings is critical in the architecture, engineering, and construction (AEC) field, where the accurate representation of complex structures and the extraction of meaningful patterns are key challenges. This paper established a framework for standardizing different forms of architectural drawings into a consistent graph format, and evaluated different Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) architectures, pooling methods, node features, and masking techniques. This paper demonstrates that GNNs can be practically applied in the design and review process, particularly for categorizing details and detecting errors in architectural drawings. The potential for visual explanations of model decisions using Explainable AI (XAI) is also explored to enhance the reliability and user understanding of AI models in architecture. This paper highlights the potential of GNNs in architectural data analysis and outlines the challenges and future directions for broader application in the AEC field.
Authors: Ko, J.; Lee, D.
Construction Application Area: Architectural detail drawing classification and error detection, design review process, quality assurance/quality control (QA/QC) reviews
Methodology: The paper establishes a two-stage framework: (1) parametrically generating 12,000+ wall section drawings with intentional errors, then converting them from raster images to graph representations using Mask2Former segmentation model; (2) training and evaluating different Graph Neural Network (GNN) architectures (GCN and GATv2) with various pooling methods (ASAP and SAGPooling), node features, and masking techniques to classify wall types and detect errors. The study also explores explainable AI (XAI) methods including GNNExplainer and SubgraphX to interpret model decisions.
Research Goal: The paper aims to develop a framework for standardizing architectural drawings into graph format and demonstrate that GNNs can be practically applied for categorizing architectural detail drawings and detecting errors, while exploring the potential of XAI to improve reliability and user understanding of AI models in architecture.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses the lack of consistent representation of diverse architectural drawing datasets, the inability of existing methods to infer high-level semantic information from recognized objects, and the need for comprehensive error detection beyond manual inspection in architectural drawings that exist in various formats and styles.
Future Research: Future work should expand the dataset to include more wall types and diverse architectural drawing styles from different regions; develop domain-specific XAI methods tailored to architectural data characteristics; enhance graph generation techniques to handle noisy data; explore advanced GNN architectures and evaluation methods; and test the model on older buildings or regions where BIM adoption is slower.
Results: The GCNConv model with SAGPooling achieved the highest wall-type classification F1 score of 0.912 without node features, while error detection tasks achieved F1 scores around 0.74-0.75 for the best configurations. GNNExplainer provided more interpretable results for error detection than SubgraphX, particularly for identifying spacing errors, missing layers, and layer arrangement errors, though overall XAI performance (F1 scores around 0.35) indicates significant room for improvement.
AI Model Used: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs): GCN (Graph Convolutional Network), GATv2 (Graph Attention Network v2); Pooling methods: ASAP (Adaptive Structure-Aware Pooling), SAGPooling (Self-Attention Graph Pooling); Segmentation: Mask2Former; XAI methods: GNNExplainer, SubgraphX
Data Types: The dataset consists of 34,800 parametrically generated 2D architectural wall section drawings across four types (CMU, Wood, Steel-framed, and Wood-framed walls with anchored/adhered veneer), including 24,000 normal samples and 10,800 error samples with three error types. Each drawing exists in three formats: colored rendered images, black-and-white images, and material ID images for ground truth segmentation, all converted into graph representations with nodes representing architectural components and edges representing their physical connections.

Title: Semantic enrichment of BIM models for construction cost estimation in pumped storage hydropower using industry foundation classes and interconnected data dictionaries
Abstract: Accurate construction cost estimation is vital for ensuring profitable project execution for both owners and contractors. However, traditional methods often rely on disparate engineering data, leading to inaccuracies and inconsistencies among stakeholders. To address this, this paper introduces an automated system that semantically enriches Building Information Modeling (BIM) models by integrating Industry Foundation Classes (IFC) with interconnected data dictionaries. By automating the cost estimation procedure, the proposed system streamlines decision-making, demonstrating significant advantages over traditional project management frameworks in a real-world pumped-storage project during the preconstruction phase. The findings also highlight future research directions for incorporating real-time engineering quotas and fluctuating market prices to enhance construction practices.
Authors: Zhang, S.; Zhang, S.; Liu, H.; Wang, C.; Zhao, Z.; Wang, X.; Yan, L.
Construction Application Area: Construction cost estimation, particularly for pumped storage hydropower (PSH) projects
Methodology: The paper develops a knowledge-based system that semantically enriches BIM models using Industry Foundation Classes (IFC) and interconnected data dictionaries. The methodology includes: (1) modeling business processes using BPMN to identify information requirements, (2) developing an ontology-based semantic enrichment framework to link BIM data with domain-specific quota standards and market prices, and (3) implementing a prototype cloud-based system with microservice architecture that automates quantity takeoff and cost estimation through semantic web technologies (SPARQL queries, Apache Jena).
Research Goal: The paper aims to automate construction cost estimation for pumped storage hydropower projects by semantically enriching BIM models with domain-specific information through interconnected data dictionaries, thereby addressing the challenge of information fragmentation and improving accuracy and efficiency in cost management during the preconstruction phase.
Gaps Addressed: The paper addresses three key knowledge gaps: (1) unclear project-level information requirements for scoping application development, (2) semantic gaps in linking BIM data with quota standards and cost regulations, and (3) lack of practical references for reengineering existing manual workflows in large-scale hydroelectric infrastructure projects. Traditional methods suffer from data silos, manual errors, and time-consuming processes due to scattered information across heterogeneous systems.
Future Research: Future research should focus on: (1) incorporating real-time engineering quotas and fluctuating market prices to enhance construction practices, (2) extending knowledge management to the construction supply chain including subcontractors and suppliers, (3) implementing electronic procurement to improve cost estimation during future project phases, and (4) expanding the automated system to more pilot projects and broader AEC sectors to facilitate industry-wide productivity improvements.
Results: The automated system demonstrated significant improvements over traditional manual workflows, reducing total delivery time from 36 days to 4.2 days primarily through automated team coordination. The case study on the Fumin PSH project successfully validated the feasibility of the approach, with the northern route recommended as the most cost-effective option with estimated indirect expenses of approximately 5,549,099.68 CNY. The system eliminated errors from document fragmentation and improved estimation accuracy through enhanced data consistency.
AI Model Used: Semantic web technologies including ontology-based reasoning, SPARQL query language, and Apache Jena framework. The system uses knowledge-based AI rather than machine learning models, leveraging Semantic Web Rule Language (SWRL) rules and the Interconnected Data Dictionary Ontology (IDDO) for semantic enrichment and automated inference.
Data Types: The paper uses multiple types of data including: (1) BIM model data in IFC format containing geometric and semantic information, (2) construction quota standards (NB/T 11010-2022, GB/T 51269-2017), (3) market price dictionaries from the China Renewable Energy Engineering Institute, (4) classification codes for hydraulic structures and construction activities, and (5) project-specific data from the Fumin PSH project including quantities for materials, labor, and equipment for activities like rock excavation, tunnel construction, and shaft drilling.

Title: Lifecycle framework for AI-driven parametric generative design in industrialized construction
Abstract: In the Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AEC) industry, design processes remain fragmented across architectural, structural, and mechanical domains, limiting integration and optimization opportunities throughout building lifecycles. This paper investigates how artificial intelligence can be leveraged to create a comprehensive framework for parametric generative design in industrialized construction that integrates multiple design disciplines and optimization criteria. The methodology employs knowledge graph question answering (KGQA) enabled by large language models (LLMs) to acquire design requirements and constraints, implements multi-objective optimization algorithms to balance competing criteria, and establishes a three-tier priority hierarchy to resolve conflicts in cross-domain design processes. The framework demonstrates significant improvements in a real-world case study, achieving 15.8 % reduction in lifecycle costs, 21.2 % decrease in energy consumption, and significantly reducing preliminary design modelling time. These findings provide valuable insights for AEC practitioners seeking to implement human-AI collaborative design workflows and illustrate pathways for integrating domain-specific knowledge with advanced AI systems.
Authors: Gao, M.Y.; Li, C.; Petzold, F.; Tiong, R.L.K.; Yang, Y.
Construction Application Area: Architectural design, structural design, MEP (mechanical, electrical, and plumbing) design, industrialized construction, building lifecycle management, design optimization, energy analysis, sustainability assessment
Methodology: The methodology employs knowledge graph question answering (KGQA) enabled by large language models (LLMs) to acquire design requirements and constraints. Multi-objective optimization algorithms (NSGA-II, genetic algorithms, random forest regressor) balance competing criteria. A three-tier priority hierarchy is established to resolve conflicts in cross-domain design processes, integrating architectural, structural, and MEP domains.
Research Goal: To develop a comprehensive AI-driven framework for parametric generative design in industrialized construction that integrates multiple design disciplines (architectural, structural, MEP) and optimization criteria across building lifecycles. The framework aims to streamline design processes, reduce costs, enhance sustainability, and improve overall building performance.
Gaps Addressed: Addresses fragmentation across architectural, structural, and mechanical domains, which limits integration and optimization throughout building lifecycles. Most existing frameworks focus on single disciplines or limited optimization criteria, lacking comprehensive lifecycle management and dynamic interdisciplinary coordination.
Future Research: Future research should develop more extensive design datasets with parametric information about standardized building components. Computational efficiency challenges should be addressed through hierarchical optimization strategies and advanced algorithms. Domain-specific fine-tuning of large language models for broader building codes and industry standards is needed.
Results: The case study demonstrates a 15.8% reduction in lifecycle costs and 21.2% decrease in energy consumption. Preliminary design modeling time was significantly reduced from 3-4 hours to approximately 5 minutes. Model 02-02 achieved 35.7% reduction in both cost and carbon emissions compared to the least efficient variant.
AI Model Used: Large Language Models (LLMs including GPT-4, BERT, Ollama), Random Forest Regressor, Genetic Algorithms (GA), NSGA-II (Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II), Knowledge Graph Question Answering (KGQA), Deep Learning (DL), Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN, CoGAN), Reinforcement Learning (RL), BiGRU, Transformer-XL, sentence transformers
Data Types: Geometric, semantic, and spatial data from BIM models and IFC files; textual documents and images for site conditions; spreadsheets and databases for simulations; building codes and regulations from Singapore; material properties, cost data, and carbon emission factors from Singapore Building Carbon Calculator (SBCC); design variants with parameters including floor area, height, aspect ratio, window-to-wall ratio.
